# PROTOCOL ANALYSIS: Verbal Reports as Data

K. Anders Ericsson and Herbert A. Simon

Although psychologists have often employed the technique of protocol analysis—or the use of the subject's own verbal reports as data—in exploring cognitive processes, the technique's nature and reliability have been poorly understood. This book finally puts protocol analysis on firm ground by examining its underlying assumptions, techniques, and limitations. It adresses such key questions as what sorts of mental events are reliable; what the role of the investigator's interpretations should be in helping to understand such data; and what mental events cannat be explained by protocols.

The authors describe a general theory of cognitive processes and structure, which, they argue, accounts for verbalization and verbal reports. The theory i presented in the forof an infor mation processing modei. Major issues surrounding the use and validity of verbal reports are taken up and empirical studies are discussed within the framework of the model. The book focuses on reports of cognitive processes, but the concepts and models it employs can be extended to such areas of verbal behavior as psychophysics, survey design, and measurement of personality traits.

## FOREWORD

In 1980, the authors proposed in The Psychological Review a theory on verbal thinking-aloud protocols as data, and provided substantial empirical support for the theory. The response to this article was so lively, and the interest in protocols that it revcaled so widesprcad that wc decided to enlarge our inquiry into a book. Thc book would makc the thcory as explicit and rigorous as possible, would cxplorce thc cmpirical evidence morc thorqughly than we could in a single journal article, and would discuss the techniques of protocol analysis.This is that book.

The idcas sct forth in this book have taken form over many years, beginning with a brief mecting bctwcen the authors in the Spring of 1975. Many pcople have spent countless hours discussing these issues with us in these years, and we would like especially to acknowledge William Chase, Gunnar Goude, and Robert Neches. A number of colleagues and friends have been kind cnough to read drafts of the book and offer thcir suggestions for improving it. Among then are Micki Chi, Donald Fiske, Goran Hagert, Barbara Hayes-Roth, Walter Kintsch, Allen Newell, Stellan Ohlsson, Gary Olson, Richard Nisbctt, and Ed Smith. We wish to thank them warmly and at the same time absolve them from responsibility for in fclicities of the product.

We are also grateful to Betty and Henry Stanton of Bradford Books, who have greatly facilitated thc transformation of manuscript into a bound book, and who have bcen understanding when deadlines have not always been met. Finally, we owc spccial thanks to Therese Groden, who has assumed responsibility for producing camera-ready copy from the raw manuscript, and in the course of doing so, has found her way through the thickcts of word-processing systems and printers at Carnegie-Mellon University.

Work on this book has becn aided by grants from the National Institute of Mental Health and thc Alfred P. Sloan Foundation.

K. Anders Ericsson

Herbert A. Simon

PROTOCOL ANALYSIS

# INTRODUCTION AND SUMMARY

After a long period of timc during which stimulus-response relations were at the focus of attention, rescarch in psychology is now sccking to understand in detail the mechanisms and internal structure of cognitive processes that produce thesc rclations. In the limiting case, we would likc to have process models so cxplicit that thcy could actually produce the predicted behavior from the information in the stimulus.

This concern for the course of the cognitive processes has revived interest in finding ways to incrcasc the temporal density of observations so as to reveal intermediate stages of thc processes. Increasingly, investigators record the directions of the subject's gaze (cye movements), and the intermediate bchaviors (movements or physical manipulations of stimulus material) that precede the solution or criterion performance. Since data on intermediate processing are costly to gather and analyze, it is important to consider carefully how such data can be interprcted validly, and what contribution they can make to our undcrstanding of the phenomena under study.

One means frequently used to gain in formation about the course of the cognitive processes is to probe the subjects' internal states by verbal methods. These methods are the topic of this monograph.

## USING VERBAL REPORTS: SOME ISSUES

Therce are several issues that we must deal with if we arc to use subjects' reports as fundamental data in psychological expcriments. First, we must respond to the strong doubts that have been expressed by many psychologists in the past about the suitability of subjects' verbalizations as scicntific data. Second, we must consider the processing that must

## 2 Protocol Analysis

take place in order to transform subjects' bchaviors (whether verbal or not) into data. Third, we must cxaminc how the cncoding of bchavior into data can bc madc objcctivc and univocal, so that the resulting data will be "hard" and not "soft." Fourth, wc must bc cxplicit about the theoretical presuppositions that are neccssarily cmbcddcd in the cncoding process. Finally, wc must specify the processes that allow us to go backward from the data to thc bchavior and thencc to infcrences about the subjccts' thought processes.

We offer a few comments on cach of these five issucs. They will reappcar frequcntly as recurrent themes throughout the monograph.

## Doubts About Verbal Data

Since the triumph of bchaviorism over "introspectively" oricnted compcting vicwpoints, verbal reports havc bccn suspcct as data. More preciscly, bchaviorism and allied schools of thought havc been schizophrenic about the status of verbalizations as data. On the one hand, verbal responses (or key punches that arc psychologically indistinguishable from verbal responscs, except that thcy arc made with the finger instead of the mouth) provide thc basic data in standard expcrimental paradigms. In a conccpt attainment expcriment, thc subjects say (or signal) "yes" or "no" when a possible instancc is presented to them. In a problem solving cxperincnt, they rcport thc answer when they find it. In a rote vcrbal lcarning experiment, they say "I)ΛX" when the stimulus syllable "CEF" is prcscnted. Thc actual performance measures coinmonly uscd-latcncies and numbers of itcms correct— are derived from these responses, and the former depend for their validity on the vcridicality of the latter.

On thc other hand, modcrn psychology has bcen dubious about verbalizations produced by subjects along the route to thcir solutions or final responses. Even more dubious has been the status of responses to experimenter probes or retrospectivc answers to questions about prior bchavior. All of these sorts of verbal bchavior are frequently dismissed as variants of the discrcdited process of introspection (Nisbett & Wilson, 1Introspcction, it has generally been argucd, may bc uscful for the discovery of psychological proccsses; it is worthless for verification. As Lashley (1923, p. 352) said, in a vigorous and widely cited attack on the method, "introspection may make the prcliminary survey, but it must be followed by the chain and transit of objective measurement."

## Extracting Data from Behavior

The notion that verbal reports provide possibly interesting but only informal information, to be verified by other data, has affected the ways in which verbalizations are collected and analyz.ed. If the purpose of obtaining verbal reports is mainly to gencrate hypothescs and ideas, investigators necd not concern themsclves (and gcnerally have not concerned themsclves) with methodological questions about data collection. As a result, there is litle publishcd litcrature on such issucs, the datagathering and data-analysis mcthods actually uscd vary tremendously, and thc details of these methods arc reportcd skctchily in rescarch publications that make use of such data.

If we are to make rapid and continuing progress in understanding human cognitive processes, this state of affairs is wholly unsatisfactory. In the first place, no clear guidclincs are provided to distinguish illegitimate "introspection" from the many forms of verbal output that are routinely treated as dataas passing the chain and transit test (see the examples above). On what thcorctical or practical grounds do we distinguish bctween the subject's "yes" or "no" in a concept attainment experimcnt and his assertion that the hypothesis he is entertaining is "small ycllow circle"? In the sccond place, no distinctions are made among such diverse forms of verbalization as thinking-aloud (TA) protocols, retrospective responses to specific probes, and the classical introspcctive reports of trained observers. All are jointly and loosely condemned as "introspection."

## Soft versus Hard Data

Some investigators call verbal reports and verbal descriptions "soft data" in contrast to simple bchavioral measures like latency or corrcctness of response, which are rcferred to as "hard." What docs this distinction mcan? In scicncc one would like to maintain as clcar a separation as possible between data and thcory. Data are supposed to derivc directly from obscrvation; theories are supposcd to account for, cxplain, and predict these observation-bascd data. IData are "hard" whcn there is intersubjective agreement that thcy correspond to the facts of the observed behavior.

Even psychoanalytically or existentially oriented psychologists will accept response latencies as data—cven though being possibly irrclevant data for cxplaining bchavior. When, however, an analyst codes a fivesccond description of a dream as "oral fixation," many psychologists would argue that this encoding is not a datum but a subjective interprctation of the data (i.e., of the verbal description of the dream). Surely, theory-laden inferences were required to derive the encoding from the verbal protocol. Data are regarded as "soft" to thc degree that they incorporatc such inferences, especially whcn the theorctical premises and rules of inference are themsclves not completcly explicit and objective. The problem with "soft" data is that diffcerent intcrprctcrs making different inferences will not agrce in their encodings, and cach interpreter is likely, wittingly or not, to arrive at an interpretation that is favorable to his thcoretical orientation.

The hard-soft distinction is orthogonal to the distinction betwcen verbal and non-verbal. The samc problems of infcrencc can cmerge in observers' attempts to understand non-verbal events (c.g. scquences of physical movements, pieccs of music). Such cvents may rcquire as much interprctation as is requircd to understand verbal sequences.

Technological advances have cnhanced our ability to treat verbal protocols as hard data. Until tape rccorders were gcncrally available, it was common practice for experimenters to takc sclective notcs of verbalizations, paraphrasing and omitting whatevcr was "unimportant." In analyzing such notcs further, it was impossiblc to distinguish the inferences from the original verbalizations. Using cncodings of verbal protocols as data has oftcn bcen made cvcn more difficult because the thcorics cmployed, explicitly or implicitly, in thc encoding werc formulated in very general terms. The scarch for general mechanisms also led to overall interprctations of cntire protocols with little concern for encoding and explicating individual protocol statements.

More recent rescarch bascd on explicit information processing models of the cognitive processes has causcd thinking-aloud verbalizations to be vicwed in a ncw light. It is now standard proccdure to make carcful verbatim transcripts of the recorded tapcs, thus preserving the raw data in as "hard" a form as could bc wished. At the same time, information processing models of the cognitive processes provide a basis for making the encoding process cxplicit and objective, so that the theorctical presuppositions cntering into that process can be examined objectively.

## Theoretical Presupposition in Encoding

Clyde Coombs, in his book A Theory of Data, shows that raw data go through a typical sequence of steps on the route from initial obscrvation to the edited and encoded form in which thcy arc used to test thcories or make predictions. These steps, which arc not neutral with respect to theory, can bc secn in the processing of protocol data as they can with other kinds of data. At the first step, theory dclimits a small portion of the universe of potentially obscrvable bchavior as being relevant. This judgment of relevance determines what behaviors should bc recorded. At the next stcp, thesc behaviors are cncoded in a manncr that is again determined on thcoretical grounds.

In the case of verbal behavior, the process bcgins with a taperecording, containing essentially all thc auditory cvents that occurred during the cxperimental session. In producing from the tape a written transcript, some selection is required. After the temporal information, repetitions, and stress have bcen uscd to segment and parse the verbal stream, most of this information is usually climinated from the transcript, except as it is captured by punctuation. We will refer to this transcription step as preprocessing.

At the next stcp, the preprocessed scgments are cncoded into the terminology of the theoretical model. This is oftcn achicved by first determining coding categories, a priori, and then having human judges make the coding assessments. If each of the scgments is to be treated as an independent datum, then the encoding of that segment must be made on thc basis of the information containcd in it, independently of the surrounding scgments. In Chapter 6 of this book, wc will discuss at some length mcthods for carrying out this kind of local encoding, and the conditions that must be met to make it possible.

Verbal protocols have bcen analyzed in two rather different ways. One method claims not to rcquire the analysis of meanings, while the other docs require it. In the first kind of analysis, subject and experimenter have agreed, by prior instruction, upon spccific signals, which may be specch signals or button presscs, for their communication. These signals arc mostly arbitrary— a subject could say "cef" instead of "yes"; communication is possible only because of thc agrccmcnt cstablished between subject and experincnter. To analyzc the recorded verbalizations under these conditions, the experimenter has only to catcgorize each speech signal into one of the agrccd-upon categorics. In theory, if not in practice, a coder should not even need to know the subject's language—assuring that no mcaningful analysis or inferencing is involvcd. A large number of paradigms in psychology use this kind of analysis. For cxample, studies using scales and multiple-choice alternatives can all be scen as instances of this method.

In the sccond kind of analysis, thc observed vcrbalizations are analyzed in terms of thcir mcanings. Evcn in this casc, the theory guiding the analysis limits thc cncoding to sclcctcd aspccts and fcatures rather than the full mcaning of the verbalization. For example, in a typical conccpt attainment task, cach instancc or stimulus can bc represented as a uniquc combination of fcatures. Each distinct conccpt can be represented by somc particular configuration of fcatures. Then cncoding sinply requires the mapping of the verbalizations onto thesc concepts and fcatures—usually a rather uncquivocal matter. Although the space of logically possible different conccpts may bc vcry large, it is severcly limitcd comparcd with thc variability of natural languagc. 'Ihus a verbalization like "rcd circles are ccf's" can normally bc cncodcd as identical with "blood-colored round ones are cef's."

The context of a particular thcory and cxperiment greatly constrains the range of possible interprctation and allows thc meaningful analysis of verbalizations to be sclcctive and incomplctc. If a theory of concept attainment is limited to the language of hypotheses, many verbalizations will not be encodcd at all— statcments like, "I wonder what I should do. I'll just guess on this onc." Many cxamples can bc cited of this kind of mcaningful analysis, whecre vcrbalizations are mapped onto a priori formal alternatives. The analysis of memory for mcaningful text has bcen studicd by Kintsch (1974) and many others. Newell and Simon (1972) analyzed tasks, idcntifying formally defincd knowledge states in terms of which subjccts' thinking-aloud protocols could be cncoded.

Many analyses of verbalizations do not fit thc above scheme, including most analyscs that scck to arrivc at an understanding of the verbalizations. In less formal kinds of analysis, the cncoding scheme is not defined formally and a priori, but the search for interpretations procceds in parallel with the scarch for an appropriate model or theory. Wc recognize clearly the need for and value of such interactive processes in the scarch for thcories in new domains, but in our own account here wc will be concerncd primarily with situations wherc the theoretical terms are fixed before the actual cncoding begins.

## Inferring Thought Processes From Behavior

It is sometimes believed that using verbal data implies accepting the subjects' interpretation of them or of the cvents that are reported.This issue of trust has its origins in our everyday experience and use of language. In order to communicate effctively with other people, we accept their word for many facts. If somcone says that he has bought a ncw car, wc generally accept his statement as truc instead of asking him to produce thc sales contract or a receipt. In a similar vcin we trust people-at least our friends—to answer questions correctly and to give us the best advice they can. However, if the issuc is important to us or we suspect ulterior motives in the responses, wc may demand more details and may review all the available cvidencc oursclves. The samc thing holds in scientific rescarch; fcw scicntists will accept another scientist's claim of finding conclusive evidence for ESP without wanting an independent review of the evidence.

Subjects' reports of their own mental states and mental processes raisc slightly different issues of trust. According to a naive theory of consciousness, subjects have the sole direct access to thcir own mental states and processes. Thc subjective fceling of onc's ability to report one's own mental expcricnces vcridically is strong. For a great many reasons, this confidence is not shared by expcrimental psychologists, who have shown that under numcrous circumstances such sclf-reports are unreliable.

However, the issuc of thc reliability of self-reports can (and, we think, should) be avoided entirely. Thc rcport "X" nccd not be used to infer that X is true, but only that the subject was able to say "X"—(i.e., had the information that cnabled him to say "X.") By following this path, wc can even show that therc is an inverse rclation bctwcen how much subjects need to be trusted and how much information they verbalize. For the more information conveyed in their responses, the more difficult it becomes to construct a model that will produce precisely those responses adventitiously—hence the more confidence we can place in a model that does predict them.

Consider, for example, the following possible interchanges bctween experimenter and subject:

1. Do you know the name of the capital of Sweden? Yes.

2 Which of these thrce, Oslo, Stockholm, or Copcnhagen, is the capital of Sweden? Stockholm.

3. Name the capital of Sweden. Stockholm.

4. (A retrospective rcport as to how thc subject arrived at an answer to Question 1): First I tried to picture where Sweden is located on a map of Europe, then (Oslo came to mind, but I remembered that it is the capital of Norway. Then Stockholm popped up and I remembered that is where the Nobel prizes are awarded: then I felt sure I could answer "yes. "

In the first case we have to trust the subject if we want to infer that he actually knows the capital, whcrcas in thc third casc it is unlikcly that hc could gencrate the correct namc unless it wcrc accessible from memory. The primary difference betwcen sccond and third cascs is that, for the second, onc could conccive of a number of processes other than memory rctricval (c.g., guessing) that would account for thc response. The fourth response, the retrospective report, also verifics that the subject has the name in memory togcther with somc rcdundant information about it that gives him confidcnce in his answer. Of course we do not havc to believe that he has given a veridical report of the process whereby he generatcd the name, although there is nothing implausible about the sequence of associations he reports.

Consider next a morc controversial cxample, which has played a role in the psychological literature on lcarning without awarcness. After a lcarning expcrimcnt, the experimenter asks the subjccts whcther they wcre aware of any relation betwecn the stimuli and responses, on the one hand, and thc rcward contingencies on thc othcr. Yes/no responses to this question are informative only if wc trust the subjccts. If a subject, however, describes the stimulus-responsc contingcncy for reward, we can be reasonably certain that he had access to this information while he was learning. On the other hand, if a subject is unable to report anything about the contingency, we cannot conclude that he wasn't aware of it during the learning process-we have solid evidence ncither for nor against awareness during thc cxpcriment. Iatcr, wc will discuss the problem of making infcrences from reports of lack of information.

Thesc cxamples illustrate that the information externalized in verbal responses often provides thc experimenter with data that climinate the need for trust in the subjcct. The cxamples also show that verbal reports may bc gcncratcd in many ways. To understand the reports, we must understand the proccsses by which they wcrc generated. In none of these respects do data from verbal reports differ from data based on other types of observations.

## Some Basic Assumptions

We can now summarize the basic assumptions that set the stage for our further explorations. Most fundamentally, we sec verbal behavior as one type of recordable behavior, which should be observed and analyzed like any other bchavior. The cognitivc processes that generate verbalizations arc a subset of the cognitivc processes that generatc any kind of recordable responsc or behavior. Hencc, we would look for the same kind of "mechanical" and complete process description of verbal bchavior as of other kinds of behavior, and we would not accept magical or privilcged proccsses as explanations for verbalizations.

Whether one can and should trust subjects' verbal rcports is not a matter of faith but an cmpirical issue on a par with the issuc of validating other types of bchavior, likc eye fixations or motor behavior. A single invalid verbal report should not force us to discard analysis of verbal reports generally. Indccd, this monograph will undcrtake to build a theory of verbalization, so that we can thcn specify when, where, and under what kinds of instructions informative verbal reports can be obtained from subjects.

Postulating that the cognitive processes underlying verbalization are a subsct of all cognitive processes implics that verbalization must comply with the constraints that have bcen identified, cxperimentally, to govern all cognitive processes. Thesc information processing constraints will provide powerful guidelines for our attempts to specify how observed verbalizations could have been gencratcd. We wish to account for verbally reported information by proposing a processing model sufficicntly powerful to regcneratc that information.

## Plan of Attack

Our first task is to describe a general thcory of cognitive processes and structurc, which, wc argue, accounts for verbalizations and verbal reports. For rcasons that have alrcady becn statcd, thc analysis must be carricd out within a framework of thcory. This framcwork must be sufficicntly general to pcrmit us to rclatc, within a unificd pcrspective, all thc kinds of data that arc commonly uscd in psychological cxperiments.

Usually, in choosing bctwcen theories, we want to pick the strongest one—thc onc that will make the strongest predictions. In the present case, where the theory we choose will influence the way in which wc encode and analyze our data, we want to pick the weakcst and most "ncutral" one that can do the job. The fewer controversial assumptions wc incorporate in the theory, the less we will bc involved in the circularity of using theory-ladcn data to test our theorics. Nevertheless, there appears to be no way of processing data that does not incorporate some thcorctical assumptions about the systcm and processcs that generatcd the data. Our particular stratcgy will be to set forth the theory in its most gencral. hcnce lcast controversial, form first, then add more specific hypotheses where they arc required.

After presenting the theory as an information processing model of cognitivc processes, we will survcy thc litcrature on verbal reporting and dcrive from it a taxonomy of rcporting procedurcs. Wc will follow this survcy with an historical review of carlier approachcs to verbal reports. We will then take up the major issues surrounding the use and validity of vcrbal reports, discussing the empirical studics within thc framcwork of a more detailed information processing model.

## THE PROCESSING MODEL

Our purpose in presenting a spccific processing model is to aid us in interpreting verbal data obtaincd from subjects and the relation of their verbal to thcir other behavior. Since the data (including thc verbal data) arc gathered in order to tcst thcorics about thc human information processing system, we arc cngaged in something of a bootstrap operation. We necd a model in order to interpret data that arc to be uscd, in turn, to test the model. Under these circumstances, our data-interpretation modcl should be as simple as possible, and it must not incorporate components that are themselves boncs of thcorctical contention. The model should be robust (i.c., compatible with a wide range of alternative assumptions about human information processing).

The spccifications wc arc about to present are simple and robust in this scnse, and, indeed, summarizc thc core that is common to most current information processing theorics of cognition. Of coursc they are not entirely neutral, for they would be hard to reconcile with an extreme form of behaviorism that denicd the relevance of central processes to the explanation of behavior. But thcy are not spccific to the view of any particular "sect" within the general information-processing tradition. o frusion  heoel, e Newel an Smon Catcr 14). and Simon (1979, Chapters 2, 3).

## General Specification

The most general and weakest hypothesis we rcquire is that human cognition is information processing: that a cognitive process can be scen as a sequence of internal states successivcly transformcd by a series of information processes. An important, and more specific, assumption is that information is stored in several memories having different capacities and accessing charactcristics: several sensory stores of very short duration, a short-term memory (STM) with limited capacity and/or intermediate duration, and a long-term memory (I.TM) with vcry large capacity and rclativcly permanent storage, but with slow fixation and access times compared with the other memories.

Within the framework of this information processing model, it is assumed that information rccently acquired (attended to or heeded)\* by the ccntral processor is kept in STM, and is directly accessible for further proccssing (c.g., for producing vcrbal rcports), whcrcas information from LTM must first be retricved (transfered to STM) before it can be reported.

This general picture is compatible with all sorts of specific hypotheses that have been put forth with respect to the dctails of the mechanisms. For examplc, some thcorists proposc that what we call "short-term memory" is not a scparatc, specialized storc but simply a portion of I.IM that is currently and temporarily activated (Anderson, 1976). Some theorists bclievc that information in STM cxtinguishcs with passagc of time, unless rehcarscd; othcrs that it is lost only when replaced. In general, these differenccs of detail do not affect the model at the levcl of spccificity rcquired for our purposes. The important hypothesis for us is that, due to the limited capacity of STM, only the most recently heeded information is accessible directly. However, a portion of the contents of STM are fixatcd in LTM before bcing lost from STM, and this portion can, at later points in timc, somctimes be retrieved from LTM.

Our specification of the system is general, but it is not vague. Specific information processing models that incorporate these features have been constructed in the form of computer programs, and these have been shown to produce a varicty of bchaviors previously observed in psychological laboratories. Verbal predictions of how such a system behaves can, thereby, bc testcd by using a computer program as a simulator. The principal modcl of this kind that guides our own thinking about these processes is thc EPAM program, duc to Fcigcnbaum (1963) and Simon, and discussed in some detail in Section 3 of Simon (1979).

We assume that any verbalization or verbal rcport of the cognitive processes would have to be bascd on a subsct of the in formation hcld in STM and I.TM. From this and thc above hypothcses, the taxonomy of vcrbalization proccdures shown in Tablc 1-1 follows in a straightforward fashion (Ericsson & Simon, 1980).

The two dimensions of Table 1-1 represent two major distinctions. First, the time of verbalization is important in determining from what mcmory the information is likcly to be drawn. Second, we make a distinction bctwcen proccdures where the verbalization is a direct articulation or explication of the stored information, and procedures whcre the stored information is input to intermediate processes, like abstraction and inference, so that the verbalization is a product of this intermediate processing.

## Detailed Specification

We now specify more fully the components of the information processing system that we have just sketchcd.The model draws upon a variety o sourcs that are summarized in Newell and Simon (1972, Ch. 14) and Simon (1979, Ch. 2.3). Few of the model's specifications arc controversial. It makes no rcal differencc, for example, whcther we assume a single homogeneous memory with different modes f activation e.g. Anderson, 1976; Shiffrin & Schncider, 1977) or several discrete memory stores (scnsory stores, STM, and I.TM). The important matters, which can bc describcd in cither terms, relate to the amounts and kinds of information that can be retaincd, and the conditions for accessing them and reporting them verbally. We will usc the conventional model of multiple memories in our description.

Recognition. Information rcceived from the sensory organs resides for a short time in memories (iconic and cchoic memories) associated with the different senses. During this time, portions of the sensory information are directly recognized and encoded with the aid of information already stored in LTM. Recognition associates the stimulus, or some part of it, with existing patterns in L.TM, and stores in STM "pointers" to thosc familiar patterns. (Thc EPAM discrimination net is a model of this recognition mechanism.) Intermcdiate stages of thc direct rccognition process (the successive stcps of discrimination), which may take only 10 to 100 mscc, do not use STM to store thcir products.

Long-Term Memory. The I'T'M may be pictured as an cnormous collection of interrelated nodes. Nodes can be accessed cither by recognition (through the discrimination nct), as just cxplained, or by way of links that associate these nodes to othcrs that have already becn accessed. Information accessed in cither way is then represented by pointers in STM. Thus, information can bc brought into STM from sensory stimuli via the recognition process, or from LTM via the association process. Association processes are much slower than direct recognition processes, requiring at lcast several hundred mscc for each associative step. Associative processes may usc STM to store intermediatc stcps. So, for cxamplc, in recalling a namc that is not immediately accessiblc, a pcrson may usc a sequence of cues to find an associative path, step by step, to the sought-for name. Such processes may last tens of seconds, or even minutes, and may leave numerous intcrmediate symbols in ST'M, where they are temporarily available for verbal reports.

Short-term Memory. The central processor (CP), which controls and regulates the non-automatic cognitivc processes, dctermines what small part of the information in scnsory stimuli and l.TM finds its way into STM. This is the information that is heeded or atlended to. The amount of information that can reside in STM at one time is limited to a small number (four?) of familiar patterns (chunks). Each chunk is represented by one symbol or pointer to information in I'TM (Simon 1979, Ch. 2.2). As new information is hccdcd, information previously stored in STM may be lost.

When a cognitive task (c.g., mental addition of a column of figures) is bcing carried out, the typical chunks in STM arc pointers to the opcrands, operators, and outputs of thc opcrations that arc bcing performed. Thus, in adding 3 to 4, pointers corresponding to thc symbols "3," "4," "PI.US," and "7" might at somc timc bc present in STM. Since, in our culture, adding two digits involves a dircct reference to I.TM ("table lookup"), no further dctail of the process would bc hecded in STM or available for verbal reports. On the other hand, if the task werc to multiply 17 by 45, STM might hold, at various points in the rcs hcarry  ulplying 5the irst interediat productIE,, "765."

We hold no brief for the details of the above description, which is intended mcrcly as an examplc of thc kinds of information wc would expect to bc hccded in STM, and to bc available, potentially, for concurrent or rcetrospcctive reports. The spccific details would depend on the particular strategies subjects uscd and the naturc of the chunks they had stored in LTM (Simon, 1979, Ch. 2.4). STM would symbolize the process only down to some modest levcl of detail (corresponding to clementary processes of a sccond or two in duration), and we would not expct to find information there about simple, automated processes (, the processes of retrieval from I.TM or recognition processes), much less about ncuronal events. Thus, the architecture of the control apparatus (CP) determines the fineness of grain of the representation of processes in S'TM.

Control of Attention. The flow of attention is diverted, from time to time, by interruptions through the highcr control mcchanism. Intermediate stages in thesc interruptions, not bcing symbolized in STM, are not reportable. Sudden movements in pcripheral vision, loud noises, eoions oeratin through thereticular st rertant cau interruption and shift in attention (Simon, 1979, Ch. 1.3). While information heee imediately beore r ter a shi i attention may smetimes allow subjects to iv a relativlyclearaccount o theinterrupion, wc would expect such information to be less complete than reports of an orderly process that is induced by thc successivc content of STM itself (c.g, a thought scqucnce during which goals in STM are guiding the thought processes).

Fixation. New information is rctained in STM during the time the CP is attending to it. In order to crcate an I.TM representation of new information that can later bc recalled, associations imust be built up by coding and imaging, as wcll as ncw tests and branches in thc rccognition nctwork. These lcarning processes, including the storage of new information in L.'TM and the addition of ncw pathways in the discrimination nct for accessing it, arc modeled in some dctail by EPAM (Simon, 1979, Scction 3). Processing of thc order of 8 to 10 scconds is required to asscmble each new chunk from its familiar components in STM, and to sre it in LTM as a new chunk (Smon, 19, Chs. .2, 2.3).

Automation. As particular processes bccome highly practiced, they become more and more fully automated. (Shiffrin & Schneider, 1977). Automation mcans that intermcdiate steps arc carricd out without being interpreted, and without thcir inputs and outputs using STM. The automation of performance is therefore quite analogous to cxecuting a computer algorithm in compiled instead of interpretive mode. Automation (and compiling) have two important consequences. Thcy greatly speed up the process (typically, by an ordcr of magnitude) and they make the intermediate products unavailable to STM, hcnce unavailable also for verbal reports.

## TYPES OF VERBALIZING PROCEDURES

The only feature common to the whole range of techniques used to obtain verbal data is that the subject responds orally to an instruction or probe. Because of the flexibility of languagc, there are virtually no limits to the probes we can insert and the qucstions we can ask subjects that will clicit some kind of verbal response.

Within our theoretical framework, we can represent verbal reporting as bringing information into attention, then, when necessary, convert-ivizl co faly,lziTecc for verbal reporting procedures is what information is heedcd. There have becn studics showing that the responsc modality does not affect the frequency of different responses. Newhall and Roderick (1936) found no differences in frequencies between vcrbal reports, button presses with fingers, or pedal presses with the fcct. This result indicatcs that the responsc is hccded symbolically, and then translated into thc appropriate overt form. (Sce Chapter 5 for further discussion.)

Two forms of verbal reports can claim to being the closest reflection of the cognitivc proccsses. Forcmost arc concurrent verbal reports—"talk aloud" and "think aloud" reports—wherc thc cognitive processcs, describcd as successive states of hccded information, are verbalized directly (sce Figure 1-1).

We claim that cognitive processes are not modificd by these verbal reports, and that task-directed cognitive processcs determinc what information is hceded and verbalizcd. We will cvaluate this claim cmpirically in Chapter 2.

A second type of verbal report is the retrospective report. A durable (if partial) memory trace is laid down of thc information hccded successively while completing a task. Just aftcr the task is finishcd, this trace can be accessed from STM, at least in part, or retrievcd from I.TM and verbalized. Rctrospective rcports bascd on information in ITM will require an additional process of rctricval that will display some of the same kinds of crror and incompleteness that arc familiar from experimental rescarch on mcmory. Both of these kinds of reports, we claim, ardirc valzationsiitiv oses.

## Recoding Before Verbalization

Various processes, and cspecially recoding processes, may intervene between the time information was hecded by the central processor (CP) and thc timc a vcrbalization is generatcd. Whcn information is reproduced in the form in which it was heeded, we will spcak of direct or Level I verbalization. When one or morc mediating processes occurs between attention to thc information and its delivery, wc will speak of encoded or Level 2 or Level 3 vcrbalization. A number of diffcrent kinds of intermediate processes bctwcen access and verbalization may modify the information. Among the important kinds are the following:

1. Recoding into verbal code (I.cvel 2 verbalization). When the internal representation in which the inforimation is originally encoded is not a vcrbal codc, it has to bc translatcd into that form. Werner and Kaplan (1963) have shown that whcn subjects gcncratc verbal descriptions of nonverbal stimuli for their own future usc, the format is compact and incorporates many idiosyncratic refcrents. When verbalizations are generated to communicate information to another pcrson, additional proccssing is required to find referents (Wcrner & Kaplan, 1963).

2. Intermediate scanning or filtering processes (Icvel 3 verbalization). When thc task instructions ask for verbalization of only sclected information, it is necessary to postulate additional processes that tcst if the hecded information is of the desircd typc. Such instructions are uscd, for cxample, in commentary driving cxperiments, in which the subjects are askcd to report all perccived traffic hazards while thcy arc driving a car (Soliday & Allen, 1972).

3. Intermediatc inference or gencrative processes (I.evel 3 verbalization). The situation is evcn morc complicatcd if the experimenter is interested in particular aspccts of the situation that a subject would not ordinarily attend to. The issue of whether the instruction to verbalizc calls for information not normally hccdcd by the subjects is central and directly rclated to the occurrence of intermediate inference and gencrative processcs. Since we will rcturn to this issue in more depth, only a brief summary will bc given hcrc of the types of information that are likcly to rcquire additional mediating processing for their generation.

In addition to verbalizing thcir ongoing thinking, subjects are sometimes askcd for verbal descriptions of thcir motor activitics, for example, what objects arc moved wherc, or where they arc looking. When this information is not hceded dircctly, as is often the casc, the subject is required to observe his or her own internal processes or overt behavior to generatc the in formation.

Experimenters arc often interested in subjects' reasons for their overt behavior and conscquently ask the subjects to verbalize their motives and rcasons, which may not be available directly or cven at all. In an exccilent review of rescarch on thc effects of persuasive messages, Wright (1980) discusses a wide rangc of biases duc to different verbal report procedures.

In sum, with Level 1 and Ievel 2 verbalization the scquence of hccded information remains intact and no additional information is heeded.On the other hand 1.cvel 3 verbalization requires attention t additional information and hence changes the sequenceof heeded inrmation.

## Retrospective Reports

In the ideal case the retrospective report is given by the subject immediatcly after the task is completed while much information is still in STM and can bc directly reported or used as retricval cues. It is clcar that somc additional cognitive processing is required to ascertain that the particular memory structures of interest are hecded. Our model predicts that retrospective reports on the immcdiately preceding cognitive activity can be accesscd and spccificd without the expcrimenter having to provide the subject with spccific information about what to rctricve. In this particular casc, the subjct will stil retain thenecesary trival cus in STM when a general instruction is given "to report evcrything you can remcmber about your thoughts during thc last problem." This form of retrospective verbal report should give us the closest approximation to the actual memory structures.

Even in this favorable casc, some problems arise that are common to all kinds of verbal reports from ITM. First, thc retrieval operation is fallible, in that other similar mcmory structures may be accessed instead of thosc created by the just-finished cognitive process. The probability of this occurring increases markedly if the subjccts have just solved a scries of similar problems. However, sincc most accessed mcmory structures contain redundant information bcyond thc cucs uscd for retrieval, subjects may usc this additional in formation to validate thc retricval as well as to increase their confidencc in the veridicality of the rctrieved information. In a subsequent section wc will discuss this type of cvaluation further and examine the rclevant thcorctical and cmpirical litcrature.

A second general problem when retricving cognitive structures is to scparatc information that was hccded at thc timc of a spccific cpisode from information acquired previously or subsequcntly that is associated with it (Mueller, 1911). For cxamplc, if a picture reminds onc of an old friend, it may be tempting to use the stored information about that friend to infer what the person in the picture looked likc. (In Chapter 3 we will discuss this issue in morc detail.) It may be possible to eliminate this artifact by instructing subjects only to report details that they can remember heeding at the time of the original cpisode (Mueller, 1911). By imposing a requirement of determinable memory as a basis for reporting, we can avoid many subjects' tendency to fill in information that they can't remember but "must" have thought.

## Inferential or Generative Processes

The most marked difference between concurrent and retrospective reporting is that rctrospective reports rcfer to a cognitive process that is completed and cannot be altered and influenced. Hence, if subjects arc requested to report information that was never heeded, they cannot possibly base thcir responses on dircct memory. The subjects can answer that thcy don't know. but often they will infcr and gcncrate an answer on the basis of information provided in thc question and other information accessible from I.I'M. Sincc retrieval from I.T'M may be an oncrous task, even in situations where the information is potentially retrievable subjects may prefer to gencrate thc in formation instead.

The most common probe that crcates this problem is the whyquestion: for example, "Why did you do this?" or "Why did you prefer that product?" In an intcresting discussion, 1.azarsfcld (1935) points to many issucs and problems in interpreting rcsponscs to why-questions, where subjects select one alternative out of sevcral possibilities. Some of the alternatives may never have becn hceded. If wc wish to find out: "Why did you buy this book?" we may reccivc, out of the same concrete experience of the respondent, quitc different answers, according to whether we stress "buy," "this," or "book." "If the respondent understood: 'Why did you BUY this book?' he might answcr, 'Because the waiting list in the library was so long that I shouldn't have got it for two months.' If hc understood: 'Why did you buy THIS book? he might tell what intcrested him cspecially in thc author. And if he understood: 'Why did you buy this BOOk?' he might report that he at first thought of buying a concert tickct with the moncy, but later rcalized that a book is a much more durable thing than a concert, and such rcasoning caused him to spend his money upon the book" (L.azarsfcld, 1935, p. 29).

The cxample is instructive in showing that a pcrson who did not actually buy the book, and hence had no spccific mcmory of the associatcd cognitive processes, could give the samc or similar answers as plausible reasons for somcone else's buying a book. Ilence, the answers can be generated (inferred) without access to a specific memory trace of the episode.

## Directed or Specialized Probing

Verbal probes differ in the comprehensiveness of the topics to be reported and the gencrality or particularity of the evcnts to be reported. Iet us first consider topic specificity. In many studies, the investigator is interested only in particular aspccts of subjccts' bchaviors. 'Thcn the verbal probe may be constructed to induce the subjects to gcnerate in formation specifically relevant to thc hypotheses under consideration. In order to help subjects retrieve the desired information from memory and to induce grcater completeness of the verbal reports, the question or verbal probe often contains contextual information. To guard against subjectivity in analyzing verbal reports, thc investigator oftcn supplics subjects with a fixed sct of alternativc responses. In contrast, a general instruction to give verbal reports typically asks subjects to tell everything they can remcmber or are thinking of while performing the task.

In most cases, vcrbosity and absencc of sclectivity in subjects' reports is not an important problem. What the subject reports is likely to be less, rather than morc, than wc should like to hear. In no study known to us using gencral instructions has the investigator complaincd that subjects have reported too much information from actual memory.

One common difficulty in probing for spccific information, especially when the subjects are offered a fixed sct of altcrnative answers, is to know that the questions conform to the internal rcpresentations the subjects are employing in their thought. Probes for types of information that subjects don't have directly accessible, or probes that provide inadcquate scts of alternatives may force subjects to intermediate and infercntial processing, and hencc producc verbal reports that arc not closcly related to the actual thought process. Morcover, when spccific, fixed-alternative probes are used, there is no way to detect from subjects' responses that this has occurred.

Since providing contextual information and prompts to subjects may aid recall from I.TM, in studies of LTM the usc of prompts and context is frequent and relativcly wcll-motivatcd. When subjects are askcd to report on immediatcly preceding cognitive processes of rclatively short duration, specific probes are more questionable and less uscful. In a logical sense, the experimenter gets just as much information from the subject in the third as in the irst two of the foowing three cases.

(1) Directed probe 1 Question: Did you use X as a subgoal? Answer: Yes.

## 22 Protocol Analysis

(2) Directed probe 2 Quesn: Did you use any sbgols? If so, whch? Answer: Yes, I used X.

(3) Undirected probe Verbal report: I was first trying to gct X and . when I attained X..

The replics in all three cases provide cvidence that the subject used X as a subgoal, yct thc cvidence is stronger in the third casc than in the sccond, and in the sccond than in the first. The vcrbalization in the first casc could casily bc gcneratcd by proccsses indepcndent of any memory for the actual thought processes. Comparing the sccond and third cases, the former communicatcs to subjccts what in formation the cxperimenter expects them to report. It may cncouragc subjccts to try to infer or gucss what particular in formation the cxpcrimenter will accept, and to generate information accordingly.

In many cases, othcr criteria are availablc for cstimating the validity of the rcports. An analysis of the task (Ncwcll & Simon, 1972) will often provide strong indications of the adequacy of vcrbalizcd information, especially in cascs with many logical possibilities for response.

Finally, different kinds of probes may have different effects upon the bchavior of subjccts. Requesting a ccrtain kind of information may suggest to subjects what aspects of the task are important. Subjects may also altcr thcir normal ways of processing so as to be able to give the requested information to the expcrinicnter on subscqucnt trials.

In studics that usc retrospective verbalization, subjects are scldom asked what they can remember about specific instances of thcir cognitive processcs. Rather, they are usually askcd to rctrospect about their thought processes in experimcnts with many trials or to answer general qucstions, and thus must try to synthcsizc all the available information after sclcctive recall. In making judgments, subjects havc access to an cxtrcmcly large base of relevant knowledge. Tversky and Kahneman (1973) have demonstratcd that subjects only rctrieve a few events or picccs of knowledgc and usc this sample to infer frequencics and probabilitics of events. Although the retrieved samplc may often be representative and the inferrcd probability judgment fairly accurate, there arc many factors influencing retricvability that do not reflect frequency. Hence, in many situations such cognitive processes will yield incorrcct judgments about frequency. Even though all the spccific information retrieved is accurate, the inferrd probability may be seriously in error. Nisbctt and Ross (1980) have given a rccent comprehensive discussion of such biasing factors in human judgment.

## Particular and General Reports

If the purpose of retrospective probing were to recover memory traces of subjects' proccsses, the appropriate instruction would bc to ask them to recall their specific thought processes during particular trials of the experiment. For at least two different reasons, such a procedure is rarely F    tal l nitive processes will bc poor and lacking in dctail. Moreover, there is a tendency for recurrent cognitive processes gradually to become automatic, so that fewer or nonc of thc intermediatc statcs of the processes for the later trials of the experiment are accessible for recall.

Sccond, many experimenters, because thcy are intcrested in general characteristics of the thought processes and not in the cpisodic details of the individual trials, probc thcir subjccts with questions of the type, "How did you do thesc tasks?" Such qucstions implicitly or explicitly request a general rather than spccific interpretation of how the subjects were performing the tasks.

There arc sevcral different ways in which subjects might arrive at descriptions of their general procedurcs, as distinct from rcports on spccific behaviors during individual trials. Onc possibility is that subjects are aware of the general procedures, or "programs," thcy arc using, use essentially thc same programs on all trials, and can recall and report these directly without referencc to the specific behavior thcy produced. Another possibility is that subjects rcmcmber some parts of their processes during particular trials, and generalizc this information into a general procedure, which they then report. A different possibility is that subjects remember some spccific tasks, regeneratc-by redoing them—the processes uscd for these tasks, and usc this information to infer the general procedures thcy may havc uscd. Finally, subjects may draw upon various kinds of prior information, such as gcncral knowledge on how one ought to do thesc tasks, to gencrate a verbal rcport describing a gencral procedure or strategy. In this casc, the verbal reports may not bear any closc relation to the actual cognitive processes (Nisbett & Wilson, 1977).

In areas of applicd psychological rescarch where verbal questioning has a long tradition, subjccts arc usually askcd about spccific events rather than for gcncral information or conclusions. In the critical incident technique proposcd by Flanagan (1954), the subjects wcre always asked to report their memory for spccific events. For example:

pilots returning from combat were asked "to think of some occasion during combat flying in which you personally experienccd feclings of acutc disoricntation or strong veitigo." Ihey were thcn askcd to describe what they "saw, heard, or felt that brought on the experience." (Flanagan, 1954, p. 329)

Interpretive probing, unlike the critical incident tcchnique, cannot be rclicd upon to produce data stemming directly from the subjects' actual scqucnces of thought processes. Thc probing proccdures cncourage or even require subjects to spcculatc and theorize about their processes, rather than leaving the thcory-building part of the cnterprise to the cxperimenter. Therc is no reason to supposc that the subjects themsclves will or can be awarc of the limitations of the data thcy are providing. Moreover, the varicty of inference and mcmory processes that might be involved in producing the rcports make thcm cxtremely difficult to intcrpret or to use as behavioral data.

## TWO CHALLENGES TO VERBAL REPORTS

It will be useful, in order to get a perspcctive on the issues, to use the abovc analysis to examinc two published papers that have somctimes been interpreted as providing strong cvidence against trusting verbal reports as data from which cognitive proccsses can bc infcrred: the first, a papcr rcporting a study by Vcrplanck and Oskamp; the second, the revicw paper on rctrospective vcrbal rcports by Nisbett and Wilson. A discussion of thesc papers will show how the information proccssing model we havc outlincd can hclp us interpret the findings of cxperiments on verbalization.

## Apparent Inadequacics of Concurrent Verbalization

In an often cited study (Vcrplanck, 1962), Verplanck and Oskamp claimcd to have shown that vcrbalized rules are dissociated from the bchavior they werc supposed to control. By having subjects verbalize the rules thcy were following in sorting illustrated cards, the cxperimenters could rcinforce either the verbal rule or the placement of cards (i.e., bchavior). To make the contingencies less noticcable, the criterion trials wcre followed by additional trials with partial rcinforcement. When correct placements were reinforced, the subjects were found to place cards correctly in7.8% of the trials; but they stated a correct or corre lated rule in only 48.4% of the trials. When correct statement of the rule was rcinforced, the subjects stated a correct or correlated rule on 92.8% of the trials, but placed the cards correctly on only 76.8% of the trials.

In a replication and analysis of this experiment, Dulany and O'Connell (1963) werc able to show that the above results could be attributed to two artifacts of the original experiment. First, in the case where correct placement was rcinforccd, by making a correction for guessing (the subjects had a 50-50 chancc of placing thc card in the correct pile when they didn't know the rulc), wc can estimate that subjc knew te corret answer i 3.6%  the trials- perentag v close to the 48.4% in which they stated the correct rule.

Second, with respect to the reinforcement of rules, Dulany and O'Connell found that the rules defined by Verplanck and Oskamp were ambiguous for thc card illustrations thcy cmploycd. In fact, naive subjects who wcre told thesc rules explicitly misplaccd the cards as frequently as did the subjects in the original cxperiment.

In a detailed analysis of the rules the subjects verbalized on each trial, Dulany and O'Connell found that on all but 11 of 34,408 trials the subjects put the card where they said they werc going to. Hence, Dulany and O'Conncll impeachcd rather thoroughly the cvidence put forth by Verplanck and Oskamp for bclicving that the rulcs subjccts verbalized were inconsistent with thcir behaviors.

Numcrous studies provide positive support for consistcncy between verbalized rules, concepts, and hypothescs and immediately preceding and succecding behavior, bcfore subjects reccive feedback. When Schwartz. (1966) askcd subjccts their rcasons for placing a card as they did, the rcasons given were consistent with the placements on all but 2 of 1,962 trials. Even more impressive, Frankcl, Ievinc, and Karpf (1970) obtained retrospective reports from subjccts about the basis for thcir responses to four earlicr discrimination-lcarning problems with 30 nonfeedback trials cach, and found that subjects could providc such reports in more than 90% of the scquences of trials.

## Apparent Inadequacies of Retrospective Reports

In a recent extensive revicw of studics permitting evaluation of retrospective verbal reports, Nisbett and Wilson (1977) have reported evidence that appcars at first sight to be very damaging to the utility of verbal reports for inferring information processes. Since their paper has receivcd widespread attention, it is important that we review their findings carefully. The authors summarize thcir main cmpirical findings thus (1977, p. 233):

Pcople often cannot report accuratcly on the effects of particular stimuli on higher order, inference-based responses. Indccd, somctimes they cannot report on the existence of critical stimuli, somctimes cannot report on thc existence of their responses. and sometimes cannot cvcn report that an inferential process of any kind has occurred.

First, we call attention to the frequent usc, in thcir summary, of the qualificrs "oftcn" and "somctimes." "Nisbctt and Wilson cite a large number of cxperiments that support thcir conclusions, but do not investigate in dctail thc conditions under which thesc conclusions do and do not hold. Moreover, they do not proposc a definite model of the cognitiv processes as a framework forinterpreting the findings they survey. Their thcoretical interprctations arc cntirely informal, resting hcavily on an undefincd distinction bctwcen introspective acccss to "content" and to process," or, as they alternativcly statc it, (1977, p.255), between access to "private facts" and to "mental processes." Thcir summary of the kinds of information to which subjects do have access is this (1977, p. 255):

... we do indeed have direct access to a grcat storehouse of private knowlcdgc .. The individual knows a host of personal historical facts; hc knows thc focus of his attention at any given point of time; he knows what his current sensations arc and has what almost all psychologists and philosophers would assert to be "knowlcdge" at least quantitatively superior to that of obscrvers concerning his cmotions, evaluations, and plans. Given that the individual docs possess a grcat deal of accurate knowledge … it becomes less surprising that people would persist in bclieving that they havc, in addition, direct access to their own cognitivc processes. The only mystery is why people are so poor at tclling thc difference between private facts that can be known with ncar certainty and mental processes to which therce may be no access at all.

Nisbett and Wilson also observe that subjects "are often capable of describing intermediatce results of a scries of mental operations (1977, p.55) i.e, that thy hold i TM and can accs the symbols that ae inputs and outputs to such operations).

We may compare this list of "private facts" and intermediate results that, according to Nisbett and Wilson, are accessible to subjects with the kinds of information that our processing model would inply that sub-coul rrt.Theiiual ows, ty y h u, his current scnsations, his emotions, his evaluations, and his plans. He knows the intermediate results of his mental opcrations. But these are exactly the kinds of information that, according to our model, would be held in STM and bc available for verbal reports.

Unfortunately, the studies revicwcd by Nisbctt and Wilson provide little data on what information is hecded during the thought processes, and what information is accessible from STM and I.TM at the time of the verbal report. Nisbctt and Wilson find that thc subjccts, when asked questions aboul their cognitive processes, frequcntly do not base their answers on memory for specific events at all, but "theorize" about their processes (1977, p. 233).

When reporting on the effects of stimuli, pcople may not interrogate a mcmory of thc cognitivc processcs that operated on the stimuli; instcad, they may basc thcir reports on implicit, a priori thcorics about the causal connection betwcen stimulus and response.

In reviewing the studies cited by Nisbett and Wilson, we can profitably raise the question of why and when subjects do not consult their memorics of cognitive processes in answering questions about those processcs. It is casy to draw the crroneous conclusion that this independence of verbal answers to questions about cognitive processes from the actual course and results of those processcs implics a general lack of accessiblc memory for such processes, or cven an unawareness of the information while the process was actually going on. But this swceping conclusion appears not to be justified.

The accuracy of verbal reports depends on the procedures used to clicit thcm and the relation bctwcen thc rcquestcd information and the actual scquence of hecded information. Invalid rcports, like those discusscd and obtained by Nisbctt and Wilson, may be duc to lack of access to thoughts (thcir claim), inadequate proccdures for cliciting verbal reports, or requcsting information that could not bc providcd even if thoughts wcre accessible. In a subsequcnt chapter (Chapter 3) we will describc in some detail what information will be hccdcd and hence reportable. Although somc studics citcd by Nisbett and Wilson did probe for such information, we will focus herc on the deviations betwcen the verbal report procedures used in many of the studics cited by Nisbett and Wilson and the procedures that, according to our model, would elicit valid retrospective reports of cognitive processes.

First, many of the verbal reports thcy discuss could be generated without accessing memory of the corresponding cognitive processes. In some of these studics, thc qucstions presented to subjects contain considerable background information from which answcrs could be generated without consulting thcir memorics. With questions like. "I noticed that you took more shock than avcrage. Why do you suppose you did?" (Nisbett & Wilson, 1977, p. 237) It is not cven clcar to us, nor probably to the subjects, that mcmory for the cognitive process should be the information source for thc answer. If subjects can gencrate their answcrs without consulting thcir mcmorics (Nisbett and Wilson showed that control subjects could do cxactly that), thcy might often prefer this method to retrieving information from memory.

Second, several aspects of the verbal report procedures reviewed by Nisbett and Wilson made the rclcvant thoughts less accessible. In most of the studics revicwed, the timc lag betwcen task and probe was sufficiently great to make it unlikcly that the rclevant information remained in STM. In Chapter 3 we will review thc rather extensive literature from general cxperimental psychology showing that time and intervening thought activity between the cognitive process and its verbal report, as well as incentive to recall memorics of the cognitive process, lead to dramatic declines in the accuracy of the verbally reported information. A rccent chapter by Gcnest and Turk (1981) and a paper by Wright and Kricwall (1980) give refcrcnces showing that such considerations of accessibility are powerful determiners of the accuracy of verbal reports for cognitive processes in tasks likc thosc discussed by Nisbett and Wilson (1977).

A tendency to generate verbal reports without access to memories will be stronger, the less readily available the memory is. When the probe is not a good retrieval cue for the relevant aspects of the memory, the subject must attempt, through conscious processing, to recall sufficient information to give an appropriate answcr. Since retrieval from LTM, even if possible, requires considerable timc and cfort, subjects, unless explicitly instructed to providc a rclativcly complete recall, may be disinclined to do so, cspccially if other ways of producing a response are hm.  t suy y Wrih anp 9) poi inc  at or h plicitly motivated to retrieve memory for thoughts in a judgment task.

Finally, in some studies revicwed by Nisbett and Wilson, subjects were asked to report information that cannot be given cven with complete access to the thought proccsses (cf. why-questions regarding causes), and information that is far from a direct recall of memory of the cognitivc processes. Our model predicts that information can be recovered by probes only if the samc information would be accessed by undirected requests for concurrent or retrospective reports. For many of the studics in the Nisbctt-Wilson review, our model would predict failure to obtain from the probes verbal information about particular instances of processes. For example, in betwcen-subjcct designs, subjects obviously cannot answer from memory of thcir processes why they bchaved differently from subjects in another experimental condition-—the processes did not include such a comparison. Hence, this information can be derived, if at all, only by comparing the descriptions of the processes provided by different scts of subjccts in thc two conditions. In other studics the subjects were asked how thcy would have rcacted if the experimental conditions had been different in a spccificd respect. Such probing for hypothetical states can never tap subjects' memories for their cognitive processes, since the information was ncver in memory. In still other studics, subjects wcrc asked, cxplicitly or implicitly, to summarize or generalizc the processes they uscd, rather than to report concretely the processes used on each trial.

Several articles have bcen published making similar criticisms of the Nisbctt and Wilson (1977) papcr, and raising other objections as well. Of particular interest are the papers discussing the problcms with verbal reports in betwcen-group designs. (Smith & Millcr, 1978). Some recent studies have shown that in corresponding within-group studics, subjects are ablc to providc vcridical verbal reports (Whitc, 1980; Weitz & Wright, 1979; Wright & Rip, 1980).

In sum, we disagree with Nisbctt and Wilson's interprctation that subjects simply were not aware of relevant information during the critical experiments. Instead, we claim that belter mcthods for probing for that awareness (concurrent or immediate retrospcctivc reports) would yield considcrable insight into the cognitivc proccsses occurring in most of the studics discussed by Nisbett and Wilson. On the other hand, we agree with Nisbett and Wilson's analysis of subjcct's rcports in situations where the subjects do not havc access to or for other reasons don't rely on mcmory for the cognitivc processes in qucstion. In such situations, Nisbett and Wilson proposc that an expcrimental subjcct infers the causes of his own behavior by relying on common-sense theorics and observable events-the same process that an observer would use to infer causes of bchavior in an observed subject. By using experimcntal situations, where common-sense theory would lead to the incorrect asscssment of causes, Nisbett and Wilson provide convincing cvidence for thcir interpretation by showing that both cxperimental subjccts and obscrvers agrcc on the incorrcct cause of the cxperimental subjects' bchaviors. (For a nice prcsentation and cxtension of thesc arguments sce Nisbett and Ross (1980).)

We think that Nisbett and Wilson's paper has becn uscful in forcing investigators like ourselves to think carefully about the rclation of verbal reports to cognitive processes. Many verbal report procedures are justly faulted by their review. Howcver, thcir results arc consistcnt with our model of concurrent and immediate rctrospective reports.

## Concluding Remarks

Our examination of two of thc most vigorous challenges to the usefulness of verbal reporting leaves intact our bclief that such reports— cspecially concurrent reports, and retrospcctivc reports of specific cognitive processes—provide powcrful mcans for gaining information about such processes. The concurrent report reveals the scquence of information heeded by the subject without altcring the cognitive process, while other kinds of verbal reports may change these processes. In retrospective reports of spccific processes, subjects generally will actually retricve the trace of the processes. In other forms of retrospective reporting, subjects, instead of rccalling this information, may report information that they have inferred or otherwise gencrated. Hence, in the chapters that follow, we will pay particular attcntion to the two spccial forms of reporting—the one concurrent, the other retrospcctive—that are most likcly to yicld direct evidencc of cognitive processes.

## VERBAL REPORTS OF COGNITIVE STATES AND STRUCTURES

Although this book focuses upon cognitive processes, the modcl and concepts it employs can be cxtended to the ron-cognitive aspects of verbal behaviors. There are several reasons for undertaking such an extension. It will permit us to identify common problems and issues in areas of psychology, like psychophysics, survey design, and measurement of personality traits, that traditionally have had little or no interaction wth cach other.In these areas, too, as in those wchave beendiscussing behaviorism has muted explicit cxamination of the status of verbal responses and reports.

First, we will propose a taxonomy of thesc other kinds of verbal reports, and will discuss briefly somc examples of rclevant rescarch. Then wc will consider two limited topics for more systematic discussion. The first of these is attitudc assessment, the sccond is the historical development of verbal reporting, with particular emphasis on introspection. All of the verbal reports with which we will be concerned in this section are clicited by probes specifying what information is to be reported. Often, also, a sct of alternatives is supplicd from which the subject has to sclect a response.

Predictions from our model about the effects of verbal reporting on thought processes will depend on the circumstances under which the verbalizations are induced. We can classify verbalizations according to the memories that are tapped and according to the verbalization instructions the experimenter gives to the subjects. With respect to the memory source of thc reported information, we can distinguish among (a) reports of stimuli that remain constant and available to the subjcct's senses while the report is being made, (b) reports of information rctained in STM, and (c) reports of information from I.IM. The ncxt threc subsections of this section will be devoted to the spccial problems that arise for each of these three kinds of reports.

## Reporting of Sensory Stimuli

At any given moment, a large amount of cxternal stimulation impinges on any human through the sensory reccptors (visual, auditory, etc.), as well as from internal visccral sourccs. Normally this information is not heedcd directly, but recognition processes access existing relevant I.TM patterns, which providc higher-level descriptions and arc in turn heeded. (In Chapter 3 we will discuss thcse recognition proccsscs and their relation to attention in some detail.) In many circumstances attention can be dircctcd toward the information in the scnsory stores (cf. Kahneman, 1973). We can focus on marks on the page wc are reading or listen for unusual faint sounds and so on. Many kinds of vcrbal reporting proccdures rely directly on our ability to process sensory information selectively.

In most psychophysical studies, subjects are instructcd as to the stimuli as well as the typcs of responses they will use. They are asked to rate how much pain the cxpcricncc causes, how loud a certain stimulus is, how far away a certain stimulus is, and so on. This rescarch has had a strong cmpirical emphasis and has becn virtually unaffected by the drastic changes in thcorctical vicws of mainstream experimental psychology. In our historical discussion, we will point to differcnces betwcen the psychophysical mcthods and the analytic introspcctivc methods, which also attempted to describe experiences in terms of the sensory units. Now we only want to sketch the relation of the psychophysical methods to our model of verbal reporting.

Since the primary goal of psychophysical rescarch has been to describe the structural relation betwcen physical stimulus and response, little attention has been paid to the mcdiating processes. However, selective attention is under attentional control and as reportable as is the final reponse. (The study citcd carlicr showing that subjects can substitute key pressing for verbal reports is a casc in point.)The rescarch mcthodology of psychophysics uses long sessions of trials to scek stable structural relations and highly automatized processes.

In a classic paper, Eriksen (1960) showed that the vcrbal report is the most sensitive index for basic perceptual processes, like discrimination. Hencc, the results from psychophysical mcthods of report are quite consistent with our modcl of verbal reporting. We would, however, likc to go a stcp further and argue that dctailing the cognitive processes involved in gencrating psychophysical reporis may prove quite useful. First, there is evidence that cognitive structures are involved cven in simple judgments, like discrimination. dramatic example is iven by Binct (1969), who showcd that the threshold for discriminating touch of two separate points of contact (comparcd to a single point of contact) could be rcduced 10 times by showing the subject the compass used. Second, different vcrbal instructions in judgments of size give different results (Carlson, 1977). Subjects give reliably different responses when asked to judge the objective sizc, the apparent sizc, and the size of the vertical projection of an object.

Converging support or the use of different cognitive processes in judgment of apparent and objectivc size was obtained by Epstcin and Broota (1975), who found objective sizc judgments to bc slower and a lincar function of the distance to the stimulus object, whercas apparent size judgments wer faster and unrclated to distance. Brunswik (95) all them to "be so certain that they could bet on the actual size," clearly influences the judgments. Finally, and probably most important, the obscrved improvement of psychophysical judgments with practicc (see Gibson, 1969) appears to implicate cognitive mcchanisms (sce Chapter 3). Some recent results by Ericsson and Faivre (1982) show that performancc in a perceptual learning expcriment can bc bcst described in terms of thc acquisition of cognitive structures indentificd from retrospective verbal reports.

A related class of learning situations involve control of body functions—likc heart rate, audiomotor performance—through biofeedback. In a very interesting revicw, Roberts and Marlin (1979) discuss the fairly cxtensive rescarch (with conflicting results) on how reported awareness mediates devclopment of control of these body functions. They define veridical content as vcrbally reported information making reference to "activities or perceptual events that are correlated with target bchavior and therefore with fecdback presentation" (Roberts and Marlin, 1979, p. 81), and discuss circumstances favorable to the generation of such reports. Thcy point to two main biasing sources. Instructions in thesc biofccdback tasks often cxplicitly tell subjects to avoid certain strategics, like regulation of brcathing rate. Other instructions give subjects incorrect information (c.g., that ratc of heart bcats is unrclated to ratc of brcathing). It is clear that such instructions will bias thc subject against reporting such information regardless of thcir thoughts. These studics indicate that subjects can report the strategics they use for achieving control of visceral functions.

In a subscquent study, Robcrts, Marlin, Kclcher, and Williams (1982) provide some supportive evidencc for the claims of validity of verbal reports made by Roberts and Marlin (1979). In most other studics, subjects are informed what visceral function is to be controlled, but this information may inducc infcrential processing, and also climinatcs the possibility of using statemcnts on what function is involvcd to validate thc verbalizcd thought.
