In a series of studies reported in Merz (1969), the effect of verbalization on performance in intclligence tests was investigated. In a study by Kesting using the Figure Reasoning test, the 13- to 17- year-old subjects who always had to say aloud how the figures were alike or different were significantly better than the subjects who were asked to say the same thing to themselves silently; but thcy were also significantly slower. In this study, there were also two other conditions with additional vcrbal interference tasks; onc group of subjects had to say "eins, eins …." rhythmically, while another group had to sing "la, la ..." while solving the test items. These conditions have the same performance (in terms of number of correct solutions and time required) as the silent verbalizing condition; which suggests that the same processes, mainly nonverbal in character, were uscd in all three conditions.

This oral-encoding interpretation is supported by Waszak (reported in Merz, 1969), who showed that hearing one's own voice in a heavy noise condition does not eliminate the improvement associated with verbalizing. The crucial mechanism underlying these results appcars to be that the instruction to explain answcrs incrcases the tendency to cncode figures and thcir differences vcrbally. This interprctation is also supported by the observation of Merz that it is difficult to induce subjects to verbalize in this kind of perceptual task.

To test the hypothesis that the instruction to verbalize made the subjects assume a more analytic problem-solving style, Hofgren (Merz, 1969) compared performancc on a parallel form of the Figure Reasoning test between a group that had previously verbalizcd on an initial form and a control group that had not. The vcrbalizing group performed significantly less well when not required to verbalizc, but still somewhat better than the control group, whose performancc hardly differed between the two occasions. Merz suggests that the loss of performance by the "verbalizers" on the second occasion was due to transfer of thought content, as the verbalizing subjects solved more test items on the first occasion, rather than to transfer of a more analytic style of thought. Supporting evidence is given in a study by Waschke (Merz, 1969): the verbalizing and control groups performcd equally on the first occasion, when simple items wcre uscd; and the pcrformance of both groups was the same on the second occasion, with respect to time and number of items correct. These investigations indicate that the instruction to verbalize forces subjects to cncode the figures orally, and that this, in turn, induces verbal symbolic processing that is especially wcll adapted to discovery of the prescribed solutions in the Figure Reasoning test.

In the above studies, performance was averaged over subjects and different tasks. We now consider evidence of differenccs between individuals, and of differential effects, on performance with tasks of varying difficulty. Merz (1969) reports a smaller study showing that with simple test items, verbalization did not hclp the subject and amounted more or less to a retrospective description of the solution "seen" immediately. The verbalized description was only hclpful with more difficult items that required some search for the right solution.

Inner speech, measured by electrical activity in the speech apparatus with Raven's Progressive Matrices items and without verbalization shows a similar pattern, in that inner speech activity increased with difficulty of the items (Sokolov, 1972). Analyzing the protocols from verbal reconstructions of the problem-solving process, Sokolov showed that the simple items were solved in a prcdominately visual way, while with the more difficult problems, verbal designations of some features of the figures were used to aid solution. In outlining a scheme for the interplay o visual and verbal processes in solving such problms, Sokolov points to the influence of verbalization in attending to features that would have gone unnoticed in the purcly visual analysis. In this interpretation, the directed verbalization provides the subject with additional noticed featurcs, which in turn facilitate performance.

There is little evidence as to whether directed verbalizing has a general r  feental effect ubjects.eal effect is by the fct that he varianceof the increas perormanc with veralz ing is equal to or less than the variance of performance under control conditions in the studies of Kesting and Waszak and Hofgren (Merz, heinvetigation okolovindicates diffrences betweejects, leading Sokolov to propose a differential reliance on verbal and visual processing; but this result is not incompatible with the idea that there may also be general effects upon all subjects.

In this context we also want to discuss the well-known effect of providing verbal labels to visual stimuli, which was originally demonstrated by Carmichael, Hogan, and Walters (1932). We will rely hcavily on the relevant parts of Riley's (1962) excellent discussion of memory for form. Carmichael et al. showed clearly that naming one of two possible labels for an ambiguous stimulus beforce its prescntation led subjects to draw the pictures more like the labcl in a subscquent memory test. A series of studies have shown that this effect is not a purely perceptual one, at least when the presentation time is sufficiently long for complete perception (Bruner, Busick, & Minturn (1952) found effects for presentations shorter than a tenth of second). Hanawalt and Demarest (1939) showed that verbal labcls presented at recall gave similar effects when the pictures had becn unlabeled at presentation. Prentice (1954) showed that presenting verbal labels with the pictures produced no bias in perception.

Additional insight into the effects of labels is provided by a study by Herman, Lawless, and Marshall (1957), who included a control group that was simply shown the pictures. (The original study by Carmichael et al. had a very small control group.) Herman et al. (1957) found that the subjects in the control group showcd as much tendency as the others to distortions of memory. However, in the control group the memories were distorted equally in the directions of the two labels, while in the experimental groups, they wcre distorted mainly toward the labcl that was presented. Yet even in the cxperimental groups a few subjects deviated toward the verbal label that was not presented. Hanawalt and Demarest (1939) found that subjects who were not given a verbal label at prescntion reported meaningful associations to the pictures, and a substantial number of these associations were similar or evcn identical to the labcls used originally by Carmichacl ct al. Hcrman ct al. (1957) confirmed this by asking a new group of 29 subjects to givc free associations to the pictures. On the average, 63 % of the associations were identical to or very similar to the ones used by Carmichael et al. Furthermore, Herman et al. (1957) showed that the effects of the labels could be markedly reduced if subjects were told in advance that they would be asked to draw the pictures later. In sum, the original effect observed by Carmicheal et al. appears to be caused by inducing subjects to use certain associations at the expense of others. Hence, it is cntirely possible, even likely, that subjects could casily pcrform this memory task with TA or retrospective report instructions without changes in performance.

## Other Verbalization Studies

None of the studies that remain to bc discusscd employ highly manipulative tasks or pictorial stimuli. In a study on clinical judgment (Baranowski, 1975), the subjects, who werc psychologists, made two successive series of judgments. On the first occasion, all subjects performed the task under identical conditions. On the sccond occasion, the subjects were divided into: (a) a group working under the same instructions as on the first occasion, and (b) a group instructed to verbalize and monitored by the experimenter who asked the subjects questions whenever "a particular profile could use more explanation" (1975, p. 21). There was no difference between control and verbalizing conditions (measured by variance accounted for by lincar and non-lincar models). However, the cross-validated linear models over the two occasions accounted for significantly less variance for the verbalizing group than for the control, suggesting that the instruction to verbalize changcd the utilization or subjective weights of the cue-variables.

In a related study, Fidler (1979) had subjects choose for admission to graduate study between two students described in terms of several attributes. Subjects gave both concurrent and retrospective reports. (Unfortunately, the concurrent reporting instruction asked subjects to provide ccrtain specific information, likc the reasons for judgments, how they evaluated information, and so on.) Retrospective reports were elicitcd by asking subjects how thcy had reached their decisions. Fidler compared the performance under different reporting instructions (including no verbal report at all). No rcliable differences in choice were observed, nor were subjects more consistent in making choices on repeatcd trials with verbal report instructions than without such instructions. When regressions were estimated to detcrmine cach subject's decision model for verbal-report trials and no-report trials, respectively, no reliable differences were found for 10 of the 13 subjects. The reliable differences in the rules for the three remaining subjects could be traced to stable changes in their decision rules that occurred in the transition between blocks of report and no-report trials.Whether thesc chans in decision rules were induced by the reporting instructions or other factors is not certain. Fidler proposes that it may be due to the inexperience of the subjects. Since they were not cxperts at the judgment task, they were aware of their decision rules, hence could report them. The consistency between reported reasons and decision rules, on the one hand, and actual choices (even on no-report trials) lends further support to that interpretation.

In a concept-learning study by Bower and King (1967), one group of the subjects was required to verbalize their hypotheses before classifying the stimuli, while a control group was not. In preparation for the experiment, the subjects described the stimuli, in order to cnsure that subject and experimenter agreed in their descriptions. Under these circumstances, we would expect that no further oral encoding need occur in the verbalizing condition. The number of irrelevant dimensions of the stimuli was varied, although the instructions indicated which two features. were relevant to the solution in each case. The requirement to verbalize hypotheses improvcd performance significantly (i.e., number of responses to criterion), but only for the first problem. Bower and King found that variation in the number of irrelevant fcatures or dimensions was only efective on this initial problem, suggesting that the verbalizing of hypotheses helped the subjects initially to ignore the irrelevant attributes. It should be noted that no training trials were used in this study. Karpf and Levine (1971) found no effect on performance in a discrimination learning experiment from asking subjects to repeat their hypotheses verbally before each trial.

In a cue-probability learning task, Brchmer (1974) required one group of subjects to describe the rule underlying their predictions just after each prediction was made, but before fecdback was reccived. The subjects' descriptions should be so explicit that another subject could understand and use it; if they did not mcet this standard, the experimenter prompted for more information. Explanations like "I guessed," or "I remembered from the previous trial," were accepted as verbal descriptions. An analysis of variance showed no significant effect or interactions associated with verbalization. In a subsequent study (Brchmer, Kuylenstierna & Liljcgren, 1975) the subjects wrote down their current hypotheses in a booklet at the beginning of the test blocks, without any significant effect on performance.

According to our model, requiring verbal explanations of behavior should not alter the normal processes unless the information required for the verbalizations would not otherwise be generated. Unfortunately, there is little evidence for the tasks uscd in the above studies about the content of undirected verbalizations. In the cue-probability experiment of Brehmer (1974) with very simple stimuli (a straight line varying in length), the number required for cxplaining the rule was most likely consciously generated cven in the silent condition. That the effects of verbalization were limited to the first trial in the Bower and King (1967) experiment could be attributed to the fact that vcrbalizing helped the subjects ignore irrelevant featurcs. Alternatively, onc might speculate that vcrbalizing may havc speeded up the generation of an internal representation, thus making the subjcct more independent of his direct perccptions. In thc study of Baranowski (1975), unlike the other studies, the subjccts were highly skilled. In our model, vcrbal explanation of automated activities would be cumbersome and change the course of the processing from a largely perceptual (recognition) to a more cognitive one. In support of this hypothesis, the time taken by the clinical psychologists to perform the task with verbalization was two or' three times the time taken in the silent condition.

## Effects from Retrospective Verbalization

We turn now to some experiments that are often cited to show that verbalizing information (even retrospectively) may change and deform it, and hence affect subsequent task bchavior. Hendrix (1947) showed that an instruction to describe a concept or principle verbally after learning reduced ability to use the concept in a transfer situation. The results were substantiated in subsequent work by Schwartz (1966) and by Phelan (1965). Careful analysis shows that these studies do not address the question of verbalization, as such, but rather verbalization of cxplicit and logical concepts.There are two issues. The first is that if the subjects do not normally organize what they learn in these experiments in vcrbalizable concepts and general principles, then verbalization forces them to generate such concepts and principles from whatevcr information is currl available to hTe orulation may not at all reflect tw in which the learning was actually encoded. For example, Phelan found that the verbal descriptions of certain pictorial stimuli tended to contain iati e fnt thos hat dh  e subjects had learned.In Chapter 3 we return to the issue of what inor mation should be reportable in such cases.

The second issue relates to the detail and explicitness called for by the instruction to verbalize. Sowder (1974), examining the effects of various sorts of verbalizations of learned gerncralizations, found no differences as compared with a control condition. He also cited two studies that found no effects of producing written descriptions of learned generalizations. Sowder proposed that the important differences between his study and Hendrix's was that Hendrix (1947) specified precisely the content of her subjects' verbalization (quantifiers, domain, and so on), while he did not.

Rommetveit (1960, 1965) and Rommctveit and Kvale (1965a, 1965b) studicd concept formation in a situation where 12- and 13-yearold subjects played on a wheel of fortune, different pictures being displayed when subjects were to win or lose, respectively. They found that instructing the subjects that they were subsequently to describe diffn beteen the "wi"  "ose"fgure, as po to just playig on the wheel, influenced retrospective descriptions of the two figures. Other procedural variations—like demonstrating before the experiment how the figures differed (Rommetveit, 1965)— tended to eliminate a (correct) tendency toward associating roundness with good figures. In these studies, therefore, the effective variablc is not verbalization per se, but directing the cognitive processes by instructions. Without such direction, verbalization appears to have no effect on the cognitive processes.

The fact that encoding and reporting verbally takes time creates a procedural difference, which is important in some studies, between the reporting and control conditions. Time is known to be an important variable in LTM phenomena. Boersma, Conklin, and Carlson (1966) allowed their subjects in the verbal report condition an additional minute to specify their encodings of each stimulus. Retention scores were superior for the verbal report condition, but the cxperimental design confounds the effect of the additional time with effects of generating a written description of the stimulus.

In sum, the empirical data from the modest number of systematic studies of the effects of verbalizing (c.g., Brehmer, 1974; Brehmer, Kuylensticrna & Liljegren, 1974, 1975; Karpf & Levine, 1971; Wilson & Spcllacy. 1972) support consistently our asscrtion that producing verbal reports of information dirctly available in propositional form docs not change the coursc and structure of the cognitive processcs. However, instructions that require the subjects to perform additional cognitive processes to generate or access the information to be reported may affect these processes.

## New Research on Effects of Verbalization

The renewed interest in psychology in using vcrbal reports as data has generated new rescarch activity aimcd at casting further light on the effects of verbalization upon cognitive processes. This new work seeks to go beyond proving the presence or absence of effects of verbalization, and to measure the extent and naturce of these effects under different experimental conditions. We will conclude our discussion by a summary of three important recent studies of this kind.

In a disscrtation, Karat (1983) analyzed the vcrbalizations of subjects solving the Tower of Hanoi with TA instructions. He found that subjects verbalized plans and goals before making some, but by no means all, of their moves. If subjects were rcquircd to give reasons for each move, as in the original study by Gagné and Smith (1962), they would have to gencrate these reasons through additional cognitive processing. Since Karat found no effects due to TA, the effects found by Gagné and Smith can very likely be attributed to their more specific and demanding verbalization instructions.

The most ambitious attempt to explore and document effects of instructions to verbalize is an unpublished study by Russo, Johnson, and Stephens (in preparation). In their study, 24 subjccts performed 55 trials of 4 different tasks with 5 different instructions. The five instructions included a control condition, concurrent verbalization, retrospective reports with the problem still in view, retrospective reports without any stimulus information, and retrospective reports cued by the eyemovements recorded during the immediately preceding solution. In the following, we will only discuss the data for the control condition, and for concurrent and rctrospcctive reports without stimulus information. The four tasks were: selection between gambles, items from Raven's progressive matrices, mental addition of thrce 3-digit numbers, and anagrams. For cach task 55 items were selected to yicld an average percent correct of 75%. The eye movements of subjects were recorded on every trial.

In this thorough study, the only reliable ovcr-all effect of verbalization was that solutions took longcr with concurrent verbalizing than in the silent condition. Although the average solution times with verbalization were longer for al four tasks, a post-hoc analysis showed that the differences were statistically significant only for gambles and anagrams, and the differences were also small relative to the variability of the solution times. For gambles, the solutions with TA took 7.5 seconds longer than the average of solutions (44.6 seconds) under the silent condition, an increase of about 17 per cent.

The analysis of accuracy of solution showed that with concurrent verbalization subjects performed the gambles task a little better than slet subjects (p < .01) whilin the addiion task concurrent verbalizrs performed slightly more poorly than silent subjects $( \mathsf { p } \ = \ . 0 6 )$ Hence concurrent verbalizing leads subjects to take longer and perform better on the gambles task, and to take longer but perform more poorly on the additions task.

To help us pinpoint the sources of differences, E.S. Johnson (personal communication) reports results from a preliminary analysis of the data. "We did not find any differences betwecn conditions for the first ten practice trials, but control subjects subsequcntly increased their accuracy in the addition task more rapidly than subjects giving verbal reports. Both concurrent and retrospective subjects improved over trials in their selection f gambles, while control subjects gradually speededup but maintained constant accuracy levels."

While awaiting the complete analysis of the protocols, eyemovement scquences, and errors, we can only speculate about the reasons for the differences in final performancc. A comparison of the verbalizations during practice trials and test trials should reveal the changes in processing that took place in the gambling task. It is noteworthy that retrospectively reporting subjccts, who did not have to think aloud, improved in performance in that task just as concurrently reporting subjects did. The difference for mental additions is especially surprising in view of the fact that earlier studies showed no effects of verbalizing in mental multiplication. From a preliminary analysis of numbers of protocol statements (Russo ct al., in preparation) it appears possible that concurrent subjects failed to speed up because they continued to verbalize simple operations that could be elicited by recognition. This "superfluous" verbalization could slow down pcrformance and interfere with recall of intermediate stcps. Verbal reporting might not alter initial performance, but might slow down changes toward automaticity during continuing practice.

In sum, Russo ct al. (in prcparation) found rcliable effects of concurrent verbalization for gambles and additions, but the differences reported are small in relation to other variability in the performances.

Since the effect was virtually the same for the concurrent and retrospective verbalization conditions, and since there was no effect of verbalization in the early trials, we are rather uncertain as to the real causes for the differences that were observed.

A recent dissertation by Gerhard Deffncr (1983) provides additional extensive information about the effects of concurrent verbalization. Using anagrams, a geometric puzzlc, and the N-term series problem (sce Ohlsson (1980) for a description), and varying task difficulty and amount of perceptually availablc information, he found that thinking aloud increased solution times, but did not affect avcrage numbers of solutions, and did not interact with the othcr cxpcrimental variables. IDeffner's analysis of his subjects' strategics, and rate and content of their verbalizations will be discussed elscwhere in this monograph.

## SUMMARY

The picture that emerges from our review of empirical studies of verbalization is quite clear and consistent. We have scen that the effects, or absence of effects, of verbalization depend upon characteristics of the tasks and of the verbalization instructions in ways that can be predicted from our model of the processing system.

When the instructional procedures conformed to our notion of levcl 1 or Levcl 2 verbalization, the studies gave no evidence that verbalization changes the course or structure of the thought processes. A small number of minor differences between verbalizing and silent subjects can most plausibly be attributed to procedural differences between the experiments conducted with the two groups.

Studies that deviated in task, instructions, or experimental procedure, from the criteria of Level 2 verbalization were mainly experiments on perceptual-motor tasks and tasks requiring visual encoding processes, and cxperiments where specific verbal content was asked for by the instructions. Evidence was presented, for these kinds of experiments, that the information requested by the experimenter was not normally available to the subject in an oral encoding, and hence that producing the verbalization requrcd intermediate processing that changed the course and structure of the thought process.

From our review of the evidence, we conclude that the processes subjects use to verbalize while thinking are neither illusory nor elusive, but can be understood and modeled.The processes associated with verbalization should be treated as an integral part of any model of the cognitive processes for a given task whenever the articulation takes the form of direct verbalization (i.e., vocalization of heeded information). The model should also include the processcs for storing information in LTM, to account for the phenomena of retrospective verbalization at the end of cxperimental trials. The gross model we have proposcd is focused on the verbalization of ongoing cognitive processes, but the postulated close link between information attended to and information stored should make it a relatively straightforward matter to model retrospective verbalization.

Finally, in the review of studies comparing different instructions to verbalize, we found substantial evidence that differences in performance were induced by telling the subjcct how to verbalize. In order to verbalize the information called for by the instructions, instead of the information he would normally have attended to, he had to change his thought processes.

# COMPLETENESS OF REPORTS

An important problem ariscs in using verbal reports as data: the processcs underlying bchavior may be unconscious and thus not accessible for verbal reporting, or at least may be reportablc only very incompletcly. Closcly related to the problem of incompletencss is the possibility that vcrbalizations, when present, inay not bc closcly related to underlying thought processes—may not bc veridical rcports of those processes or may bc epiphenomenal. In the course of the chapter, we will consider these issues of veridicality and cpiphenomenality also, but our central concern will be with the completeness of reports.

Wc bcgin by vicwing thc issucs in historical perspcctive. Then, we cxtend the model of the human in formation processing system proposed in previous chapters to cncompass the processcs associatcd with vcrbalization. Finally, we takc up the evidence on completeness of reports under four different hcadings:

1. Ability of subjects to report thcir own cognitive states;

2. Reports of recognition processes;

3. Retrospective reports of previous cognitive processes;

4. Insight.

## THREE VIEWPOINTS

The conpleteness, veridicality, and epiphcnomenality of verbal reports can be vicwcd from quite different perspectives. On thc one hand, behaviorism is gencrally skeptical about the accessibility of internal states. On thc other hand, a perspcctive that postulates that all human thinking is rational and logical finds it hard to account for verbalizations that appear not to have thcse qualities, and to account, also, for sudden leaps

## 110 Protocol Analysis

of "intuition." We need to consider cach of these perspectives in turn, and to relate them to our own information processing perspective.

## The Behaviorist View

The strongest bchaviorist objection to treating verbal protocols as veridical is the cpiphenomenality claim— the claim that the processes involved in gencrating a verbal report are totally independent of thc process that gencrates the task bchavior of the subject. A slightly wcaker form of the objection is the argument that certain types of cognitive processes are not conscious, hence that these processes and thcir contents cannot bc reported. In his famous Psychological Review articlc, Watson (1913) opposed cxtcnsive thcorizing about cognitive structures and processes. The only acceptablc facts and data were bchavior and recordable cvents that could be observed by an independent obscrver, as opposed to introspcction. Emphasis was placcti on collecting and analyzing such facts, and thcorizing was treated as a subordinate activity.

[Bchaviorism] may never makc a pretense of bcing a system. Indced systcms in cvcry scicntific ficld arc out of date. Wc collect our facts from observation. Now and thcn we sclect a group of facts and draw certain general conclusions about them.' In a fcw ycars as ncw cxpcrimental data are gathered by better methods, even thesc tentative general conclusions have to be qualificd. (Watson, 1930, p. 18)

Of particular interest is Watson's vicw of cognition. He opposed the structuralists' attempts to study cognitions dircctly and qucstioned the nccd for postulating cognitive structures and mechanisms beyond describing them in terms of stinulus-responsc connections. Watson wantcd vcry strongly to climinate the magic surrounding unobservable cognitive structures by showing that they could be understood in terms of observable cvents or at lcast potentially observable cvcnts. As his account represents an extremc, yct important, viewpoint, we will describe it bricfly without critical comments.

Watson proposed that memory can be accounted for adequately by established S-R bonds, which could have becn observed at thc time of their acquisition.He explicitly refutes the need fr "memory" as a construct.

The bchaviorist never uses the term "memory." He believcs that it has o place in an objective psycology. (Watson, 1925, p. 177)

By "memory," then, wc mcan nothing except that fact that when we mcet a stimulus again after an absence, we do the old habitual thing (say the old words and show the old visceral-emotional bchavior) that wc learned to do when we were in the presence of that stimulus in the first place. (Watson, 1925, p. 190)

Watson proposed that thinking can be scen as scquences of S-R connections that are primarily verbal.These covert verbal responses can be obscrved cither by asking the subject to think aloud, or by sensitive rratin theusular ctviy the lary nd th h ans.

The bchaviorist advances the vicw that what the psychologists have hitherto called thought is in shor nothing but talking to ourselves. (Watson, 1925, p. 191)

Watson views verbalization as bchavior, rather than as cxpression or description of a corresponding cognitive structure. In support of his argument, he notes that the verbalizations associated with many activitics, like playing golf, are totally dctachcd from actual bchavior—hencc, it is possiblc to talk about golf-playing, and cven to describe verbally how one should do it, without being able to play. Verbal reports on behavior can therefore be generated without any relation to the actual bchavior.

The behaviorist assumes that different types of bchavior are gencrated scparately, rather than bcing different expressions of common cognitivc structures and proccsscs. It was cvcn proposcd that verbal reports could bc conditioncd indcpcndently of bchavior (Verplanck, 1962). Δ study by Hull (1920) is often cited as showing that a subject's ability to describe a conccpt is not rclated to his ability to bchave according to the concept.

Sincc bchaviorism had no modcl of the structure of the cognitive system, it made no attempt to spccify the locus and charactcristics of awareness. Rescarch, cxcmplificd by a frcqucntly cited study by Grecnspoon (1955), has claimcd to show that changes in bchavior and learning can occur without awareness as asscsscd by verbal reports. Other rescarch has suggested that changes in attitudes may be unconscious, hence, unreportablc. Subjccts, whcn asked after an cxperiment how they did the task, sometimes give reports that arc inconsistent with their recorded behavior. All of these results wcre uscd by bchaviorists in support of their position that awareness is incomplete and cpiphenomenal. Thcy will bc discusscd at length in thc scction of this chapter on lcarning without awareness.

## 112 Protocol Analysis

## Rationalism

The rationalist position, almost at the opposite pole from behaviorism, secks to describe human thinking in terms of formal models from logic. Poincar's (Ghisclin, 1952) discussion of mathcmatical crcation is, for several reasons, a good cxample of thc rationalistic point of vicw. First, Poincaré is a proponent of the nccessity for assuming unconscious processes during crcative thinking. Sccond, he is very explicit in urging a rationalist explanation for thinking.

A first fact should surprise us, or rather would surprise us if wc were not so used to it. How does it happen there are pcople who do not understand mathematics? If mathcimatics invokes only thc rules of logic, such as are accepted by all normal minds; if its cvidence is bascd on principles comnon to all men, and that nonc could dcny without being mad, how docs it come about that so many persons are here refractory? (Ghisclin, 1952, pp. 22-23)

In order to explain how pcople somctimcs makc logical mistakes, Poincaré cvokes the fallability of mcmory. He argues that crrors do not occur in making inferences from premises but result from forgetting the carlier premises on which thc later inferences rcly, or remembering them only inexactly. Thus, we may have forgotten a step in a proof or may recall inaccuratcly an itcm in thc multiplication table.

Poincar attributes the superior mathematical skill of mathcmaticians to their better memory for mathematical cntitics, making it casier for them to follow and gencrate mathematical proofs. He points to the existence of a higher-level representation as crucial for mnemory.

A mathematical demonstration is not a simple juxtaposition of syllogisims, it is syllogisms placed in a certain order, and the order in which thcse cleincnts are placed is much more important than thc cleinents themselves. (Poincare, 1952, p. 24)

He clearly makes the point that "better memory" and higher-level representation arc intimatcly relatcd to cach spccifically devcloped skill—in this case in mathematics—and do not imply better memory in gencral for other kinds of material.

With his explanations of individual differenccs in terms of represcntation or memory, Poincaré (Ghisclin, 1952) can retain his view that logical inference and rational conscious scarch for alternatives is the main vehicle for human problem solving. In accounting for discoveries aaal eats hoer, n suh ra tvi ficient, and suggests that we must assume unconscious processes. We will return later o a fuller discussion of his empirical indings and hs theorics of unconscious processes.

More recently Polanyi (1969) has argued that logical inference and explicit knowledge do not span all types of knowledge available to people. Polanyi points to perception and recognition of objects as the primary examples of tacit knowledge (i.c., knowledge that is not cxplicitly spccified or specifiable). Polanyi argues, primarily on the basis of psychological evidence, that recognition is not an explicit reportable operation because subjects are, in general, unable (1) to report what particular information they relicd on to make the recognition, and () to statc what the necessary perceptual atributes are for identifying instances of that category.

We certainly recognize a hunan face at first sight and can say only from a subsequent analysis, and thcn rather inadequately, by what pariculars we recognized it. (Polani, 1969, p. 169)

The mcaning of a discasc is doubly unspccifiable. (1) We cannot identify, lct alone describe, a great number of the particulars which wc are in fact noticing when we diagnose a case of the disease. (2) Though we can identify a case of the discase by its typical appcarancc, we cannot dcscribc it adcquately, . .. (Polanyi, 1969, p. 132)

While the cvidence Polanyi adduces would create difficulty for a theory that assumes all thought processes to be applications of the rules of logic, it is not in any way incoimpatible with the modcl of thinking we are proposing here. We have assumcd that L'TM is indexed by a nct of tests that can be applied to stimuli in order to discriminate among and recognizc thcm. Nothing in this assumption implies that the person making a recognition has conscious access to thesc tests, or can statc what they are—and, indccd, the cmpirical cvidcncc shows pretty clcarly that there is no such access.

As to the ability or inability of a pcrson to describe an object or person he has just recognized, our model is silent. Such a description can be generated if (1) the descriptive characteristics have becn noticed consciously at some time in thc past and havc becn storcd in I.TM, and (2) thesc charactcristics have been indexed so that access can bc gaincd to them at the time the description is called for. A failure in ability to describe a recognizable stimulus can result from failure to attend to its characteristics (even when learning to recognize it), and it can also result from an inability to retrieve these characteristics on request.

Moreover, the characteristics that a person uses to recognize a stimulus may or may not bear a close rclation to the characteristics he has stored in memory and can retrieve if asked to provide a description. Anyone who has studicd the taxonomy of some phylum or order of plants or animals knows that his mode of recognizing particular spccies on sight bears little relation to the characteristics of those specics as recorded in formal taxonomic kcys. In fact, the characteristics given in the keys often cannot be observed without the use of the microscope and scalpel, hence cannot possibly provide the basis for direct recognition.

The unconscious processes postulated by Poincare and the tacit knowledge hypothesized by Polanyi both have genuine support in cmpirical cvidence, but this evidence can, as we have now seen, be interpreted in a way that is not inimical to the information processing hypothesis. It does inply that verbal reports, howcver complete they may be in other respects, will not cover the microstructure of recognition processcs. They will not cnable a subject to report how a recognition he has just achicved was accomplished.

On the other hand, thcre does not seem to be any basis for Poincaré's expectation that the conscious and reportable component of the thought processes should bear any close resemblance to the laws of deductivc thought laid down in formal logic. The laws of logic state conditions that a piccc of deductive reasoning must mcet in order to be valid. It says nothing about the processes uscd to discover or arrive at the valid conclusion.

Discovering a logically valid conclusion, as distinct from testing its validity, once discovered, is an inductive and not a dcductive process. It may b achievd by recognition, in which case the details o the rognition process will not gcncrally be reportable. Or it may be achieved by hcuristic, sclective scarch; in which case internediate stages of the scarch process will usually bc stored temporarily in STM, and hence will be accessible for verbal reports.

## The Information Processing View

What can wc say about the completeness of verbal reports from the vicwpoint of the information processing model we havc adopted in this volume?In this model, we cmphasize the difference betwcen the limited capacity of STM (the limits of attention) and the vast, essentially unlimited, storage capacity of I.TM. Information held in STM will be available directly to the subject, while information in l.TM will not be unless it is accessed and brought into attention. The thrce to five unrelated information structures that can be held simultancously in STM are best thought of as labcls or pointers to nodes in I.TM, where the actual information is stored. Hence, bringing such a pointer into STM is cquivalent to activating, in other models of memory, the corresponding node or nodes in I.TM.

Accessing Information in LTMI. Just as information stored in an cncyclopedia can be found readily only if appropriate cntrics are consulted in the index, so information can be accessed from 1.TM only if an appropriate accessing symbol is held in attention. Stating the conditions under which relevant information can be retrieved from 1.TM requires specifying the class of relevant information (index cntries) that will enable the system to idcntify this information and discriminate it from the rest.

For pcople who have cxpcrtise in a particular domain of knowledge, access to relevant information appears to be almost instantancous. Acquiring cxpertisc involves developing a very systematic way of indexing information, at the timc of storage, by attending to appropriate, usually multiple, indexing characteristics. Whcn somc knowledgc is required to solve a problem or answcr a qucstion, the available indexing tcrms will determine the dcsircd information, so that it can be accessed dircctly or its absence identificd.

Access can be cither dircct or indirect (that is, by association). If askcd to report the third word of the national anthem, most persons would access "national anthem" directly, but would have to count down the first versc, associatively, until the third word was cncountered. Unlcss cxactly the right stimulus (i.e., index cntry) is provided, retrieval from I.'TM may require some problem solving to find an access path, and very often a stimulus may not define univocally what meinory contents are wanted. Reports from I.TM cannot, therefore, be "complete" in the sense of guarantccing that all rclevant items have bcen retricved. Only that can bc rctrievcd which can be acccssed through cucs hcld in attention.

Storing and Indexing in ITM. It is even more obvious that information can bc retrieved from l.'TM only if has bccn stored there previously, and rctained. Thc hypothcsis about storage (fixation) that scems to us most defensible in the light of the cmpirical evidence is that if and only if information is heeded in STM for a sufficient interval of time will it bc stored (and indexcd) in I.TM. Attention (in a sufficient amount) provides thc necessary and sufficient condition for learning. This hypothesis implies that lcarning does not occur without awareness, and that the information available in I'T'M is a subsct of the information that, at some carlicr time or times, was hcld in STM.

The gcncral hypothcsis that attention lcads to fixation docs not indicate how thc stored information will be indexcd, and what stimuli will be cffective in cliciting it. Woodworth (1915) carricd out a number of verbal learning expcriments to dcterinine the conditions under which onc item, altended to in tcmporal juxtaposition to another, would be likcly to serve as an cffective stimulus for cvoking the other.

Things become associated only when thcy are continguous in cxpericnce. But is it a sufficicnt condition? Thcre is little in the cxperimental work on memory to indicate that it is sufficicnt, and much to indicatc that it is not usually dcpended on to accomplish results. The things to bc connected must bc togcther, in order to arousc the rcaction connecting thcm; but unless they arouse some such reaction, they do not become connectcd, except it be very weakly. (Woodworth, 1915)

In a wcll-known cxpcriment, Woodworth read subjects a list of forty words with the instruction to learn them in pairs so that they could respond with the second mcmber of a pair when the first mcmber was providcd as stimulus. They wcre thcn testcd on thcir ability to respond with the first itcm of a pair when thc sccond itcm of the previous pair was provided as stimulus. They succecded in only 7% of thc trials.

Thorndike (1932) subsequently replicated Woodworth's results and demonstrated thcir generality. In onc cxperimcnt, two groups of 100 and 140 subjccts wcre read ten sentences in thc same order 10 times with instructions to "listen to what I rcad with modcratc attcntion, as you would listen to a lecturc" (Ihorndike, 1932, p. 66). Aftcrwards, subjects werc givcn one or more words from a scntencc and askcd what word followcd. Subjects in thc two groups were 73% and 81% accurate, respectivcly, in reporting the word that followed a scntence's subject; but bcing probed for thc first word in a scntence by bcing given the last word of the previous scntence, they wcrc corrcct in only 2.3% and 2.8% of the cases, respectively, a levcl attributable to guessing.

In another cxpcriment, Thorndike (1932) read pairs of words and numbers to subjects. Hc designcd a list of 1,304 pairs so that certain word-number scquences like "bcaver-86 charadc-17" wcrc presented 24 times each. Subjects asked to give the numbers following words could respond correctly in 38% of the cases, but asked to give the words following numbers, wcre corrct in only.5%, a result no better than guessing.

Recognition Processes. The process wc know as "recognition" is pl  las  l I  e  s sct of characteristics of the object retrieved. When something is retrieved by recognition, thcre is also retricved, of course, all sorts of other information associated in I.TM with the item retrieved. What is not retrieved, however, are the internal steps of the recognition process itselfhence, our inability to say how we recognized somcthing.

In our modcl we assume that new memory structures are gencrated by bringing together thc contents of focal attention with information accessed from L.TM. As a result, a new structure is formed, and when this structure is accessed, it will provide a memory of the corresponding thought cpisode.

Since retrieval requires thosc cues to be present that were indexed to the I.TM content at the time of lcarning, recall is most likcly to be successful if the cucing stimulus is cncoded in the same way at rccall as it was at the original presentation. This lcads to the interesting prediction, in paircd associate learning, that the subject must rccognize having scen the stimulus itcm in order to excced chance in his recall of the response member of the pair. This prediction was confirmed by Bernbach (1967) and Martin (1967). using visual and oral stimuli, respectively.

That the cncodings of the stimulus must be cssentially the same at presentation and test for corrcct recognition was niccly demonstrated by Rubin (sce Woodworth, 1938). He showed reversible figures to his subjects: cither half could bc seen as figure or as ground.

Depending on whether the black or the white ficld is seen as figure, the stimulus will be cncoded and perceived in two different ways. Subjects were instructed to trcat onc color as figure, the other as ground. When thcy wcre subscqucntly prcscnted with old and new stimuli for rccognition judgments, with instructions to view cither the same, or the other, color as figure, their performance depended on whcther or not the color trcated as figure was the samc or had bcen reversed. In the former condition, 49% of the figures wcrc rccognizcd; in thc latter condition, only 9%—almost the same as the percentage (6%) of falsc recognitions of ncw figures not prescnted previously.

Tulving and his colleagues (cf. Tulving & Thompson, 1973) have presented considcrable cvidence to show that, for successful recognition and rccall of paired associates, stimuli must be encoded in the same way at time of test as at time of original presentation. Bower and Winzenz (1969) demonstrated that digit scquenccs were only recognized as previously scen if the digits were grouped in the same subscquences at both presentations. The cxtensive body of rescarch showing the close relation betwcen the perceptual and conceptual cncoding of the stimuli and subsequent recall is very niccly described in Bower and Hilgard (1981, Chapter 10).

Some of the strongest cvidence for the direct relation betwcen heeded information and memory comes from the extensive rescarch on memory that is incidental to performing certain tasks. Because cxcellent reviews of this literature alrcady cxist (Saltz, 1971; Smirnov, 1973), we will only sunmarize the nain findings. The information committed to memory corresponds closely to the aspects of the stimuli that must be heeded in order to perform the task (Smirnov, 1973). Incidental memory from some tasks is so good that explicit instructions to commit the information to memory do not improve performance on subscquent memory tasks. All of the above studies test only memory for physically present stinulus information; later, we will discuss memory for thoughts.

Thus, there is a wide spectrum of possibilities betwcen the cxtreme SR view, which assumes that uniquc cues are linked to unique responses, and the cxtreme rationalist position, which assumes that all information is semantically integrated, and all possible inferences are drawn (and stored) when new information is encountered. The information processing vicw, which wc have just skctched, takcs an intermediate position, making spccific predictions about the conditions under which information will be stored and the conditions under which it will be available for recall.

## Extensions of the IP Model

The information processing model describes the cognitive processes as producing a sequencc of states, cach markcd by a small collection of information in attention and STM. For the moment, wc will disregard information from the external cnvironment that is available in sensory stores, and simply consider thc successive states of hccded information in STM.

Formally, we can represent the time course of information in STM as a squc, cac clemt  whic is teupu o t proces that n  eha it su.Weiberately  frtr eciptin te procs tion n cues, his pros is a n its iat are not reportable.

Cognitive tasks vary dramatically in well-definedness and specificity, as do the relations betwcen inputs and their outputs. If the task is to find the sum of 2 and 3, the subjcct will usually access and retricve the correct answer, 5. In this case, the output is determined, at lcast semantically, by the input and process. However, if a subject is askcd to name a word starting with "A," the input is insufficicnt to determine what word will be selected. Subjects report that a word simply "occurs." (Most college students retrieve the samc word, "apple.") The retricved word is determincd by the structure and organization of l.TM. The same opaquc retrieval process makes it impossible for subjects to rcport veridically why they responded "rcd" whcn askcd to name a color—although thcy are quite willing to speculate about the reasons.

Our hypothesis is that the subject is only aware of input and output of such processes, and has no further direct information about the process itsclf. He docs not know which clements in the stimulus, which cucs, were functionally important for access to the responsc inforination. This doesn't mcan, of course, that subjects have no cognitive control over the responses thcy generatc. Thcy can cvaluate and process in other ways the information they access before responding with it. A subject, for cxample, may retrieve several words starting with "A," then retrieve additional information in order to makc a sclection among thcm. Subjects may also retrieve and apply additional constraints to the response bcfore it is verbalized (c.g., "word should bc unusual," "part of book title"). In such circunstances, subjccts can report additional information (the additional information they have retricvcd and stored temporarily in STM) beyond the stimulus and response.

There is considerable cvidence that subjects can and do conform to task instructions (the Aufgabe) in responding. If asked for a beverage they will respond with thc namc of a beveragc. Hencc information in attention and STM imposes constraints on accessed information and cognitive structures, cven though it docs not always determinc uniqucly which specific structure will bc accessed. The general and basic point is that subjects cannot gain access to information in LIM without bringing that information into STM, hcncc into attention.

The modcl we are proposing has strong implications for recognition and learning which wc wish to cxaminc in somc dctail. In the next section, we will examine cxperiments on recognition processes. Then we will revicw cvidence that suggests strongly that only information in attention is committed to memory, and evidence on the cffect of automation of responses through practice upon reportability.

## RECOGNITION PROCESSES

By recognition processes we mean the processes or mechanisms by which scnsory information is cncoded and familiar patterns and structures are identified. The Structuralists assumed that complex patterns were recognized by identifying thcir clements. and thus assumed that clements were identified prior to recognition of the pattern. The Gestalt psychologists argued that the complex patterns wcre recognized prior to identification of their parts. We agree with the Gestalt position to this cxtent: that in many situations, a complex pattern is accsscd without prior conscious awarenes of its parts.Whatever use is made of features in the rcognition process itsclf, these features nccd not be accessible to awareness.

Generally, wc recognize familiar faccs, words, and objects directly, that is, without storing in STM the fcatures cxtracted from the stimuli and uscd for discrimination. Therc is evidence, also, for direct recognition of more complex patterns and relations, cspccially when the presentation is visual.Claparède (1934) found that his thinking-aloud subjects did not report intermcdiate stages when generating interpretations and hypotheses for complex visual stimuli. Similar findings are reported for subjccts noticing rclations in gcomctry problems (Henry, 1934).

In recall and retrieval of familiar information, unless it requires problem solving with the id of successive associations, processes leave only the final product as tracc in STM. Thc phcnomcnon is so familiar that it appcars not to havc becn tested by experiment.Therc was ample evidence, however, from introspective rcports directed at the issuc of the cxistence of imagcless thought for Woodworth (1938, p.787) to reach the conclusion that "what is imagcless is not thought as much as recall."

## Evidence for One-Stage Recognition

We have argued that recognition is a onc-stage process, whose intermeiate products are not available in T for verbal reports. Let us look at the cvidence for this position, which is in direct opposition to that held carlier by the Structuralists. We will show, first, that in the recognition of complex patterns, lower-level components cannot generally be recognized prior to recognition of the larger pattern, and, second, that relatively fragmentary perceptual information often suffices for recognition of a whole complex pattern.

With respect to the first point, Johnson (1975) has shown that subjects cannot recognize a single leuer faster than the complete word containing that letter. There is even more extensive evidence for the second point. Elements of a pattern somctimes do not even nced to bc presented, much less hecded, for successful recognition of the pattern. Warren (1970) presented subjects with a recorded sentencc, replacing one or more phonemes with pure tones. Subjects almost never noticed the substitution. Warren and Warren (1970) showed that subjects could hear the correct word in a sentence even when context had to be used to disambiguate the stimulus. For example, the stimulus "ccl" was recognized, appropriately, as "whcel," "peel," or "hccl," depending on context. Similar results have bcen obtaincd by Cole (i973). Sec also, Best, Morrongicllo, and Robson (1981).

Strong cvidence for the opacity of recognition comes from some rescarch inspired by Kulpe, who was intrigucd by trained introspective observers' occasional confusions of imagincd with perccived images. In these cases the subjects were unaware of the inputs that caused them to retricve familiar structurcs. Replicating an original study by Perky (1910), Scgal (1971) showed that subjects instructed to form images of familiar objects in front of a white scrcen were influenced in thcir inagery when, without thcir knowledge, images werc projccted on the screen. For cxample, if subjects were askcd to imagine a coke glass, they were more likcly to describe a glass with "Coca-Cola" written on it when such a glass was dimly projected on the screen than when it was not.

In another study, (Segal, 1968), two incomplete red squarcs were projected dimly while subjects were forming an image of cither a plant or a violin. When questioned aftcrwards, only one of 33 subjects reported noting any projected images; twcnty of 32 subjects (about onc standard deviation above chancc) answcred corrcctly whcther the image was projected while they werc imaging the violin or while they were inaging the plant. Subjects making correct judgments would report the following kinds of grounds for thcir decisions:

Before the image of the plant that I reported, I imagined a pot of geraniums—I think that was projected.

The inage I had of a plant was funny, so I think that was projected. (Segal, 1968, p. 394)

Subjects thought that the projected images were images of plants and violins, and were confused when given a verbal description of the actual images which were neither plant-like nor violin-like. Hence their responses provide no real cvidence for awareness of the projected images.

In a followup study by Segal and Fusclla (Segal, 1971), 64 subjects generated thrce different images; an image was projected during one of these mental imaging tasks. Subjects were able to guess the period when the image was projected only at about chance levcl. When half of the subjects were shown a picturc of the projected image, thcir ability to infer when it was projected was almost doubled. When the other subjects were given a vecrbal description of thc projected image, "a triangular-shaped design of 3 grcen bars," thcir ability to infer the period of presentation was reduced to less than half. The authors argue that the perceptual similarity bctwcen projected inagc and remembercd displaycd image allowcd subjects to make more accurate judgments. It should be pointed out that the projected images are casily detected when subjects arc given only a discrimination task, and not a scparate imaging task (Segal, 1968). Thus, although thc projected image is visible, it is not scparatcly perccived during the image-generating task.

In an carly study, Pillsbury (1897) investigated the ability of trained subjects (among them Titchcncr!) to recognize misprints in bricfly prescnted words. Subjects wcre better able to dctect omission of letters than blurring or substitution of letters-opcrations more likely to preserve the shape of the presented word. Of course people frequently find typos and recognize the intended word from partial and misleading information. Perhaps more interesting, Pillsbury's subjects reported sceing lcttcrs that wcrc not presentcd. For cxamplc, a subject shown "disal," responded "deal," and rceportcd, "There may be something bc-"an" ut   iy, 19 .7.

Pillsbury also attempted to facilitate rccognition of the intended word by giving a synonym bcfore prcsenting the stimulus. In this condition, the number of mispellings correctly identified decreased by ten to twenty per cent. Subjcts occasionally saw the spoken synonym, or part o it, rather than the word presented.When "dwclling" was spoken and "acsidance" displayed, a subjct read "dwclling" and claimcd to have t tS reflected quite different mcanings from those intended. When "pane" was spoken and "winxow" presented, one subject responded "achc," and claimed to have scen "aiche."

Pillsbury also found evidence that parts of recently presented words occasionally intruded and wcre "seen." Much of Pillsbury's rescarch has been supported and cxtended with more modern cxpcrinental techniques, but without the use of verbal reports to capture the hecded information (cf. Morton, 1964; Neisser, 1967).

In an interesting study, Ellis, Shcpard, and Davics (1979) showed that subjects were able to recognize famous pcoples' faces froim partial information, cither of features or of contour. We can cxtend this analysis to rcading as wcll. For simple tcxt, comprchension may bc almost immcdiate, without intermediate steps in awareness.If texts are vague or ambigious, thcy may provide cues for triggering personal cxpericnces, a mechanism that can account for the "predictive" ability of horoscopes (cf. Snyder & Larson, 1972; Ulrich, Stachnik & Stainton, 1963).

In sum, there is strong cvidence that the recognition process is opaquc, so that subjects cannot report what cues allowed them to retrieve information from I.TM or what part of the recognized structure was actually containcd in the stimulus. Of coursc, subsequcnt to recognition, the recognized structure can be matchcd against information available to the senses. It is only under spccial circumstances (cspecially, the requirement of rapid response after brief prescntation of the stimulus) that the report of the recognized structure does not reflect the presented information veridically.

## "Spontancous" Recall

According to our modcl, retrieval of information from I.TM is a dircct function of thc in formation hccdcd—thc information accessed in I.TM will bc associatcd with some subsct of thc hccdcd information. We showcd carlicr that the goals and task information that arc hccded can effectivcly prevent irrclevant information from bcing acccsscd without conscious intermediate states or intent ("incidental" or "spontancous" recall). Hilgard (1948) describes cmpirical dcmonstrations of "spontancous recall" by Kohler and von Restorff (1935) and Bartel (1937). Kohler and von Rcstorff lct subjects solvc an arithmetic problem, then showcd them a shortcut for certain multiplication problems: $( \mathrm { a - b } ) ( \mathrm { a + b } ) = \mathrm { a } ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { b } ^ { 2 }$ Half of the subjccts were given a scrics of other arithmctic problems, while thc othcr half wcre given match-stick probleins and thcn both wcre givcn a new arithmetic problem, whcre the short-cut mcthod could be uscd. Seventy-two percent of the subjects in the match-stick group spontancously applicd the short-cut method, while only 26% of the others did so, indicating that in the absence of interference from intervening material "spontancous" recall of the short-cut occurred.

Kohler and von Restorff give the following protocol cxcerpts (translated from page 78) for spontancous recall of the short-cut.

Herc you can do this again— (a+b)(a- b). Right? But I am still not sure of that.

Perhaps I could come up with your mcthod by mysclf.

(Kohler & von Restorff, p. 78)

The subjects who failed to usc the special mcthod all recalled it when they wcre spccifically probcd for it afterwards. The information was in mcinory and was sometimcs (not always) uscd without conscious intent—that is, by recognition of its relevance to thc ncw task. Kohler and von Rcstorff (1935) and Bartel (1937) dcmonstrated thc same phenomena in experiments using a mcchanical construction sct.

Experts in thcir domain of expertisc display spontancous and immcdiate rccall of similar and relevant information (Chase and Ericsson, 1982). In contrast, novices have difficulty in retricving, or even knowing that thcy know, rclevant information (Bloom & Brooder, 1954; Allwood & Montgomery, 1981, 1982). Hencc, acccss to relevant information depcnds on thc adcquacy of the cncoding. A rich "indexing" of information may climinate the necessity for a consciously driven scarch for adequate retrieval cues.

## Recognition and Inference

From recognition of faces, it appcars that people rely on only a subset of cues to recognize acquaintances. They make many crrors of recognition, and can often recognize individuals with bcards shaved off or hair style changed (sometimes without noticing the change). We can therefore conclude that pcople, through recognition, gain access to an l.TM structurc that contains more information than was uscd in accessing it from the stimulus.With bricf presentations of stimuli, where there is no time for attending to the stimulus after recognition, subjects obtain essentially no information about what features actually evoked the recognition.

Hess (1975) showcd a group of twenty men two photographs of an attractive young woinan. The only difference bctwcen the two pictures was that one had been retouched to make the woman's pupils larger than in the other. The men responded (as measured by the sizes of their own pupils) twice as favorably to the picture with the larger pupils as to the other. When questioned afterwards, no subjects reported noticing the sizes of thc pupils. Somc reported that one photograph was "more feminine" or "prettier" or "softer" than the other. The study demonstrates that one can be influcnced by stimulus features without being able to detect which ones are responsiblc for the effect.

In naturalistic situations (espccially outside the laboratory), people can make many ccologically valid infercnces about the stimuli they encounter. If they sce a house, thcy can infer validly that it has a door, even if thcy don't notic the door. Subjects may gencrate invalid reports about stimuli by reporting thesc inferrcd features, which may or may not have actually bcen noticed. In normal pcrception there is no clear boundary bctwcen explicit observation and inferencc from the observation. Wheni a subject reports secing a stage magician make an object disappear, this is clcarly an inference rather than a dircct observation. Laboratory studies make clcar the role playcd by this intcraction bctwcen observation and inference in the recognition process.

Bruner and Postman (1949) showed subjects normal and altered playing cards for short durations in a tachistoscopc. Subjccts reported sccing (i.e., rccognizing) normal playing cards cven whcn the cards had been altered so that suits and colors wcrc mismatched. Subjects, on obscrving a red four of Spades, reported sccing cither thc four of Hearts or a black four of Spades. Whcther suit or color determincd thcir choice appcarcd to bc idiosyncratic for cach individual.

In another study, Postman, Bruner, and Walk (1951) showcd subjects strings of letters, where some strings contained a single reversed letter. The reversed lctters were correctly identificd or recognized with shorter latencics than werc required to recognize that the lctters were reversed.

## Assessment of Recognition Processes

Our postulate about rccognition processes allows us to argue against reportablc intermediate states for such processes. However, to avoid circularity, we necd to bc able to asscss indcpendcntly when recognition processes occur.

The sorts of independent evidence for recognition discussed in the previous scction almost all related to open-cndcd tasks, or tasks where the time constraints wcre too scvere to allow scquences of súccessive states to be heeded. In most cognitive tasks studicd in the laboratory, there is a uniquc responsc that is "corrcct." In such cases, sufficicnt information must be cxtracted from the stimulus to generate the correct response rcliably. Hencc, at somc prior timc, lcarning with fccdback must have occurred; the cognitive structurc that is accessed by recognition must cxist alrcady in I.TM. These two criteria give us some rather powerful mcans to discriminate rccognition processcs from the conscious processes for which verbal reports of intermediate states would bc available.

In order to sharpen our characterization of recognition processes, wc turn now to a discussion of the automatization of cognitive processes, and the cmergence of recognition processes.

## Automation of Responses

Although we hypothesize that attention is nccessary for learning or changing cognitive structures, it is no longer necessary when the same cognitivc proccss has bccn cxccuted many tines. Only a fcw studics have attempted to capture changes in reported information as a function of degrce of expertise and experience with a given type of process.

There appcars to be a close (negative) relation betwcen degree of practicc and awareness of intermediate stagcs of a process. The carly work of Ach and others suggested that the conscious content disappeared with cxtended practice and growing automaticity of thc processes." More recently, Ican and Martin (1966) found that overlearning in pairedassociate learning leads to a decrcase in the number of reported mediating associations. The work of Schncider and Shiffrin (Schncider & Shiffrin, 1977: Shiffrin & Schneider, 1977) suggests that there arc clcar differences betwcen automatic and controlled processing, in terms of speed and of accessibility for modification and learning. From all this evidence, it scems necessary to postulatc, as wc have, that many highly overlearned processes opcrate automatically without lcaving any more trace than their final result in STM.\*

We may distinguish bctwcen automatic processes that subjects already possessed prior to an cxperiment, as part of their cognitive skills, and processes whose intermediate stages became more automatic, and hencc less reportable, during the course of the cxperiment. In the case of the latter, reports from the automated processes at the cnd of the experiment will omit information about intermediate states of which the subjects were aware at the beginning of the cxperiment. We will later provide some concrcte cxamples of this phenomenon.

Recognition automates cognitive processes for a spccific task. Sincc this task is represented cognitively, the subject can change it at any timc. If he does, recognition will no longer determinc the responses. It might be thought that if a rcsponse wcre strongly cstablishcd, it would resist the owner's attempts to define thc task. Ach (rescarch reviewed by Ryan, 1970) thought he had demonstrated preciscly this—the response to a stimulus could be cxplaincd as the outcomc of a competition betwcen prcvious rooting of the response and its appropriatcness to the current task. But more recent rescarch appcars to show that he was mistaken. I.cwin (revicwed by Ryan (1970)) dcmonstrated that as many as 300 repetitions of the initial associations did not cause false responses to later, different, tasks, nor lengthen thc rcaction times. Ach's carly results probably resulted from subjects' confusions or misconceptions of what tasks they had actually been askcd to perform.

Rather similar cvidence has bcen obtained from cxperimental analysis of the Stroop cffect. In thc Stroop Tcst, subjccts are instructed to name the color of the ink uscd to print words that spcll the names of other colors. It appears (Glaser & I)olt, 1977) that in spite of thc instruction to name the color of the ink, the response corresponding to the printed word first comcs to thc subjcct spontancously, and has to be rejected actively. However, if the printed words arc semantically unrelated to color words the cffects arc small or non-existent (Klein, 1964; Kcele, 1973).

I ct us focus more directly on the devclopment of recognition-like processes in a given task as a function of amount of practicc. The initial instructions to the subjects communicate only roughly what the task is. Bcginning with the first trials, the subjects will notice or activcly scck regularities and constraints in the particular cxamples prescnted by the experimenter. When subjects discover rcdundancy in the information presented (syntactic constructions are constant, the responses stand in somc rulc-bound relation to information presented [Einstellung], or responses share a common characteristic that is not made explicit [set]), they can use thesc regularitics to improve thc efficicncy (and the stcrcotypy) of thcir response processes.

Quinton and l'ellows (1975) studicd thc cmergence of changes in subjects' cognitive processes with practice in solving three-tcrm scries problems (c.g., "Ben is taller than Gcorge; Iave is shorter than Gcorge; Who is tallest?"). They obseri cd that most of thcir subjects gradually shifted to a perceptual strategy that dcpended on recognizing problem types. Associated with thc change in stratcgy, thcy observed a dramatic reduction in solution time.

Wood and Shotter (1973) askcd subjects to respond with the appropriate kinship termn to qucstions like, "What relationship to a man is his father's sister's daughter?" They found that, with practice, subjects tended to go from meaning to formal propertics. The kinship terms were reported as organized in a spatial image. As one subject dcscribcd it (Wood and Shotter, p. 509), "! was imagining in levels . . . I go up for father, across for sister . . . I go up and down and across in levcls. After a while, up-across-down means cousin."

In an interesting study, Wood, Shotter, and Godden (1974) showed that with more practice, subjects became less able to answer uncxpected questions about thc information prescnted. After presentation of a number of five-term series problems, subjects wcre asked unexpectedly about the order of two items, which was different from the order in the original problem. Whcn this unexpectcd qucstion was asked after the first problem, 55% of the subjects gave a correct answer, but aftcr the 21st problem, only 10%.  control group was presented with twenty problems, but asked to arrange the terms in alphabctical order before thcy recived thcir first five-term scrics problem and thcir unexpected qucstion. In this casc thcy answered the uncxpccted qucstion correctly in 70% of the cases. Thesc studics show evidencc of a movement, with pracice from complete awarness o problem-solvin steps toward more efficient solution by recognition.

1.uchins' (1940) classical experiments showed that subjects, given a series of problems with the same rule for solution, solved test problems by a "blind" application of that rulc, even when a much simpler solution was possible. Evidence indicates that the effect is not the result of inadvertent mechanization, but results from subjects' deliberate choices of strategy. A nurnber of expcrinents have reduced the Einstellung effect by marking the test problems as scparate problems rather than a continuation of thc sequence of problems presented before (Jensen, 1960). Morcover, a single cxtinction trial (a problem solvable with a different rule) is sufficient to clininate the effect. 1uchins and luchins (1959) supported this interpretation in an extensive revicw of Einstellung:

What scemed to be important was not the recency of the E method, or that the test tasks immediately followed the E tasks, but the subjct's attitude: that is, whether or not he vicwed the tasks as belonging together, as members of a homogencous serics. (L.uchins & l.uchins, 1959, p. 333)

There is evidence for Einstellung cffects in the performance of experts, in whosc responses recognition processes should be prominent. When askced to "give the mathematical cxpression for all numbers that, when divided by seven, yicld a remainder of 89," mathematically trained subjects wcre more likcly than novices to noticc the semantic contradiction in the qucstion and produce the syntactically corrcct formula (Kruteiskii, 1976). Werthcimer (1945) found that mathematically trained subjects tended to usc the complex general solution of a problem requiring thcm to calculate the arca of an altar window, while novices selected a simple and "insightful" solution.

The reason cxperts rarely do worse than novices, we conjecture, is that thcir recognition processes are carcfully "dcbugged" during their cxtensivc lcarning cxpcricnces. Morcover, the cxpert can always fall back on a systematic and conscious scarch for a solution, which should always give him an advantagc over the novice.

## Perceptual-Motor Processes

Rescarch on perceptual-motor processes also demonstrates that in these wc normally have access only to ccrtain higher-levcl intermediate results.

In a recent paper. Broadbent (1977) presents cmpirical evidence for a hicrarchical organization of such processes. Most studics of perceptualmotor processes using thinking-aloud protocols and other verbal reports have cxamined problem solving with puzzles. As we reported in Chapter 2, in tasks allowing physical manipulation, Klinger (1974) found frequent higher-level verbal evaluations of unverbalized solution attempts (e.g. "Yep," "Dammit," ctc.), and verbalizations of attention-control processes (c.g., "1.et's sce," "Where was 1?" ctc.). When cngaged in perceptualmotor manipulation, subjects did not verbalize, and appeared not to be aware of, the lower-level content or structure of thcir throught processes. Ruger (1910) found that subjects could often solve one of his mechanical puzzles several times, yct provide only a limited high-level account of the internediate steps leading to the solution. It has been suggested that physical manipulation is different from thinking in not cmploying any internal (i.c., STM) representation (IDurkin, 1937).

## INFORMATION ABOUT COGNITIVE STATES

In this sction we will examinc the assertion that STM contains all the information that is availablc for report. We will first discuss subjects' reports of being closc to remembering somcthing (tip-of-the-tonguc), and their subjective ccling that thcy could recognize information they cannot recall (fecling-of-knowing). Wc will ask whcther such reportable states, having testable bchavioral conscquences, can be represented in terms of structures in STM and how these structures can be used to help extract the information presumably present but currently inaccessible in I.TM. Then we will ask how subjects can be confident about the adequacy and correctness of information they have recalled. A strict interpretation of our model argucs that the subject only knows what cucs and task information he hccded just prior to hceding the retrieved structure.

## Tip-of-the-Tongue

Jamcs (1890) is gencrally crcdited with first describing the tip-of-the-tonguc phenomenon. Early rescarch rclicd primarily on analyzing situations in which such phenomena occurred spontancously. Woodworth (1938) found that in such situations, the subjects almost invariably had additional information about the items they sought, beyond h it  h knowledge of some syllables, number of syllables, initial letter) could usually be verified as valid partial information about the word being sought.

A more systematic and controlled study by Brown and McNeil (1966) showed that subjects expressing a "tip-of-the-tonguc" fecling, but unable to recall a word, were able to report some of the letters in the word, the number of syllables, and the location of the primary stress, with much better than chance probability.

Yarmey (1973) studied the tip-of-the-tongue experience in naming famous pcople prescnted in pictures. When he asked his subjects to supply information in any one of several categorics, they tended to mention first the place where they had seen a picture of the person, then when they had last scen one. Attempts to recall initial letters and numbers of syllables of names had no clcar order preferencc.
