The amounts of such information rctrieved were substantial, regardless of whether the subject succeeded in recalling tic name, or even recognized it when the experimenter presented it.

Koriat and T.ieblich (1979) demonstrated that subjects had betterthan-chance records in gucssing thc letters and number of syllables even of words thcy didn't know. Apparently these subjects uscd information about the attributes that would bc typical for such words. Hence, memory for specific iteins and events can bc confounded by infcrence from more general memory.

In a couplc of studics Hart (1965, 1967) studicd thc validity of subjects' feclings of being ablc to rcecognize an item they couldn't recall. The questions hc put to his subjccts involved general knowledge like, "Which planet in our solar system is the largest?"Hart found that subjects wcrc able to predict rcliably whethcr thcy would subscqucntly recognize the unrecallable answers. They wcrc also able to predict, although with lower accuracy, thcir ability to recognize lcarncd paired associates.

Blake (1973), using bricf presentations of trigrams followcd by an 18-scond distractor task, found that fecling-of-knowing judgments were markedly rclated to number of letters recalled correctly.The judgments were also rclated to ability to recognize thc corrcct answers, evcn after differences in recall were held constant. These results were confirmed in a sccond experiment that climinatcd the effect of the number of letters recalled initially. The result implics that the cncoding of the trigrams was not cntircly in terms of the component letters.

## 132 Protocol Analysis

Wellman (1977), using a paradigm similar to Hart's, with pictures to bc named, asked his subjects (kindergarten, lst gradc, and 3rd grade students) (1) to makc judgments of "fccling-of-knowing," (2) to tcll what they knew about the thing pictured, and (3) to report if thcy had scen the pictured items. Judgments that thcy had scen an object proved to be better predictors of subsequent recognition than "fecling-of-knowing" judgments. The verbal comments showed a clear relation betwcen amount of knowledge about the object, on the one hand, and a fecling of knowing and successful recognition, on the other.

All of the above experiments provide evidence that the ability to make accurate judgments about one's memory or feclings is associated with the availability of related knowledge that can be accessed prior to those judgments.

## Retrieval Without Direct Recognition

The rescarch on both tip-of-thc-tonguc and fcecling-of-knowing show that I.TM is highly organized. Subjects often know how storcd information is encoded and generate the appropriate retrieval cues to access it. Similarly, subjects often know when thcy don't havc information in memory, because thcy find that the information is not available where it should be stored. For cxample, one can know that onc docsn't know the phone number of the White House. If it were present, it would surely be associated to the stimulus, "White House phonc number," hence would be recalled.

The appropriate storage of rclevant information with reasonable cucs is not mystcrious within our proposed model, which views I TM as a richly indexcd and cross-indexcd data basc. When a subject is presented with ncw information, he will access (by recognition) the relevant I.TM structures and store some subsct of the hccded in formation. At the same timc, new recognition access routes to the information will bc created (new "index cntrics"). This continuing processing and storage of heeded information will allow dircct access by recognition to many important picces of information.

Machotka (1964) argucs that our ability to judge and evaluate objc an popl rapidly ust e atrbutd onconscius prosig We would argue, on the contrary, that relevant information can be accessed dircctly because it was hccded on a previous occasion and stored with appropriate access paths. When the new stimulus is presented, these paths can be used without the requirement of an unconscious review of previous experiences.

Subjects often have to retrieve in formation that cannot be accessed directly—that has not been indexed. Everyone has stored a vast number of personal expcriences, thought cpisodes, and picccs of detailed information that can only bc recalled via an activc scarch for retrieval cues and an active cvaluation of the retrieved 1.TM structures. Only in this way can people recall how often they have gone to the movics in the past ycar, or have been swimming, or the names of all the different cars or detergents that they know. (See Chapter 5 for a discussion of these recall processes and associated verbal reports.)

When subjects are questioned about information stored in I.TM, their responses depend not only upon the presence or absence of that information, but also on whcther the cues that are present are adequate for its retricval.Inability to retrieve a mcmory structure is not equivalent to its abscnce from memory. One of the most reliable cffects in the literature on human memory is the difference between the number of stimulus items that a subject can recall and the number of items that he can recognize as having been presentcd. Recognition that something has been scen before is the most sensitive test that an in formation structure is in LTM.

The cucs provided for memory retrieval may be consistent or inconsistent with the way in which thc storcd information is indexed. When appropriate cucs are availablc, immediate recognition is triggered. In other cascs, somc of the information in thc cucing stimulus may enable a successful heuristic scarch to be made for the information, while the remaining information is uscd to chcck proposcd answers for adequacy. For cxample, if askcd to supply an 8-lctter English word whose 6th letter is e, a subject might narrow the scarch by sccking word endings of thc form e-, finding ent, and then sccking words cnding in ent. Having found one (c.g., "dissident"), it is tested for lcngth and rcjected. Another (c.g., "accident") is accepted. The entirc memory retrieval can thus be scen as an initial rctricval specification, use of somc part of this specification to acccss a memory structure, and vcrification that the memory structure is consistent with thc cntirc specification (Norman & Bobrow, 1979).

What is hecded and thus reportablc in memory retrieval without direct recognition? The retricval cucs have to bc hccded and kcpt in STM. What are reportable in recognition, according to our model, arc only the input (the cueing stimulus) and the output (the acesscd I.'TM

## 134 Protocol Analysis

structure). If the answcr is not immediatcly rccognized, the cucing stimulus may be augmented by hcuristic scarch and other problemsolving cfforts, and the intermediate products of these cfforts can also be reported. Which retrieval cucs were effective in accessing the I.TM structure can only be reported in retrospcct by recall of these intermediate products or by inference from the nature of the information recovered.

Within this framework we are able to capture rather nicely the inability of subjects to give informative reports for some of thcir cognitive processes. In recognition, the subjects simply do not have any information in STM to report. When pressed for a "reason" for their responses, they have no recourse but to rationalize (i.c., to infer after the fact what the effective cues must have been). When retrieval involves intermediate scarch steps, however, subjects can report various items that were held temporarily in STM as the "reasons" for their responses.

Turning to the positive side. what can subjects report about their retrieval processes and thcir memory structures? First, thcy can assess, with somc reliability, which of thc itcms of information they recall is accurate, and also thcir correctness in making recognition judgments. Moreover, subjects have somc ability to prcdict whcther thcy possess certain information in I.TM even in situations whcre thcy are unable to recall that in formation.

## Judgments of Confidence

Subjects can cvaluate thcir confidence in the correctness of information recalled if they can retricve additional information that is redundant with it, hcnce permits it to be checked for veridicality. A subject who has the responsc "Monrovia" simply indcxcd to thc stimulus "Capital of Liberia" may be unable to assess the accuracy of his responsc. if Monrovia, however, cvokes "Monroc was President of the United States when Liberia was founded," this provides a rcason for confidencc in the answer. Thus, without any direct cvidence from the process of retrieval, confidence can be judged. Speed of retrieval might also be a partial basis for judgments of confidencc, so that even the (rapid) immediate recognition may bc judged correct with high confidence. In a recent paper Koriat, Lichtenstcin, and Fischhoff (1980) discuss thc rclevant rescarch showing that pcople can successfully assess thc correctness of their memories with a few exceptions. However, they are overconfident in the correctness of their answers caused by overemphasizing evidence justifying the sclccted alternative and disregarding evidence contradicting it. Hencc, confidencc judgments can be improved by asking subjects to list reasons, contradictions, then choices. Judging confidence involves accessing and reviewing relevant knowledgc, and bias in confidence judgments is duc to biascd rctrieval of such knowledge.

In laboratory research, ratings of confidence in recall and recogniton have frequently ben shown tobe preictive of corrctness ( Wearing, 1971). In rescarch on eye-witness testimony, however, the relation betwcen confidence and accuracy is not uniformly positive (Deffenbacher, 1980). However, in a thorough revicw, Deffenbacher shows that in studics with high rccognition accuracy, indicative of substantial memory, a strong relation is found bctwcen accuracy and rated confidencc. It is only in studics with lower (yet rcliable) recognition accuracy that there is no relation betwcen accuracy and rated confidence.

Apparently the circumstances that yicld high accuracy also provide subjects with information on which to base thcir ratings. Some studics have sought to discover what that information is.

Loftus and Bell (1975) have studied the process of recognizing pictures that wcre viewed with thc instruction to remember them for subsequcnt recognition. At the time of judging whcther or not a picture had been presented before, the subjects were asked if their judgments rested on a spccific detail or on gencral familiarity with the picturc. Performancc was very much bctter when a dctail could be namcd than when the subjects simply citcd gcncral faniliarity. From cyc-movement records during initial presentation of the pictures in an carlier study, Loftus (1972) found that the dctail verbalizcd during the recognition trials was the detail that had reccivcd most fixations at the initial presentation.

How can we account for the numerous demonstrations that subjects' responses to memory probcs (cspecially lcading questions and providing responsc alternatives) can be altered by the cxperimenter (L.oftus, 1975, 1979)? These demonstrations of experimenter influcnce uniformly pertain to situations whcre the accuracy of recognition is low. In such situations, the actual cvidence available in mcmory is compatible with a number of alternative responses, so that the one selected may depend on the experinenter's probc (comparc Kubovy & Psotka, 1976; Kubovy, 1977). Wc are not aware of any such dcmonstration for information that subjects have memorizcd so thoroughly that they can assess confidence reliably. These results are highly consistent with our model.

## WHAT IS REPORTED?

We have now revicwcd the cvidence for an information-processing model of cognitive processes that characterizes the in formation that is in attention and Sl'M at any point in timc. We have shown that such a model is consistent with, and indecd supported by, a widc range of empirical cvidence about recognition processes. In fact, wc have been unable to identify any directly conflicting cvidence. However, in the pages that follow, we will discuss a number of studics that have argucd against various assumptions of our modcl by claiming incomplctencss and nonveridicality of verbally reported information. We will sce whcther these arguments invalidate the picture wc have drawn thus far.

Our model proposes that verbal reports are based on information currently hcld in STM, or information retricvable from I.TM that was at some previous time held in STM. In order to avoid circularity, wc must be able to state, from indepcndent sourccs of cvidencc, what kind of information is heedcd, and hence passes through STM.

Although it may be vcry hard, in gcneral, to know what information is in a subject's STM at a particular timc, thc assumptions of the information processing modcl do allow fairly strong predictions to be made. First, the modcl claims that subjects' memories of carlier thoughts and information are a subsct of the information to which thcy attended. No ncw mcmory structures can be stored in l'TM or subscquently retrieved unless thcy wcre hccded when presented. The information that will be hccded, for example, in gcnerating a mathematical proof or solving a mathematical problem will be different if the subjct alrcady has memory acccss to similar proofs or to rclevant spccial mcthods or thcorems than if he docsn't. With a knowledge of prior thcorems, the subject may short-cut many stcps in the proof.

The existence of enormous individual differences in the knowledge held in memory mcans that predictions of verbalizations must bc contingent on the retricvable inforimation of cach subjcct. In many experimental studies of thinking, investigators havc tried to avoid this difficulty by using "naive" subjectsthat is, subjects who did not have any prior expericnce with the problem domain.Gestalt psychologists were alluding, in part, to the difference betwcen naive and cxperienced subjects in their distinction betwcen productive and reproductive thinking.

In cases where wc can rule out reproductivc, or recognition-based cognitive processes, we can make strong predictions that any infornation that is recalled must have been heeded in the expcrimental situation.

Perhaps the best example of a situation where we can make this prediction is learning under experimental control, where a subject changes his behavior into behavior determined by the experimenter. Under our hypothesis, such learning can always be traced by concurrent reports of heeded information. We begin our review of evidence bearing on this prediction with studies using different kinds of verbal reports in learning experiments. We will then review all studies of which we are aware that contain apparently damaging evidence against our model.

## LEARNING WITHOUT AWARENESS

The mechanisms and processes that emerged from the associationist and behaviorist analyses of learning were so simple that it must have been very tempting to propose that these processes were unconscious and inaccessible for verbal reports. Some influential theorists, like Thorndike (1932, 1933), argued that an association could be strengthened without the subject knowing what it was, hence that the subject should not be able to report it. In an experiment by Thorndike and Rock (1934), evidence was found that appeared to support such a view. By way of introduction to our topic, we will describe that study and some follow-up studies that refute the argument. Thorndike and Rock had subjects generate words as fast as possible in response to stimulus words. Subjects were told "wrong" if the response word was meaningfully related to the stimulus word (synonym, opposite, etc.). They were told "right" if the response formed a sequential connection with the stimulus (e.g., yours → truly, up → stairs, etc.). For ambiguous or unclassifiable responses, the subjects received no feedback at all. The subjects showed gradual and variable learning, which to the experimenters implied lack of insight. Insight, they argued, should lead to abrupt, single-trial improvement in responses.

The last assertion is not correct, as shown by a follow-up study by Irwin, Kaufmann, Prior, and Weaver (1934). Subjects after explicit instruction as to what words constituted correct responses, did not learn faster than subjects not receiving any instruction. In further experimentation, Postman and Jarrett (1952) found that both informed and uninformed subjects showed gradual learning. The informed subjects learned more rapidly than the uninformed. A detailed analysis of the behavior of uninformed subjects provided a wholly parsimonious explanation of this difference. Sometime during the experiment, the large majority of the uninformed subjects were able to verbalize thc correct principle. Subjects unable to verbalize the principle showcd no improvement. Dramatic improvement was observed on and after the trial on which the principle was first verbalized. However, a much smaller, yct statistically reliable, improvement was found for trials preceding the first verbalization of the correct principle. Postman and Jarrett argue that this improvement could be duc to lcarning without awareness, or to partially correct verbalized principles. An alternative hypothesis that would account for the results observed is that subjects who incidentally made a couple of correct responses wcre more likcly to access or induce the principle. This study illustrated, incidentally, the potency of concurrent verbal reports.

## Concurrent Verbal Reports of Learning

In Chapter 1, we revicwcd the study in which Vcrplanck and Oskamp claimed to have shown that verbalized rules are dissociated from the bchavior they were supposed to control. When correct bchavior was rcinforced, thcy made 71.8% corect responscs, but only statcd thc correct rulc for thc bchavior in 48.4% of the trials. When correct statement of the rule was rcinforced, thcy statcd thc rule correctly on 92.8% of the trials, but took the correct action in only 76.8%.

Dulany and O'Conncll (1963) showcd these results derivcd from two artifacts. First, the data analysis did not corrcct for guessing; making this correction reduces the percentage of corrcct responses in the first case to 43.6%, close to the percentage of correct rule statements. Second, thcy showcd that Verplanck and Oskamp's rules wcrc somctimes ambigious. Taking account of thc ambiguity, in all but 1l of 34,408 trials the subjccts did cxactly what thcy said (in the rule statement) thcy were going to. Hencc, the cxpcriments provide no cvidence whatsoever that the rules vcrbalized werc inconsistent with the bchaviors.

Numerous studics document the support for consistency between verbalized rulcs, concepts and hypotheses and immediately prcceding and succceding bchavior, before subjects reccivc fecdback. In Schwartz (19), where subjects were asked their reasons for placing a card as they did, reasons consistent with placements were given on all but 2 of 1,962 trials. Even more impressive, Frankcl, I.evinc, and Karpf (1970) obtained retrospective reports from subjects on the basis of thcir responses to four carlier discrimination-lcarning problems with 30 non-fccdback t a,   that scul pro urt than 90% of the sequences of trials.

In an attempt to differentiate betwcen an incremental S-R theory of concept attainment and a cognitive theory, Prentice (1949) had subjects think aloud during concept attainment. In an cxperimental group the correct response was reversed after 20 trials. Prentice predicted (cognitive or one-trial learning theory) that if no mention of the relevant hypothesis was made by the subject prior to the reversal there would be no difference betwcen groups in learning times. When all subjects mentioning the relation in their protocols were delcted from the analysis, the difference between groups was about 20 trials as compared to a cognitive theory's prediction of no difference and an S-R thcory's prediction of Prentice thus iners that there are nonconscius processes opat ing.

A critical reading of Prentice shows that the difference between the groups is barely significant. To infer, therefore, that the difference is 20 trials is clcarly unwarranted. The concept-learning situation in Prentice's study involves only 8 diffcrent stimuli, cach capable of taking on 2 different values16 instances in all. Prenticc reports that most subjects usually considered cxplicit hypothcscs, and the solution cmcrged while they wcre summarizing the results of the last thrce or four trials. That attention to stimulus attributes is a primary componcnt in lcarning is shown by the fact that cxperimental subjects attending to the right rclation before reversal required about 15 trials less than avcrage to learn after reversal.

In all other studies coming to our attention where it is claimed that the learning process is unconscious, hence not reportable, thc verbal reports wcre not given concurrently with the responscs. In interpreting these studics, wc must thercfore makc further assumptions about memory in order to relate the verbal reports with the criterial bchavior. In particular, it is risky to assumc that awareness during a lcarning experimcnt can be determined by questioning after the experiment is over.

## Assessing Awareness from Post-Experimental Questioning

We consider next the situation whcre a subject is probed for information that is not thcn available in STM. Then the information must bc retricved from I.TM. Memory retricval is fallable, somctimes causing access to othcr rclated, but inappropriate, information. Further, what information can bc recalled depends on what cues and probes arc provided. Hence the completeness of the in formation retrieved will vary with the probing procedures.

The controversy over whether learning can and does occur without awareness, as evidenced by retrospcctive verbal reports froin subjects, has reccntly bccn reviewed by Brcwcr (1974). Drawing on his work, wc will scck to interpret the differenccs, primarily mcthodological, that distinguish studics finding lcarning withoul awarcness from studics not finding such lcarning. Onc typc of study has bcen criticizcd repeatedly for poor documentation of probing proccdurcs and brief postexpcrimental interviews, which are sometimes not given aftcr the lcarning trials but after an additional scrics of extinction trials (Spiclberger, 1962). This design docs not climinate the possibility that subjects retain in STM the information about the rcinforcement contingency until it is lost during overlcarning or cxtinction trials. To ensure a rclativcly complete verbal report, the probing should occur just after the last learning trials, preferably before the subject is told that the cxpcriment is over. Many of the studics of this first type scem little concerned with these considerations. Furthermorc, the verbal probes uscd have been global questions, like "What did you think the expcrincnt was about?" (Brewer, 1974) In our framework, such probes will not clicit retrospcctive memory of the subject's own cognitive processes so much as cncourage him to gencratc hypothcscs about the cxpcriment, which may or may not be rclated to those processes.

A sccond type of study, which has respondcd to the above criticisms by probing subjccts just after thc last lcarning bloc of trials and explicitly asking then or thir memorics of their cognitive processes during thc preccding trials, has gencrally becn unable to ind cvidence for lcarning without reported awareness of thc reinforcement contin-The sui  this tye hav beencriiciz in turn a leading qucstions. We have alrcady discussed this issuc in the scction on effects of probing. The nced for spccific probes is not wcll documented, ut a gneral motivation or  ivei Dulany 6Another important consideration is that the subjects often report contingencics that, while not identical with the one the experimenter reinforces, are corrclated with it (Dulany, 1962). Here failure to report the experimenter's version of the contingency may simply mcan this is not the version the subjct is using, and may ot t allimplyincompleteness in the report of the contents of STM.

In a third type of study, subjccts arc asked to verbalize during conditioning experiments according to a techniquc suggested by DeNike (1964), in which the subjcct writes down "any thoughts that come to you that have any relation to the cxperiment" (p.3). Using this procedurc, DeNikc was able to extend the claim of consistency between written thoughts and behavior, finding that subjects' bchaviors changed on the trial in which thcy wrote down thcir first correct hypothesis about the reinforcement.

Also using DeNike's techniquc, Kenncdy (1970, 1971) found that the bchaviors changed before subjects wrote down that they were confident of their hypotheses or their verbal hypothcses were confirmed. Brewer (1974) points out that Kenncdy's finding, that behavioral changes werc associated with trying out or modifying verbal hypothescs, docs not challenge, but supports the validity of the verbalizcd information.

Comparing different probing techniques with cach other indicates that the written thoughts clicited with DeNike's technique are incomplete as compared with information obtaincd with Dulany's postcxpcrimental qucstionnairc (Sallows, Dawcs, & Lichtenstein, 1971). There is also some evidence that the incompleteness may be causcd by thc requircment of written rcsponses. Silvcira (1972) found a markcd differencc in number and character of responscs clicited during a creativity test betwcen written and oral response conditons. Writing the responses or ideas, as contrasted with giving thcm aloud, lcd to cvaluation and censorship.

It has been assumed implicitly that the contents of STM after the last learning bloc are representative of the contents during the preceding trials. This is a rcasonable assumption for conccpt lcarning, and for experiments with deterministic rcinforcement schedules, where there is no inducement for the subject to rcjcct a correct hypothesis. Several studics have shown that subjects arc unlikcly to change hypotheses in responsc to positive feedback (Hcidbreder, 1924; Karpf & L.cvine, 1971). However, since subjects are highly likcly to changc hypotheses in responsc to negative fcedback, the assumption of stablc hypothescs is less tenable for probabilistically determined fecdback conditions.

## 142 Protocol Analysis

In a recent study by Williams (1977), all subjects who learned were assessed (by a procedure of the Spiclberger type (1962)) to have bcen awarc of thc rcinforcement. However, in a sccond cxperiment with probabilistic relations, Williams found cvidence of learning without awareness (assessed by the same procedure). During the cxperiment, subjects were explicitly (and falscly) told that "All thc sentences I said 'correct' to met the same necessary requirements" (Williams, 1977, p. 93). These instructions would encourage subjects to abandon correct and correlated hypotheses when negative feedback was encountered. A study by O'Connell (1965) shows clearly that if the subjects verbalize their hypotheses on cach trial, the verbalized reports account for thcir behavior cven in a non-deterministic cnvironment with partial reinforceinent.

There is a related issue of awareness of mediating associations. A scrics of studies (Bugelski & Scharlock, 1952; Horton & Kjcldergaard, 1961; Russel & Storims, 1955) have found that paired-associate learning can be facilitated by prior exposure of the subjects to the proper mediating associations. Subjects who first lcarned lists of paired associates of types A-B and B-C learned lists of type A-C faster than control groups. In all thesc studies informal post-expcrimcntal qucstioning gave no evidence that the subjects were aware of using any mediating B-list items. "None of the s was able to report any corrct appreciation of the nature of the experiment and most assuredly did not verbalize a pattern of A-B, B-C, A-C in learning the third list (Bugelski & Scharlock)."This was interprcted as evidence for unconscious mcdiation in learning.

However, the above studics can be criticized on the same grounds as the studics purporting to demonstrate concept learning without awareness; and typically wc find differcnt results in morc recent studics that probe for rctrospective information in a more controlled and ambitious manner. In a study by IDean and Martin (1966), the subjects, after reaching critcrion for the third list, read cach stimulus of this list and were then asked to tell the experinenter exactly what came to their minds when thcy saw the syllable on the screen. The subjects wcre then shown the cntire list of paired associates and asked how the list was learned. Dean and Martin found that a majority of the subjects reported using at least one mcdiating term from the previously lcarned facilitating lists. An analysis of learning rates for cach of thc reportcd mediation types (other than A-B-C mediation) showcd clcarly that thc cffective difference among groups was attributable to the occurrence or non-occurrence of A-B-C mediation. When Dean and Martin had one experimental group overlearn the paired-associate list for ten cxtra trials, they found a significantly lower occurrence of reported mediation, thus suggesting that direct and automatic processing had devcloped as a result of additional practice.

In a study following a procedure similar to that uscd in Horton and Kjeldergaard (1961). Horton (1964) used a dircct question: "Did you notice any relationship betwcen the pairs you just completed and the ones you learned carlier in the cxperiment?" Horton assessed three levels of awareness, where the highest level required naming the actual mediating items. With a varicty of experimental manipulations, he found a consistent relation betwcen mediation (facilitative effect of previously learned PA lists), and assessed awarcness. There are many differences betwcen the studies reporting awareness of mediating items and those that report no awareness. Horton suggested that the difference betwcen his study and Horton and Kjeldergaard stemmed from the factors in the cxperimental situation stinulating awareness. The two groups of studics also differ in the suictness of the lcarning criterion, which might have affected the retricvability of the mediating links. However, the most obvious difference, in our vicw, lies in the probing proccdure.

## Sorting Without Awareness of Concept

In concept lcarning, it has been observed that subjects can sclect appropriate instanccs in tcst trials without bcing able to state thc concepts thcy arc using (Ilcidbredcr, 1934, 1936; Phelan, 1965; Smokc, 1932). Although these studies have been cited in support of the notion that the verbalized information is incomplete, a more rcasonable interprctation is that the subjects cannot formulate the concept as it is defincd by the experimenter, although thcy can differentiate instances from noninstances by recognition. Corrcect selection of instances could be mediated by processes likc memorizing cxcmplars or a sct of correlated discrimination features without using a rule expressed in terns of common fcatures. A concept-formation task can cmploy a largc number of different stratcgics. If the number of different stimuli is fairly small (20 or less) subjects might cven memorize them or use a mixture of memory for exemplars and rule learning. If a subject wcre activcly cmploying a hypothesis, we would cxpect him to bc able to report it. If hc wcre relying on mcmory for excmplars, we would not expcct him to bc able to report them, for they would not all residc simultancously in STM.

Under these circumstances, the conccpt attainment task is a recognition task, and we know that recognition docs not imply ability to recall.

IThere is evidence that subjects sometimes pcrform conceptattainment or rule formation tasks by relying on similarity between the stimulus instance and evoked cxcmplars in memory (cf. Brooks). None of these processes for concept attainment can be ruled out a priori, and in fact, verbal reports during performance might be the most useful data for discriminating among them.

When Smoke (1932) asked subjects to identify the concept used by the experimenter from verbal descriptions, he found in four experiments that 20-25% of the verbal descriptions of the successful subjects were defective, and "usually too inclusive" (p. 20). This study, and other related ones, do not address the incompleteness problem as we would state it: Is the verbal report a complete and sufficient description of the information the subject actually has and uses? In Smokc's study the subjects who were classificd as unable to verbalize the concept could "almost invariably" (p. 20) draw two instances of the concept fromn memory correctly; but this does not imply that they had a complete and correct (though unverbalizable) criterion for making the sclections.

The question of completeness has been put to a more direct test in an interesting study by Wilson (1973, 1975). Hcrc, thc subjects wrote down thcir rules for positivc instances at cach trial, and were then asked to sort a test series of instances. Wilson then assessed the information transmitted in thesc verbal descriptions by having the same subjects a weck later make re-sorts from the descriptions. In addition, naive subjects who did not participate in the learning experiment were asked to sort the test serics on the basis of the individual descriptions provided by the original subjects. The results showcd that the sorts made by subjects after a wcck's dclay agrced less closely with thcir original sorts during the concept lcarning cxpcriment than thcy did with the sorts made by the naive subjects. This is cvidence that the subjects had more information at the time of the original experiment than they gave in their verbal reports. However, the correspondence between all the sorts was high, especially considering that the verbal rule was not always applicable to all instances in the test serics.

In a sccond experinent, Wilson (1974, 1975) found that the degrce of incompleteness of descriptions varicd with the stage of concept learning at which the verbalization of the rule was obtaincd. For rules verbalized cither at the very beginning of the experinent or at critcrion, almost complete agrement was obtaind betwcen sorts; but more discrepancies were found for rules verbalized at intermediate stages of acquisition. Wilson attributed thesc results to subjects' difficulties in verbalizing the complex hypothescs they cntertaincd during intermediate lcarning stages, and this interpretation was further supported by the greater length of the verbal descriptions for these stages.

Onc alternative explanation, suggested previously, would be that the instructions used by Wilson ("to verbalize a rule"—the avcrage number of words per rule was about 8) were inadequate to tap the subjects total information about positive instances. Another possible interpretation is that instances in the test serics may have served as cues for retrieval and recognition of previously presented items that were not available to the subjects when they gencrated the verbal report. Some supporting cvidence is given by a study on discrimination learning by Frankcl, I.cvinc and Karpf (1970), whcre thc subjects could give a retrospcctive description of "on what basis thcy had responded" (p. 346) that described more than 90% of thcir responses.

## Lack of Access to Relevant Knowledge

In the preceding scction we showcd that subjects may perform correctly on a concept-attainment task, yct usc the process of recognizing excmplars without generating the abstract concept. This is an cxample of a more general principle: subjects do not, in many or most situations (and even if thcy are able to access all the rclevant information), integrate all the information and abstract the higher-level relations the experimenter has built into the stimuli.

Several studies have shown that subjects somctincs can report very different amounts of knowledge rclevant to the task, without corresponding differenccs in performance (Broadbent, 1977; Hull, 1920). Other studics show that subjects can rcport accurate knowledge at thc time of probing, yct while cxccuting a cognitive process that should access that knowledgc, they report not heeding this information or report accessing somc inconsistent information instead (Allwood & Montgomery, 1981, 1982).

These findings arc inconsistent with our modcl only under a couple of (dubious) assumptions. Spccifically, onc must assumc that cxactly the samc information is retricved and hccded in thc probing situation as when performing the task. In cveryday expericnce, onc often docs not rccall information that, if recalled, would bc rclevant (e.g., while shopping, or in conversation with others). Only for highly skilled performers can we be reasonably certain that relevant knowledge will always be retricved when required. A second assumption is that the knowledge deemed relevant by the cxperimenter can be and is used by the subject to complete the task. Broadbent (1977) mentions an cxtreme case: Newton's Third I aw of Motion is logically relevant to riding a bike, yct it is unlikcly that anyone would use it to learn how to ride. In the following discussion we will be concerned primarily with showing that in the studies discussed carlier these assumptions are not satisfied, and hence that the findings are consistent with our model. A more refined model. of course, would predict the deficiencics in recall as a function of task conditions and subjects' skill levels.

One line of rescarch demonstrated that subjects may differ considcrably in what relevant information thcy report, yct not differ discernably in pcrformancc. In his classical study, Hull (1920) presented Chinese characters that had one of several simpler signs (radicals) cmbedded in them. In one experiment, Iull investigated whether learning was facilitated by prescnting the radicals in association with their correct responses, thus climinating the process of abstracting thesc from the compound characters. At the cnd of thc learning trials there was no reliable difference in correct assignment of concepts betwcen the condition in which thc radical was presented and the control condition. However, when half the subjects were asked to draw a picture of crucial features corresponding to the concept names, the concepts presented with the radicals were described twice as well as the others, as assessed by independent judges. Hull concluded:

The power to definc is thus in some cases at least a very inadequate index of the functional value of a concept (Hull, 1920, p. 84).

This study is frequently cited, wc think incorrectly, as evidence that the observable behavior is not generatcd by accessible cognitive structures. We will argue that in this expcriment the information that allowed sucts to dra a picur of tecuil atur of tconts was ony paallycsctually ti tconc behavior. Only 7% to 14% of the variability of the subjects performance i Hu t definc the conccpt (this quantity is not statistically significant). It is abundantly clear that the knowledge from the presentation of radicals was insufficient for learning the complex concepts. Identifying the cmbedded radicals was not an obvious matter. Hull (1920) showcd that marking the radical in red in the character led to considerable improvement in performance.

Broadbent and his collaborators (Broadbent, 1977) have shown that subjects lcarn to control computer simulations of traffic and of an economic system without gencrating any verbalizable knowledge about the interrelationships of thc independent variables. In onc task (Broadbent, 1977), subjets were ivn control over the transport in  city by allowing them to alter the time interval betwcen buses and to set parking fees for cars. A simple computer model of this traffic system (unknown to the subjects) determined from the two parameters the avcrage number of pcople on the buses and the number of cmpty parking spaces. The subjects were asked to achicve somc specificd level of usage of buscs and parking spaces by changing the variables under their control. Before and after thcir control experience, subjects were asked about simple relations (c.g., "Will a risc in parking fec increase or decrcasc number of cmpty parking spacs?"), and cross-rclations (c.g, "What effect will a rise in parking fees have on the load on the buses?").

With experience of the complete system, subjects improved in their ability to answer qucstions about simple relations, but ability to answer questions about cross-relations dccrcascd with cxpericnce. No rclation was found bctwcen subjects' ability to control the system and thcir ability to answcr questions. Thc lack of such a rclation may be duc, at least in part, to the high levcl of accuracy of the subjects' questionnaire responses (half of the subjects answcrcd all of the qucstionnaire questions correctly at the end of the expcriment). Although wc do not havc concurrent reports on the subjects' cognitive proccsses during the task, it is clcar that they did not find the corrcct combination of paramcters by mcntal planning, for thcy would have nccded to try out, on average, six combinations of paramcters before the corrcct one was found. It is likely that the subjects rclicd dircctly on the cffects of the last parameter change in sclecting new parameter values, and hence cxplicit memory for the crosstalk rclations may not have been necessary for their solution generated through successive approximation.

In both Hull's and Broadbent's studics, thcre is no inconsistency with our modcl, in that thcre is no cvidence that thc unreported knowledge was crucial for the cognitive processes involved in the respective tasks, hence no reason for generating it or storing it in I.TM.

The unrcliability of retricval of relevant knowledge is nicely demonstrated by pcople's ability to dctect their own crrors. Allwood and Montgomery (1981, 1982) have studicd subjects dctecting crrors in their solutions to problems in statistics. They found that subjects were able to find crrors spontancously without a directed effort to verify their solutions (Allwood, 1982). More crrors could be detected if thc cxperimenter focused the subjects' attention to some problem-solving steps. The principal factor in retrieving the correct knowledge was a broader and more intensive retrieval effort than occurs during normal problem solving. It is important to note that these subjects were not very skilled in statistics, and did not have their knowledge of statistics wcll integrated.

Being able to access knowledge in some form is not cquivalent to being able to apply that knowledge correctly. This is particularly truc for knowledge that is presented to the subject and acquired "passively." When the subject can generate the knowledge himsclf, the generation process assures that the knowledge is integrated with other knowledge. In instruction in motor skills, it is particularly obvious that bcing told, for example, how to hit a tennis ball is not sufficicnt for the hitting. Subjects may even disregard the verbal instruction and attempt to discover a way to achieve a realization of the procedure by themsclves. At the same timc, they imay be able to rctricve the original verbal instruction.

Even for cognitive skills, likc mathematics, subjects are often as effectivc in learning without instruction (i.c., by discovering thc procedurc) as in lcarning from cxplicit verbal instruction to use a spccificd proccdure (cf. Kersh & Wittrock, 1962). A similar result was obtaincd by Duncker (1945) in his analysis of subjcts solving the problemn: Why are all six-place numbers of thc form 276 276, 591 591,112 112 divisible by 13? Most of the hints given to subjects were ineffective, although they cleariy specify the general form of the solution (c.g., "If a common divisor of numbers is divisible by 13, they are all divisible by 13."). Duncker's account of the successful and unsuccessful hints was that subjects were unable to brcak up the pattern abc abc and rcalize that it is mathcmatically cquivalent to 1001 \* abc.

Haycs-Roth, Klahr, and Mostow (1981) give a detailed analysis showing how advice given to a person playing a card game like Hearts ub ai al i knowledgc about the task bcfore it can help the player guide his actions.

## RETROSPECTIVE REPORTS OF EARLIER COGNITIVE PROCESSES

In this scction we will claborate on the model described carlier for retrospective reporting, and introduce more processes, mainly inferential, that subjects rely on to gencrate information about carlicr cognitive processes and previously heeded information. First, wc will revicw briefly our model of retrospective reporting and show that memory for the just preccding cognitivc process appears to be markedly better than for carlier processes." Then we will turn to a discussion of the most frequent kind of retrospective questioning: questioning after the completion of an cxperiment session with many trials. Finally, we will discuss some other processes that can be uscd to make reliable predictions about carlier cognitive proceses without retrieving cpisodic memory for those processes.

## Model of Rctrospective Reporting

Our general model assumes that the cognitive processes lcave in I TM a subset of the originally hecdcd information in the form of a retrievable trace of connccted cpisodic memory. Retrospective reporting involves retricval of these episodic memorics and vcrbalization of thcir content.

If the subjects are asked to report retrospectively on their last previous cognitive process, it appcars that considerable cpisodic memory can be retricved from information and cucs in STM. Further, because of the redundancy of recent episodic mcmorics, incorrect information about them is not likcly to be produced. Mueller (1911) pointed out, however, that subjects somctimes confuse other rctricvable information with information actually heeded during thc processes bcing recalled. For cxamplc, a subject might report an unheeded feature of a (hccded) object (recalled from previous expcricnce with the object).

## Reports of General Cognitive Processes During Fxperiments

Requesting recall of general processes appcars to be the most common procedurc for collecting retrospective reports, and appears also to be the major source of cvidence citcd by pcople who doubt thc uscfulncss of val rortas n vidInh yrt s the subject is asked after the experiment to tell the experimenter, for example, "How did you do thesc problems?" or "Can you tell me what you wcre thinking about during the cxpcriment?" The rationale for asking such gcncral questions is that any investigator would like to have their answers—namely, a compact veridical description of thc subjects' cognitive processcs while performing the task.

Our modcl predicts that such questions can be answered by direct retrieval of the information only if the subjcct has, during his pcrformance of thc task, alrcady generated this kind of general description of his own cognitive processes. There is no rcason to suppose that subjects generally do gencrate such information. To summarize veridically their cognitivc processes, subjects would have to rctricve the cntire cpisodic trace of thcir processing to provide an appropriate data basc. Ict us consider somc of the difficulties with an "ideal" reporting model of this kind.

Finkenbinder (1914) let subjects solve a scrics of over 30 problems, obtaining introspcctive reports during and after the solution. The subjects wcrc later asked to recall the problems and thcir solutions, and the information recalled was compared with the carlicr reports. Finkenbinder did not quantify the rcsults, but reports that while subjects, especially when given cues, did recall a good dcal of information, they hardly ever recalled correctly short-cuts or crroncous solution attempts.

In a couplc of studies by Smirnov (1973) subjects wcre unexpectcdly asked to give retrospective rcports of what thcy remembered expericncing and thinking during different cpisodes, for cxamplc, as they went from their house to the office. The question was put unexpectedly one or two hours after the subjcct reachcd thc office. Below is a transcript of the first part of a typical report:

First of all I remember the moment I left the subway. What preciscly? I thought that I must quickly takc up the necessary position to gct out of thc subway and walk fast, since I was latc. I remember I was in the last car. Therefore, I could not gct out quickly and had to go into the crowd. Previously the pcople leaving the car took up the whole width of the platform. Now, in order to secure a passage for those entering the cars, officials are stationcd who turn the public from the edge of the platform. It caught my eyc that at cach post stood a man for this purpose: otherwise the public would walk at the cdge of the platform. (There follows a description of sevcral men standing at the pillars, not letting the passengers to the cdgc of the platform.) I think I did not look at the clock. The road further is a blank. I remember nothing about i.Ther is only a hazy recollection from previous tips. I walked to the gates of the university without noticing anything. Don't remember what I thought of. When I entered the gate I noticed somcone was hurrying. Who preciscly, a man or a woman, I don't remcmber. More I don't remember Now about the first half of the way. I do not remember lcaving the house.

Summarizing the reports of all subjects, Smirnov (1973) notes that virtually all the recalled information referred to experiences related to walking to the officc. However, subjccts were subjectivcly ccrtain that they must have thought about other things, yct only thought related to walking to the office could be retrieved. Such a sclective retricval can bc casily understood from thc assumption that subjects could only access retricval cucs rclated to the walking and the physical cnvironment traversed. Within this framework, we can also account for highway hypnosis (Natsoulas, 1970) whcre pcople suddenly realize that thcy remember nothing of the last twenty or thirty milcs of thc highway driven. If we assume that subjccts are primarily thinking or fantasizing using internal cues during this timc, and are able to perform the driving task ncarly automatically, no memory tracc of the cnvironment would be cxpected. In such cases, subjects cvcn lose the rctricval cucs to what they were thinking about, thus leaving a total blank.

It is fallacious to assumc complete retrievability of episodic information. All available data on subjects' memory for previously presented information and thcir cognitive processcs in cxperiments with long series of similar trials shows subjcts' memory to bc poor. If it wcrc not so, verbal learning cxperimcnts would take only a single trial.

Experiments using distinct types of stimulus material for different trials appear to yicld much better recall (Frankel, I cvine & Karpf, 1970; Burack, 1950), a result casily cxplainable in tcrms of distinctivcness of retricval cucs. If subjects uscd cxactly the same processcs on all trials, the failure to recall somc of them would not bc fatal, but wc know that subjects frequently change thcir strategies in the coursc of an cxpcriment, and may also solve differcnt problems with different mcthods. A further complication is that subjects rcly more and more on recognition to solve problems as an cxpcriment procceds, hencc may find it hardcr and harder to recall thcir most recent cognitive processes.

How does the subject go from the retrieved episodic segments to gencralizations about his proccsscs? Infcrenccs will generally bc required, hence the veridicality of reports produced in this way has little

## 152 Protocol Analysis

relevance for the veridicality of reports of directly heeded information. Subjects may commit other types of crrors. They may coinbine cpisodic memories from several different processes into a single structurc. They may also makc guesses as to the likcly processes when they cannot rcimcmber them (cf. Mueller, 1911).

Several situations are documented where subjects do not recall all relevant information but short-circuit the retrieval. When subjects report on the frequency or representativeness of a solution procedure, they may very well rcly on inferential or hcuristic rules. Tversky and Kahneman (1973) have shown in several cxperiments that frequency judgments arc closely related to ability to recall. In onc cxpcriment subjccts werc presented with a list of names of well-known pcoplc of both scxes. When the persons of one sex were more famous than those of the other, subjects overestimated the proportion of that scx in the list. Subjects also tend to judge consonants, like r and k as bcing more frequcnt in first than in third position, because words are casicr to recall when their initial letters are given. Yct, in fact, consonants like r and k are more frequent in third position. On much the same grounds, subjcts may infer that the particular solution procedurcs they rccall are representative of all such proccdures.

In a review articlc, Smedslund (1969) claims that verbal reports did not provide useful information on rapid mcntal processcs in an arithmetic task he had studicd (Smedslund, 1968). He describcd two reports that could bc proven inconsistent-o)nc with the subjcct's performance and spccd in solving the test items, the other with the typcs of items actually given. In the original paper, however, Smcdslund does not mention inaccuracy in the verbal reports, and actually quotes those reports to support some of his general results. In Smedslund's study the conditions for verbal reporting were far from optimal, for the subjccts wcrc asked after a rclatively largc number of trials to tcll how thcy did the tasks. Even under these unpromising circumstances, the verbalized information, with a fcw exceptions, sccmcd to satisfy the author as basically consistent with his observations of subjects' performance.

In a study on thc implicit learning of grammar, Rcber and Lewis (1977) explicitly claimcd inadequacy of retrospective reports:

These reports were replete with statements that conformed with ncither the rules of the grammar nor the subjects' own bchavior. (Rcber & I.cwis, 1977, p. 353)

First, a brif description of their experiment.On the irst day, subcts were asked to memorizc 15 patterns of symbols, likc Vssxxvv. The subjects were then told that these patterns were generated according to rules and askcd to construct new patterns from scrambled symbols conforming to these rules.On this anagram-like task the subjects were given no feedback.

For thrce more days, the subjects were first shown the 15 cxemplars and then askcd to do the anagram-like task. Aftcr completing this task on the fourth day, the subjects wcre shown a sct of patterns and asked to decide whcther they conformed to the rules that generated the 15 sample patterns. During this test, subjects wcre asked to verbalize reasons for cach judgment as it was madc. Finally, after the test the subjects were asked to writc and describe in as much detail as possible what thcy knew about the rules and to describe spccial stratcgics thcy uscd in thc experiment.

Thus, two types of verbal reports were collected in this study, ncither of which corresponds closely with our notion of rctrospective reports. It is interesting that Rebcr and I.cwis almost cxclusivcly criticize the general reports collected after the experiment rather than the verbalizcd rcasons given aftcr cach judgment. let us first discuss their evidencc against the post-session rulc descriptions, which is given in the form of a dctailcd analysis of an "avcrage" subject.

The subject's performancc in the anagram-likc task improved steadily from day to day, providing clear cvidencc that his cognitive processes changed and that he did not apply the same rules at all times. Reber and I.cwis compared reportcd regularitics with the properties of proposed solutions to the anagram task, with the notion that prominent features recalled should also show up as parts of the anagram solutions. Although thcy found that features that were rcported also appcared reliably as parts of anagram solutions, as cxpcctcd, thcy also found that some admissible combinations wcrc not generatcd. But in analysing thc data, the cxperimenters pooled all anagram tasks fron day 1 to day 4. Hence, we would expect regularitics discovered late in thc expcriment to have a low frequcncy of occurrence in the anagrams; this would constitute no evidence of cpiphenomenality. One reported pattern involved a combination of symbols that could not occur in the anagram tasks (although it occurred in one of the 15 learning cxamples). Given the large number of scqucnccs cxposcd in thc coursc of the experiment, and the conscqucnt possibilitics for crrors of recall, thc subjcct's reports appear to cxhibit a rathcr high lcvcl of validity. Furthcr, in 69% of the cascs where a subject rcjccted a pattern correctly and verbalized a rcason for the rcjection, this reason was "correct" in terms of the experimenter's grammar. Ihere is no cvidence that in the remaining 31% of the cases the reasons given were inconsistent with the subjects rules.

Apparent inconsistency of responses may simply be due to the fact that on successive occasions of retrieval, slightly different cucs and context lead to different responses. Reber and Allen (1978) give the following cxample from a protocol:

An M-sandwich [the subject's term for an item beginning and cnding with M]. I know the last time I saw that I said no to it. But this looks right. Perhaps you can have an

Without additional evidence, there is no reason to suppose that the subject is not reporting the actual information ("This looks right") that leads him to his response. (Rcber & Allen, 1978)

Reber and Allen (1978) point rather clearly to problems of retrieval as the source of inconsistency and "incorrectness" of vcrbalized reasons. They note that most of the "incorrect" rules concerncd features that a pattern cannot have. These rules were often discovered spontaneously, as shown by the following protocol scgment:

RM? I don't remenber secing RM before. Perhaps that's not right. No., you can't have an RM (Rcber & Allen, 1978, p. 215)

Most recently Dulany, Carlson, and Dewcy (1983) replicated the lcarning study of Rcber and Allen (1978). Along with thcir judgments of grammaticality, the subjects wcre instructed to underline the part of the letter string that made it wcll-formcd or cross out the part that made it ungrammatical. Dulany ct al. found that thc rules as (imperfcctly) undcrstood by subjccts accountcd for the individual subjects' pcrformances completely.Hence, imperfect ruls or patterns that the subject has learncd account for his responses.

## Other Processes Without Awareness

In this section we will show that all instances that have been cited of lack of awareness with or without contradictory inferential reports can be traccd to recognition processes. Wc will discuss first some studics involving stimuli that rcadily clicit responscs. IThen, in the ncxt scction, we will turn to the rather cxtensive literature on insight in problem solving and thinkingWe will argue that insight involves directaccess to pre-existig v stucur i LT.Fnall w ill css subjctu inferential strategics to extract information about previous cxperience as a substitute for cpisodic memory of cognitive processes.

In situations where subjects' spontancously generated behavior is studied, wc cannot rule out recognition processes bascd on S-R links that have bcen cstablished previously. If we kncw the subject's cntire prior history, we should, in principle, be able to identify the association that is later retrieved. I.ct us discuss a study by Rommetvcit and Kvale (1965b) already considered in Chapter 2.

Patterns were displayed to signal to subjects playing a wheel of fortunc whether they would win or not. When the cxpcrinenters asked a boy to describe the differences between positive and negative patterns, he said he did not know, although he had been able to anticipate the rewards correctly on previous trials. When pressed by the cxperinenter, he finally attempted a verbal description and gave onc that was inconsistent with the actual signals. In this and many other similar accounts, it seems appropriate to attribute the crror to absence of the information from memory rather than to inconsistency betwcen memory contents and verbal reports of them. When information is not in memory, it cannot be reported verbally.

Howcver, it still remains to show that recognition processes are sufficient to account for thc performancc. First, observe that subjects necdcd only to makc a binary prcdiction, "win" or "losc," so that any accesscd information implying likclihood of winning would suffice. In fact, subjccts achicved thcir bctter-than-chancc prcdictions by sclecting figures characterized by roundness as "winners" (Rommetveit, 1965). But subjects might alrcady have in I.'TM an association between "roundness" and "goodncss."

Morcover, cxperimental variations, likc telling the subjects that they will have to describe the figures aftcrwards and showing the subjects initially how the figures differed, may very well have changed thcir perceptions of the figures, and hcnce changcd the cues that otherwise led to recognition of some figures as 'good'. Such an cxplanation is consistent with our carlicr characterization of recognition processes.

We can usc the same type of explanation for the finding by Rees and lsracl (1935) that subjects, solving a scrics of 15 anagrams having identical structure, would sclect, for items with multiple solutions, the solution fitting the common structurc, without reporting awareness of that structure. Thc anagrams all uscd the simple permutation, 54123.

Six out of ten subjects did not report awareness of the permutation common to all the solutions, suggesting that they did not have this information at the time they made their report. How can we cxplain how the four of thesc six subjects who made no crrors sclected the saine permutation in 15 consecutive trials without rctaining knowledge of the structure in STM?

We would attribute the result to the spccial propertics of the permutation, 54123, used in this cxperiment. Subjects did not have to learn this particular pattern, but could decipher the anagram by using their normal visual scanning strategies, long since lcarned and automated: fixate at the left or ncar the middle of the stimulus, and scan from left to right. This strategy would revcal the anagran after only a couple of trials—probably in less than a second.

If this explanation is correct, then if more complex permutations were uscd, subjects would bc aware of the structures of the solutions. This is cxactly what Rees and Isracl found in thcir study.

Kaplan and Schoenfeld (1966) rccordcd subjects' cyc movements while they solved rclatively simple pernutation anagrams. The letters in the anagram wcre spaced so that the subject was able to fixate only a single letter at a time. From the cye fixations they wcre able to determine when subjects anticipated a certain permutation. They found perfect agreement bctwcen anticipation and reported awareness of the permutation structure: two subjccts both displaycd anticipation and reported noticing the structure, whcrcas one did ncither.

In various problem-solving tasks, amplc cvidencc has bcen recorded for pre-cstablished scan or motor behavior. For cxample, Lindley (1897) studicd subjcts tracing a figure without repcating any linc or starting over. Subjects tcnded to start in thc lcft-hand corner of the figurc, which made a solution inpossible. It took subjects considcrablc time to bccome awarc of the difficulty and change their bchavior—a conscquence of thcir cstablished habits of writing and reading.

We propose a rather similar account for a scrics of experiments by Wason and Evans (1975) that thcy take as evidencc for an unconscious response gencration process coupled with a scparate process for giving justification or reasons for their responses. It is entircly possible for subjects, cspecially if forcefully prompted, to generate reasons for responses cvokcd by recognition. Even when thc response process generates intermediate reportable states, subjects may provide reasons that are more acceptable socially than thosc actually responsible for gencrating the response. However, in cxpcriments where the subjects' a feel oblig to scrutinize theevidence that suggests a dissociation between hceded information and reported reasons.

The principal task cnvironment in which experimental psychologists have proposed a dissociation betwcen gencrating responses and giving rcasons for them is logical rcasoning, in particular, in tasks testing the understanding of the implication relation (Evans & Wason, 176; Wason & Evans, 1975). In these experiments, all subjects with formal training in logic are screencd out. Subjects are told that the problem has to do with four cards, cach card having a letter on one side and a number on the other.The subjects are then given the following rul:"If there is a I) on one side, then there is a 3 on the other side." They are then shown one side of cach of four cards, showing a D), B, 3, and 7, respectively. They are then told, "I want you to list for me those cards, and only thosc cards, that you must sce in order to find out whethcr the rule is true or falsc." In general, subjects want to sce the cards with I) and 3, while the "correct" cards are those showing I) and 7.

In thc model proposed by Johnson-I.aird and Wason (1970), it was proposed that subjects could bc divided according to degrees of insight. Subjects without insight would sclcct thc cards showing symbols mentioned in thc rulc. With partial insight, subjccts would also select instances where the card could falsify the rulc.With full insight, subjects would rule out the card (the one showing 3) that was irrclcvant to falsifying the rulc. Goodwin and Wason (1972) found support for this model by analyzing subjects' verbalized rcasons for sclecting cards. Requiring subjects to give written rcasons had only a small cffect toward inducing correct solutions. In fact, some subjects even changed from more nearly correct solutions to less correct solutions. This was interpreted by Goodwin and Wason as cvidencc that the subjccts' understanding fluctuated.

Wason and Evans (1975), studying subjects' responses to different rules (c.g., "If P, then not Q"), found that most subjects chose to look at cards with symbols named in the rule regardless of thc form of the rule. They advanced the hypothesis that the choice is bascd on a simple matching process identifying the names of the symbols in the rule and another process of rationalization to provide the justifications. Evans and Wason sought to test this two-stage hypothcsis by telling subjects (corrcctly or falscly) which cards should bc chosen, and asking them to give reasons for the choices. Subjects were most confident in their reasons when the cards sclcctcd by the cxperimenter were those that they themselvcs would havc choscn, cxccpt that thcy wcre about cqually willing and able to gencrate reasons with confidence that the 3 should be chosen as that it should not be. Subjects giving a rcason for not sclecting the 3 cited irrelevance. Subjects forced to give rcasons for sclecting it gave as the reason a possibility for falsification.

This result has been claimed by Evans (1975) to show that verbal reports do not reflect cognitive processes. Wc interprct it differently. Subjects presented with the original choicc task usually fail, as we have scen, to carry out a thorough cnough analysis to arrive at the correct answer. On the other hand, when presented with an answer they bclieve to be correct, they carry out additional analysis to discover why it is correct, and from that analysis, they are able to provide a justification for the answer. It is presenting an "answer" to the subjcct that changes the cognitive processes. Evans (1982) has recently presented his own interpretation of these cxperiments which is consistcnt with ours. Both he and we interpret the original decision process as a recognition process, so that subjects cannot report the intermediate steps—their "reasons."

A study by Brunswik and Hcrma (1951) appcars to bc a cornerstone in Brunswik's (1§56) argument for lack of awareness in learning of probabilistic rclations betwcen cues and outcomes. In order to understand fully thc results of this study, we havc to discuss how information about previous stimulus cvents may influence subequent perceptions. Brunswik and Herima presented thcir subjects with wcights of two different colors, onc to be hcld in cach hand. For somc pairs of wcights, the subjects wcre askcd "which of the two objects appeared heavicr at thc first moment of lifting??" (Brunswik & Herma, 1951, p. 284) Immediate impressions wcre to bc cxpressed as a snap judgment. The subjects were allowed to judge the wcights as cqual, yet discouraged from doing so. Each subject always hcld wcights of a given color in the same hand, although thc color varicd among subjects. In onc cxperimcnt. the wcights of one color wcre hcavicr most (80%) of the time. Interspersed werc trials where the wcights were cqual. During these test trials subjects tended to judge wcights in the hand that normally reccived the lighter wcights as heavier. Brunswik and Herma attributed these systematic responses to learning of a cuc-responsc relation.

After the completion of the 72 test and learning trials, subjects were ask, "In your opinion, which objects were on the average heavir, the grcen or the rcd?" (Brunswik & Herma, 1951, p. 288), and also a similar question regarding the wcights in the right and left hands. (Remember that color and position were perfectly correlateMost of the subjects (t 75%)answerd boh qusins incorrectly, but,  cours, they ror corecty or the elatinin the test trials.u do av ak  t  e  h et - tively different.)

Brunswik and Herna (1951) notc that "some" subjects explicitly asked if the question referred only to test trials, or to all trials, and were told the latter. As no separate analysis was made of these subjects, we simply don't know whcther most subjects misinterpreted the question or in fact did not remember the relative wcights in the trials without a test. Moreover, there is no evidence from the study's data that the subjects lcarned anything, if by learning we mean a stable change in behavior.

Uznadze (1966) shows that contrast effects of stimuli, like those cxhibited in thc Brunswik-Ierma cxperiment, can bc obtaincd for a varicty of stimuli—pressure, loudness of auditory stimuli, illunination, and wcight. Thcorics explaining these phenomena have been proposed, most notably Helson's (1964) adaptation-levcl thcory. These thcorics can account for the subjects' bchavior in terms of short-termn shifts in the perceptual and sensory systcms, without lcarning. In particular, it can account for thcir dcmonstration of "reversal learning" (Brunswik & Herma, 1951). In this cxperiment, thc authors showcd that after providing lighter wcights to one hand (and cliciting the judgment of "heavicr" when cqual wcights were provided on test trials), thc judgments could be reversed by giving hcavicr wcights to that hand.

An adaptation-level thcory can also account for thc "onc-trial lcarning" cffects that puzzled Gibson (1969) in her discussion of this study. Aficr a single prescntation of unequal wcights, subjects show a 90% tendency to give a biased judgment on the subscquent test trial of cqual wcights. Lacking cvidence against the adaptation-lcvel theory, we argue that it provides a more parsimonious explanation of the bchavior than docs cuc-response learning. In Gibson's revicw of studies on learning stimulus-responsc rclations in probabilistic cnvironments, shc finds very strong support, cxccpt for Brunswik and Hcrma's study, for the necessity of awareness in order for learning to occur.

## INSIGHT AND ACCESS TO SOLUTION IDEAS

Apart from cases where the subjects rcly on automatic processes, there are other forms of incompleteness in reports wherc information that was once in focal attention (in STM) is not vcrbalized. Information in STM is casily obliteratcd. In a fcw scconds, thc contents of STM can be dcstroycd or made inaccessible by requiring subjects to perform certain types of tasks—for example, repcatedly subtracting 7 from a given number (Brown-Peterson paradigm). With any shift in the locus of attention, of which this paradigm provides an cxample, the previous contents of STM bccome unavailable. If an intermediate result in a sequcnce of processes causes a direct cxccution of other processes that make full demands on STM, the intermediate result may reside for only a brief moment in SlM, and may be lost before being reported. Under thinking-aloud conditions, it has bcen observed (IDuncker, 1945) that infornation leading to the direct recognition of the appropriate action often tends not to be verbalized. Similar obscrvations have been made (de Groot, 1965) about the reports of chess grandmasters considering possible moves in chess positions.

A frequently cited study by Maicr (1931) on subjects' retrospective reports about a hint given during solution of the Pendulum Problem gives some cvidence for thc same phenomenon. Subjects who described the solution as cmcrging in a single step did not report any memory of the hint. A result of Maier's study that is less often mentioned was that all subjects who mentioncd more than onc stcp in the solution of the problem reported that the hint had been administered.

The two related mechanisms mentioned thus far-the absence of intermediate stages of acts of recognition from STM, and failure to report transient contents of STM—are fully adequate to account for the phenomena of sudden "insight" that are thc subject of so many anecdotes in thc literature of creativity (Nisbett & Wilson, 1977, pp. 240-241). The studics cited in support of sudden insight are based on retrospective accounts of "real" creative acts, often reported many years after the event. Fortunatcly, a number of studics have addressed this topic in a morc controllcd experimental cnvironment. Durkin (1937) sought to crcate favorable circunstances for "insight" with subjccts thinking aloud while solving block puzzles. While subjects occasionally reported insights, the background steps lcading to the cmcrgencc of the insightful ideas could always be deternincd from the concurrent thinking-aloud protocols. Of this kind of insight, I)urkin (1937, p. 81) says:

When it occurs, it comes with an onrush that makes it scem very sudden —an "out of the blue" experience. But it can always be found to have devclopcd gradually. The suddenness must be regarded as duc to the concealment of the background. It does not bring in a ncw kind of process. (Durkin, 1937, p. 81)

For gcometry proofs (Henry, 1934), and for a varicty of "insight" problems (Bulbrook, 1932), the thinking-aloud protocols showed that the progress to solution was cither gradual or was detcrmined by trial and crror. In ncither casc was it necessary to postulate additional kinds of processes.

Insight or illumination of a creative idea was by many carly investigators described as thc result of a pcriod of unconscious work, or incubation, following on prcliminary work in bccoming familiar with the problem (preparation). During this pcriod of allcged incubation, these accounts go, the scicntist or inventor has laid the problem aside in favor of other activities. However, it has been suggested (Woodworth, 1938) that during the pcriod of incubation, the scicntist will occasionally lapse into thinking about the problem, cven while working on a different task. Woodworth (1938) cites a study by Platt and Baker that suggests that subjects are not aware of the durations of thesc unplanncd cpisodes of concentrated thought on thc problem. Clearly, such unanticipated thought processes, if thcy occur, will bc very difficult to retrieve in retrospcct. Gcncrally such cpisodes are terminated, often abruptly, by external dcimands-as, for cxamplc, in a driving situation. It is also possiblc, as has been suggested by Simon (1966) that no new idcas arc gencrated during incubation, but instcad, Finstellung cffects that kcep attention direcied toward unproductive lincs of scarch are dissipated by forgetting during this period.

Therc is considerable cvidence, though most of it is anccdotal, that many "crcativc" idcas arc, in fact, reproductions of idcas from memory derived from the idcas of other people. Machotka (1964) mentions an art student who "generated" a poster dcsign that she later recognized as having previously scen in the window of a storc. According to Lowes' (1927) analysis of Samucl Coleridgc's phrases and imagery, it can be scen that many of them came from reading, without Coleridge being aware of the sourcecs of most of thcn. If other people's idcas can be mistaken for novel creative ones, so can ideas that actually were gencrated by the subject himself at some carlier time.

F'rom rescarch on daydrcaming (Singer, 1975) and undirected thought (Klinger, 1971), thcre arc suggcstions that such thought cpisodes are difficult to recall fully unless thc retrospective reports are obtained shortly thcrcafter, or unless the subjects labcl or rchcarse the thought content for subscquent recall. Studics using concurrent verbalization (Bertini, I.cwis, & Witkin, 1964; Kazdin, 1976; Klingcr, 1971) provide detailed and informative accounts of undirected thought processes. In sum, thinking that is not closcly rclated to the cxternal environment can somctimes be rctricved with situational clucs, but seldom otherwise, except when it is verbalizcd concurrently with the thought process.

## Insight as Recognition

I.ct us now review the cvidence wc have surveyed as to whether recognition processes, as we defincd them carlier, are sufficient to account for the phenomena reported abovc. The most important hypothesis in our model is that recognition processes simply access an existing structure in I.TM, without modifying, altering. or generating old or ncw cognitive structures. In Maier's (1931) problem-solving cxpcriment, there is no disagrcement that subjects had thc pendulum solution available in I.TM. The cvidence in IDurkin's (1937) study is cven clearcr. An cxperimental group of subjects first solvcd several small block puzzles with different configurations, while a control group did not. The large block puzzle now given to all subjects could bc broken up into subproblcms, which corresponded to the smaller puzz.les given carlier to the cxpcrimental group. Thus, only subjects in the cxperimental group had in I.T'M accessible cognitive structures corresponding to the subproblems; and predictably, only these subjects had insight cxpericnces, with solution ideas corresponding closcly to the carlier generated solution to the subproblems.

In many complex tasks it is, thcrefore, likcly that a subject will generate information that cannot all bc hcld in STM, but is accessible from LTM.
