The latencics of such solutions were predictably different fro solutions to the same problems using the "norinal" procedure.

## Problem Behavior Graphs

In some cases processing models have becn specificd even in cases where not all subjects usc the same sequences of processes, and where the scqucncc is not uniquely determincd by task analysis. Often the scquence actually uscd is dctermined by detailcd analysis of a simall number of TA protocols.

Perhaps the most influentual coding technique of this kind is the Problem Behavior Graph (PBG) (Newell, 1966, 1968; Newcell & Simon, 1972). The analysis assumes that the subject solves the problem by scarching through one or more problem spaces (i.e., scts of alternative states of knowledge). With cach transition betwcen states is associated an operator (a process that gencrates or transforms knowledge). By careful task analysis, a small number of alternative problem spaces can be proposed. Analysis of a subject's protocol, statcment by statement, reveals in which of these problem states (if any) he is opcrating. "The kcy constraint is that all changes in the knowledge state (as dcfined for the problem space) that are dctcctable in the protocol must come about through onc of the operators of the problem space." (Ncwcll, 1966, p. 34)

The anbiguity and incompleteness of a protocol may make the determination of thc subjcct's problem space difficult (Ncwcll & Simon, 1972). Neverthcless, protocols have been cncoded in terms of PBGs for a widc range of tasks, including propositional logic (Newcll & Simon, 1972), cryptarithmctic (Newcil & Simon, 1972), chess (Newcl1 & Simon, 1972: Wagncr & Scurrah, 1971). Missionarics and Cannibals (Ncwell, 1966), mental imagery (Baylor, 1973), N-tcrm scrics problems (Ohlsson, 1980), analysis of financial statements (Bouwman, 1980), and others.

Complementing most of these analyscs, computer models were constructed that regenerate more or less well the rclevant information in the protocol. Thesc analyses provide strong cvidcnce that verbalized protocols correspond closcly with thc information that has to be considercd for gencrating problem solutions. They also show that the verbalized information can bc mapped onto a process model that is sufficient for gencrating the solution from the presentcd information. There are many cxamples of such comparisons bctwcen computer models and protocols. In addition to the studics wc havc mcntioncd elsewhere, we would like to point to an analysis of human move sclection in Chincse checkers by Anzai (1977), an analysis of how job candidates arc sclccted for different jobs, by Smith and Grcenlaw (1967), and an analysis of how maladjustment is assessed from MMPI data, by Kleinmuntz (1963).

At the level of detail of the PBG, we can expect to encounter significant interpersonal differences in processing. Ihis makes it difficult to use a single computer modcl to prcdict or account for the detail of numbers of different protocols. This doesn't mcan, of course, that these models are incorrect, but rather, that at the level of detail they capture, we cannot always gcneralize across individuals. Ihis is an important finding about bchavior and about the forms we can cxpcct psychological laws to take, and it lcads us to a closcr considcration of the problem of gencralization.

## GENERALIZATIONS ABOUT COGNITIVE PROCESSES

Stimuli do not determine responses uniqucly, indcpendently of context and of the contents of a subject's I.TM. If problems admit of alternative solution paths, we will not expect all subjects to follow the same path.

Buswell (1956) asked college and high-school students to compute the answer to problems like the following:

Problem A: If 15 hens lay 5 eggs per weck for 4 wecks, what is the total value of the eggs al 6 cents each?

Problem B: At 60 cents pcr hour find the total earnings of 12 boys if they worked 8 hours per day for 5 days.

When Buswell recorded the sequences of multiplications 101 high-school students used to gcnerate thc answers, hc found that 76% selected a particular sequence for problem A, but for problein B, only 21% sclected the most popular scquence, and four different scqucnces were selected with almost cqual frequency. Esscntially the same results were obtained with a larger sample of 233 collcge students. For a more complex word problem, Buswell constructed a decision trce corresponding to the differnt solution paths. Only a few subjects (about 10%) sclected the most frcquently chosen solution path. A fairly large number of paths was chosen by one or more subject, yet thesc paths constituted only a small fraction of the possible paths.

1 l Eight Puzzle).Subjects solutions wcrc described as ordered scquences of moves. The similarity of move sequences among subjects starting from the same puzzle configuration was no greater than would be predicted by chance. Moreover, the solutions of each individual subject to repetitions of the samc problem were no closer than would be predicted by chance. Hence, no single model could be cxpected to predict the exact sequences. However, when the solutions were analyzed at a more abstract level (in terms of the attainment of certain specified configurations of tiles), most subjects followed the same orderly and predictable scquence. The same special configurations (subgoals) were attained by most subjects for most of the different problems in the same order.

Karat (1982) analyzcd consecutive solutions to a 5-disk version of the Towcr of Hanoi problem. Subjects who did not find a minimum-path solution cxhibitcd no systematic similaritics bctwecn thcir consecutive solutions.

When we present a subject with a complex task description or a picturc, wc cannot generally predict in dctail what thc subject will recognize or attend to. Recognition and retricval processes are dctermined in part by information in I.I'M, bccause the information in STM is not sufficicntly specific to dctermine a uniquc product of recognition and retrieval. This is cspecially truc when the subject perccives a large number of cqually acceptable alternatives, like the alternative moves facing a novice chess player.

From this it follows that we will be more likcly to be able to make class predictions than uniquc predictions. We can oftcn predict that the subscquently hceded information or bchavior will bc drawn from a specificd class, where the classes constitute only a small fraction of logically or thcoretically possible bchaviors or information. Consider the problems posed by Buswell (1956). Each could be solved by taking up four multiplicands in any order, and pcrforming Uhc multiplications. Hence, there are 4! = 24 admissiblc scqucnces, all lcading to the same solution, and we might expect almost any of these-but no others—to occur.

When it is impossible to predict cach step in a sequcnce with certainty, it becomes far more difficult, of course, to predict the whole sequcncc. Hence, in fitting behavioral and verbal data to models, we will usually do well to makc conditional prcdictions, that is, prcdictions of cach action as a function of the information in STM just bcfore the action was takcn. Even such predictions, as wc havc scen, may succeed only in a probabilistic sense.

Often, we can discover, and then predict, how stimuli will be represerted internally by comparing the verbalizations produccd when the stimuli are presented in different formats or with differcnt content. For some classes of problems we may also be able to predict the order in which ccrtain kinds of information will be hecded. In these situations, we can view the verbalizations as the dcpendent variables. Combining thesc two kinds of prediction, wc can determine the ST'M structures that mediate betwcen stimulus and response.

## Model-Based Coding

When prediction is guided by a model, the model identifies relevant in formation that will be hceded. No attempt is made to dcal with all the verbalizations in the protocol. In a coding system proposcd by Ericsson (1975b), the coding categories wcre sclccted with a modcl of the verbalization process in mind, similar to thc one that has just bcen skctched out. The coded statements thus represented internal information structurcs that (according to the modcl) wcre hecdcd. The coding system distinguished four types of statements:

Intentions. Information representing goals and futurc states of the subject, easily recognizcd by vcrbs of the typc, "shall," "will," "must," "have to," and so on.

2. Cognitions. Information based on attention to sclccted aspccts of the currcnt situation, easily recognized by constructions indicating presence and immediacy.

3. Planning. Information representing intermediate constructions to explore sequences of possibilitics mentally, easily recognized by conditional constructions like, "if X then Y and if Z.

4. Evaluations. Explicit or implicit comparisons of altcrnatives, easily identified by key words like "no," "yes," "dammit," "fine," and so on.

In such coding schemes, ambiguity can bc handled by omitting ambiguous statementsr alternativcly Ericsson, 175b), bydenn decision procedures for making thc assessments in ambiguous situations. Carricd to thc limit, such proccdures could makc automatic or semiautomatic coding possible.

In his study of problem solving with the Fight Puzzle (Ericsson, 1975), subjectsverbalizcd intentions to attain particular configuratons of tiles were encoded as described previously. In general, any given configuration can be achieved via different sequences of moves. The strongest prediction is that the subject will choose his move sequence from this sct. The set of admissible (efficient) move sequences was then generatcd, and the actual move sequence matchcd against it. The analysis showed that subjects followed admissible scquences with few exccptions. Most of the exceptions occurred when a subject rejected an earlier verbalized intention before the goal was attaincd, and these rejections were accompanied by verbalizations of new intentions to achieve a different configuration, or by verbalized negative evaluations of the original intention.

In the same study, the verbalized cognitions and intentions were analyzcd to determine how goals wcrc characterized, by examining each scgment that referred to the rclation bctwcen two or more tiles. In Figure 4-2, showing the goal-configuration of the Fight Puzzle, we have markcd logically uscful rclations bctwcen adjacent tiles. From a task analysis it is clcar that rclations within a column are as important and useful (i.c., the sequential relations bctwcen the 1-4 and 4-7 and 1-4-7) as the corresponding relations within a row (i.c., sequential rclation 1-2 and 2-3 and 1-2-3). However, from Figure 4-2 it is clear that subjects only mention a small fraction of the logically conccivable goals and intentions. The rclations that were verbalized all belong to the lincar sequence 1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8, which was the hypothesized representation of the goal configuration for all subjects. The fact that subjects tricd to achieve these sequential relations by putting these corrcsponding tiles in their final position could be inferred from the actual move scquences of another group of subjects (Ericsson, 1975a).

Montgomery (1977) explored the inconsistency and intransitivity of choices between gambles that had been demonstrated by Tversky (1969). The study was essentially a replication of Tversky's, exccpt that the subjects werc instructed to think aloud. Since most of the protocol statements referred to gambles and relations between pairs of gambles, Montgomery proposed a coding scheme with ninc categories based on the nature of the comparisons and pcrccivcd differences between the gambles, and the dimension (i.e., pay-off or probability) to which the statements referred.

The pooled frequencies of expressed intentions to attain certain Seq (X.Y), Seq (X,Y.Z). Place (X.Y), and Place (X.Y.Z) relations are marked on the arcs joining the numbers of the associated tiles. with an S for Seq and a P for Place. Arcs without any recorded occurrences are dotled.

Examples of coding categorics were implicil comparison ("T'll choose the one with the larger sum of moncy") and great difference ("smaller chance of winning. distinctly"). Two judges, coding all the protocols independently, rcachcd 95% agreement. In matching subjects' actual choiccs to their verbalized comparisons, simple dccision rules emerged, supporting Tvcrsky's hypothesis that subjects choose the gamble with the highest value on the dimension having the greater judged difference between gambles. However, when the differences along the two dimensions were adjudged to be approximately cqual, the subjects always selccted the gamble with the higher pay-off.

Thrce studics by Benjafield (1969) sought to identify what aspects of a problem situation determinc the choice of a solution process. Each study, with 48 subjcts, used a betwcen-group factorial design varying the logical propertics of thc problem (c.g., spccificity of goal, pyramid versus four-sided object, number of inferencc stcps to solution) orthogonally with perceptual propertics of thc problem-solving material. Sclected aspccts of the protocol statements wcre cncoded, and scparate tallics carricd out for subjects making morc or less than thc median number of errors, respectivly.Differences in the protocols werehihly correlated with the differences in subject accuracy.For example, in the tetrahedron problem, subjects with fewer crrors produced the highest fraction of solutions classified as deductivc; while in a pendulum problem, the subject's subgoal identificd from the protocol was a better predictor of the type of solution achievcd than was the goal presented explicitly to the subject by the experimenter.

From a model of the process of understanding problem instructions, Simon and Hayes (1976) predicted how subjects would represent problems described in different, but isomorphic, forms. The subjective problem represcntation could be assessed from three different aspects of the verbalized information. The results showcd that the subjects acquircd the predicted representations almost without exception.

Hucsmann and Cheng (1973) proposed a dctailed thcory to explain how subjccts induce mathematical functions to fit obscrved data. The theory proposes that the induction is achicved by a hcuristically directed generatc-and-test scarch of hypotheses, where the order in which hypothescs are generated is mostly indcpendent of the data. The conjecturc that hypotheses would bc gencrated in order of complexity was tested by calculating the corrclation bctwcen the number of operators in a hypothesized function and thc ordcr in which it was gcncratcd. The rank-ordcr corrclations for all subjects but one ranged from .30 to .95, with an average of .65. Two departures from ordering by simplicity were found. In the later stages of thcir solution cfforts, subjects tended to return to simple hypotheses gencrated carlier. For more difficult problems, not included in thc abovc analysis, subjects somctimes started out with more complex hypothescs, then backed down to sce if a simpler onc had bcen missed. Subjects also showed a bias for thc opcrations of addition, subtraction, and multiplication ovcr division and exponentiation, and a bias for procccding from lcft to right in proposing hypotheses for paramcters labclled a and b, respectively.

Recently, Johnson, Duran, Hasscbrock, Moller, Prietala, Feltovich, and Swanson (1981) compared the hypothcscs gencrated by an cxisting computer simulation program for medical diagnosis with hypotheses verbalized by subjects doing the same diagnosis task. For groups of subjects differing in their level of medical cxpertisc (students, trainces, experts), Johnson ct al. (1981) found clcar similaritics bctwcen thc average number of hypotheses generated by subjccts in responsc to different types of patient data (c.g., results from physical cxamination, EKG) and the numbers gencrated by the simulation program, and also bctwcen the order in which subjects gencrated hypotheses and the order used by the program.

It is not surprising that the closest correspondence was found for the expert subjccts, as thc simulation program was constructed by experts with the goal of expert levcl performance.

Morcover, Johnson ct al. (1981) showcd that thc less expert subjects' failure to reach the correct diagnosis was due to incorrect interprctation of cvidence or failure to link evidcnce to appropriate discase hypothcsis. By withholding ccrtain kinds of patient data from the simulation program, the incorrect diagnoses of less cxpert subjects could bc generated. The idea of degrading various components of a single model to account for individual differences is quite interesting. It has also bcen used clsewhere, for cxample, in accounting for errors in arithmctic.

## Encoding Strategies

In the cxamples cited abovc, individual protocol statements wcre cncoded. Verbalizations can also bc cncodcd on a more global basis, in terms of processing stratcgics cmploycd. Stratcgics have often bcen asscsscd through dircctcd probes, although thc subjects have usually been asked to rcport thcir individual applications of the stratcgics rather than the underlying general rules for them.

Burack (1950), defining ninc gcncral "mcthods of attack" on reasoning problcms of threc diffcrent kinds (inductivc, deductivc, and a geomctrical figure problem), assessed subjects' mcthods from retrospectivc reports. For cach problem, at least onc of the "mcthods of attack" was found to bc closely and significantly rclated to pcrformance. The gencrality of thc methods defincd by Burack has bccn challenged, bccausc none of thcm was found to be cffective on morc than onc of the problems. However, this result conforms with othcr evidencec that highly cffcctive problem-solving mcthods tend to bc specialized (Newell & Simon, 1972).

Goldner (1957) uscd TA protocols as wcll as other data to classify subjects in terms of thcir gcncral modc of solving problems. For six different kinds of problems subjcts were classificd according to whether they considercd all information (Whole approach) or concentrated on parts of the information (Part approach) and also in terms of thcir ability to use a varicty of approachcs (Flexibility-Rigidity). Each problem was scparatcly analyzcd to dcterminc what data would provide cvidence for the different modes. For example, in onc of the anagram tests generating words with many letters was taken as cvidence for the Whole approach whercas production of prcfixes and suffixcs and partial configurations of letters was cvidence for the Part approach. The score of Rigidity was assesscd by defining ccrtain behaviors as rigid, like "produces only prefixes," "makes the same error," "no manipulation of letters." In other problems the rationale for identifying the different modes is less clear. Goldner (1957) found rcliable intercorrclations of thc assessed modes with the different types of problems.

Duncan (1964), comparing vcrbal reports of strategy used with performancc on an induction problcm, found that subjects who reported using some plan or system solved problems significantly more often than subjects who did not so report. After a memory experiment, Eagle and I.citer (1964) asked thcir subjects if they uscd a mcthod or system in mcmorizing the stimulus words, and if so, what mcthod thcy used. The subjects reporting that they used a stratcgy were markedly more successful than the others.

In a recent study Egan and Grimes (1979) used retrospective reports from subjects in three-term scrics problems to assess how the presented premises (i.e., "a triangle is lighter than a square," "a circle is darker than the square") were represcnted. The majority of subjects could bc unambiguously classificd into two groups. "Abstract dircctional thinkcrs" arranged the stimulus objects from one cnd to the other of the described dimension, for example, darkness. Here is a verbal report from such a subject:

I set up a scalc with the lighiest on the far right and darkest on the far left and placed the figures on the appropriate spots. (Egan & Grimcs, 1979, p. 29)

"Concrete propertics thinkers" imagined the objects with their described physical attributes, for examplc, a fatter square was imagined to be physically larger. Here is a verbal rcport from such a subject:

I also drew a picturc, and if something was rough I would put craters in it. In my mind smooth was just plain white. (Egan & Grimes, 1979, p. 29)

Not only could Egan and Grimes (1979) show reliable performance differences between the two types of subjccts, but they also dcmonstrated rcliable differences in thc pattern of crrors. A thcorctical analysis of the processes led to testable prcdictions regarding the patterns of crrors for cach group, and thesc prcdictions wcre validated through regression analysis.

## DIRECT ASSESSMENT OF GENERAL PROCESSES

In the previous section we showed how ccrtain types of verbalizations —for example, verbalizations of goals—permit testable predictions to be made about the course of the subscquent process as well as performance. All the assessments wc have described require a task analysis to identify the subgoals along cfficient solution paths. Wc havc shown that task analysis also permits asscssment of sclected typcs of information and mental structures, such as represcntations, and prediction of the effects upon them of expcrimental manipulations.

In this section, wc explore some attempts to follow a rather different approach, which sccks to capture the truly invariant information in the thinking-aloud protocol dircctly. This approach is to code the protocols in terms of high-level, and morc or less task-independent, thcoretical cntities. Most theorics that fit this description characterize cognitive processes by thc types of processes involved, rather than the information hecded. Following such a scheme, we can describe any verbal protocol as a scquence of instantiations of a limitcd number of general processes or processing activities.

## Coding Systems

The approach we arc about to describe rests on two controversial mcthodological assumptions, one having to do with the encodability of processes, the other with the scgmentation of the protocols.

Encoding processes. Earlicr. wc argucd strongly that information (inputs and outputs to processcs, storcd in STM) is heeded, and not processes. Hence, the processes themselves cannot be encoded directly from verbalizations, but must be inferred. let us assumc, for the moment, that such inferences can bc made reliably. Given a theoretical model of problem solving, wc cncode a protocol into a sequence of instantiations of a limited number of gencral processes postulated by that modcl.

With few exceptions, general theorics of this kind are not concerned with the scquential relations among processes, but rather with the typology of processes and thcir relative frequencics. Predictions might bc madc, for cxamplc, that more skilled subjccts would usc certain kinds o proceses that less silled subjects would not use.Subjects could then bc describcd in terms of the rclativc frequcncy with which thcy rely on various general processes. In this approach, thc extensive original protocol is condensed to a vector of process frequencies, and thus convertd into numcrical magnitudes that can b analyzed by standard statistical and psychomctric methods.

The initial task, in applying this approach, is to explicate how entitics (processes) postulated by a thcory can bc idcntificd in protocols. The usual procedure has been to devclop a coding manual and provide practice for coders. Most studies report inter-rater rcliabilitics, which are gencrally quite high. Of course rcliability docs not imply validity of encoding.

Segmenting Protocols. A second methodological problem is that protocols must bc divided up so that cach segment will constitute one instance of a general process. Scgmentation is particularly important if the subscquent analysis makes usc of frequencics of processes. There have been many attempts to definc criteria for segmentation. Although the usual procedure is to determine scgments prior to cncoding them, some investigators scgment and cncodc simultancously (Kilpatrick, 1968).

Under the assumptions of our information processing model, the appropriate cues for scgmentation are pauses, intonation, contours, etc., as wcll as syntactical markers for complete phrases and scntenccs—the cues for segmentation in ordinary discoursc. Thesc are also the cues most often used for scgmentation in protocol studics. A slightly different segmentation rulc, based on separating ideas (Smith, 1971), placcs more emphasis on the actual content of the verbalizations. Goor and Sommerfeld (1975) scgmented by threc-sccond intervals, although it is doubtful that they assessed cach interval's verbalization indcpcndently of its context.

Many decisions must also be made about aggregating information over problems and over groups of subjects. Since thesc decisions are mainly determined by the rcscarch problems addressed, they will be discussed in the context of the spccific studies. Similarly, in the subsequent discussion, we will cxamine various uscs of statistical models.

For several reasons, the disscrtation of Kilpatrick (1968) is espcially interesting in illustrating the issues associatcd with this approach. This dissertation represents one of the first attempts to cncode a large number of protocols. from several subjects and problems, in terms of thc occurrences of general processcs. Furthermorc, Kilpatrick cxplorcd several different scts of categories for describing his protocols, thus demonstrating the possibility of cncoding the same protocols from more than a single perspective.Kilpatrick also discusses in some detail a number of methodological issucs, and his analyses have becn influential on subscqucnt studics that have used versions of his coding schemes.

The first sct of coding categorics uscd by Kilpatrick (1968) tries to capture the gencral problem solving hcuristics proposed by Polya (1957). In a pilot study, Kilpatrick tricd to find cvidcnce in think-aloud protocols for hcuristics corresponding to Polya's "hcuristic questions." In the protocols of his 8th-grade subjects, he couldn't find evidence for more than about half of the Polya hcuristics, but, proposing a coding scheme for this subset, he added some additional categories (computational errors, requests of assistance from the expcrimenter).

The coding assessments were made with access to the complete protocol for the problem (the tapc recording). The data used in the analysis were the frequencies of occurrence in the protocol of the coding categories. In Kilpatrick's (1968) main study, thc proposcd 30 categories werc reduced to 15, thc others bcing removed bccause of skewed distributions or lack of inter-subject gencrality (catcgorics wcre retained if the hcuristics were uscd by at least 25% of the subjects for any of the 12 problems.) Applying a strict inter-coder rcliability critcrion-that codings should be significantly related, and that the difference between coders should be non-significant for cach coding variable—the number of coding categories was reduced to cight, of which thrcc could be characterized as indicating the usce of heuristics: drawing a figure, using successive approximations, and qucstioning the existence and uniqueness of a solution.

Kilpatrick's (1968) inability to find cmpirical support for the rcmaining theorctically defincd hcuristics should be, and was, interpreted as partially rejecting the thcorctical notions and associated coding specifications, rather than invalidating the verbalizations as data. Two other studies, following Kilpatrick's procedurcs, but using high-school students (Webb, 1975) and collcge students (Lucas, 1972) as subjects, were more successful in dctccting instanccs of thc use of thc heuristics postulated by Polya (1957.The results of their studies will bc descbed later.

A sccond coding scheme uscd by Kilpatrick (1968), Lucas (1972), and Wcbb (1975) cmploys many distinctions based on activity descriptions.In his initial study, Kilpatrick tricd to design a description of the scquence of processes lcading to the solution of mathematics problems. An initial coding schemc, using a large number of different categories, was rejected because it made coding too difficult and unrcliable. A second and much simpler scheme differentiated between three types of problem-solving activities: preparation, production, and evaluation. Preparation and evaluation use only onc coding category cach, "rcading and trying to understand problem" for preparation, and "checking solution" for evaluation. Production has three catcgories: "deduction from condition," "setting up equation," and "trial and crror." The result of a production activity is also coded in one of five categories: incomplete, impasse, intermediate result, incorrect result, and correct result. Some other information was also coded (i.e., crrors in understanding, and difficulties in performing activities).

When the coding scheme was tested and its inter-coder reliability evaluated, reliabilitics were found to be satisfactory. The coding schemes devclopcd by I.ucas (1972) and Wcbb (1975) wcrc structurally very similar, but employed a larger numbcr of catcgorics. Generally, the encoding is based on local verbalized information, but in assessing "trial and crror" and "dcduction from conditions," it is less clcar how much context is nceded to make thc correct assessinent. Furthermore, our own inspection of cxamples of coded protocols used in thcse studics leaves us uncertain as to what constitutes a segment —that is, a singlc production activity.

Flaherty (1973, 1974) devised a coding procedure for analysing processes uscd by subjects solving algcbra word problems. The coding system, designcd and revised with thc hclp of a pilot study, consists of 18 categorics, many of them similar to thosc used by Kilpatrick, I ucas, and Webb. Some of the new catcgorics suggested by Flaherty were "indicates familiarity with type of problem," "notes nccd for auxiliary information." Flaherty's papcr, which makes refcrence to an unpublished doctoral thesis, provides no information about how the assessments were made or whcther inter-coder rcliabilities wcre measured.

## Applications of the Encodings

There have been several different approaches to applying cncoded data of the sorts wc have bcen discussing and to cvaluatc their validity by prcdicting problem-solving succcss or making cxperimentally tcstable prcdictions about subjccts with similar proccssing charactcristics.

Kilpatrick's Coding Schemes. Kilpatrick's (1968) study cxemplifies the notion of external validation wcll, and also undcrlines several methodological problems. His study aimed at finding problem solving patterns of 8th-grade students in mathematics. For 56 subjects, a variety of information was collected: tests to assess systematic styles, to measure cognitive styles, and to measure sclected abilities. The protocols from 12 problems were coded according to the two previously described schemas: heuristic strategies, and processes. The performance data for external valuation wcre times to solution and solution adcquacy (scorcd). A postexperimental qucstionnaire included itcms on attitudes towards mathcmatics, study habits, reflectiveness, plans for the future. Alreadyexisting scores of the subjects on IQ tests and other ability tests were also used. For cach subject, the frequencies of coding catcgories were tabulated and pooled for all 12 problems. These frequences wcre thc only products of the think-aloud data that wcre rctaincd in the analysis. First, Kilpatrick finds a large number of significant corrclations between process-scqucnce variables, obtained from the protocols, and various problem-solving scores, but discards most of them, cither because they have "obvious" intepretations, involve ovcrlap in thc encoded information, or reflect artifacts resulting from the structural propertics of the coding system. As an example of overlap in cncodcd information, thc correlation betwecn "stops without a solution" and test score is an obvious tautology, and the corrclation betwcen "errors in understanding" and score a less obvious one. An cxamplc of an artifactual correlation resulting from structural properties of the coding scheme is the relation between total frequency of activities and solution time.

After climinating all of these more or lcss spurious relations, one principal rclation remains, in Kilpatrick's (1968) data, bctwccn the protocol variables and the total test score: a correlation bctwcen the use of trial and crror and high test scores. A closer analysis of the original protocols shows that, although dcduction rather than trial and error was responsible for a majority of problem solutions, trial and crror led to incorrect solutions than  eduction .he equ chccking bchaviors was also correlated with test scores, but engaging in checking prcsupposes that a candidate solution has been found, so this relation is also largely artifactual. Both of thc rclations rctaincd were weak corrclations, of .3 and .27, respectively. Since they were identified post hoc from among 45 corrclations, significancc at the 5 per cent level for the individual corrclations is a wcak rcbuttal of randomness. A scarch for correlations betwcen the chcck-list variables (use or non-use of particular heuristics) and the performanccvariables reveale w snificant rclations, nonc of them interpretable.

ILikewise, few reliable relations were found betwcen the protocol variables and the other data that were gathered about the subjects. No interpretable corrclations were observcd betwcen French's systematic styles and the protocol variables. The only significant interpretable relations with check-list variables were betwcen the frequency of drawing figures and some sclf-report variables (reporting that onc's father does not hclp with mathematics homcwork, that onc draws figures frequently when solving problems, and that drawing figures is helpful). Some process variables showed consistent relations with measurcs of special and gencral abilities. The use of cquations was found to correlate with general reasoning, word fluency, and mathematical achievcment. Structural crrors and difficulty with process showcd ncgativc correlations with most of the same mcasures.

Sincc similar issues wcre addressed by Webb (1975), we will consider only the differences between her findings and Kilpatrick's (1968). For 40 high school students, pretcst scores wcre collected on mathematical and gencral ability, and protocols on cight problems werc codcd, using thc previously described coding schemes. Qucstionnaire data wcre also obtained. As in Kilpatrick's study, data wcrc pooled ovcr subjects and total test scores were calculated. Bccausc therc were so many variables (16 prctest variables, 23 chcck-list variables, 11 process variables), Webb decided to reduce them by thrcc component analyses, which produccd 4, 5, and 4 componcnts, respcctively. A stcpwise rcgression was carried out to assess the rclative importance for problem solving ability of cognitive ability mcasurcs versus information about the use of processes derived from the protocols. Threc components accounted for significant indcpendent variance: two components of cognitive abilities (mathematical achievement and vcrbal reasoning, with 50% and 5%, respectively), and one heuristic component (pictorial representation, with 8%). The process components accounted for less that one per cent. The frcqucncy of use of specific hcuristics differed significantly bctween problems for all but two hcuristics—"bright idea" and "dcrives solution by another method."

In Kilpatrick's (1968) analyscs of the relation bctwcen categories extracted from the protocols and performance on the math problems, subjects could be differentiated by the frequency of trial and crror search. An informal analysis of cncoded descriptions of solutions suggestcd four different groups that showcd consistent, but not statistically significant, differences for othcr recorded variables. One group of subjects was characterized by thc use of algcbra, and the other threc by their relativc frequencies of use of trial and crror mcthods.

In Webb's (1975) study, cach subject was described in terms of heuristic strategies and types of processes used. Cluster analysis was applicd to thesc scores to identify patterns in the usc of heuristics and types of processes by groups of students. The cluster analyses showed that higher-scoring students cither used a wide range of heuristic strategies and an average amount of trial and crror and cquations, or they used a large amount of deduction.

A study by Iucas (1972) was designcd to cxplorc thc relative effectivencss of hcuristically oriented instruction in the calculus as compared with more traditional teaching. In a factorial design, some of the 36 subjects were given a pretest of seven problems, while the others were not; and some subjects wcre given hcuristically oricnted instruction, the others wcre taught more traditionally. All subjects took a posttest of seven problems. Data from coded protocols, using thc coding schcmes already described, were pooled over thc problems in a test session for cach subject. The only significant differences that could be unambiguously tied to the expcrimcntal manipulation, hcuristic versus traditional tcaching, was that subjects taught hcuristically tended to recall related problems more often.

We have reviewed these experiments in some dctail to show how meager are the results that have becn obtaincd from this kind of highlevel cncoding of protocols. A little later, we will discuss a study by Gimmestad (1976), using the same cncoding scheme, that casts some light on the reason for these disappointing outcomes.

Other Coding Schemes. Goor (1974) proposcd a sceven-category coding system for analyzing think-aloud protocols from problem solving tasks. His categorics, designcd to bc complctc and mutually cxclusive, could be scen as grouping scts of activitics in terms of type and status of information uscd by them. His first catcgory is surveying given information, which we would label a little more broadly from its description as operating on given or previously generated information. The remaining catcgorics are:(2) generating ncw information or hypotheses, (3) developing or working on a hypothesis, (4) unsuccessful solution, (5) changing thc conditions of the problem, (6) sclf-refcrence or selfcriticism, and (7) silence (Goor coded all three-scond time segments). Category 6 contains verbalizations that are irrclevant to the task, and Catcgory 5 is limited to proposed changcs in thc given information. Catcgorics 2, 3, and 4 arc structurally rclated, in that rejecting a hypothesis presupposes that one has bccn gencrated.

An interesting property of this coding scheme is that it captures the intuitively important distinction betwcen previously generated (old) information and new information. However, there arc two kinds of difficulty in making that assessment. First, the judgment that two hypotheses are the same or different requires a taxonomy of the possible hypotheses for thc task. Sccond, a hypothesis can be assesscd as new only after a revicw of all the hypothescs that have been generated previously (unless the subject makes somc comment implying the novelty of thc hypothcsis, and assuming such subjective remarks are reliable). Goor and Sommerfeld (1975) report that two coders, after working together for seven hours, independently coded 2,400 segments with 94% agrecment. No mcasures of reliability were presented for the individual coding catcgorics.

In another study, Goor (1974) collected protocols on three problems for 26 students with very high test scorcs on creativity tests, and 26 students with very low scores. Goor calculated the probabilities of cach of the processes immediately succceding cach of the others (i.e., trcated the data as reflecting a Markov process). There were highly significant differences between the two groups of subjects. The "crcative" students spent more timc devcloping and generating new hypotheses, while the "uncrcative" students had more intervals of silence. An analysis of conditional probabilitics, of onc process following after another had occurred, revealed no additional differences bctween the two groups.

Another possible basis for a coding scheme to classify actions according to very general processes is Guilford's Structure of Intellect model, designcd to factor intelligence items. In a disscrtation by Smith (1971), Guilford's classification systcm was takcn as the basis for representing all the statemcnts in the protocols. Each statement was coded in terms of its content (symbolic, figural, scmantic, behavioral), its operation (cognition, memory, production, evaluation), and its product (units, classes, rclations, systcms, transformations, implications). Some examples are given below from the coding manual:

F P St. "If you draw a line you would come out with three equal squares."

Sy E Cl. "Ratios won't work."

Sy M St. "I.ct me think back how many trains there would be."

Hecded information should be readily cncoded in terms of product categorics, but in fercnces about the content and opcration catcgorics will often be problematic and require attention to the context of preceding protocol scgments. The coding manual prepared by Smith says explicitly: "the sequence or context is a significant factor in classification" (p. 101), so that the cncodings of individual statements cannot be scen as all mutually independent. For threc coders with expericnce in using the code, agreement ranged from 98 to 81 pcr ccnt (chance agrcement would be less than 25 per cent). However, a previously untraincd coder having access only to the training manual attaincd only about 56 pcr cent agreement with more expericnced coders. Data are not available on reliabilitics for individual catcgorics.

Smith's (1971) aim was to distinguish process patterns in problem solving for six groups of subjects differing in vocational interest (two levcls) and experiencc in mathematics (thrce levcls), using a total of 23 subjects. Interpretable patterns distinguishing the groups wcre found, and the results scemed to be stable over two scts of problems, administered a wcck apart. Howcver, the variance accounted for was small, and the results lack rigorous statistical support.

A common conceptualization of cognitive processes is to break them down into a sequcnce of stages, with relative homogencity of activity within cach stage. Havertape (1977) proposed thrce stages for cognitivc processes in problem solving: gctting thc inforimation, understanding thc problem, and solving the problem. Activity during cach stagc was coded according to its adequacy in achieving thc goals of that stage. The code, for cxanple, for "getting the information" was: reads problem correctly, reads problem correctly after several cfforts, rereads problem several times, reads only part of the problem, docs not read the problem. In carrying out the coding, context external to the stage being coded was used. For examplc, the sub-categorics for "understanding the problem" arc explicitly rcelated to the correctness of the subscquent problem solving.

Havertape comparcd a group of twcnty Icarning-disabled students with a group of "normal" students on 26 problems. When the subcategorics wcre mapped onto scale valucs and submitted to a multivariate analysis of variance, there were clear and significant differences between the two groups, distributed ovcr all stages, and indicating a uniform deficicncy of the learning-disabled subjects.

From a thcory of undirected thought, Klinger (1974) has proposed a st of cognitive processes that must beadded toundirected thought to producc directed thinking. Two of thesc processes, which would fall within our catcgorics of "orally coded sclf-instructions" and "evaluations" arc, according to Klinger discrminable fro think-aloud protocols.The first of Klinger's processes, control of attention, was exemplified by verbalizations like, "I.ct's see," or "Where was I"? The second class of processes, evaluation of success, was exemplified by, Y, D No , d h  My  h o assessments would require no inferences, being identifiable dircctly as parts of the protocol segments.

For one type of task, manual puzzles, Klinger (1974) derived intercoder reliabilities by comparing the two coders uscd in the study with a third coder. Rcliabilities wcre .96 for attention control scores and .82 for evaluation scores.Additional information about the occurrence and context of attention control processes was coded informally. A coder characterized each occurrencc in terms of the kind of content that had just preceded it, and in terms of the coder's impression of the subject's goal in controlling his attention at that point. No strictly defined catcgorics were used in this coding, and hence, no intercoder rcliabilities calculated.

Klinger had his subjects gencrate TA protocols under four conditions, sclected to contrast undirected with dirccted thought. Solving manual puzzles and logic problems wcre sclcctcd to illustrate directed thought, and reveric (day-dreaming with cycs opcn) and a hypnagogic condition (day-dreaming with eyes closcd) to illustratc undirected thought. Each subject servcd in both conditions. Omitting subjccts with cqual scores in both conditions, 27 of 29 subjccts had more evaluative utterances, and 24 of 26 morc utterances indicating attention control in the conditions of directed thought, than in thc conditions of undirected thought.

To analyzc think-aloud protocols for subjects sccking to understand different kinds of problems, Brcc (1969, 1975) proposed a coding scheme having over 20 different categories. Each segment of protocol was coded in terms of its locus, for example: what stcp in thc description of the problem solution it referred to, what action was takcn by the subject, and what deviations this represented from a straightforward application of the action. The actions were of two typcs: simple functions (e.g., mentioning a step, rephrasing the stcp, stating rcasons for the step), and planning functions (e.g., deciding to find a certain stcp, to go through the solution description again, and so on). "IDcviations" included difficulties in applying an action and incorrcct application of an action. In order to cnhance coding reliability, Brcc cxplicitly designcd his coding system to makc the encoding very simple, bascd just on thc protocol statement bcing cncoded "without thc codcr (the author) intcntionally referring to any previous or later passages in thc protocol." (Brce, 1969, p. 16) To guard further against bias, the identity of thc subject was unknown to the coder. These encodings were subsequently analyscd by computer to infer automatically higher-levcl and global characteristics of the process.

The subjects studied the description of the solutions to different problems by concentrating on one step of the solution at a time. Howevcr, in trying to understand a given stcp they often went back to previously studicd stcps. The first task of the computer program was to scgment the encodings into episodes that corresponded to all cognitive activity centered around the study of a single stcp. Once thcse cpisodes were identificd, the program assesscd more complex processes, like predicting a step (vcrbalizing a subsequcnt step not yct studied), explaining a step by interrclating the current step to previously presented premisses (steps), etc.

Only a few studies address explicitly the problem of testing consistency in thc pattern of processcs uscd by a particular individual over a period of timc on the same or different tasks. Bree attempted such an analysis. The frequencies of fivc fcaturcs of thc protocol steps were tabulatcd: cxplains, predicts stcps. rcasons or rephrases, requests and studics of presented information. Using the extension of Fisher's exact test to 2x5 frequency matrices. he could calculate the probability that two profiles of these fcatures had the samc underlying distributions. Intraindividual profilcs showcd grcater similarity than intcr-individual profiles. A detailed analysis showed that thc consistency of individuals arosc primarily from the "explains" dimension.

## Summary

Protocol analysis using general, task-indcpendent process categories has not grcatly extended our understanding of cognitive processes. Those relations that have becn obtained, and which are not artifactual, are wak accountin or nly a small part o the variability in perormance. Predictability is low even when the protocols arc collected for the same solutions from which performancc mcasures were derivcd. Ideally, we would collect protocol data from one sct of solution processes and make predictions for the performance of the samc individuals on another set of solutions to different problems.

It would be crroncous to conclude from these negative results that ther is o uscful information to be extracted from the protocols. The methodsof analysis used in the studis described in this scction different from those that we advocate.The processes of generating data from the protocols also incorporate numerous assumptions, which may be invalid.

It is possible that the particular sets of general processes assumed in t sud re ot theh Alteativly,  possle that problem solvers cannot be discriminated from poor ones by differences in such general, task-indepcndent processcs. Wc start with the obvious obscrvation that if somcone lacks the knowlcdge necessary for performing a task, no number of general skills willsave the day for him. Expert and excellent performance in onc domain docs not prcdict the quality of performancc in another, unrclated domain. In mathematics problem solving, Wcbb (1975) showcd that thc ovcrwhclmingly important and successful predictor was mathematical knowledge.

A study by Gimmestad (1977), relying cssentially on Kilpatrick's coding schcmcs, provides data that allow us to cxaminc one of the assumptions about task-independent processes. The assumption in question is that thc rclation betwcen rcliance on hcuristics and problemsolving pcrformance should be the same for all problems, at least in a given problem cnvironment. Analyzing the relation bctwcen the number of instantiatcd hcuristics on individual problems and pcrformance on thosc problems, she found that different hcuristics were differentially uscful for specific problems. More important, she found that the use of certain heuristics was negatively corrclatcd with success on some problems, and at least one of thc negativc rclations she detectcd was statistically significant.

In order to cvaluate thc consistency of subjects' use of hcuristics on very similar problems, shc constructed two problems that caled on the same algcbra but had different semantic content. Thcre was no indication that the subjects uscd the samc heuristics on both problems. Although it is clear from other research, for cxample Hayes and Simon (1976), that isomorphic problems are not ncccssarily perccivcd and represented in the same way, this lack of consistency in the usc of heuristics raises questions about what is actually capturcd in thcse kinds of protocol encodings.

In sum, wc bclieve that at our current levcl of understanding, an analysis of thc hceded information revealed in a protocol, in the context of a task analysis, is a morc fruitful approach to protocol analysis of cognitive processcs than is an cncoding and analysis in tcrms of taskindependent general processes.

## VERBAL REPORTS AND THEORIES

Theories in psychology have usually becn formulated in highly abstract terms. Many of them make predictions only for aggregatcd data that are pooled over items and over subjects. Somc thcorics posit mechanisms that specify, on some level, how bchavior is generatcd under different experimental conditions. In this scction, we wish to illustrate with a few examplcs the relevance and import of verbal reports for testing such theorics. In the remaining chapters of this book wc will be more concerned with how verbal reports can be uscd to generate and test detailed in formation processing modcls of task performance.

When verbal reports contradict thcories that are not disconfirmed by othcr cxpcrimental data, it is convenicnt to disregard the reports as epiphenomcnal. But, if we bclicve that the verbal reports are pertinent, and not cpiphcnomcnal, we should show that a gcncral thcory adcquatcly captures the verbal information on somc lcvcl of abstraction. We will cvaluate various ways in which implications of thcorics can bc tested against the detailed sequences of hccded in formation. Wc will also point to cases where such tests lead to rejcction of thc corresponding theory.

Protocol data have bcen uscd to support and refute proposed mcchanisms underlying various processcs. Verbal reports, even from a small number of subjects, can scrve as cvidencc that at lcast some subjects' cognitive processcs conform to a hypothcsizcd model of the scqucncc and type of information that is hccded. In such situations the verbal reports serve as cvidence for the cxistcncc of certain cognitive structures and processes, an important first step towards gaining evidence for generality and gencralizability. Similarly, evidencc of thc cxistence of some cognitive structures in the memories of some subjects can refute the universality of a theory that makes strong predicitions about the nature and range of internal cognitive states.

A sccond issuc is the relation betwcen the information-rich data on the coursc of cognitive proccsses, and mathematical models that posit general characteristics of processes. Many assumptions in mathematical models of cognitive proccsses do not have to be left as assumptions, but can be converted into testable predictions on the level of process data. A third and related issue has to do with ways of understanding, in terms of mediating processes, cmpirical rclations between observed bchavior and expcrimental manipulations.

The first publishd study using TA protocols was carried out by Watson (1920). He instructcd one of his friends to think aloud while tying to identify the function of an unfamiliar physical object.From his analysis of the protocol, Watson made two different theoretical points. First, he showed that the problem solving bchavior could not be scen as insightful or as a sequence of logically connected steps. The vicw that problem solving was always driven by a rational, delibcrativc process could thus be rcjected. (See also Henry (1934) for a similar observation on geometry thcorem proving.) Sccond, in attempting to identify the structure of the problem solving process, Watson found it to have many clements in common with trial-and-error processes observed in maze learning.

It is when wc ask him to think aloud . that we begin to grasp how relativcly crude is the process of thinking. Here we sec typified all of the crrors made by the rat in the maze: false starts appear; cmotional factors show themsclvcs, such as the hanging of thc hcad and possibly evcn blushing whcn a falsc scent is followed up. The subject rcturns again and again to his starting point as shown by his asking, "You say the given facts are so and so?" The experimenter says, "Yes," and again the subject starts off. (Watson, 1920, p.91)

However, Watson docs not make the mapping to trial-and-error processes any more explicit. For him, the important argument is that the observed structural characteristics of the process are incompatible with a rational cumulative solution process.

In criticism of Hull's (1920) asscrtion that conccpt formation is achieved by identifying common clements (in Hull's cxpcriments, identifying common radicals cmbedded in complex Chinesc idcograms), Smoke (1932) carried out two TA studics. From thc protocols, he demonstrated that subjects attended to relational attributes—that is, attributes requiring consideration of the wholc pattern—rather than local attributes derived from a physically restricted subpart of the stimulus. In support, hc presented cxccrpts of TA protocols displaying such global hypotheses. Thesc protocols wcre clcarly inconsistent with the contemporary S-R theories of conccpt formation.

Gestalt psychologists have often demonstrated the nced for postulating complex subjective structures (Gestalten) to account for subjects' cognitive processes and bchavior. Dunckcr (1926, 1945) provides perhaps the best cxample of using protocol data to rejcct other thcories and in support of his own account. IDuncker prescnts a logical argument against the sufficiency of associative and S-R thcorics to account for productive problem solving (problem solving where thc subjccts cannot rcly on memory of cxpcricncc with identical or similar problems).

Duncker carefully selected his problems so that the situation would not contain common elements with any other problem solving cxperiences of the subjects. In these situations, problems could not be solved by evoking associations with previous expericnces, but rcquired gcneral solution methods that, in turn, would direct the gcncration of more specific useful solutions.

From his analysis of the TA protocols, Duncker showed that his subjects uscd such general mcthods prior to evoking spccific solutions. Unfortunatcly, he does not report the vcrbatim protocols, but only his own functional cncodings of the subjects' verbalizatons. However, the same argument can be made as above: that any thcory of productive problem solving would nccd to account for the subjccts' acccssing, attending to, and verbalizing gencral solution mcthods.

In testing thcir thcory of anagram problem solving against information in TA protocols, Mayzner, Tressclt and Hclbock (1964) found that thcir theory could not account for many of the words hecded, cspecially words that containcd letters not contained among those presented in the anagram. The fact that subjccts hccdcd words, and not just bigrams or trigrams, suggested to the authors that thcir thcory had to be extcnded to account for the problem solving process in this task.

Grudin (1980) showcd that subjccts can and do vary thcir strategies in solving verbal analogics (i.c., A:B::C:?) bcyond what was assumed in the exising thcorics Sternberg, 1977.Sternberg's 1977theory assumcd that subjccts always looked for the rclation betwcen A and B, and thcn applicd it to C to find the right answcr. Howcver, Grudin (1980) recorded the following think-aloud protocol from a subject solving such anologies.

ITEM: BIRD:IISH:AIR: (BREATHE, SWIM, WATER)

s to h (r  in in (pause) water." (Grudin, 1980, p. 72)

This protocol clearly contradicts the theory, in that the relation betwen  and C is found and applicd to B. In two experiments analyzing latencies, Grudin (1980) demonstratcd the general occurrence of such strategics.

Dominowski (1974), scarching for the cognitive proccses underlying concpt foration, collected T protocols and retrospective reorts from a large group of subjcts, and found that no single processing model could account for the protocols of all subjects.Although he found some inter-subject similarities, there was overriding evidence for the use of several strategics involving different processes. Several other investigators have lso observe different strategis beig used t perfr he me as Girtin, 1;Anzi& Smon, 1Bu 1956). We have already discussed to some extent the problems for theorizing and prcdiction that arise when processes are not uniform, and we will rcopen this discussion later in this section.

In models of problem solving like the General Problem Solver (Newell & Simon, 1972), it is assumed that the subject holds cxplicitly defined goals and subgoals. These allow differences betwcen the current situation and the target situation to guide the problem solving process through means-cnds analysis. According to mcans-ends analysis, subjects first determine specific differences betwcen the goal and the current siuation, and then seck to climinate one of these differences by selectng an appropriate method. Rclying on TA protocols from fivc high-school students solving gcometry problems, Grccno (1976) found that meanscnds analysis could not account for all of thc observcd cognitive processes. For examplc, the subjccts oftcn gcneratcd (and vcrbalized) the goal of proving that two triangles were congruent. Mcans-ends analysis would predict that the subjcct would sclect onc of scveral possible congruency conditions (likc side-angle-side correspondence) and cvaluate its applicability, and if not successful, then procccd to cvaluate another congruency condition, ctc. However, Greeno (1976) found that the indcfinite goal of proving congruency lcd subjects to discover the correct congrucncy condition by direct recognition rather than search. Through an analysis of the protocol, he could show that thcre was no scquential testing of different possibilities. The assessment that indcfinite, rather than definitc, cxplicit subgoals wcre used was madc by three judges who indcpendently coded 24 protocols.

We have sclected these samples to illustrate how evidence that subjects heed certain types of information can often bc uscd simply and dircctly to test theoretical generalizations. The assessment that the information was actually hecded can be made with a minimum of interpretation and without strong prior thcoretical assumptions. The hccding of the information constitutes a datum to be accounted for by any theory of the corresponding cognitive process. It would be very difficult for a model to account for the subjccts' verbalizing relevant information without concluding that this information was gencratcd cn route to the solution of the problem by thc corresponding task-dirccted processes. It is, of coursc, logically possible that such information is gencrated in parallel with the actual task-orientcd processcs and without contact with them, but such an interprctation does not provide a plausible and parsimonious account of the data.

## CONCLUSION

Our main concern in this chapter has been to investigate the claim that has frcquently becn made that thinking-aloud protocols and other verbal reports are cpiphenomenal, and cannot be rclicd upon to provide evidence about the course of ongoing task pcrformancc. We have asscmbled all of the expcrimental evidence wc could find that bcars on this issue. With great consistency, this cvidencc demonstratcs that verbal data arc not in the least cpiphenomenal but instcad arc highly pertinent to and informative about subjccts' cognitive processcs and memory structures.

Human subjects arc not schizophrenic crcatures who produce a strcam of words, parallel but irrclevant to the cognitivc task thcy are performing. On the contrary, thcir thinking aloud protocols and retrospcctive rcports can rcvcal in remarkablc dctail what information they are attending to while performing their tasks, and by revcaling this information, can provide an orderly picture of the cxact way in which the tasks arc being performed: the strategics employcd, thc inferences drawn from information, the accessing of memory by recognition.

As we saw in the final scction of the chapter, verbal data often provides a powerful means for testing broad theorctical gencralizations, which can somctimes be confirncd or refuted by demonstration of the presence or absence of certain information in subjects'verbalizations. But thc real promise of vcrbal data, a promise alrcady partly realized over thc past two or three decadcs, lics in thcir use in developing and testing detailed information processing models of cognition, models that can often be formalized in computer programming languages and analyzcd by computer simulation. To take advantage of the powcr of verbal data in carrying out this cnterprisc, we must devclop a methodology for cncoding and interpreting these data. The remaining chapters of this book are ccntrally concerncd with this methodology.

# MODEL OF VERBALIZATION

Up to this point, we have aimed mainly at identifying from verbalizations what information subjects attend to. In this chapter, we will add to our model of verbalization more detail about the underlying processes, as a basis for our subsequent discussion of techniques of protocol analysis. We will not alter our earlier general model, but will strengthen the assumptions on which it is based and add some new assumptions. We will describe first, in summary form, the model for concurrent verbalization.

## GENERAL MODEL AND ASSUMPTIONS

The model of Chapter 2 showed the dependence of verbalizations on how information is stored and represented, and showed how verbalizations are coordinated with the ongoing cognitive processes. The model allowed us to predict the existence and locus of effects of instructions for various kinds of verbalizing. In this chapter, we will focus on the content of the verbalizations, and the relation of that content to the way in which information is represented internally.

The following assumptions are at the core of the model:

1. The verbalizable cognitions can be described as states that correspond to the contents of STM (i.e., to the information that is in the focus of attention).

2. The information vocalized is a verbal encoding of the information in short-term memory.

In order to be able to discuss in more detail how the contents of STM are verbalized, we need to find some way of describing the information structures it holds. For lack of a better label, we will refer to them as thoughts. Since only one thought can be verbalized at a time, we necd a mcchanism that determines which once. In addition, wc want to spccify how non-verbal thoughts are translated into verbal form. By undcrstanding this translation process we should be bctter able to infer the non-verbal representations from the observed vocalizations.

The first additional assumption we require is that thoughts are sclccted for verbalization as thcy are heeded.

3.The verbalization processes are initiated as a thought is hecded.

The second assumption relates to structure.

4The verbalization is a direct cncoding of the heeded thought and reflects its structure.

Up to this point. we have used the terms "talk aloud" and "think aloud" interchangcably. In applying our more dctailcd modcl, it will sonetines be necessary to distinguish the case where a subject utters thoughts that are alrcady cncodcd in verbal form from thc casc where the subject recodes verbally and utters thoughts that may have becn hcld in mcmory in soime other form (c.g., visually). When it is nccessary to make the distinction, we will refer to these two forms of utterance as talking aloud and thinking aloud, respectively.

In providing protocols. subjccts will not always distinguish betwcen thesc two kinds of vcrbalizations, but to somc cxtent, differcnces in instructions (scc the Appcndix to Chapter 7) may influcncc thcm to produce the one or the other. The nature of the task will often have even more influcnce on the verbalizations. When it is not clear which term better describes the verbalizations, wc will generally refer to them as "thinking aloud."

Before we discuss the cognitive processes involved in verbalizing under talk-aloud or think-aloud instructions, we necd to discuss briefly these cognitive structures that we are calling thoughts.

## Characterizing Cognitive Structures ("Thoughts")

We will have little to add to the long-standing argument over whether all mental content is verbal, or at least verbalizable (Scc McKcllar (1968) for such a discussion). Holding to our carlier limits, we will only consider heeded information that is rclevant for task-directcd cognitive processes. This restriction relieves us from considering many of the most controversial kinds of mental content.

The restriction to thoughts that reflect hecded information in STM mcans that the "strcam of thought," discusscd by Jamcs (1890), will not concern us. According to James' own characterization thcse peripheral elements are never completcly hecded. Likewise, day-drcanis and similar thoughts contain imagery that never is hecded directly. We will also cxclude feclings from the thoughts we will consider.

Many of the categories of thought we are excluding raise difficult problems of interprctation and analysis.The difficultics are not nearly so grcat for task-rclevant information acquircd through verbal instructions and verbal problem stimuli. In Chapter 3 we presented cmpirical evidencc to show that the kinds of thoughts we are considering are gencrally sufficient to account for the cognitive mechanisms in task performance.

In ncarly all linguistic analyses, the sentence is regarded as the verbal realization of a meaningful idca. Task-relevant information to which a subject attends, even if it is somctimes figural rather than verbal, can usually bc described at least approximately in terms of propositions that arc cxpressable in scntenccs. As our discussion of visual imagery in Chapter 2 suggests, however, the cncoding of somc kinds of stimulus in formation into sentences may be quite complex and difficult. For the present, we will consider stimuli that do not present this difficulty. How can wc use sentence structure to discriminate bctwcen different mental contexts in which information can bc hecded?

There are many different kinds of verbal information. Some information reflects the current problem situation. It may be an cncoding of the stimulus, or in formation that is true of the task. Verbalizations of this Current-State information arc often marked by verbs such as "bc," "have," ctc., in the prcsent tense. Some verbalizations reflcct intentions or goals to attain configurations or attributes not truc for the current situation. This Future-State information is often markcd by verbs or verb constructions like "want to havc," "likc to havc," or verbs in the futurc tense. Finally, sone information in verbalizations is modal in character. Modal-State information is hypothctical or conjectural, and verbalizations of this type are markcd by "if.." constructions or verbs like "assume," "guess," or constructions involving "maybe" or "perhaps." We scc that the primary cues for identifying these different kinds of thoughts are the verbs and their tenses. Occasionally finer distinctions in the underlying processes are made: "I remembered that "] noticcd that..," "I forgot that..." These implicit descriptions of the processes will be discussed in morc detail later.

## 224 Protocol Analysis

## Relation to Rescarch on Language

T'hinking aloud and talking aloud can be clicitcd almost instantancously by the appropriate instruction from virtually all human adults. This means that the necessary cognitivc processes nccd not be lcarncd, but rather are available and rcady for usc, suggesting that the verbalization processes must be very closely relatcd to processes uscd by pcople in cvcryday languagc bchavior. Although norimal language bchavior usually involves social interaction, its processes should not bc vcry different from thosc that produce TA protocols. In our subscquent spccification of the verbalization processcs uscd in concurrent verbal reporting wc will draw on thc publishcd rescarch on language and specch production. To be able to do this lcgitimately, wc must point out the rclation betwcen this rescarch and rescarch using verbalization of thoughts.

Studics of language production have lagged considerably behind rescarch on language and text comprchcnsion. A likcly reason is the difficulty of obtaining adcquate experimental control over the gencration of verbalizations. Morcover, subjccts may cxpress thoughts in various syntactical and lexical forms. To combat this variability, rescarchers in speech production often instruct subjects to gcncrate one scntence as fast as thcy can. A common design is to show subjects a simple picture and requirc it to be translated into words, or to display some words that have to be integratcd into a full mcaningful sentence. Other studics ask subjects to recall previously prescnted sentences from memory. Yet other studics ask subjects to dcscribe and explain more complex stimuli, like cartoons, recall personal cvcnts, or describc wcll-known cnvironments from mcmory. What mainly scts thesc studics apart from studies of concurrent vcrbalization of thoughts is thc instruction to describe some externally perceived event or objcct, often with additional linguistic constraints. Wc will later discuss rescarch contrasting verbal descriptions generatcd for other people with verbalizations gencrated for a subject's own usc. In both cases wc will argue that the obscrved verbalization corresponds to a thought, and it is primarily in thc sclcction of the thought and in somc aspects of its verbal expression that wc would expect differences.

It is cven more obvious that research on speech production will be relevant to the spccification of verbalization processes. There is now considerable cvidence (Danks. 1977oa close link beteen parts o sentencs pronoun in  singlburst (identifid by fatures oarticulation, i intonation, stress, pauses, et.and their meanig.The ial pal rlatn made Kozhevnikov and Chistovich (Danks, 1977).

A major step beyond post-hoc cvidence for such a link was taken by McNcill (Danks, 1977). He was able to show that articulation units in most cases (around 70%) could be prcdicted from the underlying conceptual structure. Thc latter was defincd a priori in terms of conceptual relations, like "is an agent of," "at location of." Syntactical boundarics accounted for most of the remaining units. Furthcrmore, McNcill showcd that hesitations and pauses were best predicted by major shifts in the conceptual structuregrammatical structure was not as good a predictor. In a later paper, McNcill (1975) revicwed further evidence for the validity of syntagmas in data on language devclopment and analyses of the coordination of gestures with language in adults. Accepting his analysis wc will add the following assunptions to our model of verbalization:

5. Units of articulation will correspond to intcgrated cognitive structures.

6. Pauses and hesitations will bc good prcdictors of shifts in processing of cognitive structures.

The tight coordination betwcen verbalization and thought, cven in normal spcech, is summarizcd by McNcill in this way:

"For many spcakers, norinal speech scems to be uttered as it is organized. The conccptual arrangements bchind spcech can be worked out at ncarly the same time the scntencc is produccd, ccrtainly not always a phrasc or sentence in advancc." (McNeill, 1975, p. 356)

In a recent study, Chafe (1979) showed that subjects recalled information from a picture in vcrbalized units that consisted of about 5 words cach with a mean duration of less than 2 scconds. Most of the units or scgments were singlc casc framcs. With the cxception of some instances (lcss than 5%) that wcrc codcd by Chafe as "mistakcs" of the speaker, the units did not exceed a single clause each.

## CONCURRENT VERBALIZATION

Having laid down these six assumptions about verbalization processes, we will now look at talk-aloud and think-aloud processcs. Since, according to our definition of talking aloud, the verbalized information is heeded in oral forn, no translation is necessary. Hence, we will start our discussion with talk-aloud before turning to a discussion of the more complex processes involved in think-aloud. In thc final scction, we will compare cvidence on these two typcs of verbalization and also explain how subjects occasionally verbalize cognitive processes and activities.

## Talk-Aloud Procedures

The instruction for talk-aloud asks the subjccts to say out loud whatever they arc saying silently to themselves. In Chapter 2 we proposed that subjects frequently attend to phonemic information that can be vocalized dircctly, and we reviewed thc many fornms of independent cvidencc that support this claim, including clectrophysiological recordings of the vocal tract, crrors, interference from concurrent chanting, and so on.

A fairly wide range of tasks call for cognitive processes operating on orally cncoded information. The most obvious are those where subjects maintain large amounts of auditorily presented phoncmic information through dircct rehcarsal. The following protocol from a delayed recall of a scqucnce of stimulus digits can serve as an cxample.
