Finally, we observe that subjects often generate internal speech spontancously and sometimes vocalize it. This internal spech may contain self instructions, like "Lct me see," "Wait a minute," etc., or more cxtensive self-dialogues.

When hecded information is alrcady encoded orally, we claim that the internal activation associated with attending to this information provides input or a process (VOCAL.IZE) without additional central processing. In talk-aloud we assume that verbalization bcgins as soon as the internal activation takes place.

The protocols we have just reproduced clearly satisfy several of the assumptions of our modcl of verbalization: Assumption 2 (the verbalized information is in STM), 3 (the vocalization is initiated by hecding), 4 (the information is encodcd directly). Since the verbalization is necessarily scrial, the apparent casc with which subjects talk aloud gives support for the scriality of attention (Assumption 1). In our extensive review in Chapter 2, we found that talking aloud did not cvcn slow down task performance.

The model's assumptions (5 and 6) about the articulatory and temporal structure of TA verbalizations are also supported by empirical evidencc. Ericsson, Chasc and Faloon (1980) showcd that a subject in the digit-span task rccalled digits in 3- and 4-digit groups with longer pauscs between predictable digit groups. The digit groups were cncoded mnemonically. The longer pauses were also shown in cxperiments to correspond to additional retricval. The subjects' retrospective reports were the carlicst and most powerful cvidcnce for these structures. Other analyses of rccall have providcd additional support.

## Think-Aloud Procedures

In thinking aloud we have vcrbalization of both orally cncodcd information and othcr kinds of thoughts. Wc would like our modcl to accommodate all kinds of thoughts—whcther simple or complex, and whcther pcrccived, gencrated through cognitive processes, or recalled from I'TM. We also rcquire that the verbalization follow the rapidly changing thought contcnt dynamically. To thrcad our way through the many issues and the large volume of cmpirical cvidence, wc will structure our discussion along the following lines.

First, we will discuss verbalization of sinplc thoughts occurring without a context of other thoughts. Then wc will turn to vcrbalization of thoughts in context. Verbalization of complex thoughts rctrieved (or gencrated) from I.TM will then bc discussed. Finally, we will discuss the temporal intcgration bctwcen thoughts and verbalization, and then examine cvidence that thinking aloud in some circumstances is incomplete.

Context-free Verbalization. We will limit our discussion to stimuli and STM contents that arc rcadily cncoded in words. This limitation does not exclude pictorial stimuli or images, providcd that they nced not bc described at too low a levcl of detail. With this limitation, thoughts-represcntational structures—can be describcd by propositions that denote one or more attributes of an object or conceptual entity, or denotc relations among scveral cntitics. Although thcre are many general schemes for represcnting propositional knowlcdge, all of which could scrve almost cqually wcll for our purposes, we will use the system proposcd by Kintsch (1974), which has thc advantage of providing a close link bctwcen the proposition and the verbalization. Such a rcprescntation appcars adequate cvcn when the story is presented through pictures (Baggett, 1975).

There is considerable support for propositional representations. In an intcresting scrics of studics, Chafc (1977) showed bricf films to 80 pcoplc. When thesc subjccts described from memory a film where a banana was transferred from an older boy to a younger boy, almost all of them (76) uscd a sentence with a verb, an agent, a beneficiary and a patient—like "The older boy handed/gave/passed the younger boy a banana." Hence there was virtually perfect agreement on the relations a entis invol.However thappars  more varabl lexical choice and syntax.Chafe (1977) gives an cxample of a verbal description given by the same spcaker aftcr sccing the saine brief film 8 wecks apart.

"First occasion: He picked up some hay and lifted it over the corral fence and into the corral. All of the animals cagerly went ater and bcgan cating the hay. Second occasion: He threw somc hay over the top rail of the corral fencc to the animals inside." (Chafe, 1977, p.47)

Apart from the omitted information in the sccond description there is high consistency in the elements of the two descriptions.

To discuss the variability, espccially observcd betwcen people in surface structure of verbalizations, we will assume that the abstract structurc using rclations and clements is thc prefcrred description of the thought. To producc a verbalization from a thought, first, lexical items must bc sclccted for cach relation and cntity; sccond, a syntactical form or order of vcrbalization must be selccted. Although many investigators have argucd that thesc processes interact, wc will initially discuss them indcpcndently.

Selection of Lexical ltems. Wc proposc two cncoding processes, NAME(X) and REFERENCH(X), to generate verbal codes for entitics.

NAMF(X) simply creates a namc for cntity X. Many visual and symbolic cntitics already have uniquc labcls. Thc cxpcrimenter often provides such labcls in the task instructions. In many tusks there are cstablishcd naming conventions known to subjects with cven a little task experience. In chess, all thc picces and cach square of thc chessboard have well-known names. In mathcmatical and othcr symbolic systems, the symbols often have convcntional names. The common language sharcd by subjects lcads them to apply the same names to ordinary physical objccts. In all such cases, the naming of cntitics and relations in the hccded proposition is unproblematic.

Chafe (1977) proposed that objects vary in terms of thcir codability, that is, the consistency of the labcls assigncd by different individuals. In analyzing the film mentioned carlicr, he observcd remarkable consistency in calling thc transerred objcct a banana. Of thc 80 subjects, 72 (90%) calld it a "banana." Thc remaining subjects called it an "object" (6%), "thing" (1%), "somcthing" (1%), and "toy auto" (1%). To what extent the deviations were due to misrecognition of the object or failure to find the labcl can not be assessed in that study. Much lower agreement was found in subjects' descriptions of an object on which children were scen playing in a play ground. Of the 80 subjects, 73 (91%) did refer to this object. However, thc most common labcls wcre "jungle gym" (19%), "object" (14%), and "structure" (11%). In fact, 20 different labels wcre used. Chafe (1977) found that subjects tendcd to use more modifiers with the names or labels for the playground objecton the averagc 2.12 modificrs wcre used; for thc banana, only .16 modifiers were used on the avcrage. We will later describe additional rescarch on verbal descriptions in the section comparing thinking aloud with other forms of verbalization.

Also rclevant to the issuc of lexical selection is the influcntial paper by Clark (1974), wherc he shows subjects' prefcrences for using unmarkcd adjectives to describe relations and attributes. For example, subjects prefer "longer" to "shorter" and "bright" to "dark." The unmarked adjective appcars to be the one that subjects assess.

Due to the direct access of the names and the fact that most names arc cstablished by convention rather than semantically derivcd, the gencration of names will not likcly affect thc subsequcnt cognitive processes. By careful design or sclection of tasks, reliance on the NAMF process can bc increased. However, there are many instances when an object or cntity cannot be uniquely referenced by its namc, for example, in cascs where scveral objects have the samc label, or whcre an object lacks a rcadily clicited name. Then wc will postulate an cncoding process called REFERENCH(X). An attribute or combination of attributes of the entity can often be used as a unique reference: "the red ball," "the large block," ctc. In other cases referencc can be madc cither to spatial location ("let," "furthest down"). or to spatial relations with other reference points ("on the table"). (Sec Olson (1970) for a discussion of the influence of perceptual context on linguistic form of refcrence). In still other cascs carlier cvents can be used to refer to the heeded entity, like "the block that used to bc on the table."

The REFERENCE process requires cognitive processing beyond that required to NAME. Wc postulate that subjects, for cconomy's sake, will tend to usc attributes, from among all those logically possible, that arc already activated and part of their representation of the current task. Howvr in cass whe the subjctiv ereentationdoe' llo generation of a verbal referent (c.g., sizes of disks in the Tower of Hanoi with morc than 3 disks). wc assume that the referencc will be based on task-irrclevant salient perceptual cucs (c.g., color).

Subjects are frec to circumvent the NAME and REFERENCE procses by usig pronouns, and expericnce shows that pronous e usd frequently in think-aloud protocols where the alternative would be to use REFERENCE(X).

Selection of Syntactical For Thoughts can b expresse tically in many ways. In view of the recent comprchensive review by Bcx, ill i iscussion here to a eapls osuch fctors.Ther hav bee ny a few studics where subjccts havc been asked to describe pictorial lC onc of the stimuli below.

In both cases most subjects preferred to. start their sentences with The star."For picture , 89% of the subjects wrote, "The star is above the line." For picture B, 69% of subjects wrote, "The star is bclow the linc." This lower frcquency in thc sccond case can be attributed to a general preferencc for "above." Onc possible inference from this study is that the star provides the perspcctive for describing the scene. The sentence produced is the implicit answer to the question, "Where is the star?"Clark and Chasc (1972) showed that the prefcrence for this syntax was entirely consistent with other subjects' latencics in a picture-sentence verification task. MacWhinney (1977), exploring the gencral evidence for the perspective hypothesis, notes that attempts to provide a perceptual context that clicits passives havc been rather unsuccessful.Using other expcrimental manipulations (c.g., eliciting questions), more success has bcen achieved.

Another major experimental method for studying sentence generation is presenting subjects with sentences and studying thcir later recall for them. For cucd recall there is a considerable effcct of the location of the cue word in the sentence. For passive constructions recall is much more often correct if the cue word is the starting point of the stimulus scntencc (cf. Bock, 1982; MacWhinney, 1977). In an interesting study by Bock and Irwin (1980) rccall of a sentencc likc, "The rancher sold the horse to the cowboy," could be changed to "The rancher sold the cowboy the horse," by referring to a "cowboy" or to an cxample of a cowboy, like "Roy Rogers," in the prcamble to the recall cue. Bock and Irwin found that giving the lexical item "cowboy" resulted in more change. In her revicw, Bock (1982) explores how far thcse syntactical sclections can be accounted for by lexical accessibility.

Another scries of studies on verbalization of thoughts present subjects with one or more words that are to be made into scntences. In reviewing his own and other investigators' studies of this kind, IDanks (1977) shows quite convincingly that a stage model, where the thought is first generated and then translated, cannot account for the data in this expcrimental situation. In one of his cxperinents subjects werc simply asked to gencrate idcas containing the stimulus words and signal when this process was completed. I.ater the same words were presented again and subjects had to verbalize the thought or indicate that they could not remember it. One of the main results was that the thought production latency was related to linguistic variables of the later produced verbalizations. Ianks interprcts thesc results to indicate somc linguistic cncoding of the ideas, even when verbalization is not requested.

Other investigators, like Taylor (1969), did not find that syntactic complexity made a difference in how long it took to start to verbalize an idca containing a singlc abstract or concrcte noun. However, Taylor did find a clcar difference in this starting latency between abstract and concrete nouns, a difference which can probably be attributed to the great difficulty of gencrating ideas involving abstract nouns. Danks (1977) argues that the effects of syntactical complexity may have bcen present, but too small for Taylor's study to find. (A study of Johnson (1966) suggests that the cffects may bc on the order of less than a tenth of a second).

In a study of very simple sentences or cven syntagmas (i.e., nounverb and noun-vcrb-object), L.indslcy (1975) found cvidencc that verb sclecton is initiatd but not complcted when subjcts start verbalizing the sentence with noun and verb.Since the initation latency was unaffected by asking the subjects to generate an additional object, thought generation and linguistic cncoding must go on in somc parallel fashion. Th rality the rsults may elmite by th act that suects employ special strategies for generating ideas that contain the visually presented words. However, these studies demonstrate quite niccly the immcdiacy of verbalization of thoughts.

In sum, vcrbalization of thoughts relics on processes for lexical and syntactical selction that can be quite variable betwcen individuals in situations where no accepted terminology has becn devclopcd and acquired. However, in many situations the vcrbalization of thoughts is quitc immediate and follows closcly thcir generation.

Verbalization of Complex Thoughts. We will make a major distinction between verbalization of newly gencrated thoughts and verbalization of thoughts recalled from memory. Generating new thoughts, under all kinds of constraints of relevance, is a slow process. Old thoughts are alrcady wcll integrated with other information in memory. Retelling a story that one has told many times is much casicr and flucnt than telling a story that is bcing gencrated as it is told. In writing letters thc constraining feature is not verbalization or writing spced, but the time taken to generate what is to bc said or communicated (Gould, 1978, 1982). Bock (1982) reviews somc studics showing that planning what to say takes much more time than deciding how to say it. (Dcese (1978, 1980) found that when subjects had previously decided what to say—but not how--thcy were much more flucnt than when they made such dccisions whilc spcaking. Goldman-Hisler and Cohcn (1970) found planncd spcech to bc more varied syntactically than cxtemporancous specch. Finally, verbal descriptions of cartoons and events are much more fluent than explanations (Goldman-Eisler, 1968; Ievin, Silverman, & Ford, 1967), which require morc thinking and analysis.

We will first discuss verbalization of ongoing productive thought and then turn to verbalization of recallcd thought or perccptually available in formation.

Verbalization of Productive Thought. Onc of the basic assumptions (Assumption 3) in our model of concurrent verbalization is that hceded information is verbalized at the time it cnters attention. For processes that use STM for storage of inputs and outputs, Assumption 1 then allows us to gencrate the following cmpirically tcstable hypothesis:

Hypothesis of Sequential Verbalization. Information required as input to some process or opcration will be verbalizcd before the output of that opcration is verbalized.

We can illustrate this hypothesis with a couple of cxamplcs and also show that it is not trivial and that altcrnativc outcomcs arc conccivable. The first example is taken from a mental multiplication task, whcre one of the component multiplications is 6\*4:

It's that I gct so mad about

In this casc wc are dcaling with recognition processes, which do not usc STM for their inputs. Before discussing rccognition processcs we present one more cxamplc. If the goal is hceded prior to sclecting means for obtaining it, then we would cxpect that it should be verbalized prior to the mcans. According to our model, it is possible that thc action is gencrated or accessed so quickly that vrbalization of the goal is omited. It is then possible that the subject generates the because construction by a subscqucnt retrieval e al from S.The t forms o verbalization are possible, yet the evidence supports ovcrwhelmingly the scquential hypothesis for nonrecognition processes. In all the protocols mentioned in Chapter 7, we rarly nd cass of reveed vealizations of inputs (preis constraints) and outputs (conclusions or results).

It is, howcver, not sufficient to show that in most cases plausible inputs are verbalized prior to plausible outputs. There are cases, where "because" and "since" constructions may bc consistent with the order in which the information was actually heeded, and hence, with the scquential hypothesis. In recognition processes the input is not available in STM prior to access of the recognized structure. However, once the sucture is recognized and in STM, that strucure can be usd as input to further tests and evaluations. I.ct us consider this hypothetical example of somebody secing somconc clsc passing in thc hall at a distance:

If we assumc that the "funny-looking" hat was not distinctivc of John, then the test could only be made aftcr the hypothesis of John had been accessed. This is also the case in the second cxample.

Hayes-Roth and Walker (1979) found subjccts who had to verify inferences from previously memorized texts relying frequently on recognition processes, for example (p. 129):

In work on geometry thcorem proving, Grceno (1976) showcd that subjects uscd indefinite subgoals (sce Chapter 4). As the final picce of evidence wc will look at the scquential relations bctwecn inputs (premises. P) and outputs (conclusions, C) in the cryptarithmctic task (Ncwell & Simon, 1972). Our cvidence comes from the intensively analyzed protocol of Subject 3.

The table shows that premises are usually verbalized before the corresponding conclusions. I.et us now carry the analysis a little further and determine whcther the six reversed verbalizations are violations of the sequcntial verbalization hypothesis.

The sequential hypothesis is violated if a necessary premise is mentioncd aficr the corresponding inferencc, when this inference was generated for the first timc. The requireinent that it has to be the first generation is important, for if the inferencc had been made carlicr, then it could have becn retricvcd from memory independently of the new inputs. After rctrieving an infcrencc from memory it may evcn be appropriatc to lesl its validity against rclevant prcmiscs. In a couple of cases, the hccdcd information is obviously a test of the gencrated inference rather than an input for its gencration. In the following instance the conclusion is uscd as input to a subsequent test:

B66 R is going to be a 7,

B369 and it's the I's that will have to be 3's,   
B70 because 3 + 3 is 6   
B71 + 1 is 7

If the premisc wcre a reflection of intermcdiate states towards the gencration of the conclusion (i.c., L=3), then the following vcrbalization would bc expectcd: "7  1 is 6 and 6 divided by 2 is 3."

In the last type of example, S3 verbalizcs information in thc because construction that is a mere statement of fact, not corresponding to in formation uscful in the gencration of the conclusion. Verbalization fromn Perceptions or Recall. When subjects are recalling thoughts from meinory or pcrcciving information in the cnvironment, thoughts can bc hccdcd much faster than thcy can possibly be verbalizcd. We will dcal with thc resulting incomplctencss in a spccial scction in this chapter. Hcrce wc will focus on verbalization of more complex thoughts and the cffects of context on the orm of the verbalizations.

I.ct us first look at effects of context and perspective. Context can bc induced by presenting cvents in a particular order. Osgood (1971) deimonstrated that the form and content of verbalized descriptions of stimulus evnts relected the structure of the perptual siuation and the subjects' presuppositions bascd on cvents prescnted previously. The extent to which objects were perceived as ncw or previously scen was dircctly linkcd to the use of indefinitc or definite determiners and pronouns. Modifiers like adjectives and advcrbs wcre uscd when needed odiscminat smiar jcts n ets.Th uctur  th p tual situation and the sequence of cvcnts is rclated to the phrase structure of the vcrbalized sentence. In a controlled experiment, Jeremy (1978) found that whcther events occurred at the sane or different times, or the same or different locations affected the structure of verbalizations about the events, but the content of the events produced no effect.

In an interesting study of subjects' attempts to comprehend the mcchanisms of sewing machines, Miyake (1982) showcd that the "mental" points of view of subjects could be assessed from their verbalizations.She showed that thcre was a rclation betwcen shifis in point of vicw and lack of understanding and that final understanding was associated with a stable point of view. The point of vicw was assessed through occurrences of verbs, like "go" and "come," and demonstratives, like "this" and "here." A vicw from above the scwing machine was, for example, inferred from verbalizations describing the nccdle as "going down" and "comes up." Converscly, a viw from below was inferred by describing thc ncedle as "comes down" and "goes up."

Thoughts that are inhcrently complex must be broken down into their components to generate a verbal code for them. Even though a thought is activated as a unit in I.TM, it will appcar as a scquence of propositions. The following cxample is taken from a subjcct, who is instructcd to think aloud whilc letting his mind wander frecly (daydream).

"The Volkswagon is very casy to rcpair by yourself, since thc cnginc is in thc back. and vcry small, and sincc there are so fcw moving parts, it is casy to rcpair." (Bcrtini, Iewis, & Witkin, 1964, p. 507).

Another example is taken from a subjcct sclecting a move in chess.

"A King side push of pawns to break up thc Black King side would take too long, becausc wc are after all undcr the necessity of protecting the Qucen's Knight pawn." (Newell & Simon, 1972, p. 742).

We postulate that verbalization of complex recalled thoughts is in many ways similar to verbalization of new sequences of thoughts. Bccause of the limits of STM capacity, complex thoughts are not kept as entitics in STM. A complex thought can bc hccdcd as a whole only in the scnsc that all the subordinate clcments are directly available for retrieval and subsequent attention. In the same way, a complex scene is not perccivcd simultancously in allits dctail. Instcad the information is accesscd piccemcal with the aid of rapid cyc movements. (Simon & Barenfeld, 1969). There is thus a continuum from rapid access to simple thoughts and stimuli, to much slower and scqucntial access to complex memorics and complex visual stimuli, to still slowcr production times for ncwly gencrated thoughts.

Ihere are only a few studics where the recall of well-integrated structures has bcen systcmatically cxamincd. In analyzing subject's verbal dcscriptions of the layout of thcir apartments, Iindc and Iabov (1975) isolated rules for the generation of the vcrbal descriptions. For cxample, they found that major rooms may be introduccd in subject noun phrases and with a definite article whereas minor rooms wcre mentioned in complements. The cvidence suggests that the structure of thinking-aloud verbalizations is indicative of subjccts' internal representations.

Similar analyses of the long-term memory representation of complex information structures have been made with verbal descriptions of planned mcals (Byrne, 1977) and with vcbally givcn road directions (Klcin, 1981; Wunderlich & Rcinclt, 1981). In a rcccnt study 1.cvclt (1982) has systcimatically studicd verbal descriptions of spatial nctworks of colored dots and has explored thc interesting probleims of memory and path-sclection involved in gencrating a scqucntial lincar description of such organized structures.

We recommend using similar tasks for warm-up exercises (sce Appendix), for cxample, asking subjccts to think aloud while dcternining thc number of windows in thcir homes. Here is an cxample of a concurrent protocol followed by a rctrospective report given immcdiately afterwards.

I.ct's scc. there's 3 windows in the living room, 3 windows in my room, 1 in the bathroom. 2 in the scwing room, that's 5 and 3, 6, 7, 8, 134 in thc kitchcn which would make 17: 3, 4 in the T'V room which would make 21, 2 in my brother's room, 23; and 1 in the upstairs bathroom, 24; and 3 in my parent's room, 27; and then 1 in the attic, 28.

The living room 3 windows, my room 3 windows, the bathroom has 1 window which comes up to 7,—the sewing room has 2 windows which is 9 which mcans I added wrong. The study has 5 which is 14. the kitchen has 4 which makes

18, the TV room has 4 which would make 22, my brother's room has 2 which would make 24, the upstairs bathroom has 1 which would make 25; parent's room has 3 would be 28 and the attic is 1, 29.

Verbal reports have often been collected in rescarch on the retrieval of sclected information from memory (Johnson, 1955; Rcad & Bruce, 1982: Whitten & L.conard, 1981: Williams & Santos-Williams, 1980). These studics show convincingly the dependence of recall upon retrieval cues, and that dependence has strong implications for the nature of the cognitive processes involved, and hence for the verbalizations. Williams and Hollan (1981) discuss a wide range of retrieval cues and structures. The following protocol scginent shows how subjects use highly organized information about locations when they are asked to recall as many names as possible of studcnts in thcir graduating class in high-school.

That's a new name. Hc livcd on Alvian Street. Ah, lct me sec, on the other side of therc, there is Bob. He was a year older and that other girl was a year older. O.K., so there is Margaret Mott and if you come up and around, there is no onc right thcre. Jim Gott lived down thcre, but I'vc already named Judy Nicholson and lct me sc if thcre is anybody on the cross strect. There was a girl that was younger and she had a brother that was a year older than me. Then there was a girl a ycar younger and thcre is Gay Masterson. I alrcady namcd her, and Barbic Tollen. They live on the sane strcet and they have no one clse on that street that went to Point Loma with us. If I kccp going down Silvcrgatc therc is someone who lived on the corner housc. It had a purple door..

(Williams & Hollan, 1981, p. 104)

In a recent dissertation, Walker (1982) studicd frcc recall of members of different catcgorics (c.g., cars, soups, and detergents) which were selected to reflect different degrees of familiarity to the subjects. As expected, subjects wcre able to generate morc names of the more familiar catcgorics. More intercstingly, Walker found that subjccts uscd a greater variety of retrieval cues for the morc familiar catcgorics. In only about 5% of thc instances werc names retrieved without a vcrbalized retrieval cue. As is shown by thc protocol scgments bclow. subjects appcared to usc very similar cognitive processes in gencrating names for both familiar and less familiar catcgorics.

Just kccp on picture where cars arc, /parking lot,/ . a sales lot / . on thc road, / Subaru Brat, / Celica, / picturing cars in the front of my dorm / .. ones I come out and sce all the time / ... Midgct / .. I know likc no names / Camaro, / my sister got hcr first Camaro. (Walker, 1982, p. 42)

Generation of names of detergents:

S: Okay. If I was a houscwife this would probably be a lot casicr to do. Unfortunately, I'm not. There's somc prctty silly commercials about that. (Walker, 1982, p. 207)

There are also a few studics of the recall or gencration of thoughts rather than names of items. Caccamise (1981) askcd subjects to gencrate idcas for inclusion in papers on different topics. Using a modificd form of the propositional analysis proposcd by Kintsch (1980), shc described the structurc and complexity of the thoughts. She found that, on avcragc, 2.5 propositions wcrc produccd for cach thought. Shc also found that most of the pauses occurrcd at points where the thought content changed. (For a more cxtensive discussion of Walkers'& Caccamise's work, scc van Dijk & Kintsch (1983).) Thought generation has also been cxplorcd as part of the writing process, in cxtensive studics by I'lower and Haycs (1978).

In sum. there are important communalities betwcen verbalization of recalled thoughts and concurrent verbalization of new scqucnces of thoughts. The less well intcgrated are the thoughts and inforimation in memory, the more constrained will be the acccss to further thoughts, and the morc dependent will access bc on activcly gcnerated retricval cucs. For both newly generated thoughts and (to a lesser degrce) thoughts recovered from memory, the cvidence shows that the sequence of verbalizations parallels closcly the scquence of thoughts.

## Verbalizing Cognitive Process

In the previous sections of this chapter we were primarily concerned with vealization  e norationThinoation oth the of processing and the input to subsequcnt processing. For our purposes we can vicw the attention to new infornation as instantancous. Since cognitive processes have a nonnegligible duration, our infcrence that we are attending to them derives from our awareness of a scequence of internal states.The information hceded during the cxccution of a process is our only source of data about the structure of that process.

In this section our ain is two-fold. First, wc will specify how descriptions of cognitive activities can bc gencrated within our model. Second, we will specify under what circunstances such descriptions are verbalizcd and when thcy bccomc an obtrusivc activity induced by instructions to verbalize.

We would like to discriminate thrce different sources of information that would allow subjects to report on activities with duration. First, in many situations the cognitive activity has an cxternal counterpart in motor activitics, which can cither be obscrved visually or by means of other information available in thc scnsory stores. Sccond, due to the limited capacity of STM, information will only reside therc for a limited time and it is possible that subjects have information about this time in STM.Third higher-leveactivits lie radng nd writing remade up of numcrous subprocesses that usc STM for inputs and outputs. Subjects' awarencss of thesc inputs and outputs cnables them to rcport the occurrence and duration of the higher-level activitics.

As processes become automated. less and less in forrnation bccomes available about thcm. This is particularly truc of many motor activities. 'Tasks involving physical manipulation do not producc dctailed verbal traccs of the motor activity. What arc vcrbalized in thcsc situations are the higher-levcl dircctions for the manipulative attempts (Ruger, 1910) and monitoring cvaluations of the sucess of attempts (Klinger, 1974).

Even though this may be the usual casc, stillthe subject can change his processing and describe the activity in detail from his perception of his own visual, kincstetic or tactilc input. Examplcs of this kind of verbalizaticn of motor and perceptual-motor activities are given by McNcill (1975). He asked subjects to dcscribc cach movement as thcy made it in highly autonatized activitics, like sitting down on the floor and rising to standing, and in other tasks, like assembling an aquariun. By analyzing the vidcotape recordings, he found a very close temporal correspondence betwcen the movements and the verbal descriptions. In the assembly task the vcrbal description was initiatcd after the motor activity was initiated, suggesting that the verbal description was cucd by the motor process. In the highly automatic activities therc was an almost pcrfcct correspondence betwcen the initiation of the verbal description and the movement, suggesting that the anticipated ncxt movement scrvcd as a cuc for the verbal description.

Although subjects are able to access and attend to motor processes and also describe thcm, in most cases this is an additional activity unrelated to the verbalization of the in formation hccded in the normal course of the process. For many motor skills the vcrbalization is highly obtrusive.

Although the instruction to "think aloud" clearly asks for verbalization of hceded information, it soinctimes evokes descriptions of activitics instead. In the following cxample of activity descriptions one should note the frequcncy of the present progressive verb tense.

B 30 I'm just scanning thc rules, nol in scarch of anything in particular.

B34 I can't find anything that's going to help me.

B35 360 Now. I'll start back on cquation 1 again.

B36 390Right now. I'm looking al cquations 2 and 3 and thinking of applying. (pausc)

(Newell & Simon, 1972, p. 511)

This protocol fraginent has a predominance of high-levcl activitics, as if the subject wcre intcrmittently asking himsclf, "What am I doing?" As wc would cxpcct, subjccts who give mainly activity descriptions verbalize at a slow rate. The subject in the cxample above spoke less than 40 words/minute. Wc would not bc safe in cxtrapolating to this kind of verbalization thc conclusions wc have rcachcd about think-aloud protocols that report mainly STM contents. Verbalization of activities may possibly influcnce the problem solving process, although we arc not awarc of specific cvidence that it docs.

Although we consider the process of attention or hceding as essentially instantancous, it may be possible to dcterminc how long a given picce of information is retained in STM. Information about future states, given premiscs, goals or subgoals will bc kept in STM as long as they actually dircct thc subjects' processing. It is therefore understandable why subjects usc the present progressivc in the following type of construction:

"Now I am thinking that I shall gct 1 up therc" (Ericsson, 1975b, p. 93)

Sirilarly for modal states in planning:

"And knowing that the two I's add up to somc number greater than 10" (Newcll & Simon, 1972, p. 264)

These kinds of verbalizations are derived from information that is hecded, but thcy are generated as responses to a probe, like "What am I thinking about?", and not dircctly as reports of the information attended to.

Cognitive activities of the third type are organized into skills that arc used rccurrently. If the skills are highly automated, reportable intermediatc states may occur infrcquently; otherwise, the attempt to report the activity may compete for processing capacity with verbal cncoding processes. Examples of such activities are reading simple text, doing arithmctic. scarching visually for displayed information, recalling information from meiory, gencrating combinations by manipulation. When such activitics are exccuted, subjects may refer verbally to thc activity and its associated goal at the time the activity is initially heeded ( "I.et me see if I can reimember how I did it last time."). The activities mentioncd can be of high or low levcl, broad or narrow scope: "I want to solve this problem first," or "I want to find the value of Y first." Items seem most likcly to be mentioned when the decision is bcing reached to take them up next, rather than while the activity is actually under way.

As subjccts acquire more skill in a domain through practicc, they devclop a more sophisticated internal represcntation and bctter methods for attaining goals. Subjects may both attend to these proccdures and plans, and also verbalize them. Initially a subject may recognize that he can apply a procedure he has uscd at an carlier time. Iater he may simply refcr to it as "the proccdurc for attaining X." Such a report is different from a reference to a procedure as it is heeded. A subject can only report that he has used a procedure previously if he can recall some of thc carlicr instances. Hence, such a report suffers all of the deficicncics of recall from l.TM, and must bc interprcted more cautiously than a report of information thal is being hceded.

## Differences Between Verbal Reports and Descriptions

A few studics address dircctly thc differenccs betwcen thinking aloud and various kinds of descriptions. To cxplore the differences betwcen internal represcntation (inncr spccch) and conmunication (cxternal specch), Kaplan (Wcrner & Kaplan, 1963) compared descriptions of non-figurative drawing and odors that wcre to be used by the subjects themsclves with descriptions that wcre to be uscd by some other naive person to identify the original stimuli. External speech was more explicit, consisting of more words per description, and cmploying relatively conventional references (i.c., to) size, shape, location) as opposcd to analogics (i.e., "looks like a talking pcnguin"). loewcnthal (l967) reports that verbal descriptions of colored shapes bccome less and less explicit, as a function of whcther the descriptions were to be uscd by another naive person, the subjects themselves several wecks later, or the subjects themsclves inmediately after the experiment.

These studics did not demonstrate the subjects' abilities to use their own or other subjects' verbal descriptions to identify the stimuli. In a study using colors as stimuli (Krauss. Vivckanantham, & Wcinheimer, 1968) and a study using abstract drawings (Danks, 1970), subjects could match thcir own verbal descriptions against the stimuli with more than 75% accuracy after a two wcck intcrval. In one condition, subjects were instructed to generate names to be used by themsclves (Own), in the other condition, to be uscd by others (Social). Matching accuracy was essentially the same in both conditions. However, rarer words were used by subjects in the Own condition, and thcir vocabularies varicd more betwcen descriptions (Krauss ct al., 1968). 1Danks (1970) includcd an additional group, instructed to gencrate thcir first associations to the figures. The matching was slightly (ten per cent) slower for these immediate associations, indicating that in the Own condition subjccts did not usc just their first associations.

In a study by Dickson, Miyake, and Muto (1977), Japanese students described 16 abstract figures in two different ways. One of the descriptions was to be analytical (c.g., "a crooked triangle whosc bottom part is missing"), the othcr mctaphorical (c.g. "the upper part of a rose vicwed from above"). A subset of descriptions was sclccted that Japanese students could identify correctly about 50% of the time. These descriptions wcre translated literally into English. When groups of American and Japancsc students wcre asked to identify the objccts from the verbal descriptions. the average accuracy was about 50% for both the analytic and metaphoric descriptions, with the Japanese group only slightly better than thc American. Only a few items showcd rcliable differences between the two groups, and many of these differences could be ascribed to cultural differences. For us, the main result is that these verbal descriptions remained valid cven after translation, and for subjects with a radically different cultural background.

In a study designcd to inquire whcther "think aloud" should be seen as a form introspection, Benjaficld (1969)instructed ongrou sut to ti alud an itupt tho rouo bje vy minute to make "introspective" rctrospective reports. More words were recorded in thc think aloud condition. In addition, Benjaficld found differences with respect to immediacy and reference. More verbs were in the present tense in the think aloud condition, and more in the past tense in the rctrospective reports. As would be predicted from our previous discussion. pronouns and fragmentary utterances occurred more frequcntly in the think aloud condition.

Ohlsson (1980) coded TA protocols to distinguish bctwcen heeded thoughts, on the one hand. and introspections, retrospective reports, and communications to the cxperinenter, on the other. Three criteria were uscd to identify introspections, which are difficult to characterize. Reports were classificd as introspections if the grammatical subject was the spcaker (. " "my head): if the verb was cpistemic "remember," "feel," "know") and if the vcrbalization did not contain specific in formation about the current problem. In the protocols of 10 subjects, cach solving six problcms, only 3.5% of the verbalizations were introspcctions, retrospcctive reports, or communications to the cxperimenter. Over 96% wcre verbalizations of currently hccded thoughts. From many other studics we can dcrive cstimates that suggest similar proportions of the two kinds of verbalizations in I'A in othcr problem domains.

## INCOMPLETE VERBALIZATION OF INFORMATION IN STM

We return now to the issue of the completeness of verbalizations, first discussed in Chapter 3. Thc central problem is that thought in non-oral form can procced much faster than spcech. Thc major cmpirical evidence for the incompletcness of information in think-aloud protocols is twofold: (1) inconsistency in how much is reportcd on repctitions of esscntially the same proccss, and (2) comparisons of think-aloud protocols with rctrospectivc reports.

When a thought structure comes into attention, it serves at the same time as the input to the processes for verbalization. The oral code has to be retrieved and gencrated, and at the same timc vocalized. Earlier, we reviewcd the cvidence that thesc processes ovcrlap in time. However direct thc processes, they operate on scmantic structurcs of nonnegligible size (syntagmas). Hencc, even in the case where an oral code for the structure is dircctly retricvable, substantial time will clapse before the vocalization can begin, and morc before it is complete. We illustrate these temporal rclations in Figure 5-3.

![](images/b3f86252addc946ae94f5191ee48d4861722862331eb4b8b0a81527c6e449dbc.jpg)  
Figure 5-3  
An Illustration of the Temporal Relation between Heeding a Thought and its Overt Verbalization. and the Impossibility of Direct Verbalization of a Conplete Series of Rapidly Occurring Thoughts

As can be seen from the figure, the time required for hceding a structurc during task performancc may be only a fraction of the time required to complctc its vocalization. This can bc shown by asking subjects to think aloud whilc carrying out a rclativcly simple task (e.g., mental paper-folding, scntencc-picture vcrification). Attention to task information moves too rapidly to allow the corresponding oral code to be gencrated and vocalized.

Onc might imaginc a subject pausing when cach new structure enters attention to provide a verbal report before continuing his work on the task. Many thinking-aloud instructions (Scc Chapter 2) cxplicitly instruct the subjects to slow down their thought processes in order to permit this. Where the task is an unfamiliar onc, the problem may not arisc, since ncw information comes into attention at a slow pacc. We will focus on situations where expericncc and skill in the task pcrmit information to bc acquircd rapidly. In these situations, a number of difficultics arise in trying to slow down the cognitive processes without changing them.

First, it is hard to control the very rapid processes that acquire new inoration by reconition.When, r xaple, a subject is san a visual stimulus for information and recognizes something, it may be impossible for him to prevent himsclf from rccognizing additional structures before he has reported the first onc—unless the visual stimulus can be removed at the moment of the initial recognition. This difficulty is likcly to arisc, for example, for subjects in the anagram task who adopt the simple strategy of acquiring infornation by rccognition until suffiint cus have ben tai toclicit t word fmmoy.

Second, some cognitive processes can only be cxecuted under time constraints. There arc many cases, cspecially in the motor domain, where processes cannot be slowcd down without drastically changing outcome and accuracy. Examples of such activitics are juggling and riding a bicycle. There are also activitics where the rapid decay of sensory stores imposes bounds on the time during which the in formation in those stores can bc acccsscd. Interruption and suppression of rehcarsal lead to a rapid loss of information from STM. Hence, wc would cxpcct that prolongcd attention to itcims in STM to allow vcrbalization will be disruptive of tasks that impose high loads on S'TM.

To assess how serious these problems of verbalization arc, we need to cxaminc thc relevant cinpirical cvidcncc. Our revicw will be carricd out in three parts. First, wc will cxamine some gencral implications of our model for individual differences and for ratc of vcrbalization. Sccond, wc will analyze cases where concurrent verbalization has been alleged to be incomplete. Finally, we will discuss techniques, like slowing subjects down or using verbal probes to keep them talking, that have been proposed to increase verbalization.

## Implications of Real-Time Assumption.

Complete verbalization of hecded thoughts, as de Groot (1965) notes, takes time and effort, thus "slowing down the thinking itself." De Groot observes that describing pcrceptually available objects, likc men on a chessboard, requires naming and referenccs to location (e.g., "Rook on c4."). Somc tasks, likc chess, have a precisc language for communicating about board configurations and moves. Other tasks, like tying knots and other motor activites, do not usually have a sharcd common vocabulary.

Speech in a foreign language illustrates how cncoding processes that arc not automatic slow processes down. Persons flucnt in a second language can usually think aloud in that language cven while thinking internally in the oral code of thcir native language or in non-oral code. In this case, there is nearly a onc-to-one mapping between structures in the oral code of the first language and the code of the second language that is used for vocalizaton. How much the thinking is slowcd down will then be a function of the subjcct's skill in the sccond language.

Individual differences. Individual differences in facility in thinking aloud are wholly consistent with our modcl. Some people are ccrtainly better able than others to vocalize thcir thoughts in normal social contexts. The markcd variety in spontancity and richness among the protocols recorded by dc Groot (1965) and Claparède (1934) may be attributcd to such differences.

Ease in verbalizing thoughts and private cxpericnces of a nonverbal typc, likc day-drcams, fantasies, and transitional imagery states. depends on possessing vocabulary and cfficicnt cncoding processes for doing so. In fact, Klinger (1971) observed that only highly verbal female subjects were ablc to give rich and flucnt verbalizations in a reverie condition containing mostly visual images.

Some individual differences in concurrent vcrbalization of thoughts observed by Johnson (1964) can also be accounted for within the framcwork of our assumptions. Some of his subjects followcd a "thinkthen-summarize" procedure, cncoding and verbalizing after the completion of a sequence of thoughts. A subject requiring more timc for encoding than was available in the coursc of thought would bc prcdicted to bchave in this way.

Individual differences may also be duc to style or convenience. We can reducc such differences by giving subjects warm-up tasks with oral information (c.g. mental multiplication, anagrams) where all subjects should bc able to produce concurrent protocols. (Sce the Appendix for further in formation about the warm-up procedure.)

Rate of verbalization. When a subject attends to a stcady strcam of non-oral information, our modcl would prcdict a rather steady verbalization, at a rate close to relaxcd normal conversation, or about a hundred words per ninutc. It appcars unrcasonable that subjects would vcrbalize at a faster rate than normal conversation in cxpcriments lasting from five minutes to scveral hours. Wc will first review cvidence from protocols that approach this upper bound. Then we will discuss some situations where the observed rates of verbalization are slower.

According to our model, scveral words correspond to each thought. Some authors have attempted to cstimate the total numbers of thoughts that receiv attention, ohers have state the nubers f thout structures of particular types. Thus, wc may try to report the rate of verbalization (words/minute), the "rate of thinking" (thoughts/minute), or the rate of language production (phrases and spcch bursts/minute). Only a few studies report onc or more of these measures.

There is not much variance among studies of TA in the average rats of verbalization. Roughly, normal relaxcd continuous talking produces about 150-200 words/minute. Thinking-aloud protocols vary from about 50 to 110 words/minute for 10 subjects in Ohlsson's study (1980) of scrics problems and the 11 subjects in Biggs' (1978) study of portfolio sclection. Roth (1965) reported that his subjects talked 30 to 50 per cent of the time, which is consistent with the ratio of conversational rates to protocol rates citcd above.

Over the 19 physics problems in the study of Simon and Simon (1978) the novice had a verbalization rate of 69.3 words/minute, but the cxpert verbalizcd at an avcrage ratc of 153.7 words/minute. Somewhat lower rates of verbalization can bc inferred from Sargent's (1940) anagram-solving study and Montgomcry and Allwood's (1978) study of problem-solving in statistics. Dcffner (1983) reports rates of verbalization in terms of letters per 4 seconds, but these can casily be converted into words/minute if wc assume 5 letters in a word on the average. Except for anagrams, he found avcrage verbalization rates for 5 different tasks betwcen 40 to 72 words/minutc, and also no systematic difference in rate of verbalization for casy and difficult problems of cach type. The rate of verbalization with anagrams was 25-30 words/minute with a reliable decrease of rate of verbalization for difficult anagrams.

Ohlsson's (1980) study shows little variation in verbalization rates from one problem to another, and littlc reduction in rate of verbalization during the course of an expcrimental session. Simon and Simon (1978) also found that verbalization did not vary with problcm. Roth (1966) found that subjects verbalizcd only 30% of thc timc when solving matchstick problems, but around 50% of the time when solving water-jug and river-crossing problems.

Words/minute is a poor mcasure for comparing amounts of verbalization from one task to another. In a protocol from the anagramsolving task, a word corrcsponds to a completc thought, whercas in many othcr domains, a thought may be cxpresscd in fivc to tcn words. The available cvidence suggests that thoughts/minute arc more ncarly comparable bctwcen tasks than words/minutc. Sargent (1940) cstimates that subjects generate hypotheses at rates ranging from 2 to 9 per minute.

In thc portfolio sclection task, Biggs' (1978) subject gencrated new operators at ratcs ranging from 3.5 to 10 per minute. Protocols on such widcly varying tasks as logic, chess, and cryptarithmctic showcd new knowlcdge states bcing generatcd at rates betwcen 8 and 11 pcr minute (Newell and Simon, 1972).

From the cvidence we have revicwed, several gencral conclusions may be drawn. The rate of verbalization undcr think-aloud instructions is a little lower, on average, than the normal rate of specch. Thc limiting factor may be the rate at which new thoughts arc hecdcd. In only one casc, thc cxpcrt subject in Simon and Simon (1978) did thc vcrbalization ratc (over 150 words/minute) evcn approach the upper limits of human vocalization abilities. Analysis of thc protocol in that case suggests that the cxpert did very little problem solving but relicd on recognition and recall of solution mcthods from memory.

Incomplete Concurrent Reporting. Cascs in the litcrature where the evidencc points to scrious incompleteness in thinking-aloud protocols fall into a few catcgories.

1. Subjects rcading text or attempting to undcrstand written problcm descriptions somctimes give rather scanty and uninformative thinking-aloud protocols (Hayes & Simon, 1974; Brcc, 1969).

2. In situations where intense cognitive activity can bc inferred, protocols bccomc sparse. Johnson (1964) found that subjects pause during verbalizing when thcy cxpericnce difficultics in solving the problem. Durkin (1937) found that subjccts wcre silent whcn they were rcorganizing thcir pcrceptions of the problem.

3. Mcdiating stcps leading directly to the solution are often not verbalizcd (Iuncker, 1945). Comparisons betwcen thinking-aloud protocols and rctrospective reports of the "solution trace" collected for thc same solutions by Durkin (1937) and Sargent (1940) confirm this kind of incompleteness.

Duncker (1945) notes that subjects often transform the original representation of thc problcm to a more constrained onc without verbalizing or, for that matter, being awarc of the shift. However, this does not constitute incomplete reporting of the information heeded by the subject. A subjcct may verbalize a scrics of representations, yct not compare them or notice thc differences. A person may transform the equation $x ( x - 3 ) = 5 x { \mathrm { ~ t o ~ x ~ - ~ } } 3 = 5$ without bcing awarc that a solution of the original problem has becn climinated. Yet the two representations may be reported accurately. Our model implics that only the inforation inattention is verbalized. In general, the fact that me thing has let STM is not itself information stored in STM, hence is not usually reported.

## Techniques for Increasing Verbalization

Several experimental techniqucs may be used to incrcase concurrent verbalization. In terms of our model, we can describe thesc techniques as being aimcd at slowing down the task-dirccted processes. An alternative is to substitute retrospective for concurrent reports. The primary cause for a flow of hceded information that is too rapid to report completely are rccognition processes clicited by information that is perceptually available.

We have found studies using at least four different techniques to secure more complete reporting:

1. Perceptual access to the stimulus may be denicd. Then subjects have to generate new states internally, and cannot rcly on rapid recognition of changing stimulus characteristics. We have alrcady discussed studics using this tcchniquc (Benjaficld, 1971; Durkin, 1937). A more cxtremc mcthod is to replace the cxtcrnal stimulus by information that must bc hcld internally (c.g., mental multiplication, addition, and paperfolding tasks).

2. Stimuli may be segmented into smaller picces of information that are prescnted at a slower rate. Wcll-known cxamples arce the use of information-displays in dccision-making, where the subjccts are shown a matrix of information with alternativcs and attributes as dimensions. The valucs are not perccptually available, but can be accessed cither by turning over cards or by requcsting the information from the experimenter. (For a review and discussion of such mcthodology, see Lchmann & Moore (1980) and Payne, Braunstcin, & Carroll (1978).)

Below we will discuss in more detail some cxamples from rcading studics whcre the text is broken up into a scquencc of physically separatcd sentences to break the flow of perceptually bascd recognition.

3. Ongoing skilled activitics may be interrupted with a signal to givc a report of the contents of attcntion. In many skilled activitics, like piano-playing, typing, and other skills whcrc real-time performance is an intcgral part of the skill, attempts to slow down the cognitive processes may altcr thcm grcatly. In his cxtensive study of typing, Book (1925) instructed his subjects to type at thcir normal spccd, and then interrupted them at unpredictable moments in timc, at which points they reported

## 254 Protocol Analysis

the information they were hecding. He noted very interesting differcnces bctween subjects that could be related to ainount of practice and could bc validatcd against dctailed analysis of thc ratcs of typing individual letters and of typing crrors. Chasc and Ericsson (1981) also used this mcthod to study the contents of attention in an cxccptional subject, whose digit-span was over 80 digits. Whilc digits wcrc rcad at thc rate of 1 digit per sccond, the subjcct was interrupted at an unprcdictable moment and asked to report all information in attcntion. The reports were systematic over trials and consistent with other analyses of performance.

4. Reminders may be given during silent pcriods to kecp verbalizing. In Chapter 2, we discusscd thc rather widesprcad practice of reminding subjccts to think aloud when thcy had becn silent for some spccificd number of seconds. Below wc will discuss somc hypotheses about thc causes of such silence and revicw a study comparing the information verbalized spontancously with that vcrbalized in responsc to reminders to kcep talking.

We have sclected two topics for fuller discussion: thinking aloud while reading, and remindcrs to vcrbalize.

Thinking-Aloud While Reading. Earlicr, we cited studics reporting that when subjccts think aloud while rcading, little more than the text itself is vocalized. One possiblc reason for this is that thc internal rcprescntation (and hencc the vocalization) simply mimics thc text. However, most modcls of rcading postulate that during reading with comprchension information must be accessed in l.TM to gencrate a coherent representation of the text's mcaning. A protocol that simply follows the text omits this kind of information.

Several investigators have modified the rcading task to slow it down and permit morc complete verbalization. Scntences were displaycd scparately with several lincs betwcen instcad of in paragraph form, and the subjects werc cncouragcd to "try to cxpress every thought that comes into your mind while rcading cach scntence" (Wacrn, 1979, p.4). A similar proccdure was devclopcd independently by Guindon (1980, 1981). According to Wacrn (1979), this procedure did not change the content of the verbalizations but only incrcased the total amount of verbalization.

However, Wacrn's (1979) mcthod of analyzing the protocols did not permit prediction of what information would be hecded, hence does not allow an cvaluation of how completely the subjects reported the hccdcd information. Ihat the amount of verbalization increased markedly under the slow-down procedure is suggestive. Moreover, when

Waern coded the protocol statements into broad categories, she found significant differences betwcen protocols produced by subjects instructed to rcad with the cxplicit purposc of applying the information to a task and those produced by subjects simply askcd to rcad. Hence, the additional verbalization was not simply a regurgitation of the text.

Guindon (1980, 1981), while comparing different models of story comprchension, obtaincd data that casts light on thc completeness of protocols produced while reading. She was particularly interested in how subjects made in ferences in order to achieve a coherent representation of the story. From a formal analysis of the stories, she was able to specify what inferences were esscntial. Subjects wcre cither instructed to think aloud, or wcre asked to give a rctrospective rcport after thcy finished reading. Subjects in both conditions indicated that the verbalization did not interfere with their performnance of the task. No differences were found in levcl of comprchension for the two reporting conditions, nor were thcre differences in the numbers of inferences generated under the two conditions.

Guindon (1980) found a fairly high degrce of verbalization of the inferenccs in the TA condition and only a slightly lower degrce in the retrospective condition. In a finer analysis (Guindon, 1981), shc showed that most of the omitted in ferenccs wcre those that could be madc by automatic recognition proccsscs: for cxamplc, disambiguation of pronoun references, and role attributions. For example, in the following text segment, the co-reference of the pronoun he has to be inferred:

The driver, visibly nervous, gcts underneath the truck. Twenty minutes later, just as the policc arrives, he emerges.

In the protocol that assesment is only given implicitly:

The driver cmerges from underncath the truck and tells the police ..

Guindon's analysis (Guindon, 1981) showed that inferences about plans, causcs, and scripts were verbalizcd in 65% of the cases, whereas pronoun references and role attributions in only 11%.

A valuable study rclating verbalizing while rcading to reading time has bcen carricd out by Olson, Mack, and Duffy (1981). Thcir subjects read two kinds of texts (stories and cssays) in two conditions. In the silcnt condition, subjects wcre shown one scentence at a time on a CRT, and sclected a new scntencc when thcy wcre rcady by pressing a key. In the TA condition, subjects read thc same text, sentence by sentence, while vcrbalizing. When the protocols wcrc segmentcd and coded into cight categorics (c.g., predictions, inferences, gencral knowledge, associations), there was grcat similarity in the nature of thc thoughts elicited from different subjects. Here are four protocols from sentence 18 in the story on "I entil."

Thc Colonel will be impressed and I entil will bc rewardcd somehow.</td></tr><tr><td>17</td><td>Maybe a cclcbration is planned (parade, ctc.) and I cntil will win thc day with a rousing wclcoming song.</td></tr><tr><td>07</td><td>Expect to read that Coloncl Carter will have some kind of of role in what&#x27;s going on with Alto and his music and Old Snccp.
