Probably a greal celebration. Suspect that I entil will probably be asked to play for him. (Olson, Mack &amp; Duffy. 1981. p. 300)</td></tr></table>

The authors were able to prcdict the avcrage silent reading times of individual scntcnces from thc nunber of inferenccs, predictions, and so on, in the protocols for these scntenccs. This rclation was only observed for storics, where subjects could successfully prcdict the continued story development, and not for cssays, which wcrc processcd rctrospectively rather than through anticipation.

Several other studics have uscd protocol analysis successfully to revcal basic processes in comprchension of text (Collins, Brown, & Larkin, 1980) and official documcnts (Flowcr, Hayes & Swarts, 1980). Somc applicd studies have uscd protocol data to improvc thc comprehension process.

Reminders to Think Aloud. When subjects think aloud they are doing somcthing more than they normally do while thinking. They are, at least, ovcrtly vocalizing thcir verbal thoughts and, in addition, encoding non-verbal thoughts into vcrbal form. Although subjccts require very little instruction to cnable thcm to think aloud, most pcople's normal modc of thinking is silent. Even after tens of hours of producing protocols, subjects never complain that they are unable to rcvert to their customary silent thinking. In order to account for the subjects' ability to think aloud in experiments, we have to assume that TA processes are controlled by a marker in STM.

For tasks involving non-verbal information, task-rclevant information can be accessed faster than it can be cncoded and vocalized.

When task-relevant processes require a lot of STM capacity, subjects stop verbalizing Duncer, 1945; Johnson, 1964) and remai silnt. However, even a brief reminder from the experimenter will start them king agai.Thee rindrs may take many fors like, leas, e at   hal Wc would recommend "Keep talking" because it is the least directive and docs not require any direct answcr to the experimenter.

Our interpretation of these phenomena is that the marker in STM dirccting the verbalization of thoughts may disappcar when task-relevant information is bcing accessed rapidly. The simple reminder reinstates the markcr. In fact, the reminder does not have to be verbal, for tones appcar to scrve just as well. In support of the vicw that, during intervals of silencc, thoughts are hccdcd but not verbalized, wc find that subjects will almost invariably resumc reporting in response to reminders to think-aloud (Ericsson, 1975b). Occasionally, however, subjects will report that they are not attending to any thoughts and that thcir minds arc "blank."

In our experience it is important to have subjects do warm-up tasks with oral information (See Appendix.). This appcars to climinate silence due to misunderstanding of the instruction to think aloud. It gives subjects practice in cxpressing thoughts dircctly without cxplaining or interrclating the information.

According to our model the in formation reported in response to a reminder should be the same as thc in formation reported spontancously, with one important difference. Whcn subjects are verbalizing concurrently, they will verbalize the information as it is hccdcd. Howcver, when subjects havc bcen remindcd to think aloud, thcy will verbalize the information in STM. But not all information resides in STM for the samc length of time. Almost any modcl of the cognitive processes would prcdict that goals will be kept in STM longer (usually, until they are satisficd) than intermcdiate rcsults, which can bc discarded as soon as thcy have becn used as imputs for subsequent proccssing. Thus, our model would predict a relativcly high frequcncy of reports of goals in response to reminders. In a study by Ericsson (1975b), the verbalized information was sorted into several gcncral catcgories, that are described in Chapter 4. lmmediatcly after reminders, goals and intentions were verbalized relatively more frequently, as compared with recognitions of relations and evaluations, than in other portions of the protocols.

## 258 Protocol Analysis

## IMPLICATIONS FOR PROTOCOL ANALYSIS

The basic assumption of our model is that the information in focal attention is vocalizcd directly ( in the case of an oral cncoding ) or vocalized after an initial cncoding into oral vcrbal codc. The obvious implication of this for analyzing rccorded vcrbalizations is that thc originally hecded information should be recovercd by decoding the "encoded" vcrbalizations (i.c. those not originally in oral form). The first step in such an analysis is to identify the verbalization units (scgments) that correspond to units of heeded information. If verbalizations rcflcct thc internal representation, we can usc syntactic information as wcll as pauses in articulation, phonemic intonation contours, and stress to uncover these units.

The sccond implication of the modcl is that cach segment is verbalized independently of thosc that precedc and follow it; hence that cach can be cncoded indepcndently, without attcntion to context.

This independence of protocol segments from context is incomplete in several respccts. First of all. pronouns and descriptive phrascs can often only bc interprcted in thc context of thc prcceding verbalizations. In most cascs, the rcferenccd cntity can be casily identificd and substituted in a pre-coding stage. There will, of course, be more difficult cascs, whcre information is heirarchically organized, and the structure of contexts complex.

A third implication of our model is that verbalizations should be encoded in tcrms of the hcedcd information they express. Thinking aloud protocols consist almost cntirely of verbalizations of hecded information. At thc current state of our knowledge, other typcs of verbalizations containcd in them (c.g., descriptions of process) present a more complex coding task. For example:

"I remcmbered that last time I tricd to substitute ..." Fictional

An appropriate inference from this segment is that the subject is attending to his memory for carlier goal-driven activities that he now recalls pcrforming. Bccause of the possibility of error in rccall from LTM, it is incorrect to infer that he aciually performed thcm carlier.

This last point is very important, and highlights a difference between some common practiccs of protocol analysis and the practices our model would recominend. Many procedures attempt to encode the processes that generate hceded information, rather than the information itsclf. But the processes can only be implicd from the information in STM, which docs not, as wc have seen, usually include information about process. Information can be brought to the attention of the subject in many different ways. It can be retrieved from LTM, or it can simply be recognized and dctccted as part of the perceptually available stimulus display. Or it can bc the product of somc process, inferential or other. Currently, we don't know cnough about the structurc and form of verbalizations to be certain that thcy contain cucs that would enable us to distinguish different historics of gencration. Encoding the occurrence of a spccific process on the basis of protocol data requires assumptions about the underlying mechanisms. Hencc it is more in the nature of a thcorctical cxplanation than a simple cncoding of data. In a later chapter, wc will discuss thc relations among raw data, interpreted data, and thcory.

# METHODS FOR PROTOCOL ANALYSIS

Having devcloped and examincd in detail a model of the way in which verbal reports are gencrated, we are now ready to discuss specific techniques for deriving valid data from them. First we will describe a number of specific mcthods of analysis that have been used, and thcn wc will cxamine them in the light of the mcthodological principles that should govern the encoding of verbal protocols.

## EARLY PROTOCOL ANALYSIS

In carly work, protocols of subjects thinking aloud consistcd of the cxperimenters' notes summarizing the vocalizations rather than direct transcriptions of the subjects' actual words.Until tape recorders bccame available (about 1945), therc were no practicable means to rccord verbalizations. Thc investigator was forced to sclcct and interpret the subject's specch in rcal time, without any chance of going back to reinterpret or rcevaluate the initial records and encodings.

The carliest documented analysis of a think-aloud protocol of which we arc awarc was made by Watson (1920). As we saw in Chapter 4, he uscd the protocol to illustrate some gencral characteristics of cognitivc proccss in problem solving, presenting his own intcrprctation and description of thc protocol with little or no cffort to providc systematic cvidence in support of his vicws. Subscquent rescarch using protocol analysis attcmptcd to take down thc idcas morc closely as they were generatcd by thc subjcct. Duncker (1926) analyzed a number of protocols for over 20 problems in scarch of the mechanisms that generated the solutions. His analysis (Duncker, 1926, 1945) was a metalcvcl description of the solution attempts and thcorctical mcchanisms that could gencrate the transitions to new ideas for possible solutions. Several studics (Bulbrook, 1932; Durkin, 1937) sct out to find the nature of insights and the conditions under which thcy occur. From their analyses of protocols, they contended that the observed processes did not imply any special phenomenon of insight but could be described in terms of other kinds of cognitive processes.

De Groot (1965) recorded cxtensive T'A protocols of chess players sclecting moves in games, and used the thcorctical framework proposed by Otto Scl. (1913) to cncode the processes he found cxpressed in them. While the protocols were recorded by hand, and hence certainly incomplete, they containcd cnough information to allow de Groot to draw a rather detailed picture of the problem-solving processes of novice and expert chess players. He was generally able to reconstruct the subjects' scarch trees, and provide examples from the protocols of the processes postulated by Selz..

The outstanding feature of most of this carly work is its exploratory nature. The main aim was to generate concepts and potential mechanisms rather than to address questions of validation and generality. The contact betwcen the protocols and the theorctical ideas was through the investigators' descriptions and interprctations of the protocols, which had to be generated in real time. Thcre could be little explicit, detailed comparison betwcen theory and the protocol record. Many mctlhodological issues simply could not be addresscd whcn the subjects' original verbalizations could not be preserved in thcir raw form.

With the introduction of tape recorders after World War II, the situation changcd markedly, for it was now possible to analyze the verbalizations without any real time constraints. The practice of transcribing thc tapes literally into typewritten form devclopcd rapidly. From written transcripts. the investigator could obtain almost instant access to all the vcrbalizations corresponding to a given process. Issues of coding rcliability could also be addressed by presenting several coders with the same raw transcript of the verbalizations.

With the cmergence of computer simulation of cognitive processes (Newell & Simon, 1956: Newcll & Shaw, 1957) models were proposed that made cxplicit claims as to what information was processcd by subjects, hence could become part of the verbal protocol. Intensive analysis was made of a small number of verbal protocols for a task, somctimes a single protocol, to demonstrate that the information in the protocol could b regencrate approximately, by the simulation model operating on the same task. Wc will have a good deal to say about this approach in this chapter.

In Chapter 4, we have already discussed another stream of literaturc, in which information in verbal protocols has been encoded at a very abstract level with the aim of uncovering gencralizable aspects of cognitive processes. As we saw there, thc majority of these studics did not test specific hypotheses or models of thc cognitive processes but sought to induce and identify regularitics and patterns in the recorded data.In many of these studies, the protocol data were cncoded into very general categorics, and these categorics were relatcd only wcakly to any process description of cognitive processes capable of performing the tasks. The link betwcen protocol and cncoding was often not cxplicit, and it was often not clcar on what basis the cncoding decisions were reached. We will not pay much further attention to thesc mcthods in this chapter.

## INTRODUCTION TO TECHNIQUES OF PROTOCOL ANALYSIS

In this scction wc describe some forms of protocol analysis that are especially uscful for comparing sequences of vcrbal bchavior with the bchavior predicted by models cxpresscd as computer programs. These mcthods rctain, as fully as possible, the contcnt of the protocol (low-levcl encoding), so as to permit very dctailed matching of thc cncodings with the trace of the program.

## Basic Assumptions

The mcthods to be described are not thcoretically neutral; they rest on a sct of assumptions about thc gcneral structurc of problem-solving processes, and about the verbal reporting process itsclf. The major theorctical assumptions that underlie these mcthods arc:

1. The subjcct's bchavior can bc viewed as a scarch through a problem space, accumulating knowledge (not always corrcct) about the problem situation as hc goes. This gradual, step-by-step accumulation of knowledge can be rcpresented by a problem behavior graph, the kth nodc of which represents the subjcct's knowledge after k steps of search.

2. Hach step in the scarch involves thc application of an operator, sclected from a rclatively small sct of task-rclevant operators, to knowledge hcld by thc subjcct in STM. Application of the opcrator brings ncw knowledge into STM, moving thc subject to a new point in the problem space.

3. The verbalizations of the subject correspond to somc part of the information he is currently holding in STM, and usually to information that has recently been acquired.

4. The information in STM. and reported by the subject, consists prinarily of knowlcdge rcquircd as inputs to thc opcrators, new knowledge produced by operators, and symbols represcnting active goals and subgoals that are driving thc activity. A goal may take the form of an intent to apply an opcrator; in which case the protocol may contain cxplicit cvidencc for thc application of operators.

The first two of these four assumptions can be viewed as weak postulates about the problem-solving process (wcak because a wide range of alternative processes can be represented as scarchcs). The third and fourth assumptions do little more than summarize the postulates about verbalization that wc have becn using throughout this book. Therefore, thcy do not introduce important new assumptions into thc protocol analysis process.

## Encoding Vocabulary

As a prcliminary to the cncoding process, the task and the protocol are analyzed to cxtract the vocabulary of objccts and rclations nceded to define the problem space and opcrators. For cxample, in the Tower of Hanoi task, a subject might refer in his protocol to "disks" and "pegs," and to "moving Disk X from Peg Y to Pcg Z.," csscntially using the language of the problem instructions. (Scc the protocol reproduced as an appendix to Anzai and Simon, 1979.) Thc subjcct may identify the disks by numbers (IDisk 1 for the smallest, say), and thc pcgs by letters (A, B, C, from left to right). Thc labcls may bc borrowcd from the problem instructions, or deviscd by thc subject. To dcscribc problem situations, thc subjcct may use rclations like "larger" and "smaller," and more complex rclational naming dcviccs like, "the ncxt smallest disk to Disk 2 on Peg A."

Protocols almost always also contain information that revcals the subject's control and cvaluative processes and goals. For cxample, the protocol mentioned above contains such statements as: "Oh it's getting there," this time it's casyI don't know or surewrong,"l have to go to C," "I will move the remaining four from B to C." (The latter two statements represent goals becausc the indicated actions requirc multiple moves.)

Synonyms. At this point, ccrtain cquivalents may be definced, to permit the protocol to be cncoded in a simpler, canonical form. For examplce, "smallest disk" may be defincd as synonymous with Disk 1, so that the former name may always be replaced by the latter in the encoded protocol. Provided that synonymity is not judged too lightly (and that anaphoric reference is not prejudged), this kind of stylization raises few problems of coding rcliability. Nevertheless, replacing terms by synonyms, cven through rcliably, may lose semantic content. The phrase "smallest disk" may provide information about the subject's perceptual cncoding that is lost by translation to "Disk 1." Whether the translation should be made or not in the process of cncoding will depend on whether the distinction makes a difference in terms of the theorctical models under study.

The cryptarithmetic protocol of Subject 3, analyzed in detail by Newell and Simon (1972), provides many examples of the possibilities of canonical recoding. $\mathrm { F o r }$ instancc, in about twenty instances where S3 draws an inference from the known numbers associaicd with a column in the problem to thosc still unknown, he gives a reason of the form "Premise (P) implics Conclusion $( \mathsf { C } )$ "These reasons are actually verbalizcd as $" \mathrm { P } .$ therefore $\mathbf { C } "$ (2 instances), $" \mathrm { P } .$ which will mean that $\mathrm { C } , \mathrm { " ~ } \mathrm { P } .$ which means that $\mathbf { C } " $ (2 instanccs), "P means that $\mathrm { C } ; { } ^ { " } { } ^ { " } \mathrm { P } ,$ which will makc $\mathrm { C } , \ " \ " \mathrm { P } .$ which leaves $\mathbf { C } , "$ "Since P, $\mathbb { C } ^ { \dag \dag }$ (2 instances), $\ " C$ bccause $\mathrm { P } "$ (5 instances), ${ } " \mathrm { P } .$ ,now $C , ^ { \mathfrak { w } } \ { } ^ { \mathfrak { w } } | \mathfrak { f } \ { } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } ,$ so C," "Suppose P, then $\mathrm { C } , \ " \ \ " \mathrm { P } ,$ S() $\mathbf { C } _ { \ast } ^ { \ast }$ and $" C .$ since $\mathrm { P } \because$ For all cxcept very spccial purposes, all of thesc cxpressions could be coded $" ( \mathbb { P }  \mathbb { C } )$ " or in somc other appropriate standard notation.

Completeness of Vocabulary. The protocol may now be scanncd to discovcr what statements cannot bc codcd in the language thus far dcfincd. Additional terms (c.g., goal cxpressions) may bc addcd to the list until as much of the protocol language as desircd has been accounted for. Expcricnce shows that, for rclativcly wcll-structured problem solving tasks, a small vocabulary (in thc dozcns and hundreds, not thousands) of terms, relations, and statement forms is sufficient to code more than ninety per cent of the language of a protocol. For cxample, the Towcr of Hanoi protocol we havc mentioned is about 2,000 words in length, but contains a vocabulary of only about 165 different words. A good rule of thumb for protocols of any considerable length is that the number of different words will be bctwcen five and tcn pcr cent of the length of thc text. Usually thc different words will be distributcd by frequcncy in a highly skcwcd way, following thc rank-frequcncy law of

Zipf (1949) and Yule (1924). This has the unfortunate consequence that about one-half of all the words that occur will occur only once in the text—a serious impcdimcnt to parsimonious cncoding.

Newell and Simon (1972) provide other cxamples of cncodings for task lik cryptarithmeticchss and ogic problems, Erisson (17) fr the Fight Puzzle. Sec Chapter 4 for additional references.

## Segmenting and Encoding Processes

For the actual cncoding, the protocol is scginented, cach seginent corresponding to a statement. If oral prose were completely grammatical, a statement would cssentially be a clause or a sentencc, but in normal spcech, statements are often abbreviated to phrases—cven to single words. Neverthcless, scgmentation is not usually difficult, and can be carricd out with high rcliability.

Coding the Segments. Fach segment is then cncoded. To the extent possible, it is coded from the information containcd in the scgment itself. When the segment is fragmentary, or contains anaphoric reference (c.g., pronouns, naming by description, and so on), context-preceding and following scgments—may nccd to be consulted to remove ambiguity, but for reasons that we will discuss later, the range of context used is kept as narrow as possible. Again, it has been found that coders who understand the task can code segments, using context, with rcliabilitics of .8 or .9. (l.ater in this chapter, inter-coder agreement will be discussed further.)

Usually the encodings of segments can be somewhat fornalized by using functional notation: R(x,y,..), wherc thc R's arc rclations, and the x, y, ctc., arguments. In fact, the wholc apparatus, including quantifiers, of the first order predicate calculus can somctimes bc used to good advantage. The relations in such an cncoding are generally dcrived from the vcrbs, prepositions, and adjectives of thc protocol, while their arguments often take the form of nouns and noun phrases. Thus, a statement likc, "I'll place 3 from A to C," might be cncoded as "Move(Disk 3; from A, to C)." or morc bricfly, "Movc(3;A,C)."

The amount of context that must be considered when encoding a statement is a function of both thc protocol and of the dcpth of the cncoding. Figure 6-1 shows a small scgment of thc Tower of Hanoi protocol mcntioned abovc, cncodcd at a rclativcly shallow level, and with slight usc of context. The statement, "29. I'll placc 3 from A to C," simply becoimes "Move(3;A,C)," no context bcing uscd to code this particular sentence.But 3. contains an implicit reference to 30, while 33 and 34, although containing four clauses, have to be cncoded as a u:"Because Goal(4 on B) & [Move(1;B,C) implis (Move(4 to B) impossible)]}, Movc(1;B,A)."

Prior Assumptions in Encoding. In encoding protocols of students solving thermodynamic fluid flow problems, Bhaskar and Simon (1977) made stronger assumptions, and cncodcd the protocol at a correspondingly decper level. Thcy assumcd that all protocol statcments could be encoded as onc of a small list of actions, likc: choose system, notice keyword, write cncrgy equation, revise cquation, find valuc of variable, solve cncrgy cquation, chcck units. Any statement in the protocol that could not be cncoded in terms of onc of thesc actions was retained as a comment. The actions, which were derivcd from a dctailcd task analysis and prcliminary examination of the protocols, generally accountcd for two-thirds or more of the protocol scgments in thesc rather highly structurcd tasks.

A third variant form of cncoding is illustrated by the detailed analysis in Ncwcll and Simon (1972), pages 165-183, of a scgment of a protocol. (A sample of this encoding is shown in Figure 6-2, bclow.)

These authors postulate thc form of the subject's production system, and scek to revcal in thcir cncoding not only the succession of knowledge statcs, but also the productions that were exccuted to move from onc knowledge state to the next.
