In this kind of encoding, thc cntirc postulated production system is taken as context for cncoding cach statement or short scgment of statements. The way in which encoding inferences can bc made and justificd is discusscd by the authors at lcngth.

The Use of Context. The use of context in coding protocols involves a tradcoff. On the one hand, interprcting protocol statements in terms of context permits a much larger part of thc scmantic content of the protocol to be retained than if contcxt is ignored. On the other hand, when context is uscd in intcrpretation, thc cvidence provided by each statement is no longer wholly indcpendent of thc evidence drawn from er statments (used to interpret the former). We will have more to say about this issue presently.

The extent to which a particular mode of coding requircs consideration of context can also be illustrated by the protocol on the Tower of Hanoi problem reported by Anzai and Simon (1979). The subject was making the actual moves in the problem on a large wooden Tower of Hanoi. She verbalized every move, without exception, and in relatively uniform language. She also verbalized soine of her goals, and some of the planning calculations that derived the next move from the current goal. A particularly clear passage from the beginning of her third trial of the problem is this one:

110. If it were five, of course, 5 will have to go to C, right?   
111. So, 4 will be at B.   
112. 3 will be at C.   
113. 2 will be at B.   
114. So 1 will go from A to C.

This is the beginning of a new trial, wherce Disk 5 cannot legally be moved. The situation, and the language (the conditional "if it were" and the "will have to") allow us to interpret statement 110 as describing a goal (G), and to encode it: (G:5 → C).

Since Disks 4, 3, and 2 also cannot bc moved in this situation, and because of the "so" in 11l, statements 111-113 are to be interpreted as a plan (P) for moves preparatory to moving Disk 5. They can be coded: (P:4→B), (P:3→C), (P:2→B). Now, with another "so," the subject deduccs that the next move should bc: 114. (M:1→C), and actually makes this move.

The protocol at the corresponding point in the fourth trial reads as follows:

Here the subject announces immediately the rcsult of her means-ends analysis to determine her first move, and then explains her reasoning ("because"). Statements 165-169 correspond in one-one fashion to 110-114. Note that the "and then" of the planning statements is not to be interpreted temporally, but as meaning "and therefore"; the actual moves will be made in the opposite temporal order.

As already remarked, the planning statcrments can be distinguished from the moves because the former do not refer to legal moves. However, the coding is much easier (and unequivocal) if we consider the context of the whole bricf cpisode at oncc, instcad of cncoding the individual statements ignoring that context.

In the 52 statements that cover the third trial in this protocol, 31 can be cncoded as moves, 6 as goals, 10 as subgoals or plans, and 5 remain to be cncoded in some other way (imainly as cvaluations). In the 56 statements made during the fourth trial, 31 report moves, 5 goals, 8 plans, and there are 12 statements of other kinds. The statements of the first trial were less highly stylized (and correspondingly harder to cncode) than those of the later trials. There were more cvaluative statements during the first trial, and more rctrospcctive stateinents of reasons for moves (the latter perhaps being partly a conscqucnce of an carly prompt from the cxperimenter, "If you can, tell me why you placed it there.") Fifteen of the first 24 statements were evaluations or gave retrospcctive rcasons for moves.

## Automation of Encoding

To incrcase the reliability and validity of encoding, various efforts can be made to automate the cncoding process. We will mention two such systems here, PAS and SAPA. Both will be discussed in more detail later in the chapter.

The most ambitious stcps in this direction were taken by Newell and Waterman (1971, 1973) with the PAS-I and PAS-II systems. A less ambitious attempt at moving toward automation of coding is the SAPA (semi-automated protocol analysis) system of Bhaskar and Simon (1977). Whilc the PAS systems, afier the problem space has bcen defined and the protocol scgmented manually, analyze the protocol automatically, to producc a problem bchavior graph, SAPA is an interactive program, spccially tailorcd to cach task environmcnt, that supports the human coder in analyz.ing successive protocol scgments.

A major advantage of automatic cncoding is that it requires all of the underlying assumptions, the vocabulary and the in ferencc rules, to be defincd cxplicitly and applicd consistently. Hencc, it makes clear exactly what assumptions have cntered into the cncoding. Reliability is perfect (given the samc protocol, the system will always reach the same interprctation), and the robustness of the cncoding to changes in the underlying vocabulary and rules can bc tested directly.

The loop is completed by testing the cncoded protocol against the trace of a computer program that purports to provide a thcory of the subject's behavior. Trace and protocol can bc expressed in precisely the same formal language, so that there is nothing problematic about the comparison. Notice that the test is really simultaneously a test of the mutual consistency of the problem space definitions, the inference rules for encoding, and the thcory of the subject's behavior.Misfits between protocol and trace may be attributable to any of these sources.This is exactly the situation we cncounter in any theory testing, where a failure of data to fit thcory perfectly may bc duc to crrors of observation, of instrument calibration, of approximation (if wc lcave "unimportant" variables out of the thcory), of incorrect auxiliary hypotheses (c.g., incorrect hypotheses about the operation of instruments), or of the thcory itscf. What we are always testing, when we confront a theory with data, is the mutual consistency of all of the presuppositions (including the theory itself) that underlie the comparison.

Semi-automated protocol analysis does not remove all of the clements of subjectivity that a completcly automated procedure does. However, cxperiments with seni-automatic protocol analysis using SAPA shows: (a) that it cnhances the rcliability with which coding can be carried out; (b) that at lcast in tasks having fairly dcfinite structure imposcd by the task cnvironment, thc assumptions built into the program do not significantly distort the cncoding; and (c) that it makes the task considcrably casier for the human coder. Rcaders who have had actual expericnce in cncoding verbal protocols will not underestimate the importance of this last point. In particular, by focussing the coder's attention on individual segments, onc at a timc, and cucing possible interprctations, an interactive system for protocol analysis permits sustained attention to the coding task over much longer strctches of time than are tolerable without it.

## Level of Analysis

'The kind of statement-by-statement encoding we havc bcen discussing so far retains most of the semantic contcnt of the protocol. For many purposes this is desirablc, or even indispcnsable. For cxamplc, if the goal is to compare thc protocol with a dctailed computer simulation of the bchavior, than as detailcd a matching as possible betwcen program trace and protocol may be desired.

On thc other hand, the full protocol also retains all of the idiosyncracics of individual behavior.If thc ain is to test thcory more globally—the commonalitics of bchavior, say, shared by a whole group of subjects-then it may be desired to cncode the protocol at a more aggregate level. What are some of the ways in which such aggregation can be accomplished?

Aggregation by Episodes. For the description of problem-solving protocols at an aggregate levcl. Newcll and Simon (1972) introduced the notion of problem-solving episodes. Ccrtain steps in solving a particular problem may be "obvious," other steps problematic. For cxample, solving a problem in physics may involve writing down the appropriate algebraic cquation, then solving it. If the cquation is simple and the subject skilled in algcbra, solving thc cquation, oncc discovered, may be non-problematic, to be summarized as a single step in thc problem behavior graph. The detail of the solution may be of no particular interest from a problem solving standpoint beyond demonstrating that the subject has mastered the basic algorithms of algebra.

Gcneralizing from this cxamplc, a subject often organizes his problem solving efforts in terms of a hierarchy of subgoals. Once this hicrarchy has been identificd, the protocol can be condensed, working in bottom-up fashion, by collapsing into a single step the scgment of the protocol that relatcs to thc achicvement of a non-problematic subgoal—one that is reached by usc of a wcll-practiccd proccdure. The process that achieves the goal may be regarded as a macro-operator. For example, a subject skilled in algebra may have a wcll-practiced procedure for solving lincar algcbraic cquations in one unknown, so that the details of his solution process afford him no difficulty and are of no psychological interest. In that case, "solve lincar cquation" can be cncoded as a single macro-opcrator that takes the subject to the ncw knowledge state containing the solution of the cquation.

An application of the cpisoding technique to the analysis of cryptarithnctic protocols at a rclativcly abstract levcl may bc found in Newell and Simon (1972), pp. 282-309. Here the aggregated protocols revealed very clcarly the differences among the problem spaces in which different subjccts wcre working, and how these differences in problem spaces affected the way in which subjects accumulated knowledge as thcy worked through the problem behavior graph. The cpisodes also revcalcd clearly the common mcans-cnds analysis hcuristic that almost all of the subjects were using to organize thcir efforts.

Aggregation by Solution Steps. In a study comparing the problemsolving processes of cxperts and novices solving kincmatics probleins in physics, a similar summarizing technique was uscd to contrast the working-forward solution paths of the cxperts with the workingbackward solution paths of the noviccs (Simon & Simon, 1978). This difference could be cxtracted objectively and reliably from the protocols by recording as an cquation cach cvocation of a principle of physics and its instantiation in terms of th data of the problm.

For example, "If the average spced was 200 meters per second, and the barrel is half a meter,  then it would have to be 1/400 of a sccond," could be coded as:

$$
1 / \mathrm { V ^ { * } } = \mathrm { T } \left( . 5 / 2 0 0 = 1 / 4 0 0 \right)
$$

where I. is length, $\mathrm { V } ^ { \star }$ is average vclocity, and T is time.

Then cach protocol can be encoded in terms of the scquence of equations cach subject evoked and instantiated, and these scquences can be compared between subjects.

Aggregation by Processes. Somctimes a number of consecutive protocol scgments refer to the cxccution of a single major process by the subject, and this whole set can be given a single encoding. For example, a major process in thc cryptarithmctic task is to process a column, taking known information and substituting it for lctters in thc column in order to derive in formation about other letters. "Each I) is 5; thercfore, T' is zero" —obtained by processing thc column that reads: I)+l)=T. The principal information that is verbalized by subjccts when processing a column is the identity of the column being processed, the information already available about it (input information), and the ncw information derivcd (output information). This information could be cncoded in some such form as: $\operatorname { P C } ( 1 ; 5 + 5 = " 1 ^ { \cdot } )  " \operatorname { I } = 0 , \operatorname { c } ( 2 ) = 1$ , wherc c(2) designates the carry to column 2.

Newell and Simon's Subject 3 is very consistent in providing in cach casc of processing a column most of the information indicated above. In 36 scgments each of which was an instancc of PC, thc number of the column being attended to was mentioned 34 times. Some 37 input quantitics were mentioned, and 21 omittcd; 43 output quantitics wcre mentioned, and 5 omitted. In four cases where a contradiction was cncountered, ncither input nor output quantitics wcre mcntioned. Of the 21 unmentioncd inputs, 8 werc carrics (of 1 or 0) and 10 wcre values of the letters I). R, or E, all of which had remaincd constant for some time. Of the 5 unmentioned outputs, 4 were carrics. Hence, by coding cach instance of PC as a single segment, and without using any context outside that segment, almost complete information could bc obtaincd from the protocol about the information to which the subject had to attend in ordcr to carry out thc process and use its rcsult.

Individual Differences. As the cxamples cited show, aggregating protocols is an effective techniquc for studying individual differences different subjects. It also suggests a comment on thc common complaint that descriptions of problem solving gleancd from protocols cannot be gencralized.

The difficultics of gencralizing about problem solving from data obtained from individual protocols arc not to be regarded simply as annoyances associated with the method of protocol analysis. The varicty in problem-solving processes is a psychological fact: Human beings differ widely in the knowledge they possess and the knowledge they evoke and apply in particular problem-solving situations. "Smcaring over" these genuine individual differences by aggregating data docs not destroy their reality; what it destroys is a large part of the in formation contained in the original data. If human beings use different strategics in solving the same problem, we cannot validate laws of behavior that assume the stratcgics to be all the sane. Psychological invariants must be consistent with, and not dismissive of, this varicty in human bchavior.

## METHODOLOGICAL ISSUES

In scicntific work we would like to scparate "facts" from the theories that might be generated to explain or account for them. The term data is usually used to denote the factual information cmploycd for tcsting thcorics. According to thc conventional wisdom, data, properly gathered, must be accepted by all scientists; theorics nced not be, for the same data might bc cxplained in scveral different ways. But matters are not quite so simple. Wc have already sccn that various sorts of theorctical commitments must bc made in the process of gathering and recording data, and all data arc consequcntly tainted by thcory. For example, thcory-bascd decisions must bc made as to what kinds of data are relevant, hencc worth gathcring. Obscrvations arc made with instruments, which arc designcd on thc basis of thcory. In thcse and other ways, thcory crceps into the gathering and recording of data.

Still, wc wish to preserve the greatest possible distance between data and theory, so that data can bc uscd to test theorics with a minimum of bias, pro or con, from preconceptions. What wc must strive for is a kind of bootstrap opcration. In designing our data-gathcring schemnes, we make minimal essential thcoretical commitments, then try to use the data to test stronger thcories. In fact, that is thc strategy wc have followed in this volume with respect to our theory of protocol analysis. On the basis of weak assumptions about short-term memory, we were able to interpret the available experimental data on verbalization, to check whether these wcak assumptions are tenablc, and to strengthen the intaocpl theory tevlizatin s.

## Translating Behavior Into Data

Coombs (1964) has discussed the interdependence of theory and data in an illuminating way, and has analyzed how the observable aspects of psychological phenomena can bc converted into data. The scquence of opcrations he describes for deriving data from traditional psychological measurements is fully parallel to the scquence for deriving data from recordings of verbal bchavior. Coombs (1964) starts the cycle with a theory that already cxists prior to any collection of observations. The theory delimits which aspccts of of potential obscrvations should be recorded and become available for further processing. Potential observations outside the limits will simply bc ignored.

Coombs describes what might be called thcory-driven science. Scicntific inquiry may also bc driven by data. Thcory should not preclude the scicntist from scarching for ncw phenomena, or from paying serious attention to phenomena he hits on adventitiously. Many important scicntific discovcrics have been made in this way, with little prior guidance or disciplinc from theory. As a sccond stcp, thc observations that have been rccorded are mapped onto the language of the thcory. If thc thcory is sufficicntly wcak, it will allow the cxpression of alternativc hypotheses (alternativc strong subthcories). We will use the term "data" to refer to the rcsult of this mapping process, which cncodes raw obscrvations in the language of theory.

It is not hard to describe the corresponding steps in analyzing verbalizations. When a tape recorder or vidco recorder is uscd to capture bchavior, a great dcal of information about the subject is irretricvably lost (c.g., his blood pressure, EEG pattern, and many other potentially observable phenomena). Transcribing rccordcd tapes involves a further sclection of information, for thc voicc inflections, for cxample, are usually ignored, or imperfectly cncoded in punctuation. Thc more difficult problems arisc, howcver, in cncoding the transcripts into the languagc of theory. Roughly, wc can describe the cncoding process as follows:

## Input→ Encoding→ Output

The input, given explicitly, corresponds to one or more units of the transcript. These units can be individual words, scqucnces of words separated by pauses, phrases, sentences, or cven complete protocols.

The output must bclong to an a priori, explicitly givcn, set of alternative cncodings. To minimize the contamination of data by ad hoc theory, the alternatives should bc cxplicated prior to accepting input for encoding. Encoding decisions arc bascd on somc spccific body of knowledge and proccdurcs hcld by thc cncodcr, whcthcr man or machinc. In the idcal casc, the cncoding would be done by a computer program, for thcn wc would know cxactly what knowledge and procedures wcre bcing used, and with what theorctical entailments. In the case of the human coder, too, it would bc highly desirable to be able to specify what inferences and knowledge arc uscd to reach the cncoding decisions. The definition of the cncoding categorics should be clear and explicit. The theoretic and practical problems of using natural language definitions in science is discusscd in depth by Mandler and Kessen (159). As wc have alrcady shown carlier in this chapter, it is possible (and, of course, desirable) to base the cncoding categorics on a formal task-analysis in terms of a spacc of knowledge states and a set of opcrators. Among thcir many advantages, such cxplicit and formal definitions of encoding categorics will hclp incrcase coding reliability.

## Context-Free Encoding

The cntanglement of data with theory is reduced and simplificd to the extent that cach output is independent of thosc that precede and follow it. In order to sccure complcte indcpcndence the encoding operations for cach output would have to be distinct, and would not refcr to the samc recorded observations. When the encoding is donc by humans, such independencc is almost impossible to attain, for coders may recall information from prcvious inputs to bias their cncodings of subscquent inputs.We have already adverted to this problem above and will discuss itin more detail later  this chapter.Thee rather enral and absac remarks can uscfully be illustrat by a few cxamples.

Memory-span Task. In the memory-span task, subjects are instuc to repeat back a scris of vcrbal symbols (. digits) in acly the order in which they were presented. If a complete match is attained, teu  "."  .T result of a sequcntial match betwcen the input string and the recorded verbalized response string.

Free Recall of Lists.Free recall of ists (c lists o wors) requires a slightly more complex cncoding opcration. The recorded verbalizations may be of thrce kinds: (1) words on the stimulus list; (2) incorrect likc, "lct me scc," "I am pretty sure thcre was an animal name on the list," or the like.

Nobody scriously questions that distinctions among these three kinds of verbalizations can be cncoded reliably and veridically by people. Yet a computer program capable of discriminating betwcen the second and third categories would be distinctly non-trivial.

Story Recall. Encoding of verbalizations becomes still more complex if the task is to recall texts or storics. The meaning of a text is not captured uniqucly in a specific string of words, but can bc expressed, at lcast approximately, in a large number of different ways. For purposes of encoding, it is necessary to have a thcorctical framework within which the semantics of the text can bc characterized.

Suppose that the meaning of the text is represented by propositions (Kintsch, 1974). Prior to cncoding, the stimulus text is analyzed into a sct of propositions that convcy its mcaning. Then, during cncoding, the verbalizations of the subject are convertcd into propositions, and these are compared for semantic cquivalence with the propositions previously derived from the text by the experimenter. In most applications of this coding mcthod, thc number of propositions successfully identified as matching text mcanings is counted, and propositions that cannot be matched arc not evaluated.

## Other Issues

From our discussion to this point, we can idcntify a number of issues that should bc discusscd in morc depth. First, there is thc sclcction of the information that is to bc includcd in thc raw data, and thc further winnowing of this information in thc preparation of the transcript. At the samc timc, the information must be scgmented, to dctermine the units that are to be input to the cncoding proccss. These issucs will be discussed in the next section.

'That discussion will be followcd by a consideration of how protocol analysis must be designcd to preserve, as far as possible, the degrees of frecdom in the raw data from contamination by thcory and cxpectations generated from the sane data. In the next main section of the chapter, we will discuss potential biases of the cncoding processes themselves.

## Selection of Information for Coding

There are many charactcristics that are desired in a transcript. The transcript should be usable by investigators with differcnt thcories and rescarch foci. Hencc, transcription should not rely on theoretical assumptions that are controversial or not generally shared by the relevant investigators. All information that is important to subsequcnt cncoding into the language of different theorics should bc preserved in the transcript.

Our discussion here is limited to situations where a single pcrson is performing a task, and does not include those where two or more persons arc working togcther, as in reaching a dccision (Paync, 1976). Wc also ignore the influence of the experimenter on the subject. Two-person interaction, cach person providing stimuli for the other, prescnts many additional problems we do not wish to dcal with hcrc. (Scc Ochs (1979) for a discussion of parent-child interactions.)

Even though most cognitive tasks clicit relativcly simple bchavior and occur in rather static perceptual cnvironments, not all of the rclevant information can be rctaincd if the transcription task is to be manageable. Through an analysis of the task itsclf, the information about bchavior and cnvironment that is relevant to task performancc can usually be defincd fairly wcll. Information about the physical cnvironment, especially if the stimulus is partly or wholly visual, is important and sometimes difficult to capture adequatcly. It is important bccause it is part of the definition of the stimulus. It is difficult to capture bccause such visual stimuli arc oficn not casy to describe verbally, and because the subject's rcfcrenccs to thcsc stimuli arc oftcn not casy to disambiguate without a rather complete record of the information that is pcrccptually available.

Cognitive tasks vary widcly in the amount of task-rclevant information that is available perccptually and in the stability or change of this information while the task is being performcd. Many tasks (chess, Tower of Hanoi) employ boards or other physical devices that are altered by the subject's moves.Other tasks, likc mathematics and physics problems, are presented visually and allow the subject to use paper and pencil. For tho problems that us a stat spla  infrmation t is t y to rccord changes in the cnvironment in the transcription of bchavior. Purcly mental problems, likc mental multiplication with oral presentation of the problem, force the subject to kecp the necessary in formation in memory. Similarly, for rctrospcctive reports, sincc the original stimulus will no longer be available, thc problem as prescnted should usually provide a sufficicnt context for interpreting the transcriptions.

The verbalizations of adults can gencrally be transcribed into ordinary words. Dictionary words may not adequately describe the verbalizations of young children (Ochs, 1979). However, cven in adult specch several aspects of the recorded verbalizations, such as intonation patterns and pauses, are not casily transcribed. Furthermore, cncoding the stress and duration of cach word and pause is tremendously timeconsuming and costly. Hence, it is important to make a thcoretically sound and uncontroversial abstraction from the raw protocol that allows reliable coding and captures the relevant information for subsequent coding opcrations.

A rather similar problem is cncountered in describing eyc movements. The direction of the gaze can be rccordcd continuously, so that it becomes important to find a timc interval over which to integratc, in order to make thc data manageablc. Becausc, for static displays, the eycs movc by sudden discrete saccades, thc scqucnce of dircctions of gaze betwcen saccades and the duration of cach of the intervals can be taken as the basic mcasurcments (Winikoff, 1967).

In our model of thc production of verbalizations, wc carlicr proposed that thcy should also bc unitized by spccch bursts. Only the durations of longer pauscs nced bc rccorded, since thcsc may indicate periods whcn thc subjcct is not obcying thc instructions to vcrbalize. Specch bursts provide only a partial solution to thc cncoding problem, however, for thcy are not always large cnough units for cncoding and assessing theorctical terms. Encoding may thcrcfore have to be based on larger, syntactic or even semantic, units.

## "Contamination" of Data by Theory

Beauty may lie in the cye of thc bcholder, but it is a major goal of scicntific mcthod that truth should lie in the data — should be objective rather than subjective. As applied to verbal data, what does this requirement of objectivity mean?

It cannot mcan that data are collected and encoded without regard to cxisting thcory. We have already scen why that cannot and should not be done. A single verbal protocol is not an island to itsclf, but a link in a whole chain of evidence, strctching far into the past and the future, that gradually develops, molds, and modifics our scicntific theorics. It needs to be processed with full attention to these linkages.

The process must be viewcd as a repcated cycle (or helix, if we believe there is constant progress along the axis). At any given time certain concepts and propositions are given credence as providing more or less plausible accounts of a body of phenomena. These concepts and propositions may constitute a single theory, or a collection of competing theories, or even a hierarchy of thcories, from very general (but weak) to very spccific (but strong), and from highly probable to barcly plausible. In the liniting, but not infrcqucnt, casc, data may cxist that cxhibit clear regularities without any current theory to describe or explain them. Such regularitics often lcad to the induction of new theorics (Simon, 1angley, & Bradshaw, 1981).

The cxisting thcories suggest new observations and experiments to be made, cither with the aim of choosing between altcrnative hypotheses, or of strengthening (or weakening) the credibility of a spccific theory when there are no competitors, or of improving and cxtending an existing theory. If existing thcorics explain only part of the phenonena, with a good dcal of residual "crror variance" or "noise," as is almost always the casc, then the new observations may introduce new variables and mcasurcments, soinctimes dcpending on ncw instrumentation, in an effort to account for the unexplained variance.

Power and Parsimony. The "quality" of a particular thcory in the face of a body of cvidence must be judged by the rclation betwcen the amount of variancc the thcory cxplains and the number of degrees of frecdom (parameters) requircd to fit thc data. Exactly what figure of merit, what function of variancc cxplained and dcgrees of freedom cmployed, should be uscd to cvaluate thcorics is a dcep problem in the modern thcory of statistics, and there is at prescnt no consensus on the precise answer. But cveryonc docs agrce that "power" and "parsimony" are the important characteristics of a successful theory, and that there is some tradeoff betwcen these two measures. If the power of a theory is cnhanccd by adding ncw variables or paramcters, its parsimony is decrcased; if it is simplificd to incrcase parsmony, powcr will usually be lost.

T power in exchange for very small losses in parsimony. On rare occasions, new formulations are found in somc domain that greatly incrcase power without loss of parsimony. These are the true "scientific revolutions" of which so much has been written in recent years.

In the literature of statistics, most attention has bcen paid to the situation whcre two thcorics are compcting to cxplain the same body of data. If the two theorics are cqually parsimonious, have the same numbers of degrces of frecdoin, then obviously the most powerful is to be preferrcd. If one is more parsimonious than the other, then some tradcoff ratio can be used to imake the choicc. The more complex, and we bclicve, the more common case is one in which only one theory has been put forth, and the task is to cvaluate it in the light of new evidence. To scc how this might be done, wc must makc an cxcursion into Bayes' Theorem.

A Bayesian Framework. Even though wc scldom havc available adcquate data to apply it numcrically, Baycs' Thcorem provides us with a framework within which wc can cxamine morc closcly the issues of power and parsimony. We will not try to attach a spurious quantification to our argument, but simply usc Bayes' Thcorem as a qualitativc guide.

The credence wc placc in a hypothesis, say $\mathrm { H } _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ on the basis of all of our previous knowledge and cvidence is called, in Baycsian thcory, its $a$ priori probability, which we will denote by ${ \sf P } _ { 0 } ^ { } ( { \sf H } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ When we analyze new data and throw it into the balancc, revising this initial probability for cach of the hypotheses we are entertaining, wc calculate a ncw probability, thc posterior probability, for each hypothesis. We will call this $\mathrm { P _ { \mathrm { 1 } } ( H _ { \mathrm { i } } ) }$ Bayes' Thcorem provides a rule for calculating these latter probabilities.

To make this calculation, we must also know the probability that cach hypothesis would produce the data actually obscrvcd. This probaby, calld the produciv probabiliy of the hypoth or the ata, will be denoted by $\mathrm { P _ { n } ( D ; H _ { i } ) }$

Consider two ürns (hypotheses) containing four balls cach. The first has thrce white and one black ball; the sccond one white and three black balls. Matters are arranged so that it is cqually likcly that we will draw a ball from cither urn. (The $a \ : / \gamma i o r i$ probability of cach hypothesis is $1 / 2 . )$ We draw a ball. and find that it is white. What is thc posterior probability that it was drawn from the first urn? Sincc, in this simple casc, the probability of drawing cach of thc balls is cxactly the same, and since therc are thrce white balls in the first urn and only one in the sccond, the answer is obviously 3/4. More gencrally, and for less simple cases, Bayes' Thcorem gives the posterior probability that the ball came from the first urn $( \mathsf { H } _ { 1 } ^ { } )$ was correct) as:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \mathrm { P } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { \phantom { } } } ( \mathrm { H } _ { 1 } ^ { \phantom { } } ; \mathrm { W } ) { = } [ \mathrm { P } _ { \mathrm { \ p } } ^ { \mathrm { \phantom { } } } ( \mathrm { W } ; \mathrm { H } _ { 1 } ^ { \phantom { } } ) \mathrm { P } _ { 0 } ^ { \phantom { } } ( \mathrm { H } _ { 1 } ^ { \phantom { } } ) ] / } & { { } } \\ { \mathrm { [ P } _ { \mathrm { \ p } } ^ { \mathrm { \phantom { } } } ( \mathrm { W } ; \mathrm { H } _ { 1 } ^ { \phantom { } } ) \mathrm { P } _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { \phantom { } } } ( \mathrm { H } _ { 1 } ^ { \phantom { } } ) { + } \mathrm { P } _ { \mathrm { \ p } } ^ { \mathrm { \phantom { } } } ( \mathrm { W } ; \mathrm { H } _ { 2 } ^ { \phantom { } } ) \mathrm { P } _ { 0 } ^ { \phantom { } } ( \mathrm { H } _ { 2 } ^ { \phantom { } } ) ] } & { { } } \\ { { = } [ 3 / 4 \mathrm { \ x } \mathrm { ~ } 1 / 2 ] / \left[ 3 / 4 \mathrm { \ x } \mathrm { ~ } 1 / 2 + \right]} \mathrm { 1 } / 4 \mathrm { \ x } \mathrm { ~ } 1 / 2  =  & { { } 3 / 4 } \end{array}
$$

Of course the formula can be generalized to an arbitrary sct of hypotheses. Then the denominator simply becomes the sum, over all of the hypotheses, of the products of thcir productive probabilitics for the obscrved data by thcir prior probabilitics.

When we are dealt a hand of thirtcen spades in bridge, wc have to choosc betwcen the hypothesis that the deck has been well shuffled, and the hypothesis that the dealer has playcd a trick on us. The productive probability of the first hypothesis (the hypothesis that a well-shuffled deck will produce a deal of thirtcen spades to onc player) is very low, but the prior probability—since wc have known our fricnd, the dcaler, as an honest person — is quite high. On the othcr hand, the productive probability of the sccond hypothesis (that a trickster will deal out a hand all of onc suit) is quitc high, although its prior probability is low. Bayes' Thcorem tells us what credence to put in our fricnd's honesty after the deal, on the basis of thesc probabilitics. The productive probabilities can be computed from the thcory of combinations, but the prior probabilitics must be bascd on our previous cxpericnce with human nature in general and our friend in particular.

Implications of Bayes' Theorem for Coding. What does Bayes' Theorem have to tell us about the problems of recording, cncoding, and interpreting verbal data? First, it has something important to say about the vencrable problem of prediction versus postdiction. What difference does it make whether wc announce a hypothcsis first, then gather and analyzc data to tcst it, or whcther wc gather the data and inducc the hypothesis from it? The rcccived wisdom is that the hypothesis is to be given grcater crcdencc in the first case than in the second. But is this correct?

We can apply Baycs' Thcorem thus. Before we gathered the new data, the hypothesis in question (whcther enunciated or not) had a certain prior probability. The hypothesis also has a definite productive probability for the new data. The same may be said for any other hypotheses that had been or could have been generated. Hence we can clat  os abil y  l wa the time when it was generated simply docs not enter into the calculation (Simon, 1977).

But the common wisdom on this matter is not wholly indefensible. Assume that hypotheses are constructed by some process, which we wil refer to as the generator. The generator will produce hypotheses on the basis of the information available to it, and presumably will be so designed as to produce hypotheses with high prior probabilitics (in the light of this information) before it produces hypotheses with low probabilities. If the gencrator has produced a particular hypothesis before ccrtain new data wcre available, then it is rcasonable to suppose that the prior probability of that hypothesis was substantial. If, however, the generator is given the ncw data as wcll, and allowcd to run until it produces a hypothesis compatiblc with thesc data, it may put forward candidates that would have been assigned low priors before the ncw data were gathered. Hencc, we cannot really assumc cqual prior probabilities in the two cases. This is presumably the basis for our greater confidence in a hypothesis that has made successful predictions, than in one that has been constructed with the data it has to fit alrcady in vicw. The former generally comes to the ncw data with a highcr prior probability than the latter.

This does not in any way imply, however, that data gathered prior to thc formulation of a hypothcsis has no probative value for the hypothesis. Even though we had not considered the possibility that our fricnd was a trickster bcfore wc pickcd up our hand of thirtcen spades, and only formulated the hypothesis at that moment, the fact that we were dealt that hand markedly incrcases the probability of the hypothesis from its previous levcl, and its posterior may now be quite largc as a result of this evidencc, and even in the face of a vcry low prior. Thc common vicw that hypotheses must be formulatcd before cxpcriments are run in order for the data to have value as cvidence is simply false. However, how much credence we should place in them on the basis of the cvidence may depend on whether or not thcy were thought of first.

In the use of protocol data, it is a common practicc, and often a necessary one, to cxtract the coding categorics and procedures from a study of the protocol itself. The model of the process that we wish to test may be constructed after cxamination of thc same data. These proccdures obviously consume some of the degrccs of frecdom of the

## 284 Protocol Analysis

data, but if the coding categorics and procedures are relatively simple and highly objective, and if thc model to be tested is parsimonious in rclation to the volume of data to be cxplaincd, then therc is nothing circular about proccding in this way. A good fit of model to data still contributes to the posterior probability of the model, and the more parsimonious the model and procedures, and the more voluminous the data, the grcater the increment in the credibility of the model.

In the same way, it is often necessary, when coding protocol segments, to take into account a wider or narrower context of cach segment. Again, this practice reduces the degrees of freedom in the data, but does not, in any absolute sense, compromise the coding procedure as a way of testing modcls of the data.

Necessity should not be made a virtue. Data cannot be uscd twice as cvidence in Bayes' formula-first in calculating the prior probability, and again in calculating the productive probability of the hypothesis being tested. Hence, when degrees of freedom in thc data are absorbed by thcir usc prior to testing, thcir absorption should be taken into account in the testing process. In general, forimal statistical methods for doing this are not available, but conccptually, the case is clear.

Morcover, a given body of data will provide stronger evidence for or against a model if the coding categories and procedures have already been constructed from analysis of the task domain, or from previously gathered protocols of other subjects, and if the model has also been formulated on the basis of other cvidence.

I.ct us now return to the situation, introduced in the previous section, where we are not choosing between alternative hypotheses, but have at most one theory to fit our data. Without alternative hypotheses, wc can not apply Bayes' formula; but wc can imagine a surrogate hypothesis, with very low prior and productive probabilities for the data, to represent all of thesc unknown alternatives.Suppose that the hypothesis we are considering (whcther invented before or after wc saw thc data) explains the data very successfully and parsimoniously.Then its productive probability for the data will be high. Examining Bayes' formula, we sce that sincc thc remaining terms in the denominator will be closc to zero, numerator and denominator will be nearly cqual, with the conscquence that the posterior probability of our hypothesis will be close to unity.

We do not wish to push this conclusion too far. But it does illustrate how data can provide credibility to a single hypothesis, cven in the absence of cxplicit alternatives with which to compare it. Moreover, the argument again docs not depend on whcther the hypothesis was generated before or after the data were produced.

Disconfirmation of Theories. Contrary to the doctrine of the Popperians, there is no "sudden death" of thcorics. Since observations have to be cncoded and interpreted, using cxisting theorctical knowledge for that purpose, before they can confront thcorics, the failure of a theory to fit observations preciscly docs not mean that it has to be rejected outright. Perhaps the fault docs not lic in thc theory, but in some of the auxiliary, mcthodological, and theorctical assumptions that have been made in thc fitting process. Classical Newtonian mechanics was not rejected simply bccause soine plancts deviatcd from the cxpected Newtonian paths. Instcad, the implicit assumption in the calculations, that there wcre no unobservcd planets, was tentativcly rejected, and a search undertaken for bodics that might account for the deviations. Twice the thcory was\_saved in this way (by thc discoveries of Neptune and Uranus); on thc third occasion (cxplanation of the precession of the perihclion of Mercury), it gave way to a compcting thcory, General Rclativity, that cxplaincd the phcnomcnon without postulating a new planet.

'The case of the overthrow of a thcory by a completely new onc is dramatic, but comparativcly rare. More often, unexplaincd variance is dcalt with by secking new variables (as in the Newtonian casc) to account for it. Hence the argument for a theory, that it explains a good deal of variancc parsimoniously, is usually morc important than thc argument against it, that it leaves much variance unexplaincd—unless, of coursc, there is a competing thcory that docs a better job. The practice of science cxcmplifics the practical wisdom of politics that "you can't beat something with nothing."

General Lessons for Methodology. The parsimony of a thcory is as hard to cvaluate as its power. Degrccs of frecdom arc uscd up not only in estimating the parameters of the modcl from cmpirical data, but also in the process of encoding and intcrprcting the data. Even in a simple physical case, the mcasuremcnt of tempcraturc, say, a grcat many degrees of frcedom lurk in the procedures for calibrating thc thcrmomcters and recording the temperature in the expcrimental situation. Since the contemporary experimentalist docs his work against an cnormous background of hcat mcasurement and instrument calibration by his prcdecessors, hc is provided with a standardized cnvironmcnt, and docs not "consume" degrces of frecdoim in fashioning his own instruments.

The casc is quite different in analyzing protocols for a new cognitive task, one that has not bcen studicd extensivcly in the laboratory. Large numbers of degrees of freedom may bc uscd up in the carly steps of data recording and data cncoding, with the result that the final interpretation of the data and its matching against the theory may appear to reflect mainly hindsight - pulling out of the data the hypotheses that have been implicitly hidden in it by the data interpretation process. This is the core of validity in the criticisms sometimes levcled against computer simulation of thinking, although it hardly justifics the cxtravagant and usually false claim that "If you are given a verbal protocol, you can always write a computer program that will simulate it."

How can the problem of parsimony be solved? The answer for verbal protocols seems to be exactly the same as the answer for any other kind of data. It has several components.

1. In choosing data for transcription, and in transcribing and encoding them, the theorctical commitments that are made should be kept as small and weak as possible. Wc have already cmphasized this principle throughout our discussion.

2. The thcorctical assumptions and presuppositions underlying the data analysis should bc made explicit. This is easy when thc analysis is donc automatically; difficult when it is done by humans.

3. The data analysis process should be not only objective and reproducible, but it should remain constant from one protocol to another. In this way, the dcgrecs of frecdoin that arc uscd by these proccdurcs arc absorbed by a constantly growing body of data. This can be donc by dividing the protocol material into two parts, developing the data anlysis scheme on one half, and testing it against the other half.

4. Convergent proccdures should be used wherever possible. For example, as much as possible of the cncoding language and concepts should be derived from an analysis of the task cnvironment, independent of the problem spaces defincd by the subjects.

5. Constant and invariant data analysis proccdures should be used over large bodis of data.It is much casicr to build a consistent ad hoc theory to explain a protocol of ll scgmcnts than onc of 200 segments.

6. Even where different thcories are needed to cxplain bchavior in different tasks, the thcorics should share as many common clements and coinponents as possiblc, so that a (wcaker) general theory can be abstracted from the spccific ones.

7. Even where different theorics are needed to explain the behavior of different subjcts in a givcn task, thc theorics should also share as many common clements and components as possible, so that a theory of the "representative subject" (the American college sophomore?) can be constructed.

Examples as Evidence. Protocol analysis sometimcs consists largely of extracting cxamples of concepts or processes from the verbalization as evidencc for theories that predict them. As Bayes' Theorem shows us, the contribution of such cxamples to the credibility of a theory depends on what alternative explanations are available. The recent mcthodological disputc about language acquisition in apcs is an interesting casc in point. Much of thc important evidence for apes' ability to combine signs creatively into novel constructs comes from anecdotes (Scidenberg & Petitto, 1979). For example, the chimpanzce Washoc combined the signs for "water" and "bird" to rcfer to a duck, and thc gorilla Koko combined the signs for "cookie" and "rock" for a stale cookie. Out of context, these episodes scem to yicld rather strong cvidence for thc creative use of language by apcs. However, Seidenberg and Petitto (1979, p. 182) point out that the water bird example is less persuasive if the ape also combined water and bird with other signs (c.g., water shoe, water banana, cookie bird, etc.). A chimpanzce named Nim (Terrace et al., 1978) combined signs into many such permutations. Although thc combinations are not all idiomatic, there is no way to tcll whethcr thcy were mcaningful to the animal; thcy may havc bcen random.

## RELIABILITY AND VALIDITY OF ENCODING

A central task in using verbally rcportcd information is to makc the encoding process as objective as possible. Without appropriate safcguards, the encoder, cxposed to a scrics of ambiguous verbal statements, may encodc thcm with a bias toward his own preferred interpretation.

Context provides a principal source of disambiguation of verbal information. Suppose a concept-formation expcriment in which a subject verbalizcs a hypothesis like:

## a dark ball

Without context, a coder might have great difficulty in dcciding what objects should be regardcd as instances of the concept defined by this phrase. Howcver, in thc context of a particular cxperiment, where all possible stimuli arc colorcd shapcs (dark bluc, ycllow, or white;

square, trianglc, rectangle, or circle), the samc hypothesis is relatively unambiguous, for it is synonymous with "bluc circle."

As a sccond cxample, in a problem-solving experiment, a subject says:

## I want to get 1 to its place.

If the cxperimenter knows the task the subject is performing, a coder can convert this verbal statement into an cxplicit goal. In the Eight Puzz.lc, for cxample, the subject is instructed to slide square tiles from a given configuration, likc thc onc shown in Figure 6-3 bclow, to a specificd goal configuration.

![](images/8be02a1cf35665e38fc156006cc5f66852d3f72b9d9acee915dcfd88a8d48f11.jpg)  
Figure 6-3  
Example of a Configuration for the Eight Puzzle (lefl); this is to be Transformed into a Goal Configuration (right)

In this context, the verbal statement is readily cncoded as the goal of getting the tile with the number l to the upper left-hand corner of the frame.

In these two examples, the disambiguation does not cven rcquire that other parts of the protocols bc cxamincd, but can be achicvcd by carcful task analysis prior to the coding of the protocols. The task analysis can idcntify, in thc first case, a spacc of possible concepts; in the second, a sct of problem configurations and goals. Mapping the subjects' verbalizations on these may then be quite objcctive and unproblematic.

Many of the same issues are involved in assuring the objectivity of cncoding as we discusscd in conncction with the objectivity of the verbal reports themsclves. Consider, for cxamplc, two cncoding situations. In both situations the cncoder knows that he is encoding verbalizations taken from a think-aloud protocol from a concept attainment cxperim.Intfrs siuaion,th s ask  avalizati statement of the correct hypothesis (c., "blue and round").The output from the encoding decision is "yes" or "no."In the sccond situation, the encoder is askcd to encode any hypothcsis that can bc stated as a combination of one of the thrce colors and one of the four shapes that occur in the task cnvironment. Now the cncoding can procced in a quite definite way from the in formation in the protocol.

A computer program called HEARSAY (Reddy & Newell, 1974), devcloped for automatic recognition of continuous human spcech, provides a uscful mctaphor for thinking about the cncoding problem. The spcech HEARSAY recognized consisted of instructions to make moves in a chess gamc. In order to identify what the spcaker said, HEARSAY used various types of information that were averaged together to determine the most likely utterance.

Onc type of information camc from the specch signal. Another type of information came from HEARSAY's chess knowledge of what moves wcre likcly (c.g., plausiblc moves) in the givcn chess position. If HEARSAY attached too much weight to its cxpectation of what move would bc madc, it would simply "hear" what it bclicvcd thc subject should have said. If HEARSA Y attended only to the speech signal, then, because of the variability of spccch sounds, it might bclicve (falscly) that the subjcct had chosen a highly implausible movc. The usc of context has both thc potcntial to improve the cncoding of vcrbal protocols by resolving ambiguitics, and the potential to bias the cncoding by relying too hcavily on prior cxpectations. Effectivc cncoding finds a middle pathway between these two extremes.

## Encoding Process and Encoders

Encoding is often cvaluated by tests of rcliability—agrcement betwcen different persons cncoding independently of each other. But rcliability is not cnough. In this section we will discuss some problcms and issucs that arisc cven whcn high inter-coder agrecment is achieved. We will be conccrned with biases toward the confirmation of hypotheses that have becn put forward, inferences of what thc subject must have said, rather than what he did say, and problems of restricting thc influcncc of previous cncoded in formation on subscqucnt coding decisions.

Independence of Encoding Judgments. After a protocol is divided into separate scgments, onc or more cncodings must bc generated for cach scgment. Wc arc interested in ways for climinating or greatly reducing the dependence among encodings of different segiments so as not to lose degrees of freedom in the data.

The problem arises bccause human coders remember their previous encodings, and other information about carlier scgments. T'raditionally, coding judgments are made about scgments in the order they werc verbalizcd. It is not uncommon for a subjcct to verbalizc an intention, like "I want to gct the first row in ordcr," or a conccpt like, "I think it must be small red squares," and then make later rcfcrence to it, likc, "I still think it is that," or "I am thinking about the samc hypothcsis." It would be incorrect to regard these subscquent verbalizations as wholly indcpendent cvidencc that the subject was hccding the hypothesis of "srall, red squares." The later vcrbalization is cvidencc that he is heeding the same hypothesis as before, but doesn't provide new evidcnce of what that hypothesis was. Not all instances of such cross refcrence are as explicit as this one, but they all pose cssentially the same problem.

A possible solution to this problem involves two things. During the segmentation, units should be defincd that are large cnough that all information for making an encoding dccision is contained in a single segment. For certain cncoding decisions. like assessing strategies, this may mean that the cntire protocol is a unit. I ater, wc will propose an altcrnative approach of cstablishing cncoding catcgorics that can bc asscssed from smaller segments.

Sccond, segments should be cncoded in random ordcr, thus climinating the possibility that the encoder will rely on information in preceding scginents to make subsequent decisions. computer-based support system for making such cncodings is described in the next section.

Bias and Inference. Two types of bias are of particular interest and concern. The first results from the encoders having prior knowledge of the hypotheses being tested. The sccond results from the cncoders assuming that subjccts will think in the same ways that they do.

With respcct to the first, the best situation is onc in which the coders are naive with respcct to thc cxpcrimental design and the hypotheses. Of course this is unimportant when the protocol information is very explicit and clcar, as it is in many problem-solving protocols. But for ambiguous and bricf protocol scgments, the danger of bias may be scrious, and inter-coder agrecment docs not mcasure coding valiity. In some recent studis (c. Waern, 197) explicit steps w taken to kecp the coders from knowing the design or the hypotheses.

The second type of bias arises because encoding often requires at least simple inferenccs, and assumes that the subjects made the same infercnces. In general, this is a quite valid assumption, uscful and even necessary for understanding other pcople's thought processes. Again, it becomes problematic when the coder is faced with an ambiguous or schematic statement. With in formation about the task, the encoder (as in our HEARSAY analogy above) may attribute to the subject the action or thought he regards as most reasonable under the circumstances. Several coders with similar backgrounds, yct different fromn thc background of the subject, could give the same incorrect interpretations. Even if the in ference is "corrcect," it is not determined by the rccorded verbalization. In thc next section we will discuss somc mcthods for restricting the inferential activity of the encoder.

## Mini Protocol Analysis System (MPAS)

This section describes an interactive computer program for encoding protocol segments. While the computer is not a nccessary component of the schemc, it facilitates presenting the protocol scgments to the coder, and also makcs thc cncodings directly availablc for further analysis on the computer.

The core idca of the schemc is to control the information uscd to cncode cach protocol scgment. Thcrc are two types of control. One aims at mceting thc criterion of independence of cncodings, and reduces the possibility of the coder relying on information from carlier protocol segments. The other excrts control over the cxtent to which the coder can infer plausible cncodings from previous knowledge, rather than relying on thc cxplicit protocol information.

MPAS presents to the coder scgments from a number of different protocols, sampled in a random order. When thc pool of segments to be samplcd is sufficicntly large, thc coder is unable to infer any rclation bctween the current segment and those prescntcd previously. In Figure 6-4, thc simple structurc of MPAS data organization is shown, togcther with an cxample of thc coder-computer intcraction. MPAS samples one of thc stored scgiments and prescnts it at thc computer terminal. Often, the scgment can bc codcd dircctly as presented, but if it is too small to provide sufficicnt information for cncoding, options in MPAS pcrmit the just preceding and succeeding scgments to bc gcncrated. Thus the context can bc gradually widencd as necessary, but information can be rctaincd as to how much context was used in cncoding.

## Encoding Reliability

We turn now to methods for cnsuring that the mapping of input to coding categories is rcliable and docs not vary among cncodcrs. In statistical terms, we wish to show that inter-coder agreement is bctter than chance, and also that the differences betwcen cncoders are not systcmatic.

Ordinarily, in tests of coding rcliability, coding catcgorics arc determincd in advancc. Wc are awarc of only onc exception, a study by Rasmussen and Jcnsen (1974), whcre the coders of protocols of subjects who wcre troubleshooting clectronic cquipment developcd their own coding categories. 'The two codcrs, who came to the task with agreedupon goals and a common thcoretic vicwpoint, gencrated quite similar categorics. (Ihere is no casy way to quantify the dcgrce of similarity.) T'here are also strik ing similaritics in the codes that different invcstigators have proposcd for the same tasks (scc Bettman & Park, 1979). Hence, it would sccm that coding catcgorics might be induccd reliably from the protocols themselves. Nevcrthcless, the studics discussed here all consider reliability in the context of fixcd, prcdctermined coding categories.

At least thrce variables play an important role in determining the consistcncy and rcliability of cncoders judgmcnts. The first is the extent to which thc cncoder must make inferences. (For cxample, cncoding a heeded thought requires fewer in ferences than cncoding the process that brought the thought to attention.) The sccond is the cxtent to which cach encoding is independent of the othcrs. Coding should be more reliable whcn cach segment is coded in terms of its own contcnt than when relations among seginents are takcn into account. We will first consider thc latter, more complicated, situations and then turn to the coding of individual scgments.

Encoding Complete Process Descriptions. The goal of protocol analysis has often bcen to generate a completc modcl of the problemsolving process. (Scc Ncwcll, Shaw, & Simon (1958) analysis of GPS modcl and protocol.) Alternatively, protocols have bccn cncoded into sequcnccs of interconnected states in a Problem Bchavior Graph (PBG). In thesc analyses, thcorctical assumptions must bc uscd to make inferences, supply missing information, and abstract protocol statcments to gencral rules. Thcrc are fcw studics whcrc intcr-coder reliability has been assessed systematically for this kind of encoding. The coding is extremcly costly in timc, and it is difficult to find voluntcers who are willing to duplicate it.

Haincs (1974) made an ambitious atteinpt to evaluate the rcliability of complete process descriptions derived from protocols. The thinkingaloud protocols wcre collected from subjects deciding to make purchases (c.g., personal clothing). Onc of thc protocols is rcproduccd as Figure 6-5. Haines used a decision trec to describe the cognitive processes. The decision tree model, commonly used in rescarch on consumer decision making, assumes that the subjccts makcs ordcred scrics of tests on the altenativs, which usually hav outcos of "yes" or "no.Depending o the test outcoc, the subject perfors further tests, rejects the item, or decides to purchase it.

Haines asked students in a computer scicnce class to derive decision trees for these protocols independently. A couple of thc students' decision trees arc shown in Figure 6-6.

From visual inspection, we can sce that there are considerable differences bctwcen the trecs. Using a quantitative mcthod devcloped by Bettman (1970), Haines (1974) dcrivcd a mcasurc of similarity. The decision trees wcre compared for cncoders analyzing the same protocol, and for a singlc cncoder analyzing different protocols. Haines found that only about half the time was there greater consistency among cncoders of a single protocol than among cncodings of different protocols by a single encoder. He concluded that authors should not publish decision trees without reporting the protocols from which they were dcrived, since the trees cannot be derived rcliably from the protocols.

Decision Trees Induced from Student Protocols

Haines' (1974) assumption that a single decision trcc underlies a subject's dccision processes is qucstionable. Iater in this chapter wc show how different cncoding procedures as well as different thcorctical idcas produce very high inter-rater agrcement. First wc wish to describe some studics that have tricd to capture the cntire cognitive process by encoding instances of complex subproccsses.

Encodings Based on Complete Protocols. In many studies only sclcctcd features of protocols arc cncoded. An cxample is a protocol obtaincd by Kilpatrick (1968) on the solution of an algcbra word problem, reported in thc study by Kilpatrick that was discussed carlier. From the protocols, Kilpatrck assessed instances where the gencral !curistics proposed by Polya (1957) wcre uscd. In thc following protocol Kilpatrick found, among other things, an instance cf "successive approxi:mation."

Subject P-10: "A barrel of honcy wcighs 50 pounds. The same barrcl with kcroscne in it wcighs 35 pounds. If honcy is twicc as hcavy as kcroscne, how much docs the cmpty barrcl wcigh?" Wcl, . first you havc to ah, find out what ah, kerosene wcighs, and the honcy wcighs, if it's twice that. And so, I have to find ah, onc numbcr to find the wcight of thc barrcl. So, say .. I takc some amount from both of thcm and this number would be half of that. So take, let's see .. (What are you thinking of?) Well, I'm trying to figure out a number that'd be, that you'd take away from both numbers and this would be half of. of this number. (What are you trying?) Wcll, first I'd probably try the cven numbers like 5, 10, 15, and so forth. it couldn't bc 5, becausc that'd make that, ah, 30. And, this, and you multiply 30 timcs 30 and you gct 60, and that's more than the total wcight already. So, then it'd havc to bc somethin', al, down by, like 20, or something likc, some-, a, a number cven lowcr. So you takc about, probably, oh, 25, somcthing likc that.... And, not 25, a little, Ict's say, 20. That'd be 15. And 20 from that would make that 30. So 15 timcs 15 is 30. So that'd makcs the, ah, ba-, the barrcl wcigh 20 pounds.

In Kilpatrick's (1968) procedure, there is no guarantee that two or more cncodings may not be extracted from the same protocol segment. Nor can one determinc precisely what information was used to make a particular cncoding. Bccause hc analyzcd a large number of protocol segments, Kilpatrick could address the methodological issues statistically. He computed the frequcncy of occurrence of cach hcuristic in cach subjects' protocols over cight problems. He then cvaluated these frequencies, and climinated data and encoding categories for which intr-rater reliabilitis were ow.An advantage o his procedurs is that the encodings can be made directly from the tape recoraings without transcription. There scens to be no objection in principle to this kind of encoding as long as thc information is aggrcgated for each problem.

Encoding Segments or Phrases. Many of the difficultics encountered in carlicr coding methods can be climinated by coding individual segments of protocols. It is casy to compare the cncodings of different raters and to report percentage agreement among them as an aggregate measure of rcliability. Later, we will discuss somc problems with such an aggregate measure.

In his study of choiccs betwcen pairs of gambles, Montgomery (1977) uscd ninc different cncoding categories. Fach category, coding a single statement, reflected a particular kind of comparison For cxamplc, "it is only 2:50 [Swcdish crowns] morc" was coded as a small difference (Sl), and "the chances of winning sccm to be cqually large" as cquality (EQ). Over 1,000 statements wcrc cncodcd by two raters with ovcrall agreement in 95% of the encodings.

Svenson (1974) asked subjects to sclect, for hypothetical purchasc, among descriptions of houscs. The houscs could bc described by values on a sct of attributes. Each utterance was cncodcd that cvaluated an attribute of a house or compared an attribute of different houses. About 90% agreement was reachcd by two coders in idcntifying attributes and valucs in 1,000 statements. Ncarly half (48%) of thc disagrecments related to evaluations that subjccts made with cxplicit hesitations.

In an even more extensive analysis, Bettman and Park (1979) took TA protocols from 68 subjects sclccting among fiftcen different microwavc ovcns, which were described by values on ninc attributes. Bettman and Park uscd seventy coding catcgorics, largcly derived from carlier coding schcmcs (Montgomcry, 1977; Paync & Ragsdale, 1978; Svenson, 1974). For two raters (who wcre allowed sometimes to assign multiple cncodings), thc avcragc agrcemcnt was ncarly 80% (78.3%) for about 2,000 encodings.

These studics show both that high inter-coder reliabilitics can be obtaincd for TA protocols of decision processes and, by comparison, that decision ncts do not provide a very good representation for these processes.

We should not gencrally be satisficd, howcver, with a single aggregate mcasure of coding rcliability. Such a measure will mainly reflect the very frequcnt coding categories, and the cncodings of infrequent catcgorics may bc much less—or more — rcliable than the average. Reliability of coding individual catcgorics can and should be tested separatcly.

There is a tradeoff betwcen fincness of cncoding and reliability, and substantial advantages in retaining rcliability even at the cxpense of finc-grain resolution. First, automation of cncoding decisions is difficult if rcliability is low. Second, low rcliability implies unccrtainty as to what information in the verbalization provides the evidence for the cncoding, hence uncertainty about the process of verbalizing the hecded information.

## Automatic and Semi-Automatic Protocol Analysis

It would be highly desirable to automate, wholly or partially, the cncoding of protocols. In this section, wc describe two rescarch efforts, mentioncd carlicr, that wcre aimed at devcloping such cncoding programs: the PAS-I and PAS-Il research of Waterman and Newcll, and the work on SAPA by Bhaskar and Simon.

We will focus on PAS-I for several reasons. It was designed to be a completcly automatic systcm, and thus to confront the cntire range of cncoding problems and to propose methods for dcaling with them. Furthermore, the performance of PAS-I was evaluated in detail, thc cvaluation providing a hclpful source of information about problems of protocol analysis.

Since the SAPA prograin was designcd to be uscd interactivcly by a human coder, it did not havc to address all of thesc problems. Each program was tested on only a singlc task - SAPA on problems in thermodynamics and PAS-I on cryptarithmctic problems. Howcver, thc principles of design underlying the systcms have broad applicability.

PAS-I. The PAS-1 program was designed to produce Problem Behavior Graphs directly from scgmented protocols.The program was so structured that information peculiar to the given task could be separated from general information. In a subscquent version, PAS-II, rules special to a given application could be read into the program. However, we will mainly describe PAS-I, which was programmed to analyze cryptarithmctic protocols.

The final product of PAS-I is the Problem Behavior Graph (PBG), which incorporates several thcorctical assumptions that constrain its al i can look at the intermediate products also, and sce where the theoretical assumptions affect the coding process.

Before PAS-I goes to work on a protocol, a problem space is defined manually, by examination of thc task and the protocol. This problem spacce delimits the kinds of encodings that can bc derivcd from the protocol statements. Thc protocol is also scgmented by hand in preparation for the automatic cncoding. The cncoding aimcd at by the PAS systems was essentially that which Newcll and Simon (1972) had previously spccificd in thcir manual encodings of protocols—that is, it was to produce a PBG showing thc successive states of knowledge attained by the subject and the productions that were cxecuted in procceding from cach state to the next.

We will first describe the basic sequence of processing in PAS-I. Next, wc will describe some important components in detail. Finally, we will raise somc mcthodological issues and discuss the results of actual runs with PAS-I and the sources of its crrors.

Basic Structure. PAS-I is organized in thrce separate programs, the latter two operating on the outputs of thcir predecessors. The segments of the protocol are first analyzed by the linguistic processor, which encodes the scgments into propositions. The output from the linguistic processor is the input to the semantic processor, which rccodes the propositions into smaller sets of primitives, and groups thc propositional information as inputs and outputs of possiblc information processcs. Finally, the output of the semantic processor is the input of the problem behavior graph generator, which gencrates a PBG for thc protocol.

Fach protocol segment is cncoded by the linguistic processor independently of thc others (cf. thc local cncoding by MPAS). Morcover, all further processing is done froin the output of this encoding, without reference to the raw protocol. Ict us consider how a propositional encoding ("semantic clements" in thc terminology of Waterman & Newell) can be generated from protocol scgments.

Watcrman and Newcll usce a keyword grammar to map thc protocol segments on propositional clements. This grammar can be scen as a formal spccification of the sct of linguistic patterns that should be mapped on a given propositional rclation. For cxamplc, in the cryptarithmctic task, thc subject is sccking to assign a given lctter (say, D) to its proper number. A protocol scgmcnt of the form "I) cquals $2 "$ or "Change I) to $2 "$ is judgcd propositionally cquivalent to $" 1 ) \ = \ 2 "$ or "(Eq D) 2)," and is so cncoded. All protocol scgments that should be mapped onto "(Eq <letter> <digit>)" are spccificd formally by the linguistic processor. Waterman and Newcll (1971) provide the following example:

<icter> dircctly followed by <prep1> and then <digit>

Start segment with "Changc" directly followed by <lctter> and thc <digit> or <letter dircctly followed by"equals" and the <digit.

where <letter> denotes one of the letters relevant to the task, and where <prcpl> refcrs to a preposition. "Change" is a kcyword that has to appcar litcrally in the text.

This template would recognize and cncode the following scgments:

) for the 2   
Use D as the number 2   
Change R to 2   
Iac  is 5   
Fach D) equals 5

In the following figure, taken from Waterman and Newell (1971), we can sce how the last scntence above is mappcd, as wcll as some more complete structures.

This type of template grammar thus provides a flexible link betwcen the propositional clements idcntificd in the task analysis and a large number of different possible protocol segments.

Waterman and Ncwcll (1972) arguc against a full-fledged linguistic and scmantic analysis of protocol segments, for scveral rcasons. The most obvious is that such an analysis would bc difficult and call for a much more complex program than PAS-I. Furthermore, since protocol segments are often incomplete scntences and not fully grammatical, syntactic information would have limitcd use. Thcy arguc that the PAS program should be more interactive, expectations bcing gencratcd by the carlicr parts of the analysis, and uscd to guide the linguistic analysis on the basis of assumptions of reasonable continuation. However, the current program analyses cach scgmcnt locally and indcpendently of previous ones— incidentally providing us with uscful information about the fcasibility of local encoding.

Evaluation of PAS-I. PAS-I was evaluated by analyzing its performanc on  rotol fo ac t subjes.  a rtn of the protocols was uscd in building thc program, the other protocol provides the best test of its generality.

This is particularly important. for it is tempting to construct patterns in the keyword grammar that usc the spccific structurcs that appcar in an individual protocol.

The output of PAS-I was compared to the cncoding of the same protocol by an cxpert human coder. Mismatches were analyzed to identify the program clements responsible for them. For both protocols, the linguistic processor was rarely responsible for thc mismatches, which spaks wel for the feasibility of ncoding protocols locally. It should be noted that a few changes were made in the keyword grammar before the second protocol was analyzed.

## 304 Protocol Analysis

We turn now to a morc detailed analysis of the failures of the linguistic processor. Most failures resulted from thc absence in the processor of appropriate categorics. For cxample, thc absence of a category corresponding to the notion of "in front of" produced the following failure:

B50. It's not possible that thcre could be another letter in front of this R is it?

EG(PEQ\*I, DLETTERRQUES)

A similar shortcoming is illustrated by thc following scgments, which contain an abstract idca.

B72. Now, it docsn't matter anywhere what the I's are cqual to —

B73. So I'm indcpendent of I. when I gct past the sccond column here.

In this case, the processor detects the letter I, and the negation in B72, and the letter I. and the column referencc in B73, producing:

B72. (NIG) IQI, \*D)

73. (THEREFORE)(L.ETTERLPLACE SECOND)

The ida is mis completely, and the corrsponding information lost irrevocably.

Another cxample from protocol 1, part 2, shows a scquence that PAS-I cannot handle.

B106. Rather-

B107. Iet's sev, how did I arrive at the point of that

B108. This is going to be a litle confusing to start trying to

B109. What's the rcasoning here

Other failurcs of PAS-I are linked to missing clucs for inferences:

PS-I encodes this segment as (FQ F \*D), where \*I) corresponds to some digit. The human encoder notices the article "an" and infers "E is even" (EVEN E).

Seldom docs the linguistic processor fail to extract information connecting references across scgments. However, in the following case it did.

B2.Any two numbers added togcther has to be an even number.   
B25. And I will be an odd number.

(ODD (PLUS \*X 1))

PAS-I is a highly promising system for automatic protocol analysis. More devclopmental cffort will be required, however, bcfore it becomes a finished system that can be put to routine usc in cncoding protocols.

SAPA. In thcir analysis of problem solving in thermodynamics, Bhaskar and Simon (1977) proposcd a scimi-automatic system for protocol analysis realized as a computer program (SAPA). The program incorporates a prediction (predicted problem space and stratcgy) of the problem solving process in this task domain. Both the scqucnce in which information will be addressed, and the dccisions that will be reached are predicted. Figure 6-8 shows the main components of the problem solving process incorporatcd in SAPA and the ordcr in which they are predicted to occur.

![](images/2f61476c18d35518fee8339386a3bfeb996dc078915ab40611db8070663c8db0.jpg)  
Figure 6-8  
Flow diagram of SAPA program

SAPA cmbodics a rough preliminary modcl of the subject's problem solving strategy. As cach ncw scgiment is takcn up, it prompts the coder by predicting the opcration the subject will apply ncxt. If the prcdiction is correct (the opcration cxpressed or implicd by the scgment corresponds to that predicted by SAPA), thc human codcr mcrely provides, from the protocol, the operator's arguments. If the prcdiction is incorrect, thc huan coder cxccutes a "break in the SA stratgy, which puts the program back on the right track again and thcn inscrts a paraphrase of the uncoded seginent as a "comment." For the protocol shown below (Tables 6-2 and 6-3), SAPA docs a vcry good job of anticipating its contents and the order in which they will appear.

The SAPA scheme for these thermodynamics problems rests on the task-dcpcndent assumption (empirically valid for thesc subjccts) that the solution process will bc built around the cquation of conscrvation of cnergy, and the special form this cquation takcs in cach particular problem. The "logic" of the solution process consists in trying to solve successively for the remaining unknowns in this cquation, aftcr it has been spccialized to fit the particular conditions of the problem.
