Bhaskar and Simon (1977) vicw the SAPA predictions as a zerocth order approximation of thc problem solving process in thc domain of problems to which it applics. "SAPA's prcdictions can be secn as a referencc point for idcntifying differences that would lead to better and more detailcd descriptions of the problem-solving processes in thermodynamics." In thcir analysis of two other protocols from the same subject, they use SAPA in cxactly this way.

Analyscs of the thermodynamics protocols could be made with the samc approach as wc havc uscd carlicr. Encodings, which could bc made idcntical with thosc required by SAPA's prompts, would be made of the protocol segments. SAPA's prcdictions about the order in which these catcgories should occur could then casily be cvaluated against the order in which the cncoding assessments appear.

SAPA-like systems for other task domains can be built with modest effort. Of course, no a priori judgement can bc rcached as to how well they will capture protocol contents in those domains.That depends on how regular the problem solving process turns out to be, and how much smilarity there is among the protocols of different subjcts.However, semi-automatic protocol analysis appcars to be a useful tool for problem domains that have a reasonable amount of structure.

## EFFECTIVE PROTOCOL ANALYSIS PROCEDURES

In this concluding sction, we will sum up our discussion by making suggestions as to which approaches to protocol analysis appcar to have the highcst potential of success. Wc have idcntificd a number of problems, and suggested solutions for somc of thcm. We will now try to show how thcse solutions follow from (or are at least consist with) our theorctical model of the processes of verbalization.

## Matching Coding Categories to Verbalizations

Our modcl asserts that verbalization is a reflection of the heeded information. If that is so, then thc more directly an cncoding scheme can capturc the units and structure of the verbalizations, the betterfor several reasons. First, the closer the match betwcen cncoding categories and vcrbalizations, the casier it will be to cncode them, and the more rcliable will bc thc cncoding. Automating thc cncoding will also be casicr. Second, the closer the match, the less will be the need to usc non-local context to make cncoding decisions. Third, thc closer the match, the larger will be the fraction of the information in the vcrbalization that can be cncodcd. In the idcal case, one should bc able to regencrate the actual verbalizations from thc information contained in the cncoding, using a simple grammar. We havc argued carlier that local encoding will usually be adequate. Howcver, new data may show that somc internal states can only be assesscd by using a much larger context than we have becn considering.

We see two substantial objections to the approach we have been describing and advocating, and a third objection that wc consider wholly invalid. Iet us start with the latter. It is still the case that protocols are often collected and analysed in the absence of a cognitive model defining what verbalizations would constitute evidence for cach coding category. We bclieve that this proccdure will be uscd less and less as the necd for a theorctical base for cncoding is understood.

Of the other two objections against our approach, the most serious is that it is cxccedingly time-consuming. Especially whcn only sclccted aspects of thc cognitive process are of intcrest, it may require inordinate effort to analyze all protocol scgments in dctail. Wc will show later how selected aspccts can be cxtracted from a protocol at the segment level without requiring a full-blown analysis of the cntire protocol.

Another objection is that we may want to abstract or extract generalizable cntitics, likc rcpresentations, rules, strategics, and so on, instcad of cncoding scgment by scgment. Evidencc about such cntitics may be scattered through the protocol, so that thcir assessment requires simultaneous consideration of the cntire cognitive processing activity. It may bc methodologically admissible to make such global asscssments, but we would argue against it on thcorctical grounds. We will argue that the protocol contains more rcliable basic information that can bc used as data for the assessment at the more general level.

In Chapter 7, we will provide several cxanples to show how goals, intermcdiate results and gencrated problcin-configurations can be cncoded scparately and how an analysis of a largc number of such cncodings can support a gencral represcntation. Wc will also discuss briefly the use of cxpcrimental mcthods to increasc the probability that all subjects will hccd a widc rangc of stimulus instanccs. When subjects all attend to the same information, their responses allow us to cvaluate the generalizability of the proposcd representations and stratcgies.

## Verbalization of General Knowledge and Rules

Generalized cntities, like representations and procedures, are dircctly verbalizcd only under cxtraordinary circumstances. A subjct will not usually say, "I will consider all logically possiblc combinations of variables X and Y that have feature 7." More often, subjects explore specific instances that wc can describe as bcing combinations of X and Y with . It knowledgc structurc thcy havc gcneratcd is an instancc of a general process or procedure with such and such characteristics. Protocols provide many instanccs of knowledge structures, but the gencrality and scope of the processes that generated them must be inferred, and is scldom indicatcd explicitly in thc protocols.

It is qu pose  ur us , relations, knowlcdge, and rules that are gencral and gencralizable in the sense that thcy apply to many situations and contexts. An obvious example is the verbalization of an hypothesis in a conccpt learning experiment. Vcrbalized knowledge clements are also gencral. In cryptarithmetic, Subject S3 of Newcll and Simon verbalized the following relation:

$$
\mathrm { " S i n c e ~ l ) + \ l > = G , G \ m u s t \ b e \ e v e n . ^ { \cdot } }
$$

an inference that applies in several situations in this and other cryptarithmetic problems.

Anindirect verbalization of a rule can be identifd in the following verbal report on mental multiplication, "First I will put the largest number on top," and the verbalization of a goal, "Have largest number on top." It is often casy and tempting to encode information logically. Howcver, it is psychologically possible and cven plausible that a concept like "largest" will not be generated or hccded unless thc number excceds somc limit of magnitude. The preconditions of this rule will not be met by numbers differing by only a small amount, contradicting the logical interpretation. Hence, abstract or general statements cannot bc translated into a formal representation without careful consideration of the context. Thus, in the context that this rule and goal was generatcd and vcrbalized, onc of the numbers was noted as bcing the largest. Yet, under what circumstances this verbalization will occur requires further spccification.

Knowledge and rules reside in I.TM. In order to bc hecded and to influence the cognitive process, thcy necd to be accessed by appropriate retricval cues. Evcn though wc have been able to identify them in a protocol on one problem, we cannot be certain that they will be accessed and uscd on other problems.

Mcta-comments are another source of general statcinents about the cognitive processes. A description of a general proccdure for dcaling with a class of problems is not a mcta-comment, for it draws on previously stored information. A clear casc of a mcta-comment is a subject's attempt to verbalizc rcgularitics in his own cognitive processes. We argucd carlier that these verbalizations are oftcn of linitcd valuc, and are clcarly abstractions, influcnccd by the episodic information that the subject can retrieve at the time.

The best singlc test for distinguishing meta-comments from verbalizations of current processes dcrives from thcir role in subscquent processcs. A verbalizcd proccdure, likc a rccipe, will bc accessed cach time the corresponding cognitive process is cxccuted. It corresponds to the representation of thc relevant knowlcdge. A mcta-comment will most likcly not be accessed and used to direct subscquent bchavior.

## Conclusion

In this chapter wc have shown how the information hecded during a cognitivc process can be idcntificd by an analysis of the corresponding verbalizations. In protocol analysis, we arc trying to infer what information was hcedcd as input for the observed verbalization. Through a carcful task analysis it is possible to dcfinc a priori a spacc of possible encodings representing the information relevant to the task. Then, encoding protocols involves finding the catcgory that cxpresses the same in formation as the verbalization.

In the carlier chapters, cspecially Chapter 5, we argued that TA verbalizations closcly match the flow of attention to infornation. By locating language production units and linguistic structures, the scequence of hceded information can be uncovered. Although the majority of encoding schemes definc thcir categories so that individual protocol segments can bc cncoded, many schemes do not attempt to uncover the heeded information, but scck to infer directly the processes that generated the hecded inforination. We arguc that, whencver possible, the original cncoding should reflect as closcly as possible the verbalized information, and should preferably be derived by automatic or semiautomatic mcans. Once verbalizations are converted to formal cncodings, thcorctical proposals for representations, processcs and strategics can be introduced to provide parsimonious accounts for the protocol data.

# TECHNIQUES OF PROTOCOL ANALYSIS

EXAMPLES

In the previous chapter we devcloped a gencral framework for considering thc cncoding and analysis of protocols. Wc raised and discussed a number of theoretical issues, and illustrated various forms of protocel analysis. In this chapter, we will cxaminc a substantial numbcr of concrete examples of protocol analysis to illuminate the more general questions that have bcen raiscd, and to provide further illustrations of techniqucs and issues. We begin with a discussion of informal mcthods of analysis. Then we discuss mcthods for in ferring thcoretical entitics from protocols. Finally we will revicw studies exploring the reliability, validity and gencralizability of protocol data, and discuss future dircction of these kinds of analyscs.

## INFORMAL PROTOCOL ANALYSIS

In order to construct a thcoretical framework and coding scheme for a set of protocols, pilot work will be nccessary to develop the coding catcgorics. Even during thesc prcliminary and cxploratory phascs of analysis thcre cxist gencral mcthods that are uscful both for undcrstanding thc cognitivc processes reflected in the protocols and for approaching the dcfinition of coding categories.

Verbalizations cannot convcy more information than was available to thc proccss that produced them. Protocol analysis may involve categorizing the verbalizations according to the processes that could have gencrated them. We wish to providc some guidclincs for making this classification uniquely and unambiguously. We assume a hicrarchy of processes, thc simplest bcing operations like Copy, which transfer information that is available from the senses or in memory. At the first step, wc cncode cach verbalization in terms of the simplest process that could account for it.

A verbalization may be a literal copy of information that is presented or has been memorized previously. In this case, the verbalization may or may not be gencrated without bcing processcd semantically. It will scldom be possible to infer with certainty whcthcr it was understood or parroted. As a sccond possibility, paraphrasing, a verbalization may copy the semantic content of inormation that is prcsented or remembcred, rather than copying literal verbal strings. In this case, the source in formation may or may not have been in oral form; if not, it has to be recoded for production. As a third possibility, infcrence, a verbalization may not be a copy, literal or semantic, of available information, but may be generated in various ways from such information. As a fourth possibility, information that was hccded at an carly tine may be recalled or retrieved.

## Example: Protocol from a Series Task

To explore thesc distinctions, wc will now cxaminc a protocol collected and transcribed by Ohlsson (1980). In his study, thc subject was given a complex variant of the 3-term scrics task. Thc subjcct, having been presented with information about the rclative order of pairs of objects, must intcgrate this information to construct a unique ordering of a whole sct of objects. The actual physical objects are not presented, so the task has to bc carricd out mentally, and without written notes. The task is difficult cnough to require considerable cognitive processing. The information given the subject consists of scvenscgments (information units), as follows:

1.07. What block is imnediately below the blue one?

## Major Processes

Let us sce how we can encode the verbalizations in this protocol in terms of the processes that produced them. Wc will cmploy a scquential decision process, first trying to account for cach verbalization as a literal copy, then as a scmantic copy, and finally, by dcfault, as newly generated. Although the classification can be donc locally segment by segment, we will report the results here by type of process.

In this situation, the primary process for literal copying is reading the information that was presented. If the subject's verbalization literally matches the text, we will infer that it was rcad.The inference might be further confirmed from records of cye movements, but cyc-movement data were not collected for this protocol. What deviations from an cxact literal match will we accept as "reading"? In the protocol (scc below), we classificd 17 scgments as produced by reading. In one of these 17 cases, the subject prcfixcd the litcral tcxt with an "and," in two othcrs, there was an omission of one or two words, for cxample:

## PI.8. [a] black block is between a red and a green [block]

Thus, the Read process accounts for almost a third of the protocol segments, 17 out of 56.

We placcd in the sccond class (scmantic copying) segments that cxpress the content of one of the seven lines of information that were prescnted, but paraphrase and do not literally copy thcm. We can further classify thesc segments by the way in which thcy deviatc from literal copying. To comply with customary rules of refcrence in English, definite articles are somctincs interchanged with indefinite articles—"[P15] the yellow one is higher up than the red one"—just after the yellow one has been mentioned. In all but one of seven occurrences of this transformation, the same object has bcen referred to in one of the two just prcceding protocol scgments. Thc onc cxccption is associated with a verb in the past tense (the only such verb in the protocol)—"[Pl.48-49] and then we had the bluc one which is immediately below the yellow."

In four other cases, all involving l.O5, the subject varies syntactical or lexical constructions while retaining the in formation content.

Processes of the sccond kind, Paraphrase, account for 11 scgments. Togcther with those accounted for by the Read process, they make up one half of the protocol.

Of vcrbalizations in the protocol that provide information about the arrangement of the blocks, 11 would scem to have rcquired generating processes (i.e., Inference processes) for thcir production. The nature of the in ferences can be scen from the list of these segments:

Sincc the thrce-way classification of segments was made on the basis of the information expressed, it is interesting to look for verbal tags of features that may distinguish gencrated from copicd information. In 8 of 11 instances of generated segments, the subject includes the word "then." The thrce exceptions can be accounted for by the fact that the samc in formation has bcen generatcd previously. We will consider here a number of examples of the process of protocol interpretation. Thc first cxamplc involves the scgment from Pl.12 through Pl.18. Pl.14 through PI.16 arc concerncd with the relations of the ycllow block with thosc bclow it. Pl.12-Pl.14 arc paraphrascd from 1.O3-1.O5, respectively; thcn Pl.15 picks up PL.12 again, and Pl.16 paraphrascs PI.14. Thc "and" in Pl.16 makcs it plausible that Pl.15-Pl.16 arc bcing considcrcd in conjunction with cach other. This produces the inferencc, prefaced by "then," in Pl.17. Pl.18 may mark the previous statement as a hypothesis.

Pl.23-29 provide a second example of the usc of "and" to mark the conjunction of propositions for an inferencc process. Herc, Pl.24 and Pl.25, conjoincd by "and," suffice to generate thc information in Pl.26-29.

Pl.38-41 provide a third cxample of the same thing. Pl.40 is derivcd from Pl.38-39, which arc conjoined by "and." Pl.41 indicates that Pl.40 is hcld only as a hypothesis, and indced, it is reviscd in Pl.42-45, whcre Pl.42, prefaccd with "but," is introduccd as a ncw premisc that requircs a rcordering. Thc intcrruption of Pl.4l by Pl.42 suggests that at Pl.39, the subject was aware that the "betwcen" was symmctric, allowing two interprctations, of which only one was verbalizcd in Pl.40. There is no indication of how Pl.42 is cvokcd at this particular moment, but having bccn cvokcd, it causes the interprctation of "betwcen" to be revised, to sclect the other hypothesis.

There is no clcar indication as to how the inference in PI.46 was arrived at. It follows logically, of coursc, from Pl.42 and Pl.44, but the subject may have infcrred it in some other way. Any account of this in ference must be hypothetical.

The inferencc from Pl.47-51 also goes from an "and" to a "then," but is slightly morc complex, for the past tense in Pl.48 suggests a backward memory reference to PL.25-27.

PI51-54 is a rather straightforward inference of the type, "X and Y, then 7." This is followed by Pl.55-56, where two new assertions are made without explicit premises. Here we would infer that the inferences are derived dircctly from an internal model of the scquential ordering of the blocks.

If we are correct in our assumption about the general structure of the "and/then" process, and bclicve that the hceded information is verbalized concurrently with awareness, then the subject will not be aware otheinfenc at the tme he is vbalizig the pri nd the nd.

In fact, there are a couple of instances where "and" is verbalized without a new inference bcing drawn. In Pl.29-31, for cxample, the third statement is not inferred from the first two, but merely represents another fact about blocks mentioned in the first two. Note the use of the definite article in Pl.31 for the objects that havc becn mentioned in the two previous statements. Again, in Pl.33-37, there is a failurc to draw an inference from conjoined premises, followed by a recovery in PIl.38-40. Note how Pl.33-34 are reordered in Pl.38-39.

In our analysis of this protocol, wc have shown how statements can be rather unambiguously classificd as reading, paraphrasing, or interpreting. Furthermorc, it appcars that these assessments can generally be made locally, that is, by cvaluating a single scgment consisting of just a few statements independently of thc broader context. We have also illustrated how we can usc this analysis, in many cases, to account for the inference processes that produced the newly gencrated in formation. Of course a careful distinction should be maintaincd betwcen such theoretical interpretations of thc mechanisms that may have produccd the protocol statements and the cncoding and classification of the statements themselves. In our discussion of the cxamples, we suggested some organizations of processes that would havc produced the statements that appearcd in the actual protocol. With some additional analysis, we could construct a formal coding schcme that would definc the kinds of information that the subject might become aware of and hced. The thcoretical interprctation could thcn be specificd in terms of scquences of protocol statements drawn from this sct, and could be cvaluated against other hypotheses. In his dissertation Ohlsson (1980) analyzcd this and other protocols in unusual dcpth, using a complcte proccss model and problem-behavior graph.

## USING A THEORY FOR PROTOCOL PREDICTION

Where a theory of the cognitive processes under investigation has already been postulated, sevcral kinds of prcdictions can bc made about the information that subjects will attend to and report orally. On the one hand, a thcory can yicld wcak predictions of the kinds of information that will be hceded in performing the task, without predicting the sequcnce in which information will bc attendcd to. On the other hand, a thcory can pcrmit inferences about the scquences of processes required to rcach a correct solution of the task, and thereby yicld strong predictions that only those sequences of processes would appcar in subjects' protocols.

To make these predictions as precise as possible, the theory of task performance needs to be complemented by a thcory of verbalization, so that wc know what part of the information that is hecded has a high probability of bcing verbalizcd, and what part has a low probability. We have tricd in the carlier chapters of this book to provide such a complementary theory.

To test a hypothesis of the first, or weaker type, wc necd to compare the sct of statcments implied by that hypothesis with the statements implicd by compcting processes. The union of thcse scts defines the totality of coding categorics that necds to bc considered in cncoding the protocols in a manner that is both comprehensive and neutral among the hypothescs.

A hypothesis of the sccond, or stronger, type predicts cither a uniquc sequence of verbalizations or a sct of acceptable scquences. Such a sequencc or sct can also bc represented as a transition network, a represcntation that may bc attractive for somc purposes. Thc two representations, as sct of sequences or nctwork, are of course logically cquivalcnt. Altcrnativc hypotheses can bc cxplicated in thc same way as scts of sequences of verbalizations, and the actual scquences can readily be compared with those prcedicted by the different hypotheses.

To make these considerations concrcte, we will carry through in some detail analyses of two kinds of cxamples. First, we will show how an analysis of identified instanccs of gencrated information can allow us to derivc and evaluate hypotheses regarding the representation of information. Then wc will show, by mcans of examples, how hypotheses about processes and strategics can be used to predict the order in which in formation is verbalized.

## CHARACTERISTICS OF GENERATED INFORMATION: REPRESENTATIONS

Determining what representation a subject is using in a problem solving ts is  a ut an ubleater. In this scn,  ill e two studics that used protocols as data to infer subjects' representations. First, we will explore different methods for characterizing heeded information using the Fight Puzzle as our example. Then we will show how representations of the Tower of Hanoi can be inferred from protocols.

Figure 7-1

## The Fight Puzzle

First, we need to describe the Eight Puzzle and say something about the experiment that produced thc protocols (Ericsson, 1975b). In the Eight Puze, subts e pred wih a 3x3 mat  u uals y spacc, the arrangement of tiles can be changed Subjects are instructed to achieve the goal arrangement (Figure 7-1b).In this experiment, subjects were given different starting configurations, but all asked to achieve the particular goal configuration shown in the figure.

![](images/15b6d73343c0facb3d3236438b4fbb615d2839cef80236806a64177d6b7c5111.jpg)  
Example of a configuration for the Eight Puzzle (left); this is to be transformed into a goal configuration (right), the one shown here being used throughout the present experiment.

The Eight Puzzle is solved by dcriving information relevant to choosing moves from a comparison of the current puzzle configuration (PC) with thc goal configuration (GC). PC and GC can be rcpresented internally and compared with cach other in many ways. For cxample, the subject can mcasure or cstimate the qualitative similarity of PC to GC, or a numerical "distance" of the onc from the othcr. Most artificial intelligence programs for solving the Eight Puzzle usc some kind of distancc mcasure that takes into account considcrable information about PC (Nilsson, 1971; Pohl, 1969). On thc other hand, a program along the lincs of the Gencral Problem Solver (Newcll, Shaw, & Sinon, 1958), would consider the "major difference" (i.c., a feature like a particular tile not in its final position) betwcen PC and GC, and would ignore most of the other information about PC. Thcse two general approaches make quite different predictions about what we might expect to find in subjects' protocols.

The protocols of the subjects wcre categorized on the basis of mainly syntactic inforination (Ericsson, 1975b). Two categories, intentions and cognitions, have major relevance to the form of representation the subjects were using. Intcntions are verbalized goals, defining the attributes of configurations subjccts are sccking to achieve (e.g., "I want to gct 3 in its corrcct place"). Cognitions are vcrbalizcd characterizations of the current PC (c.g., "I sec that 7 is in its correct place").

We will present our analysis of these two categories separately, a proccdure that will permit us to cvaluate, or at lcast comment on, the gencrality of the representation. Our analysis cimploys 274 protocol segmcnts coded as intentions, and 132 cncoded as cognitions, pooled from the protocols of ten subjects. We start by discussing intcntions.

The most striking finding about intentions is that subjects define goals in tcrms of only a few tiles (or onc). In 162 cases (59%) the goal involvcd positioning a singlc tilc; a pair of ilcs in 61 cascs (22%). Propcrties of three tiles werc mentioncd in 49 cases (18%), four tiles in two cases (1%). In no casc did a subjcct mention a goal of positioning morc than four tilcs. The majority (75%) of intentions involving single tiles cxpressed the goal of putting them in thcir "correct" position in the GC. Somc examples of such verbalizations are given in Table 7-1.

These are cxactly the kinds of goals that a GPS-like model would predict. In thc remaining cascs, subjccts vcrbalizcd "gctting the tile closer to its goal" or "getting tile in its correct row," or "getting the tile to a location different from that in GC." or "getting rid of a blocking tile."

Verbalized intentions involving two tiles are of two kinds (Ericsson, 1975b, discusses five exceptions). The tiles are cither to be placed in their correct positions, or in corrcct sequential order independently of absolute location. It might be thought that these goals would involve all pairs of tiles adjacent in the GC. In actual fact, with one exception, all such goals involvcd pairs of tiles with adjacent ordinal numbers (c.g., 1-2, c.This suggests an internal rpreentation  the GC s hown Figure 7-2.

When we turn to intentions involving three or four tiles, the same pattern cmerges. All but five instances (47) involve thc correct placement or correct scquential order of the tiles in the first or the second row of GC, that is, 1-2-3 or 4-5-6. Thc fivc other cascs, which arc not true exceptions, are analyzcd by Ericsson (1975b).

The subjects' verbal intentions that appcar in these protocols can thus bc describcd almost completcly as simplc differences falling out from a comparison of PC with GC.

Analysis of cognitions, verbal descriptions of the PC, yiclds very similar results. Of the 132 protocol scgmcnts encoded as cognitions, 54 (41%) involved the attributcs of a singlc tile, while 52 (39%) involved the attributes of a pair of tilcs, and 18 (14%), thrce tilcs. Threc instances involved noticing that tiles 1-2-3-4-5 and 1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8 followed cach other in numerical ordcr, or that 8-7-6-5-4 were in reversc numeric order.

The cognitions about single tilcs correspond with those for intentions. In 24 segments, thc subject obscrved that a tile had rcached its correct location or its correct row, or was closer than bcfore to the goal. In 24 cases, subjects obscrved that a tile's location was incorrcct, thus making explicit mention of a difference betwcen PC and GC. The remaining six scgments referrcd to tiles obstructing the placement of other tiles.

## Isomorphic Problems

Haycs and Simon (1974) proposed a model describing how subjects comprehend written problem instructions and construct an internal representation of problem information. The model was able to gencrate problem representations for the Tea Ceremony problcm, a formal isomorph of the Tower of Hanoi. Thinking-aloud protocols wcre gathered from subjects presentcd with this problem, but the protocols proved to bc rather sketchy for the interval during which subjects wcrc formulating the problem and dcvising a represcntation of it. It was difficult, from these protocols, to dctermine what representations subjects had constructed. However, as we shall sce, protocols taken during the intcrval when subjects wcre solving the problein disclosed these representations clearly.

In a subsequent study, Simon and Hayes (1976) uscd problem isomorphs that, thcir model predicted, would causc subjccts to adopt distinctly different problem representations. We will consider here two of thesc isomorphs, a move problem and a change problem, respectively.

In its usual form, thc Tower of Hanoi involves moving of transferring disks among three pcgs. Only onc disk can bc moved at a time, and a larger disk cannot bc placed on a pcg on which thcrc is alrcady a smaller disk. In this form, the Towcr of Hanoi is a move problem. The starting and goal configurations of disks and pegs are shown in Figure 7-3. Herc thc pcgs arc labclcd "A, B, C" and thc disks are labcled "] =small," "2=medium," "3=large," and will be rcferred to by these names in llthe isomorphic problems.

![](images/d35bc76cc999b5a5ead39531f7970c80feb95d543e41d845504c7b1030574439.jpg)  
Figure 7-3  
Illustration of Different Representations in the Tower of Hanoi Problem with 3

In the Tower of Hanoi, the disks retain thcir physical identities; only their locations (the pcgs on which they arc placcd) changc. Symbolically, wc can dcscribe any disk configuration as a list of thrce locations (corresponding to the thrce pcgs), and lists of the appropriate disks associated with cach location, (as shown in Figure 7-3). We will call this symbolic representation a move representation.

Alternatively, wc could represent cxactly the samc information symbolically by a list of the thrce disks, and with cach member of the list, anindication f the p with which it is asociated.We willcll s change representation.

When a move representation is uscd, then an alteration in the problem situation is recorded by removing the name of one of the disks fro its pg list and placing it on the st  a diffent peg.When change representation is used, then an alteration in the problein situation is recorded by altering the name of the peg with which some particular disk is associated. Thus, the same move in the Towcr of Hanoi is represented by different symbol-manipulating processes in the two different symbolic representations of the task. (Sce Figure 7-4.)

Isomorphs of the Towcr of Hanoi can be constructed by substituting other kinds of objects for disks and pegs, and the lcgal transformations with these objects can be described in terms of moves or changes. Below, wc give cxamples of problems in which the objects arc "monsters" and "globes," and the transformations involve cither moving a globe from one monster to another, or changing the size of the globe.

Since these two problem descriptions arc logically cquivalent (that is, isomorphic with respcct to statcs and moves), it is logically possible that a subject would use cither the change or move representation when faccd with either of the problems. The UNDERSTANI) model of Simon and Hayes (1976) made cxplicit predictions as to whcther subjects would rcprescnt particular problem statements as movc or change problems. In particular, it predicted for the cxamples presented here that the first would be represented as a movc problem, and the second as a change problem. That is to say, if thc problems wcre described to subjects as involving an object moving from one location to another, the subjects would represent it symbolically in just that way; while if it were described to them in terms of objects changing thcir sizes or other properties, the subjects would represent it symbolically in that other way.

To provide evidence of the representations that the subjects used, two different kinds of protocol statcments wcre analyzed for subjects presented with thesc problem isomorphs. The statcmcnts of the first kind were verbalized descriptions of moves, statcments of the second kind were verbalized descriptions of current problem states and configurations.

## 328 Protocol Analysis

Three five-handed extraterrestial monsters were holding three crystal globes. Because of the quantum-mechanical peculiarities of their neighborhood, both monsters and globes come in exactly three sizes with no others permitted: small, medium, and large. The medium-sized monster was holding the small globe, the small monster was holding the large globe, and the large monster was holding the medium-sized globe. Since this situation offended their keenly developed sense of symmetry, they proceeded to trunsfer globes from one monster to another so that each monster would have a globe proportionate to his own size. Monster etiquette complicated the solution of the problem since it requires:

1. that only one globe may be transferred at a time:

2. that if a monster is holding two globes, only thc larger

of the two may be transfcrred; and

3 that a globe may not be transferred to a monster who is holding a larger globe.

By what sequence of transfers could the monsters have solved this problem?

Three five-handed extraterrestial monsters were holding three crystal globes. Because of the quantum-mechanical peculiarities of their neighborhood, both monsters and globes come in exactly three sizes with no others permitted: small, meclium, and large. The medium-sized monster was holding the small globe, the small monster was holding the large globe, and the large monster was holding the medium-sized globe. Since this siluation offended their keenly developed sense of symmetry. they proceeded to shrink and expand the globes from one monster to another so that each monster would have a globe proportionate to his own size. Monster etiquette complicated the solution of the problem since it requires:

1. that only onc globe may be shrunk or expanded at a time:

2. that if a monster is holding two globes, only the larger

of the two may be shrunk or expanded; and

3. that a globe may not be transfcrred to a monster who is holding a larger globe.

By what sequence of transfers could the monsters have solved this problem?

## Isomorphic Forms of the Monster Problem

Descriptions of moves. Most of the protocol statcments describing moves provide substantial evidence for assessing the underlying representation, as the following cxamples show:

From the information in these kinds of statements. two encoders independently assessed the representation uscd by the subject for the cntire solution attempt, and assessed whether the representation was changed or modified during the solution process.

Descriptions of states. Aspects of thc representation can also be idenifid casy whn subjcts ae describing the current state the problem. Consider the following two cxamples of protocol segments:

The small monster has the large, the medium has the small, the large has the medium.

A small monster is holding a small globc, a large monster holding a medium-size globe, a mcdium-size monster holding a large globe.

The two segments clcarly refer to a list of monsters with globes of different sizcs rather than vice versa.

In one of thc many problem isomorphs that wcre studicd, the monsters transferred thcir names, and a monster could have more than one name at a timc. or none. Thc following scegment gives evidence, for this isomorph, of representation in tcrms of a list of monsters:

The guy with long name now has a long name and medium-size name.

The UNDERSTANI) modcl prcdicted the representations used by subjects in all cascs but one. The analyscs of moves and of states always yielded the same representation, and therc were no disagreements between the two cncodcrs. The UNIERSTAND modcl also predicted correctly in all cases which type of object (monster or globc) would constitute the main list.

From some informal analyses of protocols in Ncwcll and Simon (1972), we arc convinced that the rcpresentations underlying the Problem

Behavior Graph in the crypt-arithmctic and logic problems could have becn inferred or confirmcd through similar methods of analysis. It is clcar that any hypothesis about represcntation of information will, at least implicity, involve assumptions about the processes that gencrate the in formation.

## SEQUENCES OF HEEDED INFORMATION

We now turn to a scries of cxamples where hypotheses about processes and stratcgies are uscd to predict scquences of hccded information. The first two cxamples show how thc adcquacy of strategics can bc cvaluated by comparing predictions of hecdcd information with information verbalizcd in thc protocol. Such cvaluation is quite powerful cvcn when in formation is incompletely verbalized. The next pair of examples show how a complete process modcl including a modcl of verbalization, can be constructed and cvaluated. I .astly, wc present an cxample that illustratcs the many possible realizations of cognitive processes. The cxamples dcal with tasks that are quite familiar and whosc processes arc uncontroversial. We will present two analyses of mental addition, an analysis of thc 'Tower of Hanoi problem and one of mental multiplication.

## Mental Addition of Digit Sequences

Subjects wcre shown a card on which was printed a linc of digits, and were instructcd to find the sum of the digits while thinking aloud.

Task analysis. Digits can be added togcther in a large number of ways to obtain the corrcct sum. Herc we will consider, as hypotheses, only two of these ways. The first hypothesis is that the subjects form a single partial sum, adding the digits to it cumulativcly, from left to right. This model of the process, simple as it is, contains several implicit assumptions. First, it assumes that cach successive addition is carried out in a single stcp (the subject is aware of only the final result, and there are no hecdcd intermediate states). Sccond, it assunes that finding the next digit to be added is also an automatic process without intermediate steps.

The model is powerful enough to predict the exact scquence in which information will bc hceded, but it can be wcakcned to predict only the order in which the partial sums will be hccdcd (and presumably verbalized). Consider the following example, whcre the upper line plays he , and the wer in the condig ucsiv tial sums:

<table><tr><td>5</td><td>N </td><td>8 15</td><td>9 24</td><td>7 31</td><td>4 35</td><td>3 38</td><td>6 44</td><td>3 47</td><td>4 51</td></tr></table>

Ignoring sequencc (the weakest model, H2), it can be predicted that all the verbalized two-digit numbers will bclong to the following set:

1I} = {15,24,31,35,38.44.47,51}

(Note that we omit 7, which is nol a two-digit number.)

Consider, now, the alternativc hypothesis (H2) that the subject adds the digits from right to let, instcad of from left to right:

<table><tr><td>4</td><td>3 7</td><td>6 13</td><td>3 16</td><td>4 20</td><td>7 27</td><td>9 36</td><td>8 44</td><td>2 46</td><td>5 51</td></tr></table>

Model H2 makes the quite different predictions that the verbalized twodigit numbers will belong to the following set:

112 = {13,16,20,27,36.44.46,51 }

This set has only onc member in common with 11 (44).

We will now cncode three protocols, comparing the encodings with H1 and H2. Our coding alternatives will bc the spacc of all two-digit numbers, 10-99. All other information in thc protocols will be ignored.

## 332 Protocol Analysis

Evaluation of hypotheses. Before matching against the numbers prcdictcd by the weak hypothescs, we would want to eliminate duplicates from the rcported partial sums, and also thc final result (51), which is implicd by any model that generates the corrcct answer.

For Protocol 1, thc verbalized partial results all correspond to those postulatcd by H1, and none correspond with the predictions of H2. The chances that such a closc match with Hl could bc produccd by chance arc less than one in a trillion. Even if the random gencrator was constrained to produce a succession of two-digit numbers, cach not more than 9 larger than its predecessor and all of them less than thc correct sum, the probability would be less than one in a million. H1 clearly describes Protocol 1 better than H2 does.

In the case of Protocol 2, the verbalized partial sums match those predicted by H1 in six cases, but fail to match in onc. Again, thcy do not match at all with the predictions of H2. The superior fit of the protocol to H1 as compared to H2 cannot bc attributed to chance. Nevertheless, the failure to achieve a perfect fit suggests that H1 could be cxtcnded or modificd to provide a more satisfactory cxplanation of the protocol. We will come back to this point latcr.

Turning now to Protocol 3, there are only thrce matchcs with H1, and six failures to match. For thc alternative model, H2, therc are two matches and scven failures. In this case, ncither model can be preferred over a random model. Ncverthcless, informal analysis of thc protocol suggests that H1 might describe the verbalizations if some assumptions about crrors in the addition proccss wcre introduced. Again, wc will return to this issue later. Wc mention it here mainly to indicate the damaging cffect of errors on direct predictions when a single error may change the whole succeeding stream of verbalizations.

Concluding remarks. This simple cxample shows how information verbalized by a subject while thinking aloud can sometimes be predicted in a straightforward manner. For the protocols of subjects who got the correct answer, H1 makes accuratc predictions, and clcarly conforms to the evidence bctter than H2. The fact that the two models make quite different predictions facilitated the choice betwcen them.

One of the attractive features of making the test in this form is that incomplete verbalization docs not then crcate a scrious problem it simply dccrcases the power of the test in choosing betwcen alternative hypothescs. Morcover, with thc wcak hypotheses, vcry spccific predictions can be made without having to makc explicit assumptions of the sntal orde proces.Th apl in th ext section bri ut this last feature more clearly.

## Strategies for the Tower of Hanoi

How can we predict verbalizations of subjects using different strategics to solve thc same problem? In particular, can the stratcgics be identified reliably from protocols? The data are derived from a study by Neves (1977), in which he traincd subjects to use different strategies to solve the Towcr of Hanoi puzzle, and then asked them to think aloud during the solution process.

First, wc will summarize the theorctical analysis of Simon (1975), describing several different strategies that all lcad to correct solutions of the puzzle. The analysis makes cxplicit what information cnters and is retained in S'TM at all timcs. From the thcoretical models we derive the sequcncc of hceded information for a nunber of puzzlc configurations, and usc these to predict what information would be vcrbalizcd with cach strategy. Wc then cncode protocols from subjects trained in using these stratcgics, and match them against thc predictions. The Tower of Hanoi puzzle has already been described carlicr. We will be concerned with the 4-disk version. At the start of thc probolem, the disks are all on Pcg 1; the task is to move them to Peg 3.

Task Analysis. The thcoretical models of the solution process are expressed in the form of production systems. This formalism is described by Simon (1975, pp. 274-275) as follows:

A production system (Newcll & Simon, 1972) is simply an ordered sct of processes called productions. Fach production has two parts: a condition (C), and an action (A), which are usually written with an arrow connecting them: C - A.

'The reader will not mislead himself badly if he cquates the arrow in a production with the link in a spccial kind of S-R structure. The productions fall into two classcs: a general class and a spccial class called perceptual tests. The condition of a production bclonging to the general class is a tcst usually for the prescncc of absencc of a particular kind of symbol on the contents of STM. The test is cithcr satisfied or not. Whenever the test in the condition part of a gcncral production is satisficd, and only them, the action part is cxecuted. The action may be a motor act, thc performing of a perceptual test, an act of rctrieving information from long-term memory, or an act of changing the contents of short-tcrm memory. The condition part of a production inay involve a conjunction of sevcral clementary tests, and the action part may involve a sequencc of clcmentary actions. A simplc example of a gcneral production is:

# "Problem-solved" → Halt

'The condition side of this production calls for a test whcther the symbol, "Problem-solved," has bcen stored in STM. T'hc action taken in this case is to halt the cntire process. If the symbol is not found in STM, no action is taken.

The production subsystem represcnting pcrceptual tests is activated by exccuting a Test action in one of the gcncral productions. The condition of a perccptual test is a perccivable fcature of thc task environment, or a conjunction of such features. If the fcature or conjunction of features is present, and only then, the action part of the perceptual test is cxecuted. The only action of a perceptual test is to change the contents of STM. Exccution of a perceptual tcst makcs thc systcm "aware" of some perceivable feature of its cnvironment by storing information about that feature in STM.

The production system and the pcrceptual subsystcm are cach assumed to opcrate scrially only one production may be activatcd at a time. If the conditions for more than onc production are satisficd simultancously, only the first of thesc on thc list of productions is activated. Hence, the order of productions on the list is an intcgral part of the definition of the program; changing the ordcr could (and usually will) cause significant changes in the systcm's bchavior.

The advantages of production systems as programming formalisms, and the rcasons for thinking that stratcgics stored in the human central nervous system may bc organized as production systcms arc discussed in Newcll and Simon (1972, pp. 803-806), and in Ncwcll (1973). Thc particular kind of production system we are using herc has two advantages worth mentioning. First, it makes cxplicit exactly what information is hcld in short-term memory, so that processing assumptions that would place unrcalistic demands on ST'M are immediatcly dctectable. Second, the system makes cqually cxplicit what assumptions arc bcing made about perceptual capabilitics. Featurcs of the cnvironmcnt can only be taken into account by means of specific perceptual tests, and these tests can lead directly only to knowledge, not to action.

Simon (1975) describes a number of different strategies, among which we will consider herc only the goal-recursive strategy. The movepattern strategy is so different from these that it can be trivially distinguished from the other two with protocol data (Neves, 1977).

The goal-recursive stratcgy applics recursivcly a proccdure that allows the correct move to be gencrated for any configuration of the disks o the p. Basic to the procdur is the concept o a pyrami disks (i.e., the pyramid consisting of all disks of size K and smaller). The galmovig yraid () t a iven pg can beatai by recrsive application of the following procedure:

Try to move disk K to Peg (X):

If such a move has already bn made, create the goal of moving Pyramid (K—1) to Pcg (X).

If such a move can legally be made, make it.

If such a move cannot be made, create the goal of moving Pyramid (K—1) to thc Other pcg (i.e., the pcg that is ncither X nor the onc on which disk K Sslands).

T'o scc why this strategy works, consider an cxample. First, if all the disks arc in the starting position, the strategy first generates the goal of moving the pyramid of four disks to the goal peg. Sincc the largest disk cannot lcgally be moved, the goal is gencratcd of moving all the remaining disks to the other peg. Since thc largest of these remaining disks (IDisk 3) also cannot be movcd, thc goal is gencratcd of moving the pyramid of the two smallest disks to the goal pcg. This also fails, so the goal is now created of moving the smallest disk to the other pcg. This move is madc successfully. Thc goal-recursive stratcgy is sufficicently powcrful to gencrate the correct move in any configuration of disks on the pcgs. The production system implementing the goal-recursive strategy is shown in Figure 7-5, reproduced from Simon (1975).

P1. State = Problem-solved → Halt

P2. State = Done,Goal = Movc(Pyramid(k),A) Delete(STM), Goal ← Move(Pyramid(k-1),A)

P3. State = Can,Goal = Movc(Pyramid(k),A) Delete(STM), Movc(k,P(k).A)

P4. State = Can't,Goal = Movc(Pyramid(k),A) Delete(STM), Goal ← Move(Pyramid(k-1),O(P(k).A))

P5. Goal = Movc(Pyramid(k),A)→ Test(Movc(k,P(k),A))

P6. elsc→ Goal ← Movc(Pyramid(n),Goal-peg)

Figure 7-5

Production System for Goal Recursion Strategy

The perceptual strategy can be viewcd as a modification of the goalrecursive strategy (Simon, 1975). By using more sophisticated perceptual predicates and tcsts, the necd for goals of moving pyramids of disks can be climinatcd. The pcrccptual stratcgy idcntifics the biggest disk blocking a given move. The observation that a move can't be made because of Disk J is denoted Can't(J). Further, it is assumed that the largest disk not yet on the goal peg can be identificd perceptually. We will denote this disk by Biggest(J).

![](images/3e5703a1f90b27480c1ba1da48e5f2ae4643d0f6214ab7f4b61430efeac8d76f.jpg)  
Figure 7-6  
A Disk Configuration in the Tower of Hanoi Problem

The logic underlying the perceptual strategy can be illustrated with the disk configuration shown in Figure 7-6. The subject first notes that the largest disk not yet on thc goal pcg is Disk 2 (Biggest(2)), and generates the goal of moving this disk to the goal pcg. In testing the legality of this desired movc, the subject notices that it can't be made, and that IDisk I is the largest disk blocking it (Can't(1)). The subject then generates thc goal of moving Disk 1 to the pcg that is ncither the one where Disk 2 is located nor the onc to which Disk 2 is to be moved. This move can now be cxecuted.

Th production systm representation he pereptual staty, from Simon (1975) is shown in Figure 7-7.

```matlab
PI'. State = Problem-solved → Halt
P2'. Stale = Done,Goal = Move(k,P(k),A)
Delete(State), Deletc(Goal)
P3'. State = Can,Goal = Move(k,P(k),A)
→ Delete(State), Movc(k,P(k),A)
P4'. State = Can't(J), Goal = Movc(k,P(k),A)
→ Delete(STM), Goal ←— Move(J,P(J),O(P(k),A))
P5'. Goal = Movc(k,P(k),A)→ Test(Movc(k,P(k).A))
P6 Slate = Biggest(J)→ Goal ← Movc(J,P(J),Goal-peg)
P7. clsc  Test(Bggest-remaining)
```

## Figure 7-7

Prduction System or Perceptual Stray

We can predict what information will be heeded, successively, by subjects using citherof these two models in a particular puzzl configuration. To show how this is done, wc will cxamine in detail a few examples. To facilitate comparison between modcl predictions and protocol data, the two will be presented side by side for cach cxample.

Encoding Categories for Verbalizations. Neves (1977) wishcd to cncode verbalizations mentioning goals and subjccts' cvaluations of the feasibility of attaining the goals inthe current situation.There are thre possible outcomes for such an cvaluation or test (T):

(1) The goal is already attaincd or done (D);

(2) The goal can be attaincd in a single move from the current disk configuration (C);

(3) The goal cannot be attained directly from the current configuration (N).

Any goal can bc described as aiming to gct a given disk (N) on a particular peg (X): G(N,X).

This notation docs not provide for goals of moving whole pyramids rathcr than individual disks. In what follows, wc will not cncode such goals or predict thcir vcrbalization. With respect to thc pcrceptual strategy, we necd to cncode also verbalized rcasons why a goal can't be attained:"can't because of Disk K." Wc will cncode this at T(N(K)). Thc biggcst disk not yct on thc goal pcg (K) will bc cncoded as B(K). In situations where the protocol doesn't allow all aspccts of the cncoding to

## 338 Protocol Analysis

be assesscd, the missing information is codcd by the symbol "%." 'The following two cxamples show how protocol scgments will be cncoded according to these rules. (Note that numbers are used to designate both disks and pegs.)

The 4 has to go to the 3 [pcg], but the 3 [disk] is in the way. G(4,3), T(N(3))

The 4 has to o over, but there is sonthing in the way. G(4,%), T(N(%))

Empirical Evaluation of Predictions. Taking as ur example the disk configuration that wc uscd above to describe the two strategies, we will provide an explicit trace of all information entering STM for cach of the strategics, and the corrcsponding predictions of protocol scgments. Ft i:

```matlab
{Goal = Movc(Pyramid(4).3)}
Test(Move(4.3,3)). State = Done G(4,3), T(D)
{Goal = Move(Pyramid(3).3)}
Test(Move(3.3.3). Stale = Done G(3,3), 'T(D)
{Goal = Movc(Pyramid(2),3)}
Tesl(Movc(2,i,3)). Statc = Can't G(2,1), T(N(%))
{Goal = Move(Pyramid(1),2)}
Tesl(Move(1,i,2)), State = Can G(1,2), T(C)
```

The goals of moving pyramids have been put in brackets, because they are not predicted by the model (sec previous discussion). Tests of desired movcs arc interpreted as cxpressions of goals to make those moves.

For the same puzzle configuration, the information entering STM with the perceptual strategy is the following:

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \mathrm { B i g g e s t } ( 2 ) } & & { \mathrm { B } ( 2 ) } \\ & { \mathrm { G o a l } = \mathrm { M o v e } ( 2 , 1 , 3 ) } & & { \mathrm { G } ( 2 , 3 ) } \\ & { \mathrm { S t a t e } = \mathrm { C a n } ^ { \ast } ( 1 ) } & & { \mathrm { T } ( \mathrm { N } ( 1 ) ) } \\ & { \mathrm { G o a l } = \mathrm { M o v e } ( 1 , 1 , 2 ) } & & { \mathrm { G } ( 1 , 2 ) } \\ & { \mathrm { S t a t e } = \mathrm { C a n } } & & { \mathrm { T } ( \mathrm { C } ) } \end{array}
$$

Lct us look now at the thinking-aloud protocols collected for subi exibit the protocol of a subjct traind to use the goal-recursive straty for the samc puzzle configuration.

I'm looking at Peg 3, at the largest. which is already done. G(4,3), T(D)

So I move to the next to the largest, which is the 3. which is already done. G(3,%), T(1)

So I try to move thc 2, which is on Peg l. G(2,%)

Which I can't movc because a smaller one is above it. T(N(1))

So I move to Peg 2, which is the next iest, which is the 1, which   
I can move. So my move is I to 2. G(1,2), T(C)

Note that the encodings usc only verbalized information and not information about the disk configuration. If such information were consulted, it would be obvious that the undetermincd target in the sccond goal is Peg 3. The pcg for the third goal cannot bc dctermined simply to look ing at the actual configuration. To arguc that Peg 3 must have been intended would bc to go well beyond the information given. Although Disk 1 is not mentioned explicity in the fourth scgment, it can be inferred since it is the only disk smaller than Disk .For some idiosyncractic reason this subject consistently says "next to the smallest" when he means "the next smaller." In this protocol, this inconsistency can bc resolved by taking the most specific reference as the correct refcrence. In subsequent protocols, wc will interpret "next to the smallest" litcerally.

We look next at the protocol given by a subject trained to use the perceptual strategy.

The 4 and 3 arc in place.

The 2 has to go over. G(2,%)

So you have to move the I to the middle one. first. T(N(1)), G(1,2)

The first statement is problematic, as it could be argucd that it rcflects an intermediate state towards idcntifying thc biggest disk not yet on thc goal pcg. However, one could also arguc that it implies that the goals of getting Disks 3 and 4 on thc goal peg have already been attained. Since it does not reflect the sequencc of goal followcd by test, it is not codcd. The construction "goal so you havc to do X first" is rcadily scen as an climination of an obstacle, corresponding closcly to the idca bchind T(N(X)).

For the goal-recursive stratcgy, the protocol cncodings can be mapped directly onto the model predictions. There are only three minor mismatchcs. On two occasions it was impossible to assess toward which peg the disk is to be moved. in both cases the goal is vcrbalized, and it would be more accurate to refer to these omissions as lack of cxplicitness rather than incompletc verbalization. The third mismatch is the verba..ization of thc disk block ing the goal of moving Disk 2. A closer look at the corresponding protocol segmcnt shows that although the subject describes the blocking disk, he does not usc this information to generate the next goal. Thus, there is no violation of the basic structure of the goal-recursive strategy.

The protocol can also be mapped against the model for the perceptual stratcgy. Many of the vcrbalizations prcedictcd by the model are absent from the protocol: for example, the assessment of the biggest disk not yct on thc goal peg, and the two cvaluations of the goals that are generated. Hencc, the fact that the subject was instructed to use, and apparently did usc, the rccursive strategy is strongly supported by comparison of the protocol with the models.

The next protocols and model predictions are gencrated for the disk configuration shown in Figurc 7-8.

which is the I, that is on Peg 2,   
but can be moved. My move is   
therefore, I to 3.

Except for the subject not mentioning that the biggest disk was not yet on the goal peg, there is complete correspondence between the encodings of the human protocol and the predictions of the model.

Our cxamples demonstrate the range in completeness of verbalizations. Even when they are quitc incomplete, the subjects' strategies can be dctermincd with some certainty for individual disk configurations, and dctermining which model fits bcst for a number of disk configurations, the strategy can be assessed with high validity. If the disk configurations prescnted to subjects arc sclccted carefully, so that different models will make differcnt predictions for thcsc configurations, then the assessment of strategies can be made from just a fcw configurations.

## Mental Multiplication

There are several rcasons why mental multiplication of two-digit numbers is an attractive task for intensivc analysis. Farlier work (Dansereau, 1969; Dansercau & Gregg, 1967; Hitch, 1978) havc shown that subjects display a very orderly scquence of cognitivc processes in this task. This orderliness allows us to make very strong prcdictions about the order in which information is hecded, and hencc, verbalizcd. In the Appendix, sucts.We canus it ocalrat th subjevbalizatn ps, andus this calration i alyziindiiual ice in pe less stercotyped tasks.

The models we shall examine are aimed simply at identifying the information the subjcct necds in finding thc intermediate and final results of the multiplication. They leave open more fundamental issues of how the numbers are represented by the subject. The multiplication may be segmented into three major parts, to make the description casier to follow.The three major stages in multiplying dld2\*d3d4 (where each d is a digit from 0 to 9) are:

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { ( 1 ) } } & { { \qquad \mathrm { d } 1 \mathrm { d } 2 ^ { * } \mathrm { d } 4 = \mathrm { S } 1 } } \\ { { ( 2 ) } } & { { \qquad \mathrm { d } 1 \mathrm { d } 2 ^ { * } \mathrm { d } 3 \mathrm { d } 0 = \mathrm { S } 2 ( \mathrm { d } 0 = 0 ) } } \\ { { ( 3 ) } } & { { \qquad \mathrm { S } 1 + \mathrm { S } 2 = \mathrm { S } \mathrm { T } ( \mathrm { s o l u t i o n } ) } } \end{array}
$$

Our model may be described in terms of a transition nct for cach of thes stages. Wc willindicate the predicted verbalizations associated cxample 36 x 24.

Stage 1: Multiplication of d $\{ \mathrm { d } 2 ^ { * } \mathrm { d } 4 .$ In Figure 7-9, we show the transition net for this multiplication.Since it is almost self-explanatory, we will simply comment on some critical points.

F tht    he of $\mathrm { d } 2 ^ { * } \mathrm { d } 4$ I     s to be broken up into a digit (Sd), and a carry (Sc). Next ${ \mathrm { d } } 1 ^ { * } { \mathrm { d } } 4 \ ( \mathrm { \mathbf { S } } " )$ is ${ \mathrm { S } } " \left( { \mathrm { S c } } { \mathrm { ~ + ~ } } \right.$ $S ^ { \prime \prime } = S ^ { \prime } )$ . Finally, Sd and ${ \bf S } ^ { \ast }$ must be merged as $\mathbf { S } \mathbf { S } \mathbf { d }$

In a model for complete verbalization (M-C) wc assume that inputs, outputs, and operation are verbalized at cach substep. In an intermediate modcl (M-1) wc assume that in formation is verbalized at thc time it cnters attention and STM, but not when it is already in STM and rcused immcdiately as input to the next process. In a modcl for a low levcl of verbalization (M-I ) we assume that only inforimation that is ncwly gcncrated will be vcrbalizcd. ## PROCESSES WITH ALTERNATIVE REALIZATIONS

In many cognitive tasks, the cxact verbalizations of a subjcct arc not dctcrmincd uniqucly by the task, or even by the subjcct's stratcgy. Under such circumstances, assessing whether a protocol fits the prcdictions of a model is a little like judging whether a sentencc is grammatical. Linguistics has traditionally rclicd on native informants for making judmcnts of grammaticality. Only very rccently has it bcen possible to attempt such assessments automatically with the aid of computers.

In the same way, most analyses of protocol data are carried out by invcstigators who have had cxtensive expericncc with the cognitive processcs and thcir verbalization for thc task under study. Comparing a verbal protocol with the predictions of an information processing modcl involves a judgment not dissimilar to the judgment of grammaticality. Its validity depends at lcast partly on the consensus of the judges.

There are also somc structural similarities bctwcen utterances and cognitive processes that are worth noting. It is not uncommon for pcople to start to say something and then to stop and begin a new sentence that is completed. Pcople also stop midway in scntences, and then restart by repeating some words and then completing the sentence.Similar iterruptions and restarts occur in cognitive processes likc those wc have already examined in this chapter, cspecially processes like mental multiplication and addition that gradually cumulate information. In verbal reports, such interruptions or repctitions are marked by intonation or explicit comment. As far as possible, such occurrences should be identified when the verbal transcript is being preprocessed.

3 plus 0 is 3 2 and 8 is 10   
plus 8 is (uh) 11 and 5 is 15   
plus 1 is 12 and 9 will be 24   
plus 2 is 14 and 7 is 31   
plus 4 is 18 and 4 is 35   
plus 9 is 27 and 3 and 6 is 9 makes 44   
plus 0 is 27 3 and 4 is 7 makes 51   
plus 6 is (uh) 33   
plus 7 is 40   
plus 3 is 33   
no 43

## Addition of Digits

Returning to the addition task that was considered in an carlier section, we now go beyond thc original analysis, which was limited to reports of intermediate inputs and results, and undertakc a morc complete examination of thc full protocols. We shall scc how this additional information can bc used to constrain the possible modcls of thc cognitive process. Here are two protocols from the addition task:

Formal task analysis. M digits can be addcd togcther in many ways that form partial sums, and at last the final sum. At the most general level, the task can be described by a sequcncc of internal states, each containing the sct of digits not yet addcd and the partial sum just computed. The initial state would bc the sct of digits to be added, and the final state the sum of the whole sct of digits. The scquence of partial sums will bc defincd uniqucly if cach contains all the digits already summed. However, a subjcct might rctain scvcral partial sums for subsets of digits, thcse to be added together subscquently.

The formal task analysis does not providc more than hints as to how the subject might represent the task information internally. There are two kinds of information the subject needs to know at all timcs: (1) which digits have not yct bcen processed (the digits are available in a visually accessible row), and (2) the partial sums that have not yct been incorporated in the total.

Each partial sum can be represented by a onc- or two-digit number. We know that normal subjects, without extensive practicc, can retain only a fcw numbers in memory. Partial sums are particularly difficult to rctain, for they are modified and changcd frequently. From studies of short-term memory, Waugh and Norman (1965) showcd that only 4 digits could bc kcpt readily accessible in short-term memory while a task was bcing performcd. From thesc findings wc would predict that subjccts will use only a small number of different partial sums (probably onc, or possibly two) when thcy are trying to add scts of digits rapidly.

Assumc that the subject reinembers the digits to be added in terms of thcir location on the stimulus card. After more than four or five digits have bccn processed and addcd, a non-trivial amount of information is necded to kccp track of the processed and unprocessed digits. How much information is nceded depcnds on the order in which the digits are cumulated. Consider a random ordering, and an ordering from left to right, respectivcly (Thcrce is an X bclow cach processed digit.):

<table><tr><td colspan="10">Random case: 3 0 8 1 2 4 9 0 6 7 3</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>X</td><td></td><td>X</td><td></td><td>X</td><td></td><td></td><td>X X</td><td></td></tr><tr><td colspan="10">Preferred case:</td></tr><tr><td>3 0 8 1 2 4 9 0 6 7 3</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td>x x x x</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

In the random case, the identities of the five processed digits or the six unprocessed digits have to bc rememberced, a substantial memory load. In the ordercd case, the subject necd only kcep note of the last processed digit, or the one to be processed next.After a digit has been processed, attention moves to the one immcdiately to its right. We can use this large difference in STM requirements, together with our knowledge of the capacity of STM. to predict that subjects will, in fact process the digits in scquential order and not in random order.

Following Newell and Simon (1972), a similar argument can be made that limits on the processes available are the principal constraints o te procesi straty that will be us. If it is assu that the y available addition process combines two numbers into their sum, then the operations combining the stimulus digits and previously generated partial results, will be of three kinds:

Adding two digits to form a partial sum

II Adding one digit to a partial sum L1 + N = L2

III. Adding two partial sums + L,2 = I3

It is rcasonable to assume that most subjects will add pairs of digits by rccognition that is, by "tablc lookup" in the sums table that is already stored in I.T'M. However, whcn a two-digit number is added to a oncdigit number, scveral stcps may be required, and intermediate states may bc heedcd in STM. (For children, therc is good cvidence for intermediate states even in addition of single digits (Groen & Parkiman, 1972; Svenson, 1975).) Assuming that fast recognition processes will bc used when available in memory, the task of adding six digits may require various stratcgies, three of which arc illustrated in thc ncxt Figure (Figure 7-12).

## 4 2 6 3 59

Strategy 1: add single digits to partial results.

$$
4 + 2 = 6 \quad 6 + 6 = 1 2 \quad 1 2 + 3 = 1 5 \quad 1 5 + 5 = 2 0 \quad 2 0 + 9 = 2 9
$$

Strategy 2: add single digits together before adding onto partial

$$
4 + 2 = 6 6 + 3 = 9 6 + 9 = 1 5 5 + 9 = 1 4 1 4 + 1 5 = 2 9
$$

Strategy 3: combination strategy avoids 2-digit + 2-digit additions (exccpt for the number 10).

$$
4 + 2 = 6 \quad 6 + 3 = 9 \quad 6 + 9 = 1 5 \quad 1 5 + 5 = 2 0 \quad 2 0 + 9 = 2 9
$$

Figure 7-12

Comparison of Three Strategies for Addition

## 352 Protocol Analysis

These different strategies impose different loads on ST'M. With Strategy l, subjects need only move their gaze onc place to the right after cach addition. With the othcr strategics, subjects would have to look further ahcad and kccp track of sevcral partial results. If we assume that crrors are frcquently associatcd with losing the corrcct positions of "pointers," then the errors that subjccts make will be associated with the strategics they usc, and can bc uscd as data to idcntify those strategies.

What verbalizations will be cxpcct in thesc addition processes? For Type I additions $( \mathsf { M } + \mathsf { N } = \mathsf { L } )$ , we might postulate:

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \mathbf { M } } & { \mathbf { N } } \\ { \begin{array} { l l } { \mathbf { \eta } _ { 5 } } & { + } & { 3 } \\ { \mathbf { \eta } _ { \mathrm { { N P U T I } } } \mathbf { \eta } _ { \mathrm { { I 0 R } } } } & { \qquad } & { \mathrm { a c c e s s e d ~ f r o m } } \\ { \mathrm { r e c o g n i t i o n } } & { \qquad } & { \mathrm { m c m o r y } } \end{array} }  \end{array}
$$

For Type II additions $( \mathsf { I } . \mathsf { I } + \mathsf { N } = \mathsf { I } . 2 )$ , not all of the inputs may be vcrbalized. The stimulus digit may bc encoded dircctly without verbalization, and the intermediatc sum may have becn verbalized previously as output of the previous stcp, and hence not vcrbalizcd again.

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l r l r l } & { { \mathrm { ~ L 1 ~ } } } & { + } & & { \mathbf { N } } & { = { \mathrm { ~ L 2 ~ } } } \\ & { { \mathrm { ~ 1 5 ~ } } } & { + } & & { { \mathrm { ~ 4 ~ } } } & { = { \mathrm { ~ 1 9 ~ } } } \\ & { { \mathrm { p r e v i o u s l y } } } & & { } & { { \mathrm { ~ m a y ~ n o t ~ } } } & & { { \mathrm { ~ n c w l y ~ g e n e r a t e d } } } \\ & { { \mathrm { v e r b a l i z e d } } } & & { { \mathrm { ~ b e ~ v e r b a l i z e d } } } & & { { \mathrm { ~ i n t e r m e d i a t e } } } \end{array} }
$$

We would not expect verbalization of the processes involved in selccting the ncxt digit to be addcd, nor information about the "pointer" locations. These processes are likcly to be very rapid, and to operate on in formation that is not represented verbally. Evcn in this simplc addition task, the verbalizations often span scveral proccsses in sequence. For example, there are often references to scveral additions and their intermediate results, like: 6 and 3 is 9, which makes 31.

Here, the first addition (6 + 3) yiclds a result (9), which is added to the current intermediate result (evidently, 22) to give the result (31), a Typc III addition.

Advantages of Local Encoding. Wc havc now outlined a space of possiblc verbalizations of cognitive processes in thc addition task, and havc analyzed the STM limits on the stratcgics available to subjects in pcrforming the task. We have shown how protocols can be cncoded to reveal the steps taken and the information hceded. From thesc cncodings, we can draw many inferences about the organization and structure of the cognitive processes for solving the complete problems. This "bottom up" approach to encoding, in which cach verbalization is encoded ncarly independently of its context, scems to us to have advantages, where it is feasible, over more global approaches. We wish to consider these advantages in a little more detail.

In Chapter 6, wc discussed at somc length how to evaluate the evidential support for a given description of a protocol. A human encoder who uses a global approach will arguc that the mcchanisms postulatcd in his description are plausible, and will hope that other investigators will find his account persuasivc. One kind of argument that is frequently used is to show that the proposcd interprctation satisfies the constraints that are known to apply to the human information processing systcm.

Additional cmpirical support for an interpretation can be derived from a local cncoding approach. The strength of local cncoding is that cach scgment is encoded indcpendently (or ncarly indcpendently) of context. If the pattern of cach, or many, scgments can be predicted from a model, cach scgment provides an independent data point for the modcl. Consider, by way of cxamplc, thc following protocol:

## (Stimulus digits are 5 2 8 9 7 4 3 6 3 4)

<table><tr><td>Segment</td><td>Encoding</td></tr><tr><td>2 and 8 is 10</td><td>2+8=10</td></tr><tr><td>and 5 is 15</td><td>X+5=15</td></tr><tr><td>and 9 will be 24</td><td>X+9=24</td></tr><tr><td>and 7 is 31</td><td>X+7=31</td></tr><tr><td>and 4 is 35</td><td>X+4=35</td></tr><tr><td>and 3 and 6 is 9 makes 44</td><td>X+(3+6=9)=44</td></tr><tr><td>3 and 4 is 7 makes 51</td><td>X+(3+4=7)=51</td></tr></table>

If we postulate that this subject retains only onc intcrmediate sum betwcen scgments, wc can tcst this hypothesis by comparing the partial sums that arc verbalized as outputs of thc additions (e.g., 24, 31) with the unverbalized input to the subscquent addition (c.g., 35-4 = 31, 317 = 24). Thc probability that all seven intermcdiatc products would correspond in this way as a matter of chance is less than $1 0 ^ { - 6 }$ , 'The independence of the cncodings provides us with uncontaminated data for testing many hypotheses about the structure of the process.

In somc analyses of protocols, the process has bccn described in such dctail that computer programs can be constructed to retrace the cxact scquencc of steps. It should not be supposed that protocols are sufficiently informative to allow such programs to bc derived by purely deductive proccsses. As with all induction of thcorics from finite bodies of data, alternative descriptions may be possible of the same data. For cxample, if a subject adds the digits {3 6 3 4} into a sum, we cannot, from the protocol alone, determine that the order is from left to right. For suppose the protocol is: $3 + 6 = 9 ; 9 + 3 = 1 2 ; 1 2 + 4 = 1 6 .$

The alternatives are:

I ocal cncoding is hclpful in revcaling the alternative modcls that may be consistent with the protocol data. If the process of mapping the encodings on models is automatcd, possiblc interpretations may be revcaled that would be hard for a human cncoder to conceive of.

Error analysis. In our carlicr analysis of thc arithmctic task, we suggested that three types of crrors are particularly likcly to occur. The first two have to do with the gencration and rctention of internediate results. The last has to do with kccping track of the digits not yct added. In most protocols for the simple addition task, cach scgment contains at least one of the stimulus numbers and the partial result of an addition. This mcans that we can infer the partial sum bcfore and after cach addition. If we find a mismatch bctwcen thc intermediate sums in successive scgments, an crror in gcncrating or rctaining the sum is implicd. In the following protocol, two such mismatches are identified.

![](images/8acd5f55c7d69874665398531acc737383ab1212437ed8c70ac82ce190f44e3f.jpg)

The first crror (31 + 4 = 34) is most likely an crror in gencrating the su.The second (replacement o  as partial sum by ) isost likcly a memory crror. In thc protocol above, wc can find a onc-one mapping between the digits presented in the stimulus and those added, which precludes error in th sclection of the successivc digits for ition. It sees clcar (though it hasn't been don that a computer program could detect anddignos crrors of the kinds preent inthis protocol.

Inferring process models. A number of issucs arise in using protocol data to infer process modcls. We will consider three of them here:First, what generalizable inferences can be drawn from protocols; Second, how can the stability and instability of cognitive processes be studied using protocols; Third, what can we learn from retrospective reports about the addition task?

The information reported by subjects in the addition task consists almost exclusively of intermediate sums and stimulus digits.One may conclude from the absence of other information that sclection rules to choose the ncxt digits for addition and other stratcgic rules in the performancc stratcgy arc highly "automated" and governed in considerable part by recognition processes. There may, of coursc, be some differences in thesc processcs from one subject to another. Insofar as they lcad to different orderings of the processes, hencc to different sequences of digits, they can be inferred from the protocols. Examination of a large number of protocols cnables us to define the space of possible strategics, and to arrivc at conclusions about which stratcgics arc common, and which oncs rare in the population of subjects.

Buswell (1928) cites many intcresting cxamples of deviant rules for adding and subtracting numbers, infcrred from studies of school children. Bclow, we exhibit a couple of cxamples of unusual processing rules that would bc highly difficult to anticipate in advance and accommodatc in a coding scheme.
