The protocols for the first three days are virtually identical. The protocol for Day 5 is structurally the samc, although the verbalization of some steps has disappcared. This particular subject, at least, was very consistcnt over the pcriod of a weck in his organization of his cognitive processes while performing this task.

Since we do not have retrospective reports for this task, we can only hypothesizc what they will contain. We would cxpect that wc would obtain little information from them bcyond what is obtainablc from the concurrent protocols. The prediction stems from the hypothcsis that information not reported in the concurrent protocols was not heeded urigh atioout c t, henc is not available in I.TM for the retrospective report.

## RELIABILITY OF VERBAL REPORTS

The reproducibility of data on repcated test occasions is critical to its interprctation. An obvious approach to showing reproducibility is to give the same subjects thc same tasks on scveral occasions, and then compare thc verbal reports. In Chapter 3, wc have alrcady discussed the complex issues involved in rcproducing cognitivc processes cxperimentally. Systematic differenccs might arise from one reproduction to the next because of specific memory for the previous solution to the task, gradually acquired skill in the task, and generalizcd learning of skills applicablc to the task domain. Even in a single session, cognitive processcs in the same task may show considerable variation. Thus it is hard to produce identity in cognitive processes on two successive occasions.

In one particular kind of situation we are assured that successive verbal reports relect, accurately or not, the same cognitive process. This is when we obtain concurrent and retrospectivc reports from the same subject for the same task performance. In Chapter 5. we discussed some general differences between concurrent and retrospectivereports that are predicted by our model. The retrospcctive report may, in particular, be expected to be less complete than the concurrent onc, but they should agrec on thc information they both contain. In this scction, wc will review somc cvidence for this prediction of consistency.

There are only a few studies of the rcliability and consistency of verbal reports. We will review most of thc studics wc arc acquainted with together with some of our own data. We will organize our prescntation around scvcral studies that have addresscd both thc issucs of reliability and consistency. Our discussion will make cxtcnsive use of individual protocols, to give the reader somc fccling for the kinds of behavior that may be cncountered. Because of thc limitcd availability of data, we will draw only very tentativc gencralizations from thcm.

## Protocols for Anagrams

In this first example, we compare a concurrent protocol on an anagram problem with the same subject's retrospectivc rcport. We will carry out the comparison with local cncoding of the protocols, and will show that the rcsulting evaluation could bc carricd out automatically, without the intervention of subjective human judgment.

At the core of an anagram-solving procedurc, we assume, is a gencrate-and-test process, where alternativcs are generated under constraints. The constraints are imposcd by the subject's judgment that certain combinations of lcttcrs are "likcly" building blocks for words. In the protocols we will cncode thesc constraints togcther with the alternative words the subject gencratcs as possible solutions. To formalize matters, we will encode the constraints as:

## C:XYZ...

where XY7.. represent a scquential letter combination that the subject is testing, and the alternatives as:

## A:<Word>.

In this particular case, it would be casy to map the concurrent protocol on the retrospective onc automatically. Four of the five clements of the latter are matchcd by unique clements of the former. Only onc ncw element appears in the retrospective protocol; it mostly differs from the concurrent one by omission, as predicted. Since there are 1,230 logically possible constraints that thc subject could usc, the odds against agreement between two randomly gencrated constraints are about 1:106.

## Series Problems

Hagert (1980a, 1980b) has collected protocols from a subject solving fivc-term scries problems (an cxtcnsion of 3-term scries problems). His findings relate both to the relation bctwcen concurrent and retrospective reports for the same solution, and the relation betwcen thrce solutions to the same problem gencrated at onc-ycar intervals.

At the first session the subject was askcd to solve two five-term serics problems whilc thinking aloud, and was then askcd to give retrospective reports for his solution to the first problem and then his solution to the second problem. Although the two problems described different situations (pcople with different names in different situations), and uscd different primitives (i.c., "Icftmost" and "rightmost," instead of e and "right", the two problems were logical isomorphs.The names of the pcople in the two problems werc the same and the solutions identical.

In the retrospective reports from the first occasion, Hagert found that the subject was able to rcmember almost nothing from the solution.

The retrospective and concurrent reports differ most strikingly in the presence of several unsuccessful solution attempts in the latter. The instructions given to the subjects may account for thcir absence from the retrospective reports. Thc subjects wcre askcd: "IDid you remember anything from thc sccond problem? Is thcrc any information left over in which stcps you built thc scrics?" (Hagcrt, 1980a, p.43) There were markcd differences in thc information rcported for the two solutions. (Remember that the first solution was recalled first.) From these results Hagert infers that the information was stored·temporarily in a working mcmory of intermediate duration, although other interprctations are certainly possible. The suppression of information from the first solution can be labcled, although not cxplaincd, as "retroactive inhibition."

In Hagert's (1980b) analysis of the three successive protocols for the first problem, taken at one-ycar intcrvals, he notes that the protocols are not by any mcans identical. Even the solution paths arc different. On the irst occasion, the subject's first proposcd solution is incorrect, but the second onc, correct. A ycar later, thc first proposcd solution is also incorrect, although different from the onc a ycar carlier, while the second is again correct. On the final occasion, the subject gencrated the correct solution directly.

Hagert built a computer model, in the form of a production system, that prcdicted quite accuratcly thc information in 98% of the protocol segments from the first occasion. Howcver, the modcl could account for less than half of the fragments from the other two protocols. But with local and minor changes, the model could account for up to 90% of the segments in these protocols. The protocol from the second occasion could be described accurately if two processing crrors were allowed when p nvol    on  seul ordrings.Modifying a single rule involved in checking the correctness of the tentative orderings improved the description of the third protocol. Although thee modifications were post hoc changs, with the bent o hindsight, still the analysis indicates that the subjcct's cognitive processes remained substantially the same at a stratcgic levcl through three problem-solving sessions extending over two years.

## Concept Formation

In a study of concept attainment, Johnson (1962, 1964) gave some of his subjects the samc problem twice (the same sequences of instances and non-instanccs of the concept).In no casc did thc subjects' verbalizations indicate that they recognized the problem the second time. Given our carlier discussion of subjects' poor mcimory for sequences of instances in concept learning, thcir failurc to recognize the problems is quite plausible.

Of three students who cach solved the same problem twice, one solved Problem 5 in two different ways, "although hcr basic approaches werc about the samc." (Johnson, 1962, p. 38) Another subject solved Problem 2 twice "in identical ways, even down to making thc samc type of comment at the same point in both solutions." (Johnson, 1962, p. 38) A third subject failed to find the solution of Problem 4 twice, "both times in a manner characteristic of all hcr failures." (Johnson, 1962, p. 38) Johnson concludes that the method and strategy component of cognitive processes remains constant but that lower-level dctail may vary from trial to trial.

Johnson published the protocols only for the third problem. To makc the discussions of the protocols mcaningful, wc nccd to describe some of the details of the experiment. Subjects wcrc presented with a shect of patterns arranged in order. (Ihc samc shcet was uscd for all problems.) The subjects wcrc told that cach pattern could be classified uniqucly according to some rulc into two catcgories labcled by "rcd" and "green."The subjects wcre askcd to comc up with the rule by predicting the color for cach successive pattern with fecdback from the cxpcrimenter. The subjects had to write down the correct color for cach instance, hence at all times had available perceptually the information about all instances that had so far been presented. Each pattern consisted of five circles, which were either white or black, a total of 32 different possibilities.

Because the protocols are very lengthy, we will reproduce only the initial portions, and show how they were cncoded by Johnson. We will then discuss his encoding of the remaining portions.

Although the protocols are obviously different, there are some similarities in the hypotheses gencrated. For cxample, at Trial 3, the subject appcars, from both protocols, to noticc the positional relevance of the last circles. Whether similar consistency would appcar in protocols from other subjects wc cannot tcll. I.ct us, instead, look at Johnson's encoding of the protocol, including the later portions not reproduced here.

In thc ncxt table, we have translated Johinson's codes into English without comment. Johnson only spccifically cncoded hypothcscs that uscd features of the pattern. Hypotheses mentioning the scqucnce of reds and greens arc simply called sequential. Predictions bascd on similarities with previously prescnted instances with known color associations are called actuarial. The most striking similarity in the table between the behavior in the two sessions is the subject's tendency to bcgin with positional attributes and patterns and cnd up with actuarial predictions. Unfortunatcly, we havc littlc data from which to judgc to what extent thc same similarity appcars in comparisons between subjects.

Johnson was able to simulatc the hypothesis generating process for all his subjects using a single computer program and only varying a small numbcr of parameters. In the discussion of the simulation, Johnson notes that the subject whose protocol is given above never gcnerated hypotheses of the typc required for Problem 4 (i.e., "grcen if 2nd and 5th circles have thc same color"), and for thc same reason failed to solve three other problems. Johnson concluded, and wc would concur, that the similaritics in processes betwcen scssions reflect consistencics in subject's stratcgics and representations over time, rather than reproduction of cxplicit processing scquences.

## A Memory Task

Ericsson and Polson (in preparation) studied a waiter who could memorize dinner orders from more than 16 pcople without external memory aids. When we are looking at a highly skilled and rapid performan o ths i w woul expt h bily  the ct n sructure of the cognitive processing. Ericsson and Polson create a experimental analoguc to the restaurant situation by sclecting a subset of items (8 different steaks, 5 durations of cooking, 5 salad dressings, and 3 different starches). A computer gencrated a random combination of thesc items for simulated dincrs (representcd by photographs). The experimenter read the complete order (4 items) as the subject (the expert waiter) pointed to the corresponding facc. The subject was cncouraged to take all the orders as rapidly as possible without making crrors in the subscqucnt recall.

The waiter did not recall the orders as presented, but instead ralled them by category, that i rst  the alad resigs, the the staks, .For items within a category, he ford groups or chunks o four items. To remember these items, he relied on various mnemonic encodings, for example, remembering the first letter for salad dressings. The four letters for the dressings could often be recoded as an English word. The cxperimenters, to study the specificity of the memory skill, substituted items from other domains for dinner items, as shown in Table 7-5:

The subject used the same or similar cncodings for the corresponding itcms in the "dinncr" and "expcrimcntal" conditions. For example, he uscd letter recodings for the flowers and salad dressings, order information for time units and temperatures, and patterns for metals and starches.

As expected, the two concurrent protocols are remarkably similar. The differences relate to very local processes and not to the basic strucuofh iThe eropecv orts ble cacoth s coy or more closcly than they do the corresponding concurrent protocols. The protocol study, while only a single example of verbalizcd data from highly skilled performance, should demonstrate the feasibility of cxamining the stability and adaptation of processcs under varying conditions. There have been similar findings of rcliability in other rescarch on a 1 l nosis (Wortman, 1966).

## Summary

The comparisons of concurrent and rctrospective reports in this chapter and Chaptcr 5 are in substantial agrecment in showing that the reports arc mutually consistent, the rctrospective reports often omitting information contained in the concurrent reports. Comparison of thinkingaloud protocols for the same task on successive occasions shows greater variability. For somce highly practiced skills, likc mental addition and the memorization of our skilled waiter, thcre is remarkable consistency from one occasion to another. In the case of protocols where subjects had only a little experience with thc task, therc is littlc cvidencc that subjects reuscd specific information from a previous solution or acquircd gcncralized knowledge or skill for the task domain (Hagert's subject on the third occasion may bc a partial cxccption to this last claim). The variability observed in thc protocols may be vicwed as "local" (low-level) variability in the cognitive processes.

Morc important than the fact that there is somc variability is the equally prominent fact that there is grcat stability of the successive protocols in terms of stratcgics and representations.

## 372 Protocol Analysis

## CONCLUDING REMARKS AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS

In the beginning of this book we proposed that cognitive processes could be describcd as sequences of heeded information and cognitive structures and that vcrbal reports correspondcd to this hccdcd information. The examples discussed in this chapter illustrate how verbalizations can be cncoded and matched against hypotheses about the structure and sequence of the hecded in formation. The rather extensive literature using protocols as data has, for the most part, rclicd on mcthods and encoding schcmcs that are similar to those described herc, which we view mainly as extensions and cxplications of previous studies.

In the beginning of this book we argucd that verbal reports should be scen as onc of many sources of data about cognitivc processcs and structures, and that modcls and mcthods accounting for verbal data should not bc different, in principle, from accounts of other typcs of data. It is essential that the mcthodology for using vcrbal rcports be wholly intcgrated with the main strcam of expcrimental psychology and other arcas of cognitive science. Many of the most recent studies have quite successfully cmbedded the collection of verbal reports within expcrimcntal designs and have addrcsscd issucs of generalizability. However, wc nccd many more studics of this kind to provide models for verbal reports analysis.

Considerable progress has also bcen made toward intcgrating the morc dctailed analyses and modcls of verbal reports with cxisting theorics and modcls. Maybe the greatest challenge is presented by the mathematical models of performance of groups of subjects. We necd to be able to identify the structural and process characteristics that give rise to thesc confirmed regularitics of performancc. In Chapter 4 we made a first attempt to show how such a mapping can bc carricd out between gencral characteristics of heeded information and rcgularities of performance. We also want to point to Einhorn, Kleinmuntz, and Klcinmuntz's (1979) analysis showing the consistcncy bctween process models derivcd from verbal reports and the more traditional mathematical regression models. However, much more thcorctical and analytical work is required to achieve a complcte integration of these different bodics of data and theory.

In thc beginning of Psychology, many influcntial psychologists vicwed verbal reports, and more preciscly introspection, as the only valid method for data-collection in psychology. At a later period, during the rei o bhaviorism,vrl eorts walost totall rcted as ata.

It is now time for verbal reports to reassume their position as a rich source of data, combinable with other data, that can be of the greatest value in providing an integrated and full account of cognitive processes and structures.

In this appendix we will give some practical advice and information on how to clicit various types of verbal reports under standard conditions. Tasks and situations that require variations of the standard procedure are discusscd in Chapter .We will distinguish three types of verbal reorts, namcly talk-aloud, think-aloud and retrospective reports. Apart from discussing the verbal reports individually we will also describe the recommended procedurc, wherc both think-aloud and retrospective reports are clicited for the samc process. We should point out that the suggestions expressed herc are bascd on our experiencc and intuitions, rather than experimental studics. We hope such systematic studies will be made in the future.

First, we will briefly comment on some variables relating to the expmcntal situation, then we willdescribe instructions and warm up procedures for the two different types of verbalizing procedures.

In many studics we want to collect verbal reports for cognitive processes that are no different from those occurring in traditional cxperiments. Apart from the instruction to verbalize and thc production of the verbalization, the only differences are the presence of the monitoring expcrimenters and of the tape recorder.

After a short timc subjects become accustomed to both the experimenter and tape-recorder. In many tasks, especially problem-solving tasks, subjects get so involved in the task that little notice is taken of the environment, and situational factors have no real cffect. For other cases, wc havc found it better to keep the tapc-rccorder and the experimenter outsidc the view of thc subject. Most high quality tapc-rccorders are able to adapt their sensitivity automatically and thus givc good rccording quality cven when placcd slightly bchind the subject. The cxperimenter can also be scated bchind the subjcct. Evidence from verbalization of free associations in clinical situations suggest that it is bctter to have the analyst outside the view of the subject, like bchind the coach (Havens, 1973; Frcud, 1963, pp. 135-156). If rcmindcrs, like "kccp talking", are used, the subject will not fecl obliged to address the experimenter or turn around to answer him. The number of verbalizations that are social and directed to the cxperimenter may be uscd to evaluate how much the experimenter has intrudcd (cf. Ohlsson, 1980).

## TALK-ALOUD

When we arc interested in preserving the temporal propertics of the cognitive process we may ask our subjects to talk aloud. Of course, it is only for tasks wherc we can assume subjccts rcly on oral codcs that we can cxpect to gct much verbalization with such an instruction. The proccdure we usc contains a gcncral instruction and a couple of warm-up tasks. The warm-up tasks are sclcctcd to bc particularly easy to use "talk-aloud" with and onc's for which the cognitive processcs are well understood. Here is a samplc instruction for talk-aloud.

## INSTRUCTION:

In this experiment we are interested in what you say to yourself as you perform some tasks that we give you. In order to do this we will ask you to TALK ALOUD as you work on the problems. What I mean by talk aloud is that I want you to say oul loud everything that you say to yourself silenly. Just act as if you are alone in the room speaking to yourself. if you are silent for any length of time I will remind you to keep talking aloud. Do you understand what I want you to do?

Good, before we turn to the real experiment, we will start with a couple of practice problems. I want you to talk aloud while you do these problems. First, I will ask you to multiply two numbers in your head.

So talk aloud while you multiply 24 times 34!

## Good!

Now I would like you to solve an anagram. I will show you a card with scrambled letters. It is your task to find an English word that consists of ll the presented letters. For example. if the scrambled letters are KORO, you may see that these letters spell the word ROOk. Any questions? Please "talk aloud" while you solve the following anagram!

<NPEPHA = HAPPEN>

Good!

These warm-up cxercises have several purposes. First, we want subjects to begin talk aloud under circumstances where it is comparatively casy. Sccond, we want to make certain that all subjects talk aloud. If particular subjects appear not to verbalizc as much as normal subjects, one can give them more practice problems. Furthermore, one can ask them for a retrospective report after they complete the task. Either the subject will notice the discrepancy bctween' talk-aloud protocol and retrospection, or the cxperimenter will see that the subject did talk aloud. In our experience it is vcry rarc that subjects do not spontancously verbalize in a normal fashion after a couple of practicc problems. It also happens that subjects occasionally "think aloud" 'rather than "talk aloud". In this case subjects will verbally describc thc spatial arrangement of numbers in a mental multiplication and rcfer to them by their spatial location. Subjects can then bc asked to do the corresponding task silently to realize that they takc much longer while vcrbalizing, or the experimenter can ask "Did you say X to yourself silently?" Simply recording the time often leads subjects to the more rapid "talk aloud".

Sclecting tasks for warm-up excrcises is not casy. Somctimes mental multiplications are too difficult and embarrass thc subject. For such subjcts mental addition of 2- or 3-digit numbers may bc more appropriate. Other possible tasks arc, "Find as many words as possible thiat rhyme with "beef," "Gcnerate as many words as possible using the letters like 'ONDTERH!'."

## THINK-ALOUD WITH RETROSPECTIVE REPORTS

Rather than considering think-aloud and retrospective reports separately, we will discuss them as parts of a more gencral procedure, which we recommend be uscd whcther think-aloud or retrospective reports or both be given for the same cognitive process. The main rcason for combining them in a common warm-up procedurc is that for cognitive processes of intermcdiate duration wc cxpcct the "think aloud" protocol and the retrospective report to contain basically the samc information. Hence by having the subject give both reports for the same cognitive process we are in a position to assess completencss of thc think-aloud protocol and assure that the retrospective rcport contains an actual record of the cognitivc process. A general instruction with warm-up cxamples is given bclow.

In this experiment we are interested in what you think about when you find answers to some questions that I am going to ask you to answer. In order to do this I am going to ask you to THINK ALOUD as you work on the problem given. What I mean by think aloud is that I want you to tell me EVER YTHING you are thinking from the time you first see the question until you\_give an answer. I would like you to talk aloud CONSTANTLY from the time I present each problem until you have given your final answer to the question. I don't want you to try to plan out what you say or try to explain to me what you are saying. Just act as if you are alone in the room speaking to yourself. It is most important that you keep talking. If you are sileni for any long period of time I will ask you to talk. Do you undersland what I want you to do?

Good, now we will begin with some practice problems. First, I want you to multiply these two numbers in your head and tell me what you are thinking as you get an answer.

"What is the result of multiplying 24 x 36"

Good, now I want to see how nuch you can remember about what you were thinking from the time you read the question until you gave the answer. We are interested in what you aclually can R EMEMBER rather than what you think you must have thought. If possible I would like you to tell about your memories in the sequence in which they occurred while working on the question. Please tell me if you are uncertain about any of your memories. I don't want you to work on solving the problm again, just report all that you can remember thinking about when answering the question. Now tell me what you remember.

Good. Now I will give you two more practice problems before we proceed with the main experiment. I want you to do the same thing for each of these problems. I want you to think aloud as before as you think about the question, and after you have answered it I will ask you to report all that you can remember about your thinking. Any questions? Here is your next problem.

"How many windows are there in your parent's house?"

Now tell me ll that you can rmember about your thinking.

Good, now here is another practice problem. Please think aloud as you try to answer it. There is no need to keep count, I will keep track for you.

"Name 20 animals".

Now tell me allthat you can remember about your thinking.

This warm-up procedure is bascd on the observation that it helps subjects verbalize oral information concurrently. More importantly, the easy and dircct verbalization of such information makes it clcar to the subject that think-aloud involves concurrent verbalization of heeded thoughts rather a rctrospectivc and explanatory report, which subjects may be more familiar with. Hence, the first task, or morc if that is requircd, is the same as uscd to clicit talk-aloud vcrbalization.The nly difference is that with think-aloud instruction wc admit or even encourage verbalization of information that was cncoded in non-verbal .Thevell e is o pet th bct ih warup tsk he or she is comfortable in thinking aloud and provides reports of the same information in both retrospective and concurrent reports. Occasionally the expcrimenter may be required to point out differences bctween information reported during think-aloud and retrospectively reported information. Also, subjects may use a more retrospective mode of reporting during think-aloud, or during the retrospective report engagc in analysis of why they thought in a certain way, cspccially if they recognize making crrors. (For a morc dctailcd discussion of deviation from think-aloud and retrospective report, scc Chapter 5, 6, and 7). Most of these deviations can be sct straight by simply repcating key phrases of the gencral instruction. Some subjects may be bencfitted by interspersing silcnt trials, for which the solution timc is recordcd, to assure that they are not changing their mode of thought to accomodate verbalization.

During the actual cxperiment we rccommend that both think-aloud and retrospective reports be rccorded. Even for cognitive processes of long duration, where we know that thc retrospcctive report will be incomplcte, it will be quite useful. In this case, it will more clcarly convey the general structure of the process, as most of the dctailed information will not bc retricved, and retricval will use the higher-level organizational cues, like subgoals, or recall cues.

Wuerzburg psychologists, 54

Although the theory of verbal reports is psychologically interesting in and of itself, this book will undoubtedly be read for its more practical aspects, as well. It provides an excellent introduction for those who wish to learn protocol analysis and a valuable reference for those who use it. Ericsson and Simon give a detailed discussion of how to code and analyze protocols, including a significant number of worthwhile examples. It is very fitting that one of the pioneers of the modern use of verbal data, Herb Simon, should be one of the authors of this important and sorely needed book."

—MICHELENE T. H. CHI, UniverSity of Pittsl

This is an extremely important book. The use of verbal reports as data has been important in a number of areas of cognitive psychology. Protocol Analysis describes the strengths and weaknesses of the method and provides a conceptual framework within which to interpret the data generated by it. This is the kind of book that will become required reading for practitioners of cognitive science."

GARY M. OLSON, The University of Michig gan

This book is extremely well written. I believe it may persuade senior researchers in the field who have not used protocol analysis in the past to incorporate it in future work. More important I believe it will be an effective device for educating young psychologists in this important methodology. Thus, I would expect it to be useful both as a reference book and as a textbook."

BARBARA HAYES-ROTH, Stanford University
